This is the same coercion rule from Level 2’s vectors page, just showing up during import instead of inside c(). A CSV column is read in as one vector — and if even a single cell in an otherwise-numeric column contains genuine text that isn’t a recognized missing-value code, R has no choice but to make the entire column character, exactly the way c(1, 2, "N/A", 4) would become c("1", "2", "N/A", "4").
c(125, 123, "N/A", 117)   # the whole vector becomes character - just like a CSV column would
The fix isn’t really about variable types at all — it’s about making sure every genuine missing-value code is included in na.strings before import, exactly as the previous two pages covered. Once R correctly recognizes "N/A" (or "Unknown", or any other disguised code) as missing rather than as literal text, the column parses as numeric the way it should.
# Without na.strings covering "N/A" - forced to character by that one text value
read.csv("file.csv")$systolic_bp

# With na.strings covering "N/A" - correctly parses as numeric
read.csv("file.csv", na.strings = c("NA", "N/A"))$systolic_bp
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import
text:
49.2 Demonstration

# Importing without recognizing "N/A" as missing
broken <- read.csv("data/participants_messy.csv", skip = 2)

class(broken$systolic_bp)

[1] "character"

mean(broken$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE)

Warning message:
argument is not numeric or logical: returning NA
[1] NA

# Importing with na.strings correctly covering every missing-value code
fixed <- read.csv(
  "data/participants_messy.csv", skip = 2,
  na.strings = c("NA", "999", "N/A", "Unknown", "")
)

class(fixed$systolic_bp)

[1] "integer"

mean(fixed$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 122.4444
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import
text:
49.3 Explore
Run class(broken$bmi) and class(fixed$bmi) side by side — bmi has the same problem, caused by the disguised "Unknown" value instead of "N/A". Confirm that fixing na.strings resolves both columns’ type problems simultaneously, since both were victims of the exact same underlying issue.
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import
text:
49.4 Solve the Problem

participants_messy <- read.csv(
  "data/participants_messy.csv", skip = 2,
  na.strings = c("NA", "999", "N/A", "Unknown", "")
)

class(participants_messy$systolic_bp)

[1] "integer"

mean(participants_messy$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 122.4444
The original error — argument is not numeric or logical — is gone entirely, not because you worked around it, but because the true root cause (a disguised missing-value code silently forcing the column to character) was fixed at the source, during import.
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import
text:
49.5 Interpret the Result
class(participants_messy$systolic_bp) now correctly reports "integer", and the mean systolic blood pressure among participants with a valid reading is 122.4 mmHg — a plausible, trustworthy number, now that the column is genuinely numeric rather than text that merely looked numeric to a human reader.

Common Mistakes


Trying to fix a wrong column type after import, rather than fixing the cause during import. You can patch this after the fact with as.numeric() (Level 1), but it’s more fragile — better to prevent the coercion in the first place with correct na.strings, so every function downstream sees the right type immediately.

Assuming a “numeric-looking” column is safely numeric without checking class(). A column can contain 999 perfectly numeric-looking values and one stray piece of text, and the entire column still silently becomes character — always verify with class() or str(), never assume from appearances.

Not connecting today’s error message to yesterday’s fix. argument is not numeric or logical: returning NA is exactly the symptom of the disguised-missing-value problem from the previous two pages — recognizing this connection saves you from re-diagnosing the same root cause from scratch every time it appears in a new column.
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import
text:
49.6 Practice Problem
Without na.strings set to catch "Unknown", what is class(broken$bmi)? After correctly setting na.strings, what does it become? Confirm both with code.
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import
text:
49.7 Challenge Problem
A colleague says: “I fixed it — I just wrapped the column in as.numeric() after importing, and now mean() works.” Explain, in two or three sentences, what their fix does and does not solve, compared to fixing na.strings at import time (hint: think about what happens to the disguised text value itself under each approach, and whether every future column in future files would need the same manual patch).
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import
text:
49.8 Key Takeaways

A single unrecognized text value anywhere in an otherwise-numeric CSV column forces the entire column to import as character — the same coercion rule from Level 2’s vectors page, just triggered during import.
The right fix is making na.strings complete and correct at import time, not patching the column’s type afterward.

argument is not numeric or logical: returning NA is a strong signal that a column you expected to be numeric actually imported as character — check class() immediately.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Why does a numeric-looking CSV column sometimes import as character?

R randomly assigns types
A single unrecognized text value (like a disguised missing-value code) forces the whole column to character
CSV files cannot contain numeric columns
The column has too many rows


True or false. Fixing na.strings at import time is a more robust solution than patching a column’s type with as.numeric() after the fact.

Predict the output.
x <- c(10, 20, "missing", 40)
class(x)

Find the error. A student sees argument is not numeric or logical: returning NA from mean() and responds by wrapping the column in as.character() to “match its type.” What’s wrong with this response?


Show answers


b) A single unrecognized text value forces the whole column to character, exactly like c() coercion from Level 2.

True. Fixing na.strings addresses the root cause during import; patching afterward with as.numeric() works but must be repeated manually for every affected column, in every file, going forward.
[1] "character"

as.character() moves in the wrong direction entirely — the goal is a numeric column for mean() to work; the actual fix is recognizing the disguised missing-value code via na.strings at import (or as.numeric() afterward as a less robust patch), not converting further into character.
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import
text:
49.9 Where you’ve arrived
You started this level with a single clean read.csv() call from Level 1 and a vague sense that real data might not always be so cooperative. You’ve now read and written CSV, Excel, and .rds files, permanently fixed the working-directory problem with RStudio Projects, and — across the last four pages — taken a genuinely messy file apart piece by piece: extra header rows, disguised missing-value codes, fragmented text categories, and the coercion trap that ties them all together.
Level 4 picks up exactly here, with the systematic, scalable tools — case_when(), ifelse(), and more — for actually fixing the messiness you now know how to detect.
crumbs: 49  Understanding Variable Types After Import

title: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
text:
What you’ll build toward
By the end of this level you’ll take participants.csv — and the messier files from Level 3 — and turn them into datasets you’d actually trust for analysis: missing data handled deliberately, duplicates gone, variables named and typed the way your analysis needs, new clinically meaningful variables built from the ones you have, and categorical variables recoded and collapsed the way a real research question demands.

title: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

Identifying, removing, and replacing missing values
Detecting and removing duplicate records
Renaming variables and converting between character, numeric, and factor types
Creating new variables and recoding existing ones

Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: take a genuinely messy patient dataset — wrong types, duplicated rows, inconsistent missing-data codes — and turn it into something you’d trust enough to actually analyze, using nothing but base R.
By the last page, you’ll have built case_when() fluency — the single tool that replaces most of the nested ifelse() logic you’d otherwise be tempted to write, and the bridge into Level 5’s tidyverse.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset
text:
50.1 The R Concept
colSums(is.na(df)) applies is.na() to every column of a data frame at once, then sums each column’s TRUE/FALSE results — giving you a missing-value count per column in a single line:
colSums(is.na(df))
For an even quicker, broader first look, summary() on a whole data frame reports basic statistics for every column simultaneously, including an NA's count for any column that has missing values:
summary(df)
Between the two: colSums(is.na(df)) gives you exactly the missing-count-per-column answer your supervisor asked for; summary() gives you that plus a broader statistical snapshot (min, max, mean for numeric columns; counts for character columns) in one glance.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset

title: 50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset
text:
50.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

colSums(is.na(participants))

          id          age          sex       county  systolic_bp diastolic_bp
           0            6            0            0            4            0
         bmi       smoker
           5            0

summary(participants)

       id             age            sex               county
 Min.   :  1.00   Min.   :18.00   Length:100         Length:100
 1st Qu.: 25.75   1st Qu.:36.25   Class :character   Class :character
 Median : 50.50   Median :49.00   Mode  :character   Mode  :character
 Mean   : 50.50   Mean   :47.43   NA's  :6
 3rd Qu.: 75.25   3rd Qu.:58.75
 Max.   :100.00   Max.   :81.00
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset

title: 50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset
text:
50.3 Explore
Run colSums(is.na(participants)) and confirm the total missing cells matches what you’d get by adding the six individual sum(is.na(participants$column)) calls from earlier levels: 6 + 4 + 5 = 15. Then try sum(colSums(is.na(participants))) — a single number summarizing the entire dataset’s missingness at once.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset

title: 50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset
text:
50.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

colSums(is.na(participants))

          id          age          sex       county  systolic_bp diastolic_bp
           0            6            0            0            4            0
         bmi       smoker
           5            0
You can now answer your supervisor completely and precisely: “Three variables have missing data — age (6 participants), systolic_bp (4 participants), and bmi (5 participants). The other five variables — id, sex, county, diastolic_bp, and smoker — are fully complete.”
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset

title: 50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset
text:
50.5 Interpret the Result
colSums(is.na(participants)) reveals the missingness pattern across the entire dataset in one line: 15 missing cells total, concentrated in exactly three numeric variables, with no missingness at all in the categorical variables or diastolic_bp. This kind of complete picture — rather than checking columns one at a time and hoping you didn’t miss one — is exactly what a supervisor or a methods section of a paper expects before any analysis proceeds.

Common Mistakes


Checking missingness column by column and losing track. With more than a handful of variables, it’s easy to forget to check one — colSums(is.na(df)) checks all of them, guaranteed, every time.

Confusing colSums() with rowSums(). colSums(is.na(df)) counts missing values per column (down each column); rowSums(is.na(df)) counts missing values per row (across each row) — genuinely different questions, both useful, easy to mix up by name alone.

Treating summary()’s output as the full picture. It’s an excellent quick overview, but for character columns it only reports Length, Class, and Mode — not the counts or categories table() would show. Use both tools, not just one.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset

title: 50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset
text:
50.6 Practice Problem
Run rowSums(is.na(participants)) and then table(rowSums(is.na(participants))) — this tells you how many participants have 0 missing values, how many have exactly 1, and so on. How many participants have zero missing data across all 8 variables?
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset

title: 50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset
text:
50.7 Challenge Problem
Using colSums(is.na(participants)), identify which single variable has the most missing data, and calculate what percentage of the 100 participants that represents (using arithmetic from Level 1).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset

title: 50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset
text:
50.8 Key Takeaways


colSums(is.na(df)) reports the number of missing values in every column of a data frame at once, replacing repetitive one-column-at-a-time checks.

summary(df) gives a broader statistical snapshot of every column simultaneously, including an NA's count where relevant.

rowSums(is.na(df)) answers a different, complementary question: how much missing data does each individual row (participant) have.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does colSums(is.na(df)) return?

The total number of rows in df

The number of missing values in each column
The number of missing values in each row
A logical vector the same size as df



True or false. colSums() and rowSums() answer the same question.
Predict the output. If a data frame has 3 columns and column x has 2 missing values while y and z have none, what would colSums(is.na(df)) show for x?
Write the code. Write the single line of code to check missing-value counts across every column of a data frame called survey_data.


Show answers


b) The number of missing values in each column.

False. colSums() summarizes down each column (per variable); rowSums() summarizes across each row (per observation) — different, complementary questions.
2
colSums(is.na(survey_data))
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
50  Identifying Missing Values Across a Dataset

title: 51  Removing Missing Values
text:
51.1 The R Concept
na.omit() removes any row with at least one missing value, across every column of a data frame:
na.omit(df)
complete.cases() gives you more control — it returns a logical vector flagging which rows have no missing data, which you can then use with the row-indexing you learned in Level 2, optionally restricted to just the columns you care about:
complete.cases(df)                    # TRUE for rows complete across ALL columns
complete.cases(df[, c("age", "bmi")]) # TRUE for rows complete in just these two columns
df[complete.cases(df), ]              # keep only the fully complete rows
The distinction matters: na.omit() is quick but all-or-nothing across every column; complete.cases() lets you decide exactly which columns’ completeness actually matters for the analysis at hand — precisely your supervisor’s request.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 51  Removing Missing Values
text:
51.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

nrow(participants)

[1] 100

# na.omit() - removes a row if ANY column has a missing value
complete_all <- na.omit(participants)
nrow(complete_all)

[1] 85

# complete.cases() restricted to just the 3 columns that matter for this analysis
keep <- complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "systolic_bp", "bmi")])
complete_relevant <- participants[keep, ]
nrow(complete_relevant)

[1] 85
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 51  Removing Missing Values
text:
51.3 Explore
These two approaches happen to agree here (85 rows) because age, systolic_bp, and bmi are the only columns with any missingness at all in this dataset (Level 4’s first page confirmed id, sex, county, diastolic_bp, and smoker are fully complete). Try nrow(na.omit(participants[, c("sex", "county")])) — since neither column has any missing data, what would you expect, and does the result confirm it?
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 51  Removing Missing Values
text:
51.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Complete-case analysis restricted to exactly the variables your supervisor cares about
keep <- complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "systolic_bp", "bmi")])
participants_complete <- participants[keep, ]

nrow(participants_complete)

[1] 85

mean(participants_complete$age)

[1] 47.22353
Notice mean() no longer needs na.rm = TRUE at all here — every remaining row is already guaranteed complete for these three variables, so there’s nothing left for na.rm to exclude.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 51  Removing Missing Values
text:
51.5 Interpret the Result
85 of the 100 participants have complete data across age, systolic_bp, and bmi — the other 15 are missing at least one of the three. Restricting to exactly these three columns (rather than na.omit()’s all-or-nothing approach across every column) matters in general, though here the two approaches happen to produce the same 85 rows since no other columns in this particular dataset have any missing values.

Common Mistakes


Using na.omit() on a whole data frame when only a few columns matter. If a dataset had missingness in a column irrelevant to your analysis, na.omit() would still drop those rows unnecessarily — complete.cases() restricted to relevant columns avoids discarding usable data.

Losing track of how many rows were dropped. Always compare nrow() before and after — silently going from 100 to 85 rows without noticing is a common way to accidentally misreport a sample size in a report.

Forgetting that removing rows changes more than one column at once. Once you filter to complete cases for age, systolic_bp, and bmi, every other column (like smoker) also loses those same 15 rows — even though smoker itself had no missing data.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 51  Removing Missing Values
text:
51.6 Practice Problem
Using complete.cases(), create a subset of participants that is complete for bmi alone, and report how many rows remain (it should match the earlier missingness count from the previous page).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 51  Removing Missing Values
text:
51.7 Challenge Problem
Compare nrow(na.omit(participants)) to nrow(participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "systolic_bp", "bmi")]), ]). They should match in this dataset — explain, in one or two sentences, why they match here, and describe a hypothetical change to the dataset that would make them produce different results.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 51  Removing Missing Values
text:
51.8 Key Takeaways


na.omit() removes any row with missing data in any column; complete.cases() lets you restrict that check to just the columns relevant to your analysis.
Always compare row counts before and after removing missing data, and report how many rows (and why) were excluded.
Removing rows for missingness in one variable also removes those same rows’ data for every other variable — a side effect worth being deliberate about.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does na.omit(df) do?

Replaces missing values with 0
Removes any row with at least one missing value, across all columns
Removes only missing values in numeric columns
Removes columns with missing data


True or false. complete.cases() can be restricted to check completeness in just a subset of columns.
Predict the output. If df has 50 rows and 5 have missing age, and no other column has missing data, what would nrow(na.omit(df)) return?
Write the code. Write the code to keep only the rows of survey_data that are complete for the column income.


Show answers


b) Removes any row with at least one missing value, across all columns.

True. complete.cases(df[, c("col1", "col2")]) restricts the completeness check to just those columns.
45

survey_data[complete.cases(survey_data[, "income"]), ] (or equivalently, survey_data[!is.na(survey_data$income), ])
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 52  Replacing Missing Values
text:
52.1 The R Concept
You can assign directly to the subset of a vector identified by is.na(), replacing only the missing positions and leaving everything else exactly as it was:
x[is.na(x)] <- replacement_value
The most common simple replacement is the variable’s own mean or median — this is called mean imputation (or median imputation), and it’s a reasonable, simple starting strategy, though it does understate the true variability in the data (a limitation Level 12’s multiple imputation addresses more rigorously later in the course).
df$bmi[is.na(df$bmi)] <- mean(df$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)
Notice the na.rm = TRUE is still required inside the mean() call on the right-hand side — you’re calculating the mean from the non-missing values, in order to fill in the missing ones.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 52  Replacing Missing Values
text:
52.2 Demonstration

bmi_values <- c(22.1, NA, 27.5, 24.0, NA, 30.2)

# The mean, calculated only from the non-missing values
mean(bmi_values, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 25.95

# Replace only the NA positions with that mean
bmi_values[is.na(bmi_values)] <- mean(bmi_values, na.rm = TRUE)
bmi_values

[1] 22.10 25.95 27.50 24.00 25.95 30.20
Notice the two originally missing positions are now both filled with 25.95 — the mean of the four originally non-missing values — while every real, originally observed value is completely untouched.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 52  Replacing Missing Values
text:
52.3 Explore
Try running mean(bmi_values) again after the replacement above (no na.rm needed now, since there are no more NAs) — compare it to the original 25.95. It’s identical, which makes sense: replacing missing values with the mean can never change the mean itself, only reduce the apparent spread of the data (a limitation worth remembering).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 52  Replacing Missing Values
text:
52.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Preserve the original column so you can still compare before/after if needed
participants$bmi_imputed <- participants$bmi
participants$bmi_imputed[is.na(participants$bmi_imputed)] <- mean(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)

sum(is.na(participants$bmi_imputed))

[1] 0

nrow(participants)

[1] 100
All 100 participants now remain in the dataset — none dropped — with the 5 originally missing BMI values replaced by the sample mean, kept in a clearly separate bmi_imputed column so the original, unmodified bmi column is still available for comparison or a different analysis strategy later.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 52  Replacing Missing Values
text:
52.5 Interpret the Result
sum(is.na(participants$bmi_imputed)) returning 0 confirms every missing BMI value has been filled in, and nrow(participants) still showing 100 confirms no participant was dropped — directly satisfying your supervisor’s request to retain the full sample, at the cost of slightly understating how much BMI actually varies across the 5 imputed participants.

Common Mistakes


Overwriting the original column directly. Replacing participants$bmi in place destroys the record of which values were originally missing — always create a new column (like bmi_imputed) so you can compare, audit, or try a different imputation strategy later.

Forgetting na.rm = TRUE inside the replacement calculation itself. mean(participants$bmi) without na.rm = TRUE returns NA (Level 1), and assigning NA to replace NA accomplishes nothing.

Treating mean imputation as a universally correct default. It’s a simple, defensible starting point for a small amount of missing data, but it artificially shrinks the variability of the imputed variable — Level 12 covers more rigorous approaches (multiple imputation) for when this matters more.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 52  Replacing Missing Values
text:
52.6 Practice Problem
Using participants$age, create a new column age_imputed with the missing age values replaced by the median age (median(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)) rather than the mean. Confirm sum(is.na(participants$age_imputed)) is 0.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 52  Replacing Missing Values
text:
52.7 Challenge Problem
Explain, in two or three sentences, why replacing missing values with the mean is a reasonable default for a small fraction of missing data (like 5 out of 100 BMI values here) but becomes more questionable as the fraction of missing data grows larger — say, if 40 of 100 values were missing instead.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 52  Replacing Missing Values
text:
52.8 Key Takeaways


x[is.na(x)] <- value replaces only the missing positions in a vector, leaving every other value untouched.
Mean (or median) imputation is a simple strategy that keeps every row in the dataset, at the cost of slightly understating the imputed variable’s true variability.
Always store an imputed variable in a new column, preserving the original (with its real missing values intact) for comparison or a different strategy later.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does x[is.na(x)] <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE) do?

Removes missing values from x

Replaces missing values in x with the mean of its non-missing values
Replaces every value in x with the mean
Converts x to character


True or false. Mean imputation increases the apparent variability of a variable.

Predict the output.
x <- c(10, NA, 20, NA, 30)
x[is.na(x)] <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
x


Find the error.
participants$bmi[is.na(participants$bmi)] <- mean(participants$bmi)



Show answers


b) Replaces missing values in x with the mean of its non-missing values.

False. It decreases apparent variability — imputed values all equal the mean, reducing the overall spread compared to the true, unobserved data.

[1] 10 20 20 20 30 (the mean of 10, 20, 30 is 20, filled into both NA positions).
The right-hand side mean(participants$bmi) has no na.rm = TRUE, so it returns NA — and assigning NA to replace NA accomplishes nothing. It should be mean(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 53  Detecting Duplicates
text:
53.1 The R Concept
duplicated(x) returns a logical vector, TRUE for any element (or, for a data frame, any row) that is an exact repeat of an earlier one:
duplicated(df)
Critically, duplicated() marks only the second and later occurrences as TRUE — the first occurrence of a value is always FALSE, even though it’s the one being repeated. This asymmetry is deliberate: it directly tells you which rows you could safely remove while keeping exactly one copy of each.
sum(duplicated(df))    # how many duplicate rows exist
df[duplicated(df), ]   # view just the duplicate rows themselves
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 53  Detecting Duplicates
text:
53.2 Demonstration

# A simple example to see the FALSE/TRUE pattern clearly
ages <- c(45, 62, 45, 38, 62, 62)
duplicated(ages)

[1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE
Notice: the first 45 and the first 62 are both FALSE (not yet a repeat of anything); the second 45 and every repeat of 62 after the first are TRUE.

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Simulating the two-research-assistants scenario: duplicate participant #1's row
participants_dup <- rbind(participants, participants[1, ])

nrow(participants_dup)

[1] 101

sum(duplicated(participants_dup))

[1] 1
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 53  Detecting Duplicates
text:
53.3 Explore
Run which(duplicated(participants_dup)) — this tells you the exact row position of the duplicate, rather than just how many exist. Then run participants_dup[duplicated(participants_dup), ] to view the duplicated row’s actual contents.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 53  Detecting Duplicates
text:
53.4 Solve the Problem

participants_dup <- rbind(participants, participants[1, ])

# How many duplicate rows exist?
sum(duplicated(participants_dup))

[1] 1

# Which row is it, and what does it contain?
participants_dup[duplicated(participants_dup), ]

    id age    sex  county systolic_bp diastolic_bp  bmi smoker
1.1  1  46 Female Oakford         125           79 35.1     No
You’ve now confirmed the research assistants’ suspicion precisely: participant #1’s row was entered twice, and you know exactly what it contains — ready to remove on the next page.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 53  Detecting Duplicates
text:
53.5 Interpret the Result
duplicated() correctly flags the second copy of participant #1 as a duplicate (TRUE) while leaving the first, original entry as FALSE — exactly the behavior you need to identify and later remove the redundant copy without losing the original.

Common Mistakes


Expecting both copies of a duplicate to be flagged TRUE. Only the second (and any later) occurrence is TRUE; the first is always FALSE. If you want to see all copies of anything that has a duplicate, you’d need a different check (like duplicated(x) | duplicated(x, fromLast = TRUE)).

Confusing exact row duplicates with duplicate IDs. duplicated(df) by default checks whether every column matches exactly — two rows with the same id but a corrected age value would not be flagged as duplicates by this check (a distinct, trickier problem covered in this page’s challenge).

Assuming duplicates can’t happen in “clean” data. They’re extremely common in real research data entry — merged files, resubmitted forms, and exactly this page’s two-research-assistants scenario are all frequent causes.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 53  Detecting Duplicates
text:
53.6 Practice Problem
Using participants_dup from this page, run table(duplicated(participants_dup)) and interpret both counts: how many rows are marked FALSE (unique or first occurrence) and how many are marked TRUE (a repeat).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 53  Detecting Duplicates
text:
53.7 Challenge Problem
Suppose instead the second research assistant’s entry for participant #1 had a typo — age recorded as 47 instead of the correct 46, with every other column identical. Would duplicated() still catch this as a duplicate? Explain why or why not, and describe (in words) what you’d need to check instead to catch a “duplicate participant with conflicting values” like this one.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 53  Detecting Duplicates
text:
53.8 Key Takeaways


duplicated(df) flags rows that are exact repeats of an earlier row; the first occurrence is always FALSE, later copies are TRUE.

sum(duplicated(df)) counts duplicate rows; df[duplicated(df), ] shows you their actual contents.

duplicated() only catches exact row matches — a repeated participant with even one differing value (like a corrected typo) will not be flagged by this check alone.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. For c(5, 5, 5), what does duplicated() return?

TRUE TRUE TRUE
FALSE TRUE TRUE
FALSE FALSE TRUE
FALSE FALSE FALSE


True or false. duplicated() marks the first occurrence of a repeated value as TRUE.

Predict the output.
x <- c("a", "b", "a", "a")
sum(duplicated(x))

Write the code. Write the code to view just the duplicate rows of a data frame called survey_data.


Show answers


b) FALSE TRUE TRUE — the first 5 is not yet a repeat.

False. The first occurrence is always FALSE; only subsequent repeats are TRUE.

[1] 2 — the 3rd and 4th "a" are both repeats of the 1st.
survey_data[duplicated(survey_data), ]
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 54  Removing Duplicates
text:
54.1 The R Concept
Two equivalent approaches remove duplicate rows, keeping the first occurrence of each:
df[!duplicated(df), ]   # keep only rows where duplicated() is FALSE
unique(df)               # shortcut: does exactly the same thing for a whole data frame
unique(), which you first met on vectors in Level 2, works the same way on entire data frames — it’s really just a more convenient wrapper around !duplicated(df).
nrow(df)                  # before
nrow(unique(df))          # after - should be nrow(df) - sum(duplicated(df))
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 54  Removing Duplicates
text:
54.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants_dup <- rbind(participants, participants[1, ])
nrow(participants_dup)

[1] 101

# Approach 1: explicit logical indexing
participants_fixed <- participants_dup[!duplicated(participants_dup), ]
nrow(participants_fixed)

[1] 100

# Approach 2: the unique() shortcut - identical result
participants_fixed2 <- unique(participants_dup)
nrow(participants_fixed2)

[1] 100

identical(participants_fixed, participants_fixed2)

[1] TRUE
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 54  Removing Duplicates
text:
54.3 Explore
Confirm identical(participants_fixed, participants) — after removing the duplicate you added, does the dataset exactly match the original, untouched participants? This confirms the fix is complete and introduced no side effects beyond removing the one duplicate row.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 54  Removing Duplicates
text:
54.4 Solve the Problem

participants_dup <- rbind(participants, participants[1, ])

nrow(participants_dup)

[1] 101

participants_clean <- unique(participants_dup)

nrow(participants_clean)

[1] 100

sum(duplicated(participants_clean))

[1] 0
Exactly 100 rows remain — one copy of every participant, with the accidental double-entry from the two research assistants fully resolved.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 54  Removing Duplicates
text:
54.5 Interpret the Result
nrow(participants_clean) returning to 100 (down from the temporarily inflated 101), combined with sum(duplicated(participants_clean)) confirming 0 remaining duplicates, gives you concrete proof the dataset is now exactly as it should be — ready to hand back to your supervisor with confidence, not just a hopeful assumption.

Common Mistakes


Removing duplicates before confirming which rows they actually are. Always run the detection step (previous page) first, so you know what you’re removing and can sanity-check the before/after row counts make sense.

Assuming unique() and duplicated() disagree in some way. They don’t — unique(df) is defined to produce exactly the same result as df[!duplicated(df), ]; either is correct, and which one you reach for is purely a matter of style.

Not rechecking after removal. Running sum(duplicated(participants_clean)) afterward and confirming it’s 0 is a cheap, valuable habit — don’t just assume the removal worked because no error appeared.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 54  Removing Duplicates
text:
54.6 Practice Problem
Create test_dup <- rbind(participants, participants[50, ], participants[50, ]) (duplicating row 50 twice, so it appears three times total). Confirm nrow(test_dup) is 102, then use unique() to reduce it back to 100, and verify with sum(duplicated(...)).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 54  Removing Duplicates
text:
54.7 Challenge Problem
Recall the previous page’s challenge: a duplicate participant with one differing value (like a corrected age) would not be caught by duplicated() or unique(), since they only catch exact row matches. Describe, in words, what additional check based on id alone (ignoring the other columns) would be needed to catch this trickier kind of duplicate — you don’t need to write the code, just describe the logic.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 54  Removing Duplicates
text:
54.8 Key Takeaways


df[!duplicated(df), ] and unique(df) are equivalent ways to remove duplicate rows, keeping the first occurrence of each.
Always detect and confirm duplicates before removing them, and reconfirm with sum(duplicated(...)) afterward that none remain.
Both approaches only catch exact row matches — a “duplicate” participant with even one differing value requires a different, ID-based check (not covered by duplicated() alone).


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does unique(df) do?

Removes all rows with missing data
Removes duplicate rows, keeping the first occurrence of each
Removes the first occurrence of any repeated row
Sorts the data frame


True or false. df[!duplicated(df), ] and unique(df) produce the same result.
Predict the output. If df has 20 rows and 3 are exact duplicates of earlier rows, what would nrow(unique(df)) return?
Write the code. Write the code to remove duplicate rows from a data frame called survey_data, using the unique() shortcut.


Show answers


b) Removes duplicate rows, keeping the first occurrence of each.

True. They are equivalent — unique() is a convenience wrapper around the same logic.
17
survey_data <- unique(survey_data)
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 55  Renaming Variables
text:
55.1 The R Concept
names(df) (Level 2) can be assigned to directly, and — combined with logical or position-based indexing — you can rename specific columns without disturbing any others:
names(df)[names(df) == "old_name"] <- "new_name"
Read this carefully: names(df) == "old_name" produces a logical vector, TRUE at the one position matching "old_name"; that logical vector then indexes into names(df) on the left-hand side, replacing just that one name.
For renaming several columns at once, you can also assign a full replacement vector for names(), or rename by position if you know exactly which column index you need:
names(df)[5] <- "sbp"   # rename the 5th column specifically, by position
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 55  Renaming Variables
text:
55.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

names(participants)

[1] "id"           "age"          "sex"          "county"       "systolic_bp"
[6] "diastolic_bp" "bmi"          "smoker"

names(participants)[names(participants) == "systolic_bp"] <- "sbp"
names(participants)[names(participants) == "diastolic_bp"] <- "dbp"

names(participants)

[1] "id"     "age"    "sex"    "county" "sbp"    "dbp"    "bmi"    "smoker"

# The data itself is completely unchanged - only the column labels
head(participants$sbp)

[1] 125 123 130 117 139 117
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 55  Renaming Variables
text:
55.3 Explore
Try renaming by position instead: names(participants)[5] <- "systolic_bp_renamed". This works too, but is riskier — if the column order ever changes (say, after a join in Level 6), a position-based rename would silently rename the wrong column. Name-based renaming (matching on the current name, as in the solution) is more robust against exactly this kind of accidental reordering.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 55  Renaming Variables
text:
55.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

names(participants)[names(participants) == "systolic_bp"] <- "sbp"
names(participants)[names(participants) == "diastolic_bp"] <- "dbp"

names(participants)

[1] "id"     "age"    "sex"    "county" "sbp"    "dbp"    "bmi"    "smoker"
Every script you write from this point forward can now use participants$sbp and participants$dbp, matching your department’s data dictionary exactly, with the underlying blood pressure values themselves completely unchanged.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 55  Renaming Variables
text:
55.5 Interpret the Result
The column names now read sbp and dbp instead of systolic_bp and diastolic_bp, but every value in every row is identical to before — renaming a column is purely a labeling change, with zero effect on the data itself, exactly as you’d want when the only thing that needed to change was compliance with a naming standard.

Common Mistakes


Renaming by position when the column order might change. A position-based rename (names(df)[5] <- ...) silently renames whichever column is currently 5th — if a join or reordering operation shifts columns around later, this becomes a subtle, hard-to-spot bug.

Misspelling the old name in a name-based rename. names(df)[names(df) == "Systolic_BP"] <- "sbp" (wrong capitalization) matches nothing — the logical vector is all FALSE, so no column gets renamed, with no error or warning at all.

Forgetting that renaming doesn’t reorder columns. After renaming, sbp and dbp still sit in the same position in the data frame as systolic_bp and diastolic_bp did — renaming and reordering are two entirely separate operations.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 55  Renaming Variables
text:
55.6 Practice Problem
Rename the bmi column to bmi_kg_m2 (a more explicit, unit-labeled name), using the name-based approach, and confirm with names() that it worked.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 55  Renaming Variables
text:
55.7 Challenge Problem
Rename sex to sex_reported and smoker to smoking_status in a single block of code (two separate rename lines are fine), then use str() to confirm both the new names and the underlying data are exactly as expected.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 55  Renaming Variables
text:
55.8 Key Takeaways


names(df)[names(df) == "old_name"] <- "new_name" renames a column by matching its current name, leaving the underlying data completely untouched.
Name-based renaming is more robust than position-based renaming, which silently breaks if column order changes later.
Renaming never reorders or modifies the data itself — it only changes the label used to refer to a column.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does names(df)[names(df) == "age"] <- "participant_age" do?

Deletes the age column
Renames the age column to participant_age

Changes every value in the age column
Creates a new column called participant_age alongside age



True or false. Renaming a column can change the values stored in it.
Predict the output. After names(df)[names(df) == "bmi"] <- "bmi_kg_m2", what would df$bmi return?

Find the error.
names(df)[names(df) == "Age"] <- "participant_age"
assuming the actual column is named age (lowercase).



Show answers


b) Renames the age column to participant_age.

False. Renaming only changes the column label; the values themselves are completely unaffected.
An error ($ operator is invalid for atomic vectors-style issue) or NULL — depending on context, but effectively: bmi no longer exists as a name, since it was renamed to bmi_kg_m2.
The capitalization doesn’t match ("Age" vs. the actual "age"), so the logical comparison is all FALSE and nothing gets renamed — no error is raised, which makes this mistake easy to miss.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric
text:
56.1 The R Concept
as.numeric() alone only works on text that’s already a clean, valid number — anything else (extra units, stray letters) produces NA with a warning, exactly as you saw in Level 1. The fix is to clean the text first, then convert:
gsub(pattern, replacement, x) removes (or replaces) a pattern of characters throughout a string — here, used to strip out anything that isn’t a digit or a decimal point:
gsub("[^0-9.]", "", x)   # remove everything except digits and decimal points
weight_text <- "68.5 kg"
weight_clean <- gsub("[^0-9.]", "", weight_text)   # "68.5"
weight_numeric <- as.numeric(weight_clean)          # 68.5
The pattern "[^0-9.]" is a small piece of regular expression syntax: [0-9.] means “any digit or decimal point,” and the leading ^ inside the brackets means “not” — so the whole pattern matches “anything that is not a digit or decimal point,” which gsub() then deletes (replacing it with "", empty text).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric

title: 56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric
text:
56.2 Demonstration

weight_raw <- c("70", "68.5 kg", "72kg", "unknown")

# Strip out anything that isn't a digit or decimal point
weight_stripped <- gsub("[^0-9.]", "", weight_raw)
weight_stripped

[1] "70"   "68.5" "72"   ""

weight_numeric <- as.numeric(weight_stripped)

Warning message:
NAs introduced by coercion

weight_numeric

[1] 70.0 68.5 72.0   NA
Notice "unknown" correctly became NA — stripping every character from it left an empty string "", which as.numeric() correctly cannot interpret as a number, and appropriately converts to NA with a warning rather than guessing.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric

title: 56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric
text:
56.3 Explore
Try gsub("[^0-9.]", "", "72 KG") (uppercase, with a space) and confirm it still correctly strips down to "72" — the pattern removes any non-digit, non-decimal character, regardless of what that character actually is.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric

title: 56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric
text:
56.4 Solve the Problem

weight_raw <- c("70", "68.5 kg", "72kg", "unknown", "65.2 kg")

weight_clean <- as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", weight_raw))
weight_clean

[1] 70.0 68.5 72.0   NA 65.2

mean(weight_clean, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 68.925
The messy, unit-contaminated text column is now a clean numeric vector, with the genuinely unreadable "unknown" entry correctly and appropriately converted to NA rather than silently guessed at.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric

title: 56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric
text:
56.5 Interpret the Result
The mean weight among the four participants with an interpretable value is 68.925 kg — a real, usable number, obtained only because the units and inconsistent spacing were stripped away before attempting the numeric conversion, rather than attempting as.numeric() directly on the original, unit-contaminated text.

Common Mistakes


Calling as.numeric() directly on unit-contaminated text. as.numeric("68.5 kg") fails (returns NA with a warning) because of the trailing " kg" — the units must be stripped first.

Writing a pattern that’s too aggressive or too narrow. "[^0-9.]" removes anything that isn’t a digit or period; if your data used commas as decimal separators (common outside the US), you’d need a different pattern, or to convert commas to periods first.

Not checking for new NAs after conversion. Always compare sum(is.na(x)) before and after a cleaning step like this — genuinely unreadable entries (like "unknown") should become NA, but you want to confirm that’s the only thing generating new missingness, not a pattern mistake affecting otherwise-valid entries.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric

title: 56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric
text:
56.6 Practice Problem
Clean and convert height_raw <- c("165cm", "172.5 cm", "180", "n/a") into a numeric vector, then calculate the mean height among the valid entries.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric

title: 56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric
text:
56.7 Challenge Problem
A dosage column contains c("5mg", "10 mg", "7.5mg", "twice daily") — the last entry isn’t a dose at all, just free-text scheduling information mistakenly entered in the wrong column. Clean and convert this column, and explain why "twice daily" correctly becomes NA even though it doesn’t look like a disguised missing-value code the way "unknown" did.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric

title: 56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric
text:
56.8 Key Takeaways


as.numeric() alone cannot handle text with embedded units or extra characters — it must be cleaned first, typically with gsub(), before conversion.

gsub("[^0-9.]", "", x) strips away everything except digits and decimal points, a common first step for numeric-looking but unit-contaminated text.
Always check for newly introduced NAs after a cleaning-and-converting step, to confirm they’re the entries you expected (genuinely unreadable text), not a pattern mistake.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does gsub("[^0-9.]", "", "72kg") return?

"72kg"
"72"
"kg"
NA


True or false. as.numeric("68.5 kg") correctly returns 68.5, ignoring the units automatically.

Predict the output.
as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", "15.2 mmHg"))

Write the code. Write the code to clean and convert dose_raw <- c("5mg", "10mg", "unclear") into a numeric vector.


Show answers


b) "72" — everything except digits and the decimal point is stripped away.

False. as.numeric() alone fails on unit-contaminated text; the units must be removed first with something like gsub().
[1] 15.2
as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", dose_raw))
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
56  Changing Variable Types: Character to Numeric

title: 57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character
text:
57.1 The R Concept
as.character(x) performs the basic conversion — a number becomes its text representation:
as.character(42)   # "42"
But a zero-padded, prefixed code like "P001" needs more than a bare conversion — it needs formatting. sprintf() (or the more R-idiomatic formatC()) builds a formatted string from a template:
sprintf("P%03d", 1)     # "P001" - %03d means "an integer, zero-padded to 3 digits"
sprintf("P%03d", 42)    # "P042"
sprintf("P%03d", 100)   # "P100"
Reading "P%03d": P is literal text that appears as-is; %03d is a placeholder meaning “insert an integer here, padded with zeros to at least 3 digits wide.”
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character

title: 57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character
text:
57.2 Demonstration

as.character(42)

[1] "42"

class(as.character(42))

[1] "character"

# Basic conversion alone doesn't zero-pad
as.character(1)

[1] "1"

# sprintf() builds the exact formatted code needed
sprintf("P%03d", 1)

[1] "P001"

sprintf("P%03d", c(1, 42, 100))

[1] "P001" "P042" "P100"
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character

title: 57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character
text:
57.3 Explore
Try sprintf("P%02d", 1) (2 digits instead of 3) and sprintf("P%04d", 1) (4 digits) — compare the outputs to see exactly how the number after %0 controls the padding width. What happens if the number itself already has more digits than the specified width, like sprintf("P%02d", 100)?
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character

title: 57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character
text:
57.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$patient_code <- sprintf("P%03d", participants$id)

head(participants$patient_code)

[1] "P001" "P002" "P003" "P004" "P005" "P006"

class(participants$patient_code)

[1] "character"

tail(participants$patient_code)

[1] "P095" "P096" "P097" "P098" "P099" "P100"
Every participant now has a properly formatted, zero-padded code exactly matching the partner clinic’s system, ready for the merge in Level 6.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character

title: 57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character
text:
57.5 Interpret the Result
participants$patient_code now holds character strings like "P001" and "P100", generated directly from the numeric id column — the original id column remains untouched and numeric, while patient_code provides the specific text format the partner system requires, without you needing to type all 100 codes by hand.

Common Mistakes


Using as.character() alone when zero-padding is required. as.character(1) gives "1", not "001" — a bare type conversion doesn’t add formatting; sprintf() (or formatC()) is needed for that.

Getting the width wrong in sprintf(). sprintf("P%02d", 100) — asking for 2-digit padding on a number that’s already 3 digits — doesn’t truncate anything; it simply uses as many digits as the number actually needs, so the padding number should be at least as large as your largest expected ID.

Forgetting the new code is a completely separate column. participants$patient_code doesn’t replace participants$id — both coexist, since you’ll likely still want the numeric id for calculations, and the character patient_code specifically for the external merge.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character

title: 57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character
text:
57.6 Practice Problem
Create a visit_code column formatted as "V" followed by a 2-digit zero-padded visit number, for visit numbers c(1, 5, 12) (expected output: "V01", "V05", "V12").
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character

title: 57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character
text:
57.7 Challenge Problem
Your partner clinic’s system actually uses a different prefix depending on county: "R" for Riverdale, "O" for Oakford, and so on. Without yet knowing the tidyverse tools from Level 5, describe in words (you don’t need full working code) how you might combine substr() or manual character logic with sprintf() to build a per-county prefix — this is intentionally a preview of a genuinely awkward base-R problem that Level 5’s case_when() and paste0() will make far more elegant.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character

title: 57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character
text:
57.8 Key Takeaways


as.character() performs a basic numeric-to-text conversion; it does not add formatting like zero-padding.

sprintf("P%03d", x) builds a formatted, zero-padded string from a numeric template — the 03 controls the minimum digit width.
Store a formatted ID code in a new column, keeping the original numeric column intact for any calculations that still need it.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does as.character(7) return?

7
"7"
"07"
07


True or false. as.character() alone can zero-pad a number to a fixed width.

Predict the output.
sprintf("ID%03d", 5)

Write the code. Write the code to convert the numeric vector c(3, 27, 150) into zero-padded 3-digit codes prefixed with "S" (e.g., "S003", "S027", "S150").


Show answers


b) "7"


False. as.character() performs a plain conversion with no formatting; sprintf() or formatC() is needed for zero-padding.
[1] "ID005"
sprintf("S%03d", c(3, 27, 150))
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
57  Changing Variable Types: Numeric to Character

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
58.1 The R Concept
as.factor(x) performs a basic character-to-factor conversion, but — exactly like Level 2’s factors page warned — it defaults to alphabetical level order, which is not necessarily the order you want for a reference category. Building the factor explicitly with factor() and a specified levels argument gives you full control:
factor(x, levels = c("reference_category", "other_category"))
smoker_factor <- factor(participants$smoker, levels = c("No", "Yes"))
Whichever category is listed first in levels becomes the factor’s reference level — the one every other category is implicitly compared against in a model like the ones you’ll build starting in Level 11.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
relevel() as an alternative



If a factor already exists with the wrong reference level, relevel(x, ref = "No") changes just the reference level without rebuilding the whole factor from scratch. Both approaches — specifying levels up front, or relevel() afterward — arrive at the same result.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
58.2 Demonstration

smoker_raw <- c("Yes", "No", "No", "Yes", "No")

# Basic conversion - defaults to alphabetical order ("No" happens to come first anyway here)
as.factor(smoker_raw)

[1] Yes No  No  Yes No
Levels: No Yes

# Explicit, deliberate order - not relying on alphabetical coincidence
smoker_factor <- factor(smoker_raw, levels = c("No", "Yes"))
smoker_factor

[1] Yes No  No  Yes No
Levels: No Yes

levels(smoker_factor)

[1] "No"  "Yes"
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
58.3 Explore
Try a category pair where alphabetical order would not match your intended reference level — for instance, treatment_group <- c("Treatment", "Control", "Treatment", "Control"). Run as.factor(treatment_group) and notice "Control" happens to come first alphabetically anyway; now imagine categories named "Drug A" and "Placebo" instead, where alphabetical order ("Drug A" first) would not match the reference level a researcher would actually want ("Placebo" as baseline) — exactly the situation explicit levels protects against.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
58.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$smoker_factor <- factor(participants$smoker, levels = c("No", "Yes"))

levels(participants$smoker_factor)

[1] "No"  "Yes"

table(participants$smoker_factor)

 No Yes
 75  25
"No" is now explicitly the first, reference level — guaranteed by the deliberate levels argument, not by alphabetical coincidence — ready for Level 11’s modeling functions to correctly interpret smoking status as “smoker vs. the reference non-smoker group.”
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
58.5 Interpret the Result
levels(participants$smoker_factor) confirms "No" is listed first, and table() confirms 75 non-smokers and 25 current smokers in the sample — the factor is now both correctly typed for modeling and explicitly, deliberately ordered, rather than relying on alphabetical order to happen to produce the right reference category.

Common Mistakes


Relying on as.factor()’s alphabetical default without checking whether it matches your intended reference category. It happens to work for "No"/"Yes" (alphabetically, "No" comes first anyway), but this is coincidence, not something to depend on for every categorical variable.

Assuming factor order doesn’t matter until you actually build a model. It matters immediately for how table(), plots (Level 8), and summaries display the category — not just for eventual regression output.

Forgetting a factor still displays its labels, even though it’s stored as integers internally. smoker_factor prints as "Yes"/"No" text, exactly as expected — the underlying integer codes (Level 2) are an implementation detail, not something you need to manage directly.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
58.6 Practice Problem
Convert participants$sex into a factor with "Male" as the reference (first) level, even though "Female" would come first alphabetically. Confirm with levels() that "Male" is listed first.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
58.7 Challenge Problem
A colleague’s model output describes an effect as “relative to Riverdale,” implying county was converted to a factor with Riverdale as the reference level. Write the factor() call that would produce this, given the five counties in participants$county (Cedar Falls, Millbrook, Oakford, Riverdale, Springvale) — you determined the correct full set of counties back in Level 2.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor
text:
58.8 Key Takeaways


as.factor() performs a basic conversion but defaults to alphabetical level order, which may not match your intended reference category.

factor(x, levels = c("reference", ...)) gives explicit, deliberate control over which category is the reference (first) level.
Getting the reference level right matters immediately for tables and plots, not just for eventual regression models in Level 11.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What determines a factor’s reference level, when built with factor(x, levels = c(...))?

Whichever category is most frequent
Whichever category is listed first in levels

Whichever category is alphabetically last
It’s always chosen randomly


True or false. as.factor() always produces the same reference level as an explicit factor(x, levels = ...) call.

Predict the output.
f <- factor(c("High", "Low"), levels = c("Low", "High"))
levels(f)

Write the code. Write the code to convert a character vector outcome <- c("Improved", "Not Improved") into a factor with "Not Improved" as the reference (first) level.


Show answers


b) Whichever category is listed first in levels.

False. as.factor() defaults to alphabetical order, which only matches an explicit levels specification by coincidence.
[1] "Low"  "High"
factor(outcome, levels = c("Not Improved", "Improved"))
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R
58  Changing Variable Types: Character to Factor

title: 59  Creating New Variables
text:
59.1 The R Concept
Assigning to a column name that doesn’t yet exist in a data frame creates it, exactly as assigning to a brand-new object name creates that object (Level 1):
df$new_column <- <some calculation using existing columns>
Because columns are vectors of matching length (Level 2), arithmetic between two columns happens element-wise — row 1’s calculation uses row 1’s values from each column, row 2 uses row 2’s values, and so on, automatically, all at once.
df$pulse_pressure <- df$systolic_bp - df$diastolic_bp
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 59  Creating New Variables
text:
59.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Before: no such column exists
"pulse_pressure" %in% names(participants)

[1] FALSE

participants$pulse_pressure <- participants$systolic_bp - participants$diastolic_bp

"pulse_pressure" %in% names(participants)

[1] TRUE

head(participants[, c("systolic_bp", "diastolic_bp", "pulse_pressure")])

  systolic_bp diastolic_bp pulse_pressure
1         125           79             46
2         123           80             43
3         130           77             53
4         117           72             45
5         139           79             60
6         117           64             53
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 59  Creating New Variables
text:
59.3 Explore
Run summary(participants$pulse_pressure) to see the range of pulse pressure values across the sample. Then check sum(is.na(participants$pulse_pressure)) — since pulse_pressure is calculated from two columns that each have some missing values, what would you expect this count to be, compared to the missingness in systolic_bp or diastolic_bp alone?
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 59  Creating New Variables
text:
59.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$pulse_pressure <- participants$systolic_bp - participants$diastolic_bp

mean(participants$pulse_pressure, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 49.35417

sum(is.na(participants$pulse_pressure))

[1] 4
The new pulse_pressure variable is calculated for every participant with both a systolic and diastolic reading, with a mean of about 49.4 mmHg across the sample — ready to hand back to your supervisor as a genuinely new, derived clinical variable.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 59  Creating New Variables
text:
59.5 Interpret the Result
sum(is.na(participants$pulse_pressure)) returning 4 (matching exactly the 4 participants missing systolic_bp, since diastolic_bp has no missing values at all) confirms that a calculation is only as complete as its least complete input — any row missing either ingredient produces a missing result, which propagates the same way any arithmetic with NA does (Level 1).

Common Mistakes


Expecting a new variable to be complete even when its inputs aren’t. A derived variable inherits missingness from every column it depends on — pulse_pressure is missing wherever systolic_bp (or, in general, either ingredient) is missing.

Overwriting an existing column by accident. Assigning to a column name that does already exist replaces it silently, with no warning — always double check names(df) if you’re not sure whether a name is already taken.

Forgetting the calculation is element-wise. df$systolic_bp - df$diastolic_bp computes 100 separate subtractions, one per row, all at once — not a single overall subtraction of two summary numbers.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 59  Creating New Variables
text:
59.6 Practice Problem
Create a new column bp_ratio calculated as systolic_bp / diastolic_bp, and report its mean (excluding missing values).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 59  Creating New Variables
text:
59.7 Challenge Problem
Create a new column age_at_next_checkup representing each participant’s age one year from now (age + 1). Then explain, in one sentence, why this new column will have exactly the same number of missing values as age itself — no more, no fewer.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 59  Creating New Variables
text:
59.8 Key Takeaways

Assigning to a data frame column name that doesn’t yet exist creates it; assigning to one that already exists overwrites it.
Arithmetic between two columns happens element-wise, row by row, automatically — the same rule from Level 2’s vectors applies directly to data frame columns.
A derived variable is missing wherever any of its input columns are missing, inheriting incompleteness from its ingredients.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does df$new_col <- df$a - df$b do if new_col doesn’t already exist?

Raises an error
Creates a new column calculated element-wise from a and b

Overwrites column a

Does nothing until new_col is explicitly declared first


True or false. A new column calculated from two existing columns will always have zero missing values, regardless of its inputs.
Predict the output. If df$x <- c(10, NA, 30) and df$y <- c(1, 2, 3), what would df$x - df$y produce?
Write the code. Write the code to create a new column bmi_times_10 equal to participants$bmi multiplied by 10.


Show answers


b) Creates a new column, calculated element-wise from a and b.

False. A derived column is missing wherever any of its input columns are missing — it cannot be more complete than its least complete ingredient.
c(9, NA, 27)
participants$bmi_times_10 <- participants$bmi * 10
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 60  Recoding Variables
text:
60.1 The R Concept
Recoding a variable’s labels uses exactly the same targeted-assignment pattern from the replacing-missing-values page, just with a real category condition instead of is.na():
df$new_col <- df$old_col                          # start from a copy
df$new_col[df$old_col == "Yes"] <- "Current smoker"
df$new_col[df$old_col == "No"]  <- "Non-smoker"
For a two-category recode like this one, ifelse() (covered properly two pages from now) is often a faster one-line alternative — but building the recode with explicit logical indexing first makes the underlying mechanics completely transparent, which is the point of this page.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 60  Recoding Variables
text:
60.2 Demonstration

smoker_raw <- c("Yes", "No", "No", "Yes", "No")

smoker_label <- smoker_raw   # start from a copy, not the original
smoker_label[smoker_raw == "Yes"] <- "Current smoker"
smoker_label[smoker_raw == "No"]  <- "Non-smoker"

smoker_label

[1] "Current smoker" "Non-smoker"      "Non-smoker"      "Current smoker"
[5] "Non-smoker"
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 60  Recoding Variables
text:
60.3 Explore
Try table(smoker_raw, smoker_label) — a cross-tabulation confirming every "Yes" became "Current smoker" and every "No" became "Non-smoker", with no mismatches. This kind of before/after cross-check is a valuable habit any time you recode a variable.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 60  Recoding Variables
text:
60.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$smoking_status <- participants$smoker
participants$smoking_status[participants$smoker == "Yes"] <- "Current smoker"
participants$smoking_status[participants$smoker == "No"]  <- "Non-smoker"

table(participants$smoker, participants$smoking_status)

     Current smoker Non-smoker
  No               0         75
  Yes             25          0
Every "Yes" correctly recoded to "Current smoker" (25 participants) and every "No" to "Non-smoker" (75 participants), confirmed by the cross-tabulation showing no unexpected mismatches — the report-ready labels your supervisor asked for.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 60  Recoding Variables
text:
60.5 Interpret the Result
The cross-tabulation’s diagonal pattern — "Yes" always paired with "Current smoker", "No" always paired with "Non-smoker", and zero off-diagonal counts — confirms the recode is exactly correct, with every original category mapped to precisely one new label and nothing left ambiguous or mismatched.

Common Mistakes


Recoding directly into the original column, losing the original labels. Starting from a copy (df$new_col <- df$old_col, then modifying the copy) preserves the original smoker column for any downstream code that still expects "Yes"/"No".

Forgetting to recode every category. If a variable has more than two categories and you only recode some of them, the un-recoded values silently remain in their original form, mixed in among the newly recoded ones.

Not verifying the recode with a cross-tabulation. table(old, new) is a fast, nearly foolproof way to catch a mismatched or incomplete recode before it reaches a report.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 60  Recoding Variables
text:
60.6 Practice Problem
Recode participants$sex into a new column sex_label, converting "Female" to "F" and "Male" to "M". Verify with a cross-tabulation.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 60  Recoding Variables
text:
60.7 Challenge Problem
Your supervisor changes the request: instead of "Current smoker"/"Non-smoker", they want "Current smoker", "Former smoker", and "Never smoker" — a three-category recode that the raw "Yes"/"No" data cannot actually support (it was never collected with a former-smoker distinction). Explain, in a sentence or two, why no amount of recoding code can fix this — what would actually need to change, further upstream, to support this request?
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 60  Recoding Variables
text:
60.8 Key Takeaways

Recoding uses the same targeted-assignment pattern as replacing missing values, applied to a real category condition instead of is.na().
Always recode into a new column, preserving the original for any code that still depends on the original labels.

table(old_column, new_column) is a fast, reliable way to verify a recode mapped every category correctly and completely.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What is the safest way to recode a variable?

Overwrite the original column directly
Recode into a new column, preserving the original
Recode only some categories and leave the rest as-is
Recode without checking the result


True or false. table(old_column, new_column) is a useful way to verify a recode was applied correctly.
Predict the output. If x <- c("A", "B", "A") and you recode "A" to "Group 1" and "B" to "Group 2", what would the recoded vector be?
Find the error. A student recodes smoker but only handles "Yes", forgetting to also recode "No". What happens to the "No" values in the new column?


Show answers


b) Recode into a new column, preserving the original.

True. It’s a fast, reliable cross-check that every original category mapped correctly to its new label.
c("Group 1", "Group 2", "Group 1")
Since the new column started as a copy of the original, the un-recoded "No" values remain exactly as "No" — silently unrecoded and inconsistent with the newly labeled "Yes" values.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 61  Combining Categories
text:
61.1 The R Concept
Combining categories extends the recoding pattern from the last page, using %in% to check membership in a set of values rather than equality with just one:
x %in% c("value1", "value2")   # TRUE if x matches ANY of these, not just one
df$new_col[df$old_col %in% c("CategoryA", "CategoryB")] <- "Combined Group"
This is the natural extension of == (from the recoding page) to “matches one of several things” rather than “matches exactly this one thing” — precisely what’s needed when multiple original categories all collapse into the same broader group.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 61  Combining Categories
text:
61.2 Demonstration

county <- c("Cedar Falls", "Millbrook", "Oakford", "Riverdale", "Springvale", "Cedar Falls")

# Check membership in a set, rather than equality with a single value
county %in% c("Cedar Falls", "Millbrook")

[1]  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE

region <- county   # start from a copy, same pattern as recoding
region[county %in% c("Cedar Falls", "Millbrook")] <- "North Region"
region[county %in% c("Oakford", "Riverdale", "Springvale")] <- "South Region"

region

[1] "North Region" "North Region" "South Region" "South Region" "South Region"
[6] "North Region"
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 61  Combining Categories
text:
61.3 Explore
Run table(county, region) — a cross-tabulation confirming every one of the five counties mapped into exactly one of the two intended regions, with no county left unmapped and no county mapped to both.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 61  Combining Categories
text:
61.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$region <- participants$county
participants$region[participants$county %in% c("Cedar Falls", "Millbrook")] <- "North Region"
participants$region[participants$county %in% c("Oakford", "Riverdale", "Springvale")] <- "South Region"

table(participants$region)

North Region South Region
          41           59

# Verify every county mapped correctly and completely
table(participants$county, participants$region)

              North Region South Region
  Cedar Falls           20            0
  Millbrook             21            0
  Oakford                0           17
  Riverdale              0           16
  Springvale             0           26
Every county correctly collapsed into exactly one of the two intended regions — 41 participants in the North Region, 59 in the South Region — with the cross-tabulation confirming no county was missed or double-counted.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 61  Combining Categories
text:
61.5 Interpret the Result
The two-way table’s diagonal-block pattern — Cedar Falls and Millbrook entirely in the North Region column, Oakford, Riverdale, and Springvale entirely in the South Region column, with zero counts everywhere else — confirms the regional grouping is complete and correct, exactly matching your supervisor’s five-counties-into-two-regions request.

Common Mistakes


Using == instead of %in% for a multi-category match. county == c("Cedar Falls", "Millbrook") does not do what you might expect — it compares element-by-element in a way that rarely matches your intent; %in% is the correct tool for “matches any of these.”

Leaving a category unmapped by accident. If you forget to include one of the five counties in either group, those rows keep their original county name in the new region column, silently mixed in among the properly grouped values — the cross-tabulation check catches this immediately.

Assuming combining categories is the same operation as recoding. Recoding (previous page) is one-to-one; combining categories is many-to-one — the logic (%in% vs. ==) reflects that real difference.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 61  Combining Categories
text:
61.6 Practice Problem
Combine participants$county into three regions instead of two: "Cedar Falls" and "Millbrook" as "North", "Oakford" and "Riverdale" as "Central", and "Springvale" alone as "East". Verify with a cross-tabulation.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 61  Combining Categories
text:
61.7 Challenge Problem
Your supervisor later asks for a different grouping of the same five counties for a separate report. Explain, in a sentence or two, why storing the first regional grouping in a new column (region) rather than overwriting county directly makes it easy to add this second, different grouping (perhaps region_v2) without losing either version or re-deriving anything from scratch.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 61  Combining Categories
text:
61.8 Key Takeaways


%in% checks membership in a set of values, extending the single-value == comparison from the recoding page to “matches any of these.”
Combining categories is a many-to-one operation — several original categories collapse into one broader group — distinct from recoding’s one-to-one relabeling.
A two-way table(old_column, new_column) cross-tabulation confirms every original category was mapped exactly once, with nothing missed or double-mapped.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does x %in% c("A", "B") check?

Whether x equals "A" and "B" simultaneously
Whether x matches either "A" or "B"

Whether x is alphabetically between "A" and "B"

Whether x is missing


True or false. Combining categories and recoding a variable are the same operation.

Predict the output.
x <- c("Red", "Blue", "Green")
