x %in% c("Red", "Green")

Write the code. Write the code to combine "Yes" and "Sometimes" into a single category "Any use" in a vector called alcohol_use, leaving "No" unchanged.


Show answers


b) Whether x matches either "A" or "B".

False. Recoding is one-to-one; combining categories is many-to-one — a genuinely different mapping.
[1]  TRUE FALSE  TRUE
alcohol_use[alcohol_use %in% c("Yes", "Sometimes")] <- "Any use"
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
62.1 The R Concept
A logical comparison already produces exactly the right underlying information — TRUE/FALSE — and R’s as.numeric() converts TRUE to 1 and FALSE to 0 automatically:
as.numeric(condition)
df$is_hypertensive <- as.numeric(df$systolic_bp >= 130)
This is a two-step idea compressed into one line: first, df$systolic_bp >= 130 produces a TRUE/FALSE vector (Level 1); then as.numeric() converts that directly into the 1/0 coding that modeling functions expect.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
Keeping it logical vs. converting to numeric



Some analyses are perfectly happy with a plain logical (TRUE/FALSE) column instead of numeric 1/0 — R treats them equivalently in most arithmetic and modeling contexts. Converting explicitly with as.numeric() is mainly about matching a convention (like a data dictionary, or a modeling function’s specific expectations) rather than a strict technical requirement.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
62.2 Demonstration

sbp <- c(118, 132, 145, 121, 138)

sbp >= 130

[1] FALSE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE  TRUE

as.numeric(sbp >= 130)

[1] 0 1 1 0 1
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
62.3 Explore
Try sum(as.numeric(sbp >= 130)) — since 1s and 0s sum directly to a count, this immediately tells you how many values met the condition, without needing a separate counting step. Compare this to sum(sbp >= 130) (summing the logical vector directly, with no explicit as.numeric() at all) — do they give the same answer?
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
62.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$is_hypertensive <- as.numeric(participants$systolic_bp >= 130)

table(participants$is_hypertensive, useNA = "always")

   0    1 <NA>
  50   46    4

sum(participants$is_hypertensive, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 46
The useNA = "always" argument to table() reveals something important here: 4 participants have NA for is_hypertensive, not 0 — because their systolic_bp was itself missing, and a comparison against a missing value correctly produces NA rather than a guessed FALSE.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
62.5 Interpret the Result
46 of the 96 participants with a recorded systolic blood pressure meet the hypertension threshold of 130 or higher — and critically, the 4 participants with missing blood pressure data correctly show NA for this new indicator too, rather than being silently (and wrongly) counted as 0, “not hypertensive.” This is exactly the kind of correct missing-data propagation Level 1 introduced and this level has reinforced throughout.

Common Mistakes


Assuming a binary indicator is automatically complete. Just like any derived variable (this level’s creating-new-variables page), is_hypertensive inherits missingness from systolic_bp — a missing blood pressure produces a missing indicator, not a 0.

Forgetting useNA = "always" when checking a table with potential missing data. Plain table(participants$is_hypertensive) silently omits the NA count entirely, which could make you think the variable is more complete than it actually is.

Choosing an arbitrary threshold without a clinical or research justification. >= 130 reflects a real, standard clinical hypertension threshold — always be able to justify why a particular cutoff was chosen, not just that one was chosen.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
62.6 Practice Problem
Create a binary indicator is_obese, equal to 1 if bmi >= 30 and 0 otherwise, correctly propagating missing values. Report how many participants are flagged 1.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
62.7 Challenge Problem
Create a binary indicator is_high_risk, equal to 1 only if a participant is both hypertensive (systolic_bp >= 130) and a current smoker (smoker == "Yes"), using & (Level 1). Report how many participants meet both criteria, and explain what happens to a participant missing systolic_bp when this combined condition is evaluated.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 62  Creating Binary Variables
text:
62.8 Key Takeaways


as.numeric(condition) converts a TRUE/FALSE comparison directly into 1/0, matching the coding convention most modeling functions expect.
A binary indicator inherits missingness from whatever variable it’s built from — a missing input produces a missing indicator, not a default 0.

table(x, useNA = "always") explicitly shows missing-value counts that a plain table(x) would silently hide.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does as.numeric(c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)) return?

c("TRUE", "FALSE", "TRUE")
c(1, 0, 1)
c(0, 1, 0)
An error


True or false. A binary indicator built from a column with missing values will itself have no missing values.

Predict the output.
x <- c(125, NA, 140)
as.numeric(x >= 130)

Write the code. Write the code to create a binary indicator high_pulse_pressure, equal to 1 if pulse_pressure > 50 (using the variable created earlier this level) and 0 otherwise.


Show answers


b) c(1, 0, 1)


False. Missingness propagates: a missing input produces a missing (NA) indicator, not a default 0.
[1]  0 NA  1
participants$high_pulse_pressure <- as.numeric(participants$pulse_pressure > 50)
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 63  Conditional Statements: if
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 63  Conditional Statements: if
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 63  Conditional Statements: if
text:
63.3 Explore
Try passing a whole vector’s worth of values into an if condition, like if (c(TRUE, FALSE)) { print("test") }, rather than a single value. In current versions of R, this produces an error — if’s condition must be a single, length-one logical value, not a vector — a helpful guardrail that stops you from confusing if (a single decision) with the vectorized, per-row tools (ifelse(), next page) that this level has used throughout.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 63  Conditional Statements: if
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 63  Conditional Statements: if
text:
63.5 Interpret the Result
The script correctly reports "Enrollment complete" because nrow(participants) is exactly 100 — a single, one-time decision about the dataset as a whole, genuinely different from asking “which individual participants meet a condition,” the per-row question every earlier page in this level has answered with vectorized comparisons.

Common Mistakes


Trying to use if on an entire column at once. if (participants$age >= 65) fails, because participants$age >= 65 is a vector of 100 values, not one — if needs a single TRUE/FALSE. For per-row decisions across a whole column, you want ifelse() (next page), not if.

Forgetting curly braces for multi-line blocks. A single-line if can skip the braces (if (x) print("yes")), but as soon as more than one line needs to run conditionally, { } are required to group them together.

Confusing if/else (a programming control-flow structure) with ifelse() (a vectorized function). They sound almost identical and solve related but distinct problems — this level’s next three pages exist specifically to make that distinction clear.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 63  Conditional Statements: if
text:
63.6 Practice Problem
Write an if/else block that prints "Sample size is adequate" if nrow(participants) is at least 50, and "Sample size may be too small" otherwise.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 63  Conditional Statements: if
text:
63.7 Challenge Problem
Write an if/else if/else block (a third option, else if, chains an additional condition) that checks mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE) and prints "Younger sample" if under 40, "Older sample" if 60 or above, and "Middle-aged sample" otherwise.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 63  Conditional Statements: if
text:
63.8 Key Takeaways


if/else makes a single, one-time decision in your code based on one TRUE/FALSE condition — not a per-row operation across a whole column.

if’s condition must be a single logical value; passing a vector produces an error, a useful guardrail against confusing it with vectorized tools.

if/else and ifelse() (next page) are related but distinct tools, and this level’s next few pages build out the full picture of when to use each.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What kind of condition does if expect?

A vector of any length
A single TRUE or FALSE value
A character string
A data frame


True or false. if can be used directly on an entire column of a data frame to make a per-row decision.

Predict the output.
x <- 42
if (x > 100) {
  print("big")
} else {
  print("small")
}


Find the error.
if (participants$age >= 65) {
  print("senior")
}



Show answers


b) A single TRUE or FALSE value.

False. if requires a single logical value; a whole column is a vector of 100 values, which if cannot evaluate directly — that’s exactly what ifelse() is for.
[1] "small"

participants$age >= 65 is a vector (100 values), not a single value — if cannot evaluate it directly. The fix is to use ifelse() for a per-row result, or reduce the condition to a single value (like any() or all()) if a one-time decision is genuinely intended.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 64  ifelse()
text:
64.1 The R Concept
ifelse() is the vectorized sibling of if/else — it evaluates a condition across an entire vector at once, returning one value for every TRUE and another for every FALSE:
ifelse(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false)
ifelse(participants$age >= 65, "Senior", "Non-senior")
This is exactly the tool the previous page’s binary-variables and recoding pages were building toward manually with logical indexing — ifelse() compresses that same idea (a condition, a value for TRUE, a value for FALSE) into a single function call.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 64  ifelse()
text:
64.2 Demonstration

ages <- c(72, 45, 68, 30, 81)

ifelse(ages >= 65, "Senior", "Non-senior")

[1] "Senior"     "Non-senior" "Senior"     "Non-senior" "Senior"

# Compare to the manual, logical-indexing approach from earlier this level
age_group <- rep(NA_character_, length(ages))
age_group[ages >= 65] <- "Senior"
age_group[ages < 65]  <- "Non-senior"
age_group

[1] "Senior"     "Non-senior" "Senior"     "Non-senior" "Senior"
Both approaches give an identical result — ifelse() is simply a faster, more compact way to express exactly what the earlier logical-indexing pattern was already doing.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 64  ifelse()
text:
64.3 Explore
Try ifelse(c(72, NA, 68) >= 65, "Senior", "Non-senior") — what happens to the NA position? ifelse() correctly propagates missingness: if the condition itself is NA (because the underlying age is missing), the result is NA too, neither "Senior" nor "Non-senior" — exactly the correct behavior, consistent with everything this level has taught about missing data.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 64  ifelse()
text:
64.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$age_group <- ifelse(participants$age >= 65, "Senior", "Non-senior")

table(participants$age_group, useNA = "always")

Non-senior     Senior       <NA>
        83         11          6
Every participant is now labeled "Senior" or "Non-senior" in one line, with the 6 participants missing age correctly showing NA rather than being forced into either category.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 64  ifelse()
text:
64.5 Interpret the Result
11 of the 94 participants with a recorded age are 65 or older, 83 are under 65, and the 6 participants with missing age data correctly remain NA for this new label — ifelse() produced, in a single line, exactly the same correct, missing-data-aware result the manual logical-indexing approach would have taken several lines to build.

Common Mistakes


Using if instead of ifelse() on a whole column. This is exactly the error the previous page’s Explore and Challenge sections set up — if cannot evaluate a multi-element vector; ifelse() is built specifically for that.

Forgetting ifelse() still requires exactly three arguments in order. ifelse(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false) — reversing the last two silently swaps your logic ("Senior" for people under 65, and vice versa) with no error to warn you.

Assuming ifelse() always returns the type you expect. ifelse() can sometimes silently convert types in surprising ways (particularly with dates or factors) — this is precisely the motivation for if_else(), covered on the next page, which is stricter about this.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 64  ifelse()
text:
64.6 Practice Problem
Create bp_group using ifelse(), labeling participants "Elevated" if systolic_bp >= 130 and "Normal" otherwise. Confirm missing values propagate correctly with table(..., useNA = "always").
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 64  ifelse()
text:
64.7 Challenge Problem
Nest two ifelse() calls to create a three-category age_category: "Youth" (under 18), "Adult" (18–64), and "Senior" (65+) — using the pattern ifelse(condition1, "Youth", ifelse(condition2, "Adult", "Senior")). Test it on c(15, 45, 70) and confirm all three labels appear correctly.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 64  ifelse()
text:
64.8 Key Takeaways


ifelse(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false) applies a condition across an entire vector at once, unlike if, which requires a single value.

ifelse() correctly propagates NA: a missing input produces a missing (NA) result, never a guessed category.
Nested ifelse() calls can build more than two categories, though this gets unwieldy quickly — a limitation case_when() (this level’s final page) resolves much more cleanly.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does ifelse(x > 10, "high", "low") return for x <- c(5, 15)?

c("low", "high")
c("high", "low")
A single value, "high" or "low"

An error, since x has more than one element


True or false. ifelse() requires a single-value condition, exactly like if.

Predict the output.
ifelse(c(10, NA, 30) > 20, "big", "small")


Find the error.
ifelse(participants$age >= 65, "Non-senior", "Senior")
A student intends "Senior" for age 65+.



Show answers


a) c("low", "high") — ifelse() evaluates every element of the vector.

False. ifelse() is specifically vectorized — it works across an entire vector, unlike if, which requires exactly one value.
[1] "small" NA "big"
The second and third arguments are swapped — this labels people under 65 as "Senior" and 65+ as "Non-senior", backwards from what’s intended. It should be ifelse(participants$age >= 65, "Senior", "Non-senior").
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 65  if_else()
text:
65.1 The R Concept
if_else(), from the dplyr package (part of the tidyverse, arriving properly in Level 5), works exactly like ifelse() for straightforward cases, but is stricter about types: if the TRUE result and the FALSE result aren’t compatible types, if_else() raises a clear error immediately, rather than silently coercing one of them into something unexpected.
library(dplyr)
if_else(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false)
# ifelse() silently converts dates to plain numbers - a common, hard-to-spot bug
ifelse(is_followup, visit_date, NA)

# if_else() raises a clear, immediate error instead of silently corrupting the type
if_else(is_followup, visit_date, NA)
The fix, once you know about the problem, is usually to make the NA explicitly typed to match: if_else(is_followup, visit_date, as.Date(NA)) for a date column, for instance — if_else() forces you to be explicit rather than letting a mismatch pass silently.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 65  if_else()
text:
65.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

# A straightforward case - if_else() behaves identically to ifelse()
ages <- c(72, 45, 68)
if_else(ages >= 65, "Senior", "Non-senior")

[1] "Senior"     "Non-senior" "Senior"

# A type-mismatch case - ifelse() silently causes a problem, if_else() catches it
is_followup <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
visit_date <- as.Date(c("2026-01-05", "2026-01-06", "2026-01-07"))

ifelse(is_followup, visit_date, NA)

[1] 20458    NA 20460
Notice the dates silently became plain numbers (each date’s internal numeric representation) — exactly the bug from the opening problem.

if_else(is_followup, visit_date, NA)

Error in `if_else()`:
! `false` must be a <Date>, not `NA`.
if_else() refuses to guess, and instead tells you exactly what went wrong — the FALSE result must be a proper Date, not a bare, untyped NA.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 65  if_else()
text:
65.3 Explore
Fix the error by explicitly typing the NA: if_else(is_followup, visit_date, as.Date(NA)). Confirm this now works correctly, preserving the Date type throughout, with no silent conversion to plain numbers.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 65  if_else()
text:
65.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

is_followup <- c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)
visit_date <- as.Date(c("2026-01-05", "2026-01-06", "2026-01-07"))

visit_date_clean <- if_else(is_followup, visit_date, as.Date(NA))
visit_date_clean

[1] "2026-01-05" NA           "2026-01-07"

class(visit_date_clean)

[1] "Date"
The colleague’s bug is now fixed and, more importantly, made impossible to silently reintroduce — if_else() would immediately raise an error again if the types ever mismatched in the future, rather than quietly corrupting the column the way ifelse() did.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 65  if_else()
text:
65.5 Interpret the Result
class(visit_date_clean) correctly reports "Date", confirming the column’s type was preserved throughout — the baseline visit correctly shows NA (a real, typed missing date) rather than the number 20459 (or similar) that ifelse() would have silently produced.

Common Mistakes


Assuming if_else() and ifelse() are simply two names for the same thing. They agree on simple cases, but if_else()’s stricter type-checking is a deliberate, meaningful difference — not a stylistic quirk.

Being annoyed by an if_else() error instead of reading what it’s telling you. The error message in this page’s demonstration says exactly what’s wrong (false must be a <Date>, not NA) — it’s surfacing a real problem ifelse() would have hidden, not being unnecessarily strict for no reason.

Forgetting to load dplyr. Like any package function, if_else() requires library(dplyr) (or library(tidyverse)) first.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 65  if_else()
text:
65.6 Practice Problem
Using if_else(), recreate the age_group variable from the previous page ("Senior" for 65+, "Non-senior" otherwise) on participants$age, and confirm it produces the same result as the ifelse() version.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 65  if_else()
text:
65.7 Challenge Problem
Explain, in two or three sentences, why a strict, error-raising function like if_else() is generally safer for production analysis code than a permissive, silently-converting one like ifelse() — even though the strict version is sometimes more annoying to work with in the moment.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 65  if_else()
text:
65.8 Key Takeaways


if_else() (from dplyr) behaves like ifelse() on straightforward cases, but raises a clear error rather than silently converting types when the TRUE and FALSE results don’t match.
This strictness catches real bugs — like a Date column silently becoming plain numbers — immediately, rather than letting them surface much later and harder to trace.
When if_else() errors on a type mismatch, the fix is usually to make both results explicitly the same type (like as.Date(NA) instead of a bare NA).


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does if_else() do differently from ifelse()?

It works only on numeric data
It raises an error rather than silently converting mismatched types
It cannot handle missing values
It requires no package to be loaded


True or false. ifelse() can silently convert a Date column into plain numbers.

Predict the output.
if_else(c(TRUE, FALSE), "Yes", "No")


Find the error.
if_else(is_followup, visit_date, NA)
where visit_date is a Date column.



Show answers


b) It raises an error rather than silently converting mismatched types.

True. This is exactly the bug if_else() is designed to catch and prevent.
[1] "Yes" "No"
The bare NA doesn’t match the Date type of visit_date; it needs to be as.Date(NA) instead, so both possible results share the same type.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.1 The R Concept
case_when(), from dplyr (arriving properly in Level 5), evaluates a series of conditions in order, top to bottom, and assigns the value from the first one that matches:
library(dplyr)
case_when(
  condition1 ~ value1,
  condition2 ~ value2,
  condition3 ~ value3,
  TRUE ~ default_value
)
Each line reads naturally: “condition ~ (results in) value.” The final TRUE ~ default_value line acts as a catch-all — since TRUE always matches, it captures any row that didn’t match an earlier condition (equivalent to the final else in a nested ifelse() chain, but far more readable at a glance).
case_when(
  bmi < 18.5 ~ "Underweight",
  bmi < 25   ~ "Normal",
  bmi < 30   ~ "Overweight",
  TRUE       ~ "Obese"
)
Notice this reads almost like a sentence: “if BMI is under 18.5, Underweight; if under 25 (having already excluded under-18.5), Normal;” and so on — each condition is only evaluated for rows that didn’t match anything above it.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

bmi_values <- c(17.2, 22.5, 28.9, 33.1, NA)

case_when(
  bmi_values < 18.5 ~ "Underweight",
  bmi_values < 25   ~ "Normal",
  bmi_values < 30   ~ "Overweight",
  TRUE              ~ "Obese"
)

[1] "Underweight" "Normal"      "Overweight"  "Obese"       NA
Notice the NA BMI correctly produces NA in the result — even the catch-all TRUE ~ "Obese" doesn’t fire for a missing input, because case_when() (like ifelse() and if_else()) correctly propagates missingness rather than guessing.
Compare this to the equivalent, harder-to-read nested ifelse() from Level 2:

ifelse(bmi_values < 18.5, "Underweight",
  ifelse(bmi_values < 25, "Normal",
    ifelse(bmi_values < 30, "Overweight", "Obese")))

Both produce the same result — case_when() simply reads far more clearly once there are more than two categories involved.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.3 Explore
Try adding a fifth category to the blood pressure classification: "Normal" (< 120), "Elevated" (120–129), "Hypertension Stage 1" (130–139), "Hypertension Stage 2" (140–179), and "Hypertensive Crisis" (180+), using case_when() on participants$systolic_bp. Notice how each additional category is just one more readable line, rather than another level of nesting.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$bp_category <- case_when(
  participants$systolic_bp < 120 ~ "Normal",
  participants$systolic_bp < 130 ~ "Elevated",
  participants$systolic_bp < 140 ~ "Hypertension Stage 1",
  participants$systolic_bp < 180 ~ "Hypertension Stage 2",
  TRUE                            ~ "Hypertensive Crisis"
)

table(participants$bp_category, useNA = "always")

            Elevated                Normal Hypertension Stage 1
                  18                    32                    17
Hypertension Stage 2   Hypertensive Crisis                  <NA>
                  29                     0                     4
Five clinically meaningful categories, built in five clean, parallel lines — no nested nightmare of nine mismatched parentheses to debug, and the 4 participants with missing systolic_bp correctly show NA rather than being forced into any category.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.5 Interpret the Result
The blood pressure classification is now complete across all five clinical categories, with the missing-data behavior correctly preserved (4 participants with missing systolic_bp remain NA, not defaulted into "Normal" or any other category) — exactly the behavior you’d want, and considerably easier to read, extend, or hand off to a colleague than an equivalent nested ifelse() chain would have been.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting conditions are checked in order, top to bottom. If you accidentally list a broader condition before a narrower one (like systolic_bp < 180 before systolic_bp < 120), the narrower condition below it will never be reached, since the broader one already matched first.

Forgetting the final TRUE ~ default catch-all. Without it, any row that doesn’t match any listed condition becomes NA in the result — sometimes exactly what you want, but often an unintentional gap if you forgot a category.

Using = instead of ~. case_when()’s syntax specifically requires the tilde (~), not an equals sign — a common typo for anyone used to other assignment-style syntax in R.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.6 Practice Problem
Using case_when(), classify participants$age into "Child" (under 18), "Adult" (18–64), and "Senior" (65+). Confirm the result with table(..., useNA = "always").
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.7 Challenge Problem
Rebuild Level 2’s four-category bmi_category variable using case_when() instead of nested ifelse(), and confirm with table() that it produces exactly the same category counts as Level 2’s original version (3 Underweight, 28 Normal, 39 Overweight, 25 Obese).
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.8 Key Takeaways


case_when() evaluates conditions in order and assigns the value from the first match — far more readable than nesting ifelse() calls for three or more categories.
A final TRUE ~ default_value line acts as a catch-all for any row that matched nothing above it.
Like ifelse() and if_else(), case_when() correctly propagates missing values — a missing input never gets forced into the catch-all category.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In case_when(), what does the final TRUE ~ "default" line do?

Nothing; it’s optional decoration
Catches any row that didn’t match an earlier condition
Overrides every other condition
Only applies to missing values


True or false. case_when() conditions are checked in order, and the first match wins.

Predict the output.
x <- c(5, 15, 25, NA)
case_when(
  x < 10 ~ "low",
  x < 20 ~ "medium",
  TRUE   ~ "high"
)


Find the error.
case_when(
  age = "Senior" ~ age >= 65,
  TRUE ~ "Non-senior"
)



Show answers


b) Catches any row that didn’t match an earlier condition.

True. Conditions are evaluated top to bottom, and the first TRUE match determines the result.
[1] "low"    "medium" "high"   NA
The condition and value are reversed, and = is used instead of ~. It should be age >= 65 ~ "Senior".
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: 66  case_when()
text:
66.9 Where you’ve arrived
You started this level with a vague sense that real data needs fixing before it can be trusted, and a stack of tools scattered across Level 1 through Level 3 for spotting problems. You’ve now built a complete, systematic toolkit for actually fixing what you find: identifying, removing, and replacing missing values; detecting and removing duplicates; renaming and retyping variables in every direction; building new variables and recoding, combining, and binarizing categories; and — across these last four pages — the full progression from a single if decision to ifelse()’s vectorized version, if_else()’s type-safe version, and case_when()’s clean, multi-condition version.
That last tool, case_when(), is also your bridge into Level 5: it’s a dplyr function, and Level 5 is where you meet the rest of the tidyverse — filter(), mutate(), summarise(), and the pipe |> — that will make everything you’ve done so far in base R read even more like the sentence you’d say the question in out loud.
crumbs: Level 4 — Data Cleaning with Base R

title: Level 5 — Tidyverse
text:
What you’ll build toward
By the end of this level you’ll rewrite nearly everything from Level 4 more readably: selecting and filtering with select() and filter(), sorting with arrange(), building and summarizing variables with mutate() and summarise(), grouped analysis with group_by(), quick counts and unique values with count() and distinct(), tidyverse-style renaming, applying one operation across many columns with across(), and chaining every one of these together with the native pipe |> into a single, readable pipeline.

title: Level 5 — Tidyverse
text:
Topics in this level

select(), filter(), arrange()
mutate() and summarise()
group_by(), count(), distinct()
rename() and across()
The native pipe |>
Chaining multi-step analyses

title: Level 5 — Tidyverse
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: your professor asks you to identify every participant 65 or older with elevated blood pressure — in one readable pipeline, not a maze of nested brackets and repeated df$ references.
By the last page, you’ll write a single chained pipeline — import, filter, group, summarise, arrange — that reads, top to bottom, almost exactly like the research question it answers.

title: 67  select()
text:
67.1 The R Concept
select(), from dplyr (part of the tidyverse), picks columns by name, with no quotes and no brackets required:
library(dplyr)
select(df, col1, col2, col3)
select(participants, id, age, county)
You can also exclude columns with a minus sign, mirroring Level 2’s negative indexing:
select(participants, -bmi)         # everything except bmi
select(participants, -c(bmi, smoker))   # everything except bmi and smoker
And several helper functions let you select by pattern rather than typing every name: starts_with("sys"), ends_with("_bp"), contains("bp") — useful once a dataset has dozens of similarly named columns.

title: 67  select()
text:
67.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

simplified <- select(participants, id, age, county)
head(simplified, 3)

  id age    county
1  1  46   Oakford
2  2  53 Riverdale
3  3  59   Oakford

without_extras <- select(participants, -bmi, -smoker)
names(without_extras)

[1] "id"           "age"          "sex"          "county"       "systolic_bp"
[6] "diastolic_bp"

bp_only <- select(participants, ends_with("_bp"))
head(bp_only, 3)

  systolic_bp diastolic_bp
1         125           79
2         123           80
3         130           77

title: 67  select()
text:
67.3 Explore
Try select(participants, county, age, id) — notice the order of the columns in the result matches the order you listed them in, not their original order in the dataset. This is a genuinely useful side effect: select() can reorder columns, not just filter which ones appear.

title: 67  select()
text:
67.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

handout_data <- select(participants, id, age, county)

head(handout_data)

  id age    county
1  1  46   Oakford
2  2  53 Riverdale
3  3  59   Oakford
4  4  49 Millbrook
5  5  61 Millbrook
6  6  18 Riverdale

ncol(handout_data)

[1] 3
Exactly the three columns your professor asked for, in the order requested, with the code itself reading almost like the request: “select id, age, county.”

title: 67  select()
text:
67.5 Interpret the Result
handout_data now contains only the three requested columns, ready to export (Level 3’s write_csv()) or print directly for the handout — and reading the select() call back, its intent is immediately obvious in a way participants[, c("id", "age", "county")] requires a moment more thought to parse.

Common Mistakes


Quoting column names inside select(). Unlike base R’s bracket indexing, select() uses unquoted column names directly — select(participants, "age") still works, but select(participants, age) is the idiomatic tidyverse style.

Forgetting the minus sign drops, rather than keeps, a column. select(participants, -age) returns every column except age — a common first-encounter mix-up.

Forgetting to load dplyr. Like every package function since Level 1, select() requires library(dplyr) (or library(tidyverse)) first.

title: 67  select()
text:
67.6 Practice Problem
Use select() to create a data frame with just county, smoker, and bmi, in that order, and confirm with names() that the columns appear in the order you specified.

title: 67  select()
text:
67.7 Challenge Problem
Use select() with a helper function (starts_with(), ends_with(), or contains()) to select every column whose name contains the text "bp", without typing systolic_bp or diastolic_bp by name individually.

title: 67  select()
text:
67.8 Key Takeaways


select(df, col1, col2, ...) picks columns by unquoted name, reading more directly than base R’s bracket-and-quotes indexing.
A minus sign (-col) excludes a column rather than selecting it; the order columns are listed in becomes their order in the result.
Helper functions like starts_with(), ends_with(), and contains() select columns by name pattern, useful for datasets with many similarly named variables.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does select(df, -age) do?

Selects only the age column
Selects every column except age

Removes all rows where age is missing
Renames the age column


True or false. Column names inside select() must be quoted.
Predict the output. What columns (and in what order) would select(df, county, id) return, if df originally has columns in the order id, age, county?
Write the code. Write the select() call to keep only id and smoker from participants.


Show answers


b) Selects every column except age.

False. select() uses unquoted column names as its idiomatic style, though quoted names also work.

county first, then id — in the order listed in the select() call, not the original order.
select(participants, id, smoker)

title: 68  filter()
text:
68.1 The R Concept
filter() keeps only the rows where a condition is TRUE, using column names directly with no df$ prefix and no comma-based row/column indexing:
filter(df, condition)
filter(participants, age >= 65 & systolic_bp >= 130)
Multiple conditions can also be separated by commas instead of & — filter() treats a comma-separated list of conditions as “all of these must be true,” identical to combining them with &:
filter(participants, age >= 65, systolic_bp >= 130)   # equivalent to using &

title: 68  filter()
text:
68.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Base R, from Level 2 - for comparison
base_r_result <- participants[participants$age >= 65 & participants$systolic_bp >= 130, ]
nrow(base_r_result)

[1] 4

# dplyr - same result, more directly readable
tidy_result <- filter(participants, age >= 65 & systolic_bp >= 130)
nrow(tidy_result)

[1] 4

identical(base_r_result, tidy_result)

[1] TRUE

title: 68  filter()
text:
68.3 Explore
Try filter(participants, age >= 65, systolic_bp >= 130) (commas instead of &) and confirm it produces the identical result. Then try filter(participants, county == "Oakford" | county == "Millbrook") — using | (or) to match either of two counties at once, extending Level 1’s logical operators directly.

title: 68  filter()
text:
68.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

at_risk <- filter(participants, age >= 65 & systolic_bp >= 130)

at_risk

   id age    sex      county systolic_bp diastolic_bp  bmi smoker
1  31  70   Male     Oakford         145           85   NA     No
2  51  66   Male Cedar Falls         133           77 34.5     No
3  62  81   Male   Millbrook         153           95 30.3    Yes
4  84  67 Female   Riverdale         134           79 26.8     No
Exactly 4 participants meet both criteria — ready to hand to your professor as the flagged, at-risk subgroup, in one readable line rather than a bracket-and-comma expression.

title: 68  filter()
text:
68.5 Interpret the Result
Four participants — ages 70, 66, 81, and 67 — are both 65 or older and have elevated systolic blood pressure (130+), spanning four different counties and including one current smoker. This is exactly the clinically relevant subgroup your professor asked you to identify, produced by a single filter() call whose logic is legible at a glance.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting filter() needs no df$ prefix. filter(participants, participants$age >= 65) still technically runs, but defeats the point — filter(participants, age >= 65) is the idiomatic, intended style.

Using = instead of ==. Exactly the Level 1 trap, still relevant here: filter(participants, sex = "Female") is a syntax error inside filter() (R interprets = as a named-argument assignment) — it must be sex == "Female".

Confusing & (and) with | (or), or forgetting the distinction entirely. filter(participants, age >= 65 & systolic_bp >= 130) requires both conditions; filter(participants, age >= 65 | systolic_bp >= 130) requires only either one — very different result sets.

title: 68  filter()
text:
68.6 Practice Problem
Use filter() to find every participant who is a current smoker (smoker == "Yes") and has a BMI of 30 or higher. Report how many participants meet both criteria.

title: 68  filter()
text:
68.7 Challenge Problem
Use filter() with %in% (Level 4) to find every participant living in either Riverdale or Cedar Falls, then separately confirm this matches the count you’d get by adding table(participants$county)’s counts for those two counties individually.

title: 68  filter()
text:
68.8 Key Takeaways


filter(df, condition) keeps only rows matching a condition, using column names directly with no df$ prefix or bracket-comma syntax.
Multiple conditions can be combined with & (and), | (or), or by separating them with commas (equivalent to &).

filter() produces exactly the same result as base R’s logical row indexing (Level 2) — the difference is entirely in readability, not correctness.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does filter(df, age >= 65 & bmi >= 30) return?

Every row where either condition is true
Every row where both conditions are true
Only the age and bmi columns
A single logical value


True or false. Separating conditions with a comma inside filter() behaves like combining them with &.
Predict the output. How many rows would filter(df, TRUE) return, for any data frame df?

Find the error.
filter(participants, sex = "Female")



Show answers


b) Every row where both conditions are true.

True. A comma-separated list of conditions in filter() is equivalent to combining them with &.
Every row — TRUE matches everything, so filter(df, TRUE) returns the entire, unfiltered data frame.

filter() requires == for comparison, not = (which R interprets as a named-argument assignment inside the function call). It should be filter(participants, sex == "Female").

title: 69  arrange()
text:
69.1 The R Concept
arrange() sorts a data frame’s rows by one or more columns, in ascending order by default:
arrange(df, column)          # ascending (smallest first)
arrange(df, desc(column))    # descending (largest first)
arrange(participants, age)          # youngest first
arrange(participants, desc(age))    # oldest first
Unlike sorting a plain vector (where only the values themselves move), arrange() reorders entire rows together — every column’s value for a given participant stays correctly matched to that same participant, just in a new row position.
You can also sort by multiple columns; ties in the first column are broken by the second:
arrange(participants, county, desc(age))   # by county, then oldest-first within each county

title: 69  arrange()
text:
69.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

youngest_first <- arrange(participants, age)
head(youngest_first, 3)

  id age    sex     county systolic_bp diastolic_bp  bmi smoker
1  6 18 Female  Riverdale         117           64 24.0     No
2 33 18 Female    Oakford         149           94 30.3    Yes
3 36 18   Male Springvale         152           88 23.1     No

oldest_first <- arrange(participants, desc(age))
head(oldest_first, 3)

  id age    sex      county systolic_bp diastolic_bp  bmi smoker
1 62 81   Male   Millbrook         153           95 30.3    Yes
2 19 80 Female Cedar Falls         109           79 28.4     No
3 17 79 Female  Springvale         117           73 21.1    Yes

title: 69  arrange()
text:
69.3 Explore
Try arrange(participants, county, desc(age)) — sorting first by county (alphabetically), then by age (oldest first) within each county. Compare this to arrange(participants, desc(age), county) — sorting by age first, with county only breaking ties. Notice these produce genuinely different orderings, since the first column listed always takes priority.

title: 69  arrange()
text:
69.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

oldest_first <- arrange(participants, desc(age))

head(oldest_first, 5)

  id age    sex      county systolic_bp diastolic_bp  bmi smoker
1 62  81   Male   Millbrook         153           95 30.3    Yes
2 19  80 Female Cedar Falls         109           79 28.4     No
3 17  79 Female  Springvale         117           73 21.1    Yes
4 50  74   Male   Millbrook         114           75 33.5     No
5 31  70   Male     Oakford         145           85   NA     No
The five oldest participants (ages 81, 80, 79, 74, and 70) are now at the top, ready for your professor to spot-check — and notably, two of the five oldest participants (ages 81 and 70) do have elevated systolic blood pressure, echoing the pattern the previous page’s filter() began to surface.

title: 69  arrange()
text:
69.5 Interpret the Result
arrange(participants, desc(age)) reordered every row by age, oldest first, while keeping each participant’s full record intact and correctly matched — participant #62 (Millbrook, age 81) is now row 1, with every one of their other values (blood pressure, BMI, smoking status) correctly still attached to them, not shuffled independently.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting desc() for descending order. arrange(df, age) sorts smallest-first by default; without wrapping the column in desc(), you’ll get the opposite order from what you intended for a “largest first” request.

Assuming arrange() changes the underlying data. Like filter() and select(), arrange() returns a new, reordered data frame — the original participants object is completely unaffected unless you explicitly reassign the result back to it.

Not realizing column order matters for multi-column sorts. arrange(df, a, b) and arrange(df, b, a) can produce different orderings — the first-listed column always takes sorting priority, with later columns only breaking ties.

title: 69  arrange()
text:
69.6 Practice Problem
Use arrange() to sort participants by bmi in ascending order, and identify the participant with the lowest recorded BMI.

title: 69  arrange()
text:
69.7 Challenge Problem
Sort participants by county (alphabetical, ascending) and then by systolic_bp (descending) within each county, and report the participant with the highest blood pressure in Springvale specifically.

title: 69  arrange()
text:
69.8 Key Takeaways


arrange(df, column) sorts rows in ascending order by default; arrange(df, desc(column)) sorts descending.
Sorting a data frame reorders entire rows together, keeping every column correctly matched to its original participant.
Multiple sort columns break ties in order — the first column listed always takes priority.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does arrange(df, desc(age)) do?

Sorts by age, youngest first
Sorts by age, oldest first
Removes the age column
Filters out missing ages


True or false. arrange() modifies the original data frame in place, without needing reassignment.
Predict the output. After arrange(df, x), which row would have the smallest value of x?
Write the code. Write the code to sort participants by bmi, largest first.


Show answers


b) Sorts by age, oldest first.

False. Like other dplyr verbs, arrange() returns a new data frame; the original is unchanged unless reassigned.
The first row.
arrange(participants, desc(bmi))

title: 70  mutate()
text:
70.1 The R Concept
mutate() creates (or modifies) one or more columns at once, using column names directly with no df$ prefix — and, crucially, later new columns within the same mutate() call can reference earlier ones:
mutate(df,
  new_col1 = <calculation>,
  new_col2 = <calculation using new_col1>
)
mutate(participants,
  pulse_pressure = systolic_bp - diastolic_bp,
  is_hypertensive = as.numeric(systolic_bp >= 130)
)
This mirrors Level 4’s case_when() and ifelse() exactly in spirit — mutate() is simply the tidyverse’s version of “assign a new column,” the same way filter() was the tidyverse’s version of logical row indexing.

title: 70  mutate()
text:
70.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants_new <- mutate(participants,
  pulse_pressure = systolic_bp - diastolic_bp,
  is_hypertensive = as.numeric(systolic_bp >= 130)
)

names(participants_new)

[1] "id"              "age"             "sex"             "county"
[5] "systolic_bp"     "diastolic_bp"    "bmi"             "smoker"
[9] "pulse_pressure"  "is_hypertensive"

head(participants_new[, c("systolic_bp", "diastolic_bp", "pulse_pressure", "is_hypertensive")], 3)

  systolic_bp diastolic_bp pulse_pressure is_hypertensive
1         125           79             46               0
2         123           80             43               0
3         130           77             53               1

title: 70  mutate()
text:
70.3 Explore
Try referencing a just-created column within the same mutate() call: mutate(participants, pulse_pressure = systolic_bp - diastolic_bp, high_pp = as.numeric(pulse_pressure > 50)). Confirm high_pp correctly uses pulse_pressure, even though both were created in the very same call — mutate() processes its arguments in order, top to bottom.

title: 70  mutate()
text:
70.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants <- mutate(participants,
  pulse_pressure = systolic_bp - diastolic_bp,
  bmi_category = case_when(
    bmi < 18.5 ~ "Underweight",
    bmi < 25   ~ "Normal",
    bmi < 30   ~ "Overweight",
    TRUE       ~ "Obese"
  ),
  is_hypertensive = as.numeric(systolic_bp >= 130)
)

table(participants$bmi_category, useNA = "always")

Normal Obese Overweight Underweight       <NA>
    28    25         39           3           5
All three derived variables from your professor’s request were added in a single mutate() call, and the bmi_category counts (28 Normal, 25 Obese, 39 Overweight, 3 Underweight, 5 missing) exactly match the counts you first computed with nested ifelse() back in Level 2 — the same result, produced more readably.

title: 70  mutate()
text:
70.5 Interpret the Result
Three new derived variables — pulse_pressure, bmi_category, and is_hypertensive — now exist in participants, all added in one step whose logic is easy to read top to bottom, rather than three disconnected participants$... <- ... lines scattered through a script.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting mutate() returns a new data frame. Like filter(), select(), and arrange(), mutate() doesn’t modify participants in place — you must reassign the result (participants <- mutate(participants, ...)) to keep the new columns.

Assuming column order within mutate() doesn’t matter. It does — a later argument can reference an earlier one within the same call (as in the Explore step), but not the reverse; arguments are evaluated top to bottom, in the order written.

Recreating Level 4’s case_when() logic from scratch instead of reusing it. mutate() doesn’t replace case_when(), ifelse(), or arithmetic — it’s the container they go inside; all of Level 4’s conditional tools remain exactly as useful here.

title: 70  mutate()
text:
70.6 Practice Problem
Use mutate() to add a bp_ratio column (systolic_bp / diastolic_bp) and an age_group column ("Senior" for 65+, "Non-senior" otherwise, using ifelse()) in a single call.

title: 70  mutate()
text:
70.7 Challenge Problem
Use mutate() to add a risk_score column that equals 1 for every hypertension criterion a participant meets, summed across being 65+, having BMI 30+, and being a current smoker (hint: this needs three separate as.numeric() comparisons added together within one mutate() expression). Report the maximum risk_score observed in the dataset.

title: 70  mutate()
text:
70.8 Key Takeaways


mutate(df, new_col = ...) creates or modifies one or more columns at once, using bare column names with no df$ prefix.
Later columns within the same mutate() call can reference earlier ones, evaluated top to bottom.
Like other dplyr verbs, mutate() returns a new data frame — reassign the result to keep the changes.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does mutate(df, x2 = x * 2) do?

Removes column x

Creates a new column x2 equal to x times 2
Filters rows where x equals 2
Renames x to x2



True or false. Within a single mutate() call, a later column can reference one created earlier in the same call.
Predict the output. After df <- mutate(df, y = x + 1), if the original df had no column y, does df now have one?
Write the code. Write the mutate() call to add a column bmi_rounded equal to round(bmi, 0).


Show answers


b) Creates a new column x2 equal to x times 2.

True. mutate() evaluates its arguments in order, so later ones can reference earlier ones within the same call.
Yes — since the result was reassigned back to df, the new y column now exists.
mutate(df, bmi_rounded = round(bmi, 0))

title: 71  summarise()
text:
71.1 The R Concept
summarise() collapses a data frame into a single summary row (or one row per group, as you’ll see next page), using the same “no df$ prefix” style as mutate():
summarise(df,
  stat1 = <summary function>(column1),
  stat2 = <summary function>(column2)
)
summarise(participants,
  n_participants = n(),
  mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
  mean_bp = mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE)
)
n() is a special summarise()-only helper that counts rows — it needs no argument because it always counts whatever rows are currently being summarised.

title: 71  summarise()
text:
71.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

summarise(participants,
  n_participants = n(),
  mean_age = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
  mean_bmi = mean(bmi, na.rm = TRUE)
)

  n_participants mean_age mean_bmi
1            100 47.42553 27.12316
Notice the result is a data frame with exactly one row — summarise() always collapses, no matter how many rows or columns the input had.

title: 71  summarise()
text:
71.3 Explore
Try adding min_age = min(age, na.rm = TRUE) and max_age = max(age, na.rm = TRUE) to the same summarise() call, alongside the existing statistics. Confirm all five summary values appear together in the single output row.

title: 71  summarise()
text:
71.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

study_summary <- summarise(participants,
  n_participants  = n(),
  mean_age        = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
  mean_systolic   = mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE),
  mean_bmi        = mean(bmi, na.rm = TRUE)
)

study_summary

  n_participants mean_age mean_systolic mean_bmi
1            100 47.42553      129.8438 27.12316
Your professor’s one-line summary is now a single, tidy row: 100 participants, mean age 47.4, mean systolic blood pressure 129.8, mean BMI 27.1 — every number na.rm = TRUE correctly excluded missing values from, exactly as mean() did back in Level 2.

title: 71  summarise()
text:
71.5 Interpret the Result
study_summary collapses all 100 rows of participants into one summary row with four statistics. This is the tidyverse’s answer to the same “describe the dataset in a few numbers” task you did with individual mean() calls in Level 2 — now assembled together in a single readable step.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting na.rm = TRUE inside summarise(). This is exactly the Level 2 trap, still fully in force — mean(age) returns NA the instant age contains even one missing value; summarise() doesn’t change this base R behavior at all.

Confusing summarise() with mutate(). mutate() keeps the same number of rows and adds columns; summarise() collapses down to one row (or one row per group) — mixing them up is one of the most common early tidyverse errors.

Expecting n() to take an argument. n() always counts the current rows automatically — n(age) is an error; just write n().

title: 71  summarise()
text:
71.6 Practice Problem
Use summarise() to compute the number of participants, the mean diastolic_bp, and the maximum bmi in a single call.

title: 71  summarise()
text:
71.7 Challenge Problem
Use summarise() to compute what percentage of participants are current smokers (hint: mean(smoker == "Yes") * 100 computes this directly, since TRUE/FALSE are treated as 1/0). Confirm your percentage matches the smoker count from Level 2’s table(participants$smoker) (75 No, 25 Yes).

title: 71  summarise()
text:
71.8 Key Takeaways


summarise(df, stat = <function>(column)) collapses a data frame down to one summary row.

n() is a summarise()-only helper that counts the current rows, with no argument needed.

na.rm = TRUE is still required inside summary functions whenever a column has missing values — summarise() doesn’t change this base R rule.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does summarise(df, avg = mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) return?

The original data frame, unchanged
A one-row data frame with a single column avg

A vector of every value of x

An error, since summarise() needs group_by() first


True or false. summarise() always returns the same number of rows as the input data frame.
Predict the output. What would summarise(participants, total = n()) return?

Find the error.
summarise(participants, mean_age = mean(age))



Show answers


b) A one-row data frame with a single column avg.

False. summarise() collapses down to one row (or one row per group, covered next page) — never the original row count.
A one-row data frame with a column total equal to 100 (the number of rows in participants).

age has missing values, so mean(age) without na.rm = TRUE returns NA — the same Level 2 rule still applies inside summarise(). It should be mean(age, na.rm = TRUE).

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.1 The R Concept
group_by() doesn’t change how the data looks when printed — it tags the data frame as “grouped,” so that every subsequent summarise() (or mutate()) is computed separately within each group instead of across the whole dataset:
grouped <- group_by(df, grouping_column)
summarise(grouped, stat = <summary function>(column))
by_county <- group_by(participants, county)
summarise(by_county, mean_bp = mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE))
The result has one row per group — five rows for five counties — instead of summarise()’s usual single overall row.

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

by_county <- group_by(participants, county)

county_summary <- summarise(by_county,
  n_participants = n(),
  mean_bp = round(mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
)

county_summary

# A tibble: 5 x 3
  county      n_participants mean_bp
  <chr>                <int>   <dbl>
1 Cedar Falls             20  128.16
2 Millbrook               21  133.30
3 Oakford                 17  129.71
4 Riverdale               16  124.36
5 Springvale              26  131.46
Five rows, one per county — n() inside a grouped summarise() counts rows within each group, not the whole dataset.

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.3 Explore
Try grouping by two columns at once: group_by(participants, county, smoker), then summarise(..., n = n()). Confirm the result has one row for every county-by-smoking-status combination that actually occurs in the data (not necessarily all 10 possible combinations, if some don’t occur).

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

by_county <- group_by(participants, county)

county_summary <- summarise(by_county,
  n_participants = n(),
  mean_age = round(mean(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
  mean_bp  = round(mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
)

county_summary

# A tibble: 5 x 4
  county      n_participants mean_age mean_bp
  <chr>                <int>    <dbl>   <dbl>
1 Cedar Falls             20    50.58  128.16
2 Millbrook               21    48.00  133.30
3 Oakford                 17    42.20  129.71
4 Riverdale               16    51.27  124.36
5 Springvale              26    45.40  131.46
Millbrook has the highest mean blood pressure (133.30) of all five counties, while Riverdale has the lowest (124.36) — exactly the county-by-county comparison your professor asked for, computed in two group_by() + summarise() lines instead of five repeated subset-and-mean calls.

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.5 Interpret the Result
Splitting participants by county and summarising within each group reveals real variation base R’s single overall mean (129.84 from the previous page) completely hid: Millbrook’s average blood pressure (133.30) is nearly nine points higher than Riverdale’s (124.36) — the kind of pattern a single ungrouped summary can never surface.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting group_by() alone doesn’t summarise anything. group_by(participants, county) just tags the data as grouped — printing it looks nearly identical to the ungrouped data frame. The grouping only takes visible effect once you call summarise() (or mutate()) afterward.

Leaving a data frame grouped after you’re done with it. If you group_by() and then later run mutate() for something unrelated, the mutation will unexpectedly happen within groups. Use ungroup() afterward if you need fully ungrouped data again.

Forgetting na.rm = TRUE still applies per group. Missing values are excluded within each group’s calculation, not just once overall — the rule from Level 2 still holds, just applied five times instead of once.

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.6 Practice Problem
Use group_by() and summarise() to compute the number of participants and the mean bmi for each smoker status ("Yes" vs "No").

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.7 Challenge Problem
Use group_by(participants, county) followed by summarise() to find which county has both the highest mean BMI and the highest proportion of smokers. Are they the same county?

title: 72  group_by()
text:
72.8 Key Takeaways


group_by(df, column) tags a data frame as grouped, without changing its printed appearance by itself.
Following group_by() with summarise() computes statistics separately within each group, producing one row per group.
Always ungroup() (or start a fresh pipeline) once you’re done with grouped operations, to avoid accidentally grouping later steps.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does group_by(df, county) do by itself, before any summarise()?

Immediately computes county means
Tags the data frame as grouped by county, with no visible summary yet
Deletes all columns except county

Sorts the data frame by county


True or false. summarise() after group_by(df, county) produces one row per county.
Predict the output. If participants has 5 distinct counties, how many rows would summarise(group_by(participants, county), n = n()) return?

Find the error.
grouped <- group_by(participants, county)
grouped
# expecting to already see county-level means here



Show answers


b) Tags the data frame as grouped by county, with no visible summary yet.

True. Grouped summarise() computes one row per distinct group value.
5 rows — one per distinct county.

group_by() alone doesn’t compute anything — it only sets up the grouping. A summarise() call must follow to actually see county-level statistics.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.1 The R Concept
count() counts rows per group in one step — no group_by() needed first:
count(df, column)
count(participants, county)
This is exactly equivalent to summarise(group_by(participants, county), n = n()) from the previous page, but in a single readable call. You can also count combinations of multiple columns at once, and sort by frequency with sort = TRUE:
count(participants, county, smoker)          # every county-by-smoker combination
count(participants, county, sort = TRUE)     # counts ordered largest first

title: 73  count()
text:
73.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

count(participants, county)

       county  n
1 Cedar Falls 20
2   Millbrook 21
3     Oakford 17
4   Riverdale 16
5  Springvale 26
Note the column is automatically named n — matching exactly what table(participants$county) reported back in Level 2, just returned as a proper data frame instead of a table object.

count(participants, county, sort = TRUE)

       county  n
1  Springvale 26
2   Millbrook 21
3 Cedar Falls 20
4     Oakford 17
5   Riverdale 16
sort = TRUE reorders the rows largest-count-first, instantly surfacing that Springvale is the largest county in the study.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.3 Explore
Try count(participants, smoker) and confirm it matches Level 2’s table(participants$smoker) result (75 “No”, 25 “Yes”). Then try count(participants, county, smoker) — notice the result has one row for every county-and-smoking-status combination that actually appears, not all 10 mathematically possible combinations.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

county_smoker_counts <- count(participants, county, smoker)

county_smoker_counts

        county smoker  n
1  Cedar Falls     No 17
2  Cedar Falls    Yes  3
3    Millbrook     No 15
4    Millbrook    Yes  6
5      Oakford     No 11
6      Oakford    Yes  6
7    Riverdale     No 12
8    Riverdale    Yes  4
9   Springvale     No 20
10  Springvale    Yes  6
All 10 county-by-smoking-status combinations appear (every county has at least one smoker and one non-smoker), giving your professor exactly the cross-tabulation table() alone (Level 2) could only produce for a single column at a time without extra work.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.5 Interpret the Result
county_smoker_counts breaks the earlier single smoker count (75 No, 25 Yes) down further by county — for example, Oakford has the highest proportion of smokers relative to its size (6 of 17, roughly 35%), while Cedar Falls has the lowest (3 of 20, 15%).

Common Mistakes


Trying to count() a column that doesn’t exist or is misspelled. Like every dplyr verb, count() uses bare column names — a typo (count(participants, contry)) produces a “column not found” error, not a silent empty result.

Forgetting count() already includes an implicit group_by(). You don’t need to call group_by() first — count(participants, county) handles the grouping and summarising in one step internally.

Confusing count()’s output column name. The count column is always named n by default (not count or freq) — referencing county_smoker_counts$count will fail; it must be county_smoker_counts$n.

title: 73  count()
text:
73.6 Practice Problem
Use count() to find how many participants fall into each combination of sex and smoker. Which combination is least common?

title: 73  count()
text:
73.7 Challenge Problem
Use count(participants, county, sort = TRUE) to confirm Springvale is the largest county, then use count(participants, county, smoker) to determine whether Springvale also has the largest number of smokers (not necessarily the same as the largest proportion).

title: 73  count()
text:
73.8 Key Takeaways


count(df, column) is a one-line shortcut for group_by() + summarise(n = n()), with the result column always named n.

count() can take multiple columns to count every combination that actually occurs in the data.

sort = TRUE reorders the result largest-count-first, useful for quickly spotting the most (or least) common categories.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What is count(df, column) equivalent to?

filter(df, column)

group_by(df, column) + summarise(n = n())

arrange(df, column)
select(df, column)


True or false. The count column produced by count() is always named n.
Predict the output. What would count(participants, smoker) return, given 75 “No” and 25 “Yes” values?
Write the code. Write the count() call to count participants per combination of county and sex, sorted largest-first.


Show answers


b) group_by(df, column) + summarise(n = n()).

True. count()’s result column is always named n by default.
A two-row data frame: smoker = "No", n = 75 and smoker = "Yes", n = 25.
count(participants, county, sex, sort = TRUE)

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.1 The R Concept
distinct() returns the unique rows (or unique combinations of specified columns), with no count attached:
distinct(df, column)
distinct(participants, county)
This is the tidyverse counterpart to Level 3’s unique(participants$county) — but because distinct() operates on a data frame rather than a single vector, it can also find unique combinations across several columns at once:
distinct(participants, county, sex)   # every county-sex combination that occurs

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

distinct(participants, county)

       county
1     Oakford
2   Riverdale
3   Millbrook
4  Springvale
5 Cedar Falls
Five distinct counties — the rows appear in the order they were first encountered in the dataset (row 1 is Oakford because participant #1 lives there), not alphabetically.

nrow(distinct(participants, county))

[1] 5

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.3 Explore
Try distinct(participants, county, sex) and check nrow() on the result. With 5 counties and 2 sex categories, there are at most 10 possible combinations — confirm whether all 10 actually occur in the data, or whether some combination is missing entirely.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

unique_counties <- distinct(participants, county)

nrow(unique_counties)

[1] 5

unique_county_sex <- distinct(participants, county, sex)

nrow(unique_county_sex)

[1] 10
Five distinct counties confirmed, matching what unique(participants$county) reported in Level 3 and what count(participants, county) implied by returning 5 rows on the previous page. All 10 possible county-and-sex combinations occur at least once in the data.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.5 Interpret the Result
distinct() confirms there are exactly 5 unique counties in the study — no unexpected typos or extra category hiding in the data (recall Level 3’s messy dataset, where inconsistent capitalization made “13 distinct-looking values” collapse to only 5 real counties after cleaning). This clean dataset’s county column has no such problem.

Common Mistakes


Confusing distinct() with count(). distinct() returns only the unique values themselves, with no counts attached; count() returns the unique values plus how many rows had each. Reach for distinct() when you only care “what values exist,” and count() when you also care “how many of each.”

Expecting distinct() to sort its output. Unlike arrange(), distinct() returns unique values in the order they first appear in the data, not alphabetically or numerically — combine with arrange() afterward if sorted output matters.

Forgetting distinct() on a whole data frame (no columns specified) checks every column. distinct(participants) with no column names looks for entire duplicate rows across all columns — a different (and much rarer) kind of duplicate than a single repeated county value.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.6 Practice Problem
Use distinct() to find every unique combination of smoker and sex that occurs in the dataset, and report how many there are.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.7 Challenge Problem
Compare nrow(distinct(participants, county, smoker)) to the number of rows produced by last page’s count(participants, county, smoker). Should these two numbers be the same? Explain why or why not.

title: 74  distinct()
text:
74.8 Key Takeaways


distinct(df, column) returns the unique values of a column (or unique combinations of several columns), with no counts attached.

distinct() is the tidyverse counterpart to Level 3’s unique(), extended to work across multiple columns at once.
Rows appear in first-encountered order, not sorted — pair with arrange() if sorted output is needed.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. How does distinct() differ from count()?


distinct() returns unique values with counts; count() doesn’t

distinct() returns unique values with no counts; count() returns unique values with counts
They are identical in every way

distinct() only works on numeric columns


True or false. distinct(participants, county) sorts the counties alphabetically.
Predict the output. If smoker has only two possible values (“Yes” and “No”), how many rows would distinct(participants, smoker) return?
Write the code. Write the code to find every unique combination of county and smoker in participants.


Show answers


b) distinct() returns unique values with no counts; count() returns unique values with counts.

False. distinct() preserves first-encountered order, not alphabetical order.
2 rows — one for "Yes", one for "No".
distinct(participants, county, smoker)

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.1 The R Concept
rename() renames columns with a direct new_name = old_name mapping — no names() indirection required:
rename(df, new_name = old_name)
rename(participants, sbp = systolic_bp, dbp = diastolic_bp)
Read the argument order carefully: it’s new name first, old name second — the reverse of how you might instinctively say it aloud (“rename systolic_bp to sbp”), but consistent with every other tidyverse verb’s new_column = <expression> pattern (mutate(), summarise()).

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

renamed <- rename(participants, sbp = systolic_bp, dbp = diastolic_bp)

names(renamed)

[1] "id"      "age"     "sex"     "county"  "sbp"     "dbp"     "bmi"
[8] "smoker"
systolic_bp and diastolic_bp are gone from the column names; sbp and dbp have taken their place, in the same column positions.

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.3 Explore
Try rename(participants, participant_id = id) and confirm with names() that only id changed, while every other column name — including its position in the data frame — stayed exactly the same. rename() never reorders columns, unlike select().

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

for_collaborator <- rename(participants,
  sbp = systolic_bp,
  dbp = diastolic_bp
)

names(for_collaborator)

[1] "id"      "age"     "sex"     "county"  "sbp"     "dbp"     "bmi"
[8] "smoker"

head(for_collaborator[, c("sbp", "dbp")], 3)

  sbp dbp
1 125  79
2 123  80
3 130  77
The dataset is ready to share with your professor’s collaborator, using the clearer sbp/dbp names — with every value untouched, only the column labels changed.

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.5 Interpret the Result
for_collaborator has identical data to participants, just with two columns relabeled. This is purely cosmetic — no values changed, no rows were reordered, and nrow()/ncol() are identical to the original.

Common Mistakes


Reversing the argument order. rename(participants, systolic_bp = sbp) would try to rename a column called sbp (which doesn’t exist yet) to systolic_bp — the opposite of the intended renaming. Always read it as “new = old.”

Expecting rename() to reorder columns. Unlike select(), rename() never changes column order or drops any columns — it only relabels the ones you specify, leaving everything else (including position) untouched.

Forgetting to reassign the result. Like every other dplyr verb covered so far, rename() returns a new data frame; participants itself is unaffected unless you write participants <- rename(participants, ...).

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.6 Practice Problem
Use rename() to rename bmi to body_mass_index and county to region, and confirm with names() that both changes took effect while all other column names and their order stayed the same.

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.7 Challenge Problem
Compare Level 4’s base R approach (names(df)[names(df) == "old"] <- "new") against rename() for renaming three columns at once. Which approach scales better as the number of renamed columns grows, and why?

title: 75  rename()
text:
75.8 Key Takeaways


rename(df, new_name = old_name) relabels a column directly — argument order is new name first, old name second.

rename() never reorders or drops columns; it only changes labels, leaving positions and all other columns untouched.
Like other dplyr verbs, rename() returns a new data frame and must be reassigned to keep the change.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In rename(df, new_name = old_name), which comes first?

The old name
The new name
Order doesn’t matter
Neither — both must be quoted strings in a vector


True or false. rename() can change the order of columns in a data frame.
Predict the output. After df2 <- rename(df, patient_id = id), does df still have a column called id?

Find the error.
rename(participants, systolic_bp = sbp)
# intended to rename systolic_bp to sbp



Show answers


b) The new name comes first, then =, then the old name.

False. rename() only changes column labels — it never reorders or drops columns.
Yes — rename() returns a new data frame (df2); the original df is completely unaffected.
The argument order is reversed. Since participants has a column named systolic_bp (not sbp), this call tries to rename a nonexistent sbp column. It should be rename(participants, sbp = systolic_bp).

title: 76  across()
text:
76.1 The R Concept
across() applies one function to several columns inside summarise() or mutate(), instead of writing one summary expression per column:
summarise(df, across(c(col1, col2, col3), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))
summarise(participants, across(c(age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, bmi), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))
The ~ starts a short formula-style function, where .x stands in for “whichever column across() is currently processing” — the first time through, .x is age; the second time, .x is systolic_bp; and so on. This is the same “apply one operation across many things” idea as Level 10’s loops, just expressed as a single dplyr call rather than an explicit for loop.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

summarise(participants,
  across(c(age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, bmi), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))
)

       age systolic_bp diastolic_bp      bmi
1 47.42553    129.84375        80.44 27.12316
Four means, computed with a single across() expression — each result column keeps the original column’s name, since no renaming was requested.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.3 Explore
Try naming the results by wrapping across()’s function argument in a named list: across(c(age, bmi), list(avg = ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))). Notice the output columns become age_avg and bmi_avg — across() automatically builds column names by combining the original column name with the function’s label.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

numeric_summary <- summarise(participants,
  across(c(age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, bmi), ~ round(mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE), 2))
)

numeric_summary

    age systolic_bp diastolic_bp   bmi
1 47.43      129.84        80.44 27.12
All four means computed and rounded in one across() call — the same result you’d get from four separate mean(..., na.rm = TRUE) expressions, but without repeating the pattern four times.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.5 Interpret the Result
The four numeric variables average out to age 47.43, systolic BP 129.84, diastolic BP 80.44, and BMI 27.12 — matching exactly the individual means computed on earlier pages, now produced by one concise instruction instead of four nearly-identical ones.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting the ~ before the function. across(cols, mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)) tries to evaluate mean(.x, ...) immediately (before .x has any meaning) and errors; the ~ is what tells R “treat this as a function to apply later, once per column.”

Using . instead of .x. Older tidyverse code sometimes used a single dot (.) as the placeholder; modern across() uses .x — mixing the two conventions is a common source of confusion when reading examples from different tutorial vintages.

Applying across() to non-numeric columns by accident. across(everything(), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)) would try to compute a mean of county (text) and fail — always specify which columns are actually numeric, or use a selector like where(is.numeric).

title: 76  across()
text:
76.6 Practice Problem
Use across() inside summarise() to compute the maximum value (not mean) of age, systolic_bp, and bmi in a single call.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.7 Challenge Problem
Use across() combined with group_by(participants, county) to compute the mean age and mean bmi for each county in a single summarise(across(...)) call, rather than writing mean_age = ... and mean_bmi = ... as two separate lines.

title: 76  across()
text:
76.8 Key Takeaways


across(c(col1, col2, ...), ~ <function>(.x, ...)) applies one function to several columns inside summarise() or mutate().
The ~ marks a formula-style function, where .x stands in for whichever column is currently being processed.

across() eliminates copy-pasting the same summary expression once per column, the same repetition-avoidance principle behind Level 9’s functions.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does .x represent inside across(cols, ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE))?

The entire data frame
Whichever column across() is currently processing
The number of columns in cols

A fixed column named x



True or false. across() can only be used with mean(), never other summary functions.
Predict the output. How many result columns would across(c(age, bmi, systolic_bp), ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)) produce inside summarise()?

Find the error.
summarise(participants, across(c(age, bmi), mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE)))



Show answers


b) Whichever column across() is currently processing.

False. across() works with any summary function — sum(), max(), sd(), or a custom function.
3 result columns — one for each of age, bmi, and systolic_bp.
