anti_join() does the opposite: it keeps only the rows of the first data frame that do not have a matching key in the second — again, adding no new columns:
anti_join(df1, df2, by = "key_column")
anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")
Both are filtering joins — unlike left_join()/inner_join()/right_join()/full_join() (all “mutating joins” that add columns), semi_join() and anti_join() only ever return a subset of the first data frame’s own rows and columns.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

has_followup <- semi_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

nrow(has_followup)

[1] 15

names(has_followup)

[1] "id"           "age"          "sex"          "county"       "baseline_bmi"
15 rows — the participants with a follow-up match — but notice: only baseline’s own 5 columns, with nothing from followup attached (unlike inner_join(), which would have added followup_sbp and followup_smoker too).

lost_to_followup <- anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")

lost_to_followup$id

[1] 16 17 18 19 20

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.3 Explore
Try anti_join(followup, baseline, by = "id") — reversing which file is first. Confirm this returns the 5 new recruits (ids 21-25) instead of the 5 dropouts — anti_join()’s result depends entirely on which data frame is listed first, exactly like every join so far.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

lost_to_followup <- anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")
new_recruits <- anti_join(followup, baseline, by = "id")

lost_to_followup[, c("id", "county", "age")]

   id      county age
16 16   Millbrook  45
17 17  Springvale  79
18 18     Oakford  26
19 19 Cedar Falls  80
20 20     Oakford  55

new_recruits$id

[1] 21 22 23 24 25
Two clean, separate reports for your professor: exactly the 5 baseline participants lost to follow-up (ids 16-20, ages ranging from 26 to 80), and exactly the 5 new recruits who joined only at follow-up (ids 21-25) — each extracted with a single filtering join, no unwanted columns attached.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.5 Interpret the Result
semi_join() and anti_join() answer “which rows match (or don’t)?” without the complexity of merged, NA-filled columns — often exactly what’s needed when the goal is identifying who falls into a group, not analyzing their combined data. Compare this to Page 10’s inner_join(), which answers a related but different question (“give me the matched data, combined”) by adding columns rather than just filtering rows.

Common Mistakes


Reaching for inner_join() when you only needed a filter. If your goal is just “which participants match,” semi_join() gives a cleaner result than inner_join() followed by manually dropping the unwanted follow-up columns.

Forgetting anti_join()’s direction matters enormously. anti_join(baseline, followup, ...) (dropouts) and anti_join(followup, baseline, ...) (new recruits) answer completely different questions — mixing them up reverses which group you’re actually reporting on.

Expecting semi_join() to add any columns at all. Unlike every other join in this level, semi_join()’s result never has more columns than its first argument — if you need followup’s columns too, semi_join() is the wrong tool; reach for inner_join() instead.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.6 Practice Problem
Use semi_join(followup, baseline, by = "id") to find which follow-up participants also have a baseline record. How many are there, and does this match inner_join()’s row count from Page 10?

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.7 Challenge Problem
Confirm that nrow(semi_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")) + nrow(anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")) equals nrow(baseline) exactly. Explain in words why this must always be true for any two data frames and any key column.

title: 90  semi_join() and anti_join()
text:
90.8 Key Takeaways


semi_join(df1, df2, by = "key") keeps only df1’s rows that have a match in df2, with none of df2’s columns added.

anti_join(df1, df2, by = "key") keeps only df1’s rows that have no match in df2 — again, no new columns.
Both are filtering joins, distinct from the “mutating joins” (left_join(), inner_join(), right_join(), full_join()) that combine columns from both data frames.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. How many columns does semi_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") return, if baseline has 5 columns and followup has 3?

8 (5 + 3)
5 (only baseline’s columns)
3 (only followup’s columns)
2 (only the shared key and one other)


True or false. anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") and anti_join(followup, baseline, by = "id") return the same rows.
Predict the output. Given 20 baseline ids and 15 with a follow-up match, how many rows would anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") return?
Short answer. When would you prefer semi_join() over inner_join(), even though both only keep matching rows?


Show answers


b) 5 — semi_join() never adds columns from the second data frame, regardless of how many it has.

False. The two calls answer different questions entirely — dropouts (baseline-only) versus new recruits (followup-only) — and generally return completely different rows.
5 rows — the baseline ids (16-20) with no matching follow-up record.
When you only need to know which rows match, not to actually combine data from both files — semi_join() avoids adding unwanted columns that you’d otherwise have to select() away after an inner_join().

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.1 The R Concept
Real analyses frequently need both reshaping and joining — reshape one dataset into an analysis-ready shape, then join in additional variables from an entirely different source before the real analysis can happen:
df |>
  pivot_longer(...) |>          # reshape to long
  filter(...) |>
  pivot_wider(...) |>            # reshape back to wide, aligned for calculation
  mutate(change = ...) |>        # compute the per-unit value
  left_join(other_df, by = "id") # bring in variables the first file never had
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")
baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")

bp_change <- bp_wide |>
  pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"), names_to = "visit", names_prefix = "sbp_", values_to = "sbp") |>
  filter(visit %in% c("visit1", "visit3")) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = sbp) |>
  filter(!is.na(visit3)) |>
  mutate(change = visit3 - visit1)

nrow(bp_change)

[1] 26

bp_change_with_age <- left_join(bp_change, baseline[, c("id", "age")], by = "id")

sum(is.na(bp_change_with_age$age))

[1] 10
10 of the 26 complete-case participants have no usable age — 9 simply aren’t in baseline_enrollment.csv at all (this visits study enrolled a broader group than the baseline file covers), and 1 (participant 13) is in baseline_enrollment.csv but has a missing age value there.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.3 Explore
Try filter(bp_change_with_age, is.na(age)) and inspect which ids these are. Confirm 9 of them are ids outside baseline_enrollment.csv’s range entirely (using setdiff()), and 1 (id 13) is inside the range but has its own pre-existing missing age.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.5 Interpret the Result
Answering your professor’s question required chaining nearly every tool from this level: pivot_longer() and pivot_wider() to align visit1 and visit3 for a per-participant change score, filter() to handle dropouts (Page 7), and finally left_join() to bring in age from a completely separate file — with 10 participants necessarily excluded from the final age-based comparison because that information simply doesn’t exist for them. The under-50 group’s larger average blood pressure drop is a genuine pattern in this small dataset, but with 7 and 9 participants per group, it’s a hypothesis to investigate further (Level 11), not a settled conclusion.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting that joining across studies often means partial coverage, not full coverage. bp_visits_wide.csv and baseline_enrollment.csv weren’t guaranteed to describe the same participants — 9 of 26 simply had no baseline record at all, which is normal and expected, not a data error to “fix.”

Silently dropping unmatched rows without reporting how many. The move from 26 complete-case participants to 16 with usable age is a meaningful reduction — always state it explicitly rather than letting the final summary table’s row count speak for itself.

Treating a striking small-sample pattern as a confirmed finding. The under-50 group’s larger blood pressure drop, based on just 9 participants, is worth flagging and investigating further — not yet reported as if it were a validated result.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.6 Practice Problem
Redo this pipeline joining in sex instead of age from baseline_enrollment.csv, and compare mean blood pressure change between male and female participants (among those with a usable sex value).
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.7 Challenge Problem
Redo the join using full_join() instead of left_join() between bp_change and baseline. Does nrow() of the result change? Explain what full_join() would add here that left_join() didn’t, given that baseline might contain enrolled participants who aren’t in bp_visits_wide.csv at all.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together
text:
91.8 Key Takeaways

Real analyses often require reshaping and joining together — reshaping to compute a per-unit value, then joining to bring in variables that weren’t part of the original file.
Joining across two independently collected files frequently produces partial, not full, coverage — always report how many rows lack a match, and why that’s expected rather than an error.
A striking pattern found in a small, joined subset should be reported as a lead worth investigating, not treated as a final answer.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Why was left_join() (not inner_join()) used to bring age into bp_change?


left_join() runs faster
To keep every complete-case visits participant, even those without a baseline match, rather than silently dropping them

inner_join() doesn’t exist in dplyr
There is no difference between the two here


True or false. Every participant in bp_visits_wide.csv also appears in baseline_enrollment.csv.
Predict the output. If 26 participants have complete visit data and 10 lack a usable age, how many remain in the final age-group summary?
Short answer. Why should the under-50 group’s larger average blood pressure drop be reported cautiously, even though the calculation itself is correct?


Show answers


b) To keep every complete-case visits participant, even those without a baseline match, rather than silently dropping them (an inner_join() would have removed them immediately, hiding the coverage gap).

False. 9 of the 26 complete-case participants have no matching id in baseline_enrollment.csv at all.
16 participants (26 minus the 10 lacking a usable age).
The comparison rests on only 7 (50+) and 9 (under 50) participants — small enough that the pattern could easily be due to chance rather than a real, generalizable effect; it’s worth investigating further, not yet reporting as a confirmed finding.
crumbs: 91  Level 6 Capstone: Reshaping and Joining Together

title: Level 7 — Descriptive Statistics
text:
What you’ll build toward
You’ll return to data/participants.csv — the same 100-participant dataset from every earlier level — and this time describe it numerically rather than clean or reshape it: central tendency, spread, quantiles, frequency tables, cross-tabulations, and correlation. You’ll finish by assembling everything into a single descriptive report, the kind of numeric summary that opens almost every health study.

title: Level 7 — Descriptive Statistics
text:
Topics in this level

Mean, median, and mode — and a base R trap around the word “mode” itself
Range, variance, and standard deviation
Quantiles, percentiles, and the interquartile range (IQR)
summary() and the five-number summary
Frequency tables and proportions with table() and prop.table()
Cross-tabulations between two categorical variables
Correlation with cor()
A capstone: assembling a full descriptive statistics report

title: Level 7 — Descriptive Statistics
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: write the first paragraph of a health report — the “Table 1” description of your study sample that every published study opens with — entirely from numbers you compute yourself, correctly handling the missing values that have run through this dataset since Level 1.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.1 The R Concept
The mean is the sum of all values divided by how many there are — sensitive to unusually large or small values (“outliers”). The median is the middle value once the data is sorted (or the average of the two middle values, for an even count) — much less affected by outliers. The mode is the most frequently occurring value:
mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
median(x, na.rm = TRUE)
R has no built-in function for the statistical mode. The function called mode() in base R does something completely unrelated — it reports an object’s storage type ("numeric", "character", etc.), not its most frequent value:
mode(participants$age)
That’s age’s storage type, not its most common value — a well-known trap for anyone coming from other statistical software. Finding the actual statistical mode requires table() (Level 4) instead:
table(x)[which.max(table(x))]

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text: [1] "numeric"

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 47.42553

median(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 49
The mean (47.43) and median (49) are reasonably close here, suggesting age isn’t wildly skewed by extreme outliers — though they’re not identical, so it’s still worth reporting both.

mode(participants$age)

[1] "numeric"
Exactly the trap described above — mode() reports the storage type, telling you nothing about which age value is most common.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.3 Explore
Try table(participants$age) directly and scan the output for the largest count. Then try sort(table(participants$age), decreasing = TRUE)[1:3] to see the three most frequent ages at a glance, without scanning the whole table by eye.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

mean_age <- mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
median_age <- median(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

age_table <- table(participants$age)
mode_age <- names(age_table)[which.max(age_table)]

mean_age

[1] 47.42553

median_age

[1] 49

mode_age

[1] "53"

age_table[mode_age]

53
 5
Mean age 47.43, median age 49, and the single most common age is 53 (occurring 5 times) — three different, complementary answers to “what’s typical,” none of which is simply “more correct” than the others.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.5 Interpret the Result
The mean pulls slightly lower than the median here, hinting at a slight leftward skew (a few younger participants pulling the average down relative to the middle value) — consistent with what you’d expect from a health study spanning ages 18 to 81. The mode (53) tells a different kind of story: it doesn’t describe “the middle” or “the average,” just which single value happens to repeat most often — useful for categorical data, but often less informative than the mean or median for a wide-ranging numeric variable like age.

Common Mistakes


Using mode() expecting the statistical mode. As demonstrated above, mode() in R reports an object’s storage type — this is one of the most common traps for beginners, especially those coming from Excel or other statistical software where “mode” always means “most frequent value.”

Forgetting na.rm = TRUE for mean and median alike. Both mean() and median() return NA by default if any value is missing — the Level 2 rule applies to both identically, not just to mean().

Assuming mean and median should always be close together. They coincide closely for roughly symmetric data, but can diverge substantially for skewed data (like income, where a few very high earners pull the mean well above the median) — a difference between them is itself useful information, not an error.

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.6 Practice Problem
Compute the mean, median, and mode of bmi. Are the mean and median close together, or do they diverge notably? What might that suggest about the shape of the BMI distribution?

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.7 Challenge Problem
Write your own small function (Level 9 preview) called get_mode() that takes a vector and returns its most frequent value using table() and which.max(), handling NA values appropriately. Test it on participants$county (a categorical variable, where “mode” is a much more natural summary than it is for age).

title: 92  Mean, Median, and Mode
text:
92.8 Key Takeaways

Mean, median, and mode each describe “what’s typical” differently — mean is sensitive to outliers, median is not, and mode identifies the single most frequent value.
R’s built-in mode() function reports storage type, not the statistical mode — use table() combined with which.max() instead.
A meaningful difference between mean and median can itself be an informative finding about a variable’s distribution shape.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does mode(participants$age) actually return in base R?

The most frequent age
The middle age value
The storage type of the age column (e.g., "numeric")
An error, since mode() doesn’t exist


True or false. median() is generally less sensitive to extreme outliers than mean().
Predict the output. If age has 94 non-missing values (an even count), how is the median calculated?

Find the error.
most_common_age <- mode(participants$age)



Show answers


c) The storage type of the age column — mode() in base R is unrelated to the statistical mode.

True. The median only depends on the middle value(s) once sorted, so extreme values don’t pull it the way they pull the mean.
As the average of the two middle values once all 94 non-missing ages are sorted.

mode() doesn’t compute the statistical mode in R — it returns the object’s storage type. Finding the actual most frequent age requires table(participants$age) combined with which.max().

title: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
93.1 The R Concept
The range is simply the difference between the maximum and minimum values:
max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)
# or, more directly:
range(x, na.rm = TRUE)   # returns c(min, max)
Variance measures the average squared distance of each value from the mean — squaring makes every deviation positive and emphasizes larger deviations more heavily:
var(x, na.rm = TRUE)
Standard deviation is the square root of variance, which brings the measure back into the same units as the original data (unlike variance, which is in squared units) — making it the far more commonly reported of the two:
sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
crumbs: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
93.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

range(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 18 81

var(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 226.0105

sd(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 15.0336
Ages range from 18 to 81 (a 63-year span), with a standard deviation of about 15 years — meaning a “typical” participant’s age deviates from the mean (47.43) by roughly 15 years in either direction.
crumbs: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
93.3 Explore
Try computing sd(participants$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE) and sd(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE). Compare their relative sizes to their means — a standard deviation of 15 means something very different for a variable averaging 47 than it would for one averaging 130.
crumbs: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
93.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

age_range <- range(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
age_sd <- sd(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

bp_range <- range(participants$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE)
bp_sd <- sd(participants$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE)

age_range

[1] 18 81

age_sd

[1] 15.0336

bp_range

[1]  90 179

bp_sd

[1] 18.8709
Age spans 63 years with an SD of about 15; systolic blood pressure spans 89 points (90 to 179) with an SD of about 18.9 — both meaningfully spread out, but on entirely different scales, which is exactly why reporting the units alongside any spread measure matters.
crumbs: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
93.5 Interpret the Result
A standard deviation of 15.03 years for age tells you that most participants fall within roughly 32 to 62 years of age (mean ± 1 SD) — a genuinely wide range for this study. Systolic blood pressure’s SD of 18.87 similarly indicates substantial participant-to-participant variation, not a tightly clustered sample. Neither the range nor the SD by itself tells the complete story — the range is entirely determined by just two extreme values, while the SD reflects the spread of every value at once, which is why SD is usually the more informative of the two for describing a full distribution.

Common Mistakes


Reporting variance instead of standard deviation in a written summary. Variance is in squared units (e.g., “years²”), which is rarely meaningful to a reader — always convert to standard deviation (or report both, with SD emphasized) before writing up results.

Relying on the range alone to describe spread. The range depends entirely on the two most extreme values in the entire dataset — a single unusually high or low measurement can make the range look far more dramatic than the data’s typical spread actually is.

Forgetting na.rm = TRUE for var() and sd(), not just mean(). Every one of these functions follows the same Level 2 rule: any missing value makes the result NA unless explicitly told to remove it first.
crumbs: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
93.6 Practice Problem
Compute the range, variance, and standard deviation of bmi. Given BMI’s mean (27.12) and SD, would a BMI of 40 be unusually far from typical, or well within the range you’d expect for this sample?
crumbs: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
93.7 Challenge Problem
Compute the standard deviation of systolic_bp separately for smokers and non-smokers (hint: use participants$systolic_bp[participants$smoker == "Yes"] from Level 2’s logical indexing, or filter() from Level 5). Does blood pressure appear more variable within one smoking group than the other?
crumbs: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation
text:
93.8 Key Takeaways

Range is the distance between the minimum and maximum values — simple, but sensitive to just two extreme data points.
Variance measures average squared distance from the mean; standard deviation (its square root) restates that spread in the data’s original units, making it far easier to interpret.
A measure of central tendency (Page 1) and a measure of spread (this page) together give a far more complete picture than either alone.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What units is standard deviation expressed in, relative to the original data?

Squared units
The same units as the original data
Always a percentage
Unitless


True or false. The range is affected only by the two most extreme values in a dataset, regardless of how the rest of the data is distributed.
Predict the output. If every value in a vector were identical, what would sd(x) equal?

Find the error.
cat("The typical age varies by about", var(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE), "years from the mean.")



Show answers


b) The same units as the original data — this is precisely why SD is reported far more often than variance.

True. The range depends entirely on the minimum and maximum; every other value in between has no effect on it whatsoever.

0 — with no variation among identical values, there is no spread at all.
This reports var() (variance, in squared years) as if it were a spread measured in years — but variance and standard deviation are not interchangeable. It should use sd(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE) instead, which is in the same units (years) as the original data.
crumbs: 93  Range, Variance, and Standard Deviation

title: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range
text:
94.1 The R Concept
A quantile is a value below which a given proportion of the data falls. The quartiles are the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles — dividing the data into four equal-sized groups:
quantile(x, na.rm = TRUE)                    # 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100% by default
quantile(x, probs = 0.9, na.rm = TRUE)        # just the 90th percentile
The interquartile range (IQR) is the distance between the 1st and 3rd quartiles — the span of the middle 50% of the data, deliberately ignoring the most extreme quarter on either end:
IQR(x, na.rm = TRUE)
A common rule of thumb flags a value as a potential outlier if it falls more than 1.5×IQR below Q1 or above Q3.
crumbs: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range

title: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range
text:
94.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

quantile(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)

     0%     25%     50%     75%    100%
  15.60   23.35   27.70   30.20   38.60

IQR(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 6.85
The middle 50% of BMI values falls between 23.35 and 30.20 — a fairly narrow band — while the full range (15.6 to 38.6) is much wider, hinting that a modest number of participants sit further out toward either extreme.
crumbs: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range

title: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range
text:
94.3 Explore
Try quantile(participants$age, probs = c(0.1, 0.9), na.rm = TRUE) to find the 10th and 90th percentiles directly, rather than the default quartiles. This tells you the age range containing the middle 80% of participants, ignoring the most extreme 10% on either end.
crumbs: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range

title: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range
text:
94.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_q1 <- quantile(participants$bmi, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
bmi_q3 <- quantile(participants$bmi, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
bmi_iqr <- IQR(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)

lower_bound <- bmi_q1 - 1.5 * bmi_iqr
upper_bound <- bmi_q3 + 1.5 * bmi_iqr

lower_bound

     25%
13.075

upper_bound

    75%
40.475

sum(participants$bmi > upper_bound | participants$bmi < lower_bound, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 0
No participants fall outside the 1.5×IQR outlier bounds (13.075 to 40.475) — a BMI of 40 would actually just barely exceed the upper bound, but the highest BMI actually recorded in this study is 38.6, comfortably within the “not unusual” range by this rule.
crumbs: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range

title: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range
text:
94.5 Interpret the Result
BMI’s middle 50% (23.35 to 30.20) is fairly tightly clustered, and even the full range (15.6 to 38.6) doesn’t produce any values flagged as outliers by the standard 1.5×IQR rule. This is a genuinely useful finding to report: it tells your professor there’s no single extreme participant unduly influencing the mean or standard deviation reported on the previous two pages.

Common Mistakes


Confusing quartiles with percentiles. Quartiles are simply specific percentiles (25th, 50th, 75th) — quantile(x, 0.25) and the “1st quartile” are the exact same number, just described with different vocabulary.

Forgetting the 1.5×IQR rule identifies potential outliers, not certain errors. A value outside these bounds deserves a closer look (was it a data entry mistake, or a genuinely extreme but valid measurement?) — it should never be deleted automatically just for falling outside the rule.

Applying na.rm = TRUE inconsistently across quantile() and IQR(). Both functions follow the same missing-value rule as every other summary statistic in this level — omit na.rm = TRUE and a single NA in the data makes the whole result NA.
crumbs: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range

title: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range
text:
94.6 Practice Problem
Compute the 1st and 3rd quartiles and the IQR for systolic_bp, then use the 1.5×IQR rule to check whether any participants have unusually high or low blood pressure readings.
crumbs: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range

title: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range
text:
94.7 Challenge Problem
Compute the 5th and 95th percentiles of age using quantile(participants$age, probs = c(0.05, 0.95), na.rm = TRUE). How many participants fall outside this middle-90% range? Compare this count to what the 1.5×IQR rule would flag for the same variable.
crumbs: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range

title: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range
text:
94.8 Key Takeaways


quantile(x, probs, na.rm = TRUE) computes any percentile you specify; the default call returns the quartiles (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%).

IQR(x, na.rm = TRUE) measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data, ignoring the most extreme quarter on either end.
The 1.5×IQR rule flags potential outliers for further investigation — it’s a starting point for judgment, not an automatic deletion rule.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does quantile(x, 0.5, na.rm = TRUE) return?

The mean
The median (50th percentile)
The mode
The standard deviation


True or false. The IQR uses the full range of the data, from minimum to maximum.
Predict the output. If Q1 = 20 and Q3 = 40, what is the IQR?

Find the error.
# "Any value outside 1.5xIQR is a data entry error and should be deleted"



Show answers


b) The median — the 50th percentile is exactly the median by definition.

False. The IQR uses only the 1st and 3rd quartiles (the middle 50%), deliberately excluding the most extreme values at either end.
20 (40 − 20).
The 1.5×IQR rule flags potential outliers worth investigating, not confirmed errors — a value outside these bounds could be a genuine, valid, if unusual, measurement. It should never be deleted automatically without first checking whether it’s a real observation.
crumbs: 94  Quantiles and the Interquartile Range

title: 95  The summary() Function
text:
95.1 The R Concept
summary() produces the “five-number summary” (minimum, 1st quartile, median, 3rd quartile, maximum) plus the mean, all in one call — and automatically reports a count of missing values if any exist, with no na.rm needed:
summary(x)

title: 95  The summary() Function
text:
95.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

summary(participants$age)

   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's
  18.00   36.25   49.00   47.43   58.75   81.00       6
One call reports the minimum (18), 1st quartile (36.25), median (49), mean (47.43), 3rd quartile (58.75), maximum (81), and missing count (6) — everything from the previous three pages, condensed into a single readable line.

title: 95  The summary() Function
text:
95.3 Explore
Try summary(participants) — calling summary() on the entire data frame at once, not just one column. Notice it produces a compact summary of every column simultaneously: numeric columns get the five-number-summary treatment, while character columns (like county) get a simpler length/class/mode description instead.

title: 95  The summary() Function
text:
95.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

summary(participants$bmi)

   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's
  15.60   23.35   27.70   27.12   30.20   38.60       5

summary(participants$systolic_bp)

   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's
  90.00  116.75  128.50  129.84  145.25  179.00       4
Both variables’ complete descriptive profiles — spread, center, and missingness all at once — ready to drop directly into your professor’s report without needing four separate function calls per variable.

title: 95  The summary() Function
text:
95.5 Interpret the Result
summary() confirms what the earlier pages found piece by piece: BMI’s mean (27.12) and median (27.70) sit close together, suggesting a roughly symmetric distribution, while systolic blood pressure’s mean (129.84) and median (128.50) are similarly close. Both variables report a handful of missing values (5 and 4 respectively) automatically — no separate sum(is.na(...)) call needed.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting summary() needs no na.rm argument. Unlike mean(), median(), var(), sd(), and quantile(), summary() handles missing values automatically, reporting them in a dedicated NA's row rather than requiring na.rm = TRUE.

Applying summary() to a character column and expecting numeric output. summary(participants$county) reports length, class, and mode (the storage type trap from Page 1) rather than quartiles — summary() behaves differently depending on the column’s type.

Treating summary()’s output as a data frame you can immediately manipulate. summary() returns a special table-like object formatted for reading, not something you’d typically pipe into filter() or mutate() — for further computation, go back to the individual functions (mean(), quantile(), etc.).

title: 95  The summary() Function
text:
95.6 Practice Problem
Run summary(participants) on the whole data frame and identify which columns show an NA's count, and which don’t.

title: 95  The summary() Function
text:
95.7 Challenge Problem
Compare summary(participants$age)’s reported mean to the value from mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE) computed on Page 1. Confirm they match exactly, then explain in words why summary() doesn’t need an explicit na.rm argument to produce this correct result.

title: 95  The summary() Function
text:
95.8 Key Takeaways


summary(x) reports the minimum, quartiles, mean, maximum, and missing-value count for a numeric column in one call.

summary() automatically excludes missing values from its calculations and reports their count separately — no na.rm argument required.

summary() behaves differently for character or factor columns than for numeric ones, and can also summarize an entire data frame at once.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Which of these does summary() NOT report for a numeric column?

Mean
Standard deviation
Median
Maximum


True or false. summary() requires an explicit na.rm = TRUE argument to handle missing values correctly.
Predict the output. If a column has no missing values at all, would summary() still show an NA's row?
Short answer. Why might you still want to compute sd() separately, even after running summary()?


Show answers


b) Standard deviation is not part of summary()’s output — only the five-number summary plus the mean.

False. summary() handles missing values automatically, reporting their count in a dedicated row without any na.rm argument needed.
No — the NA's row only appears when there is at least one missing value to report.

summary() doesn’t report standard deviation at all, even though it’s one of the most commonly cited spread measures alongside the mean — sd() must still be called separately to get it.

title: 96  Frequency Tables and Proportions
text:
96.1 The R Concept
prop.table() takes a table of counts (from table()) and converts every count into a proportion of the total:
counts <- table(x)
prop.table(counts)
Multiplying by 100 converts proportions into percentages:
round(prop.table(counts) * 100, 1)

title: 96  Frequency Tables and Proportions
text:
96.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

smoker_counts <- table(participants$smoker)
smoker_counts

 No Yes
 75  25

prop.table(smoker_counts)

  No  Yes
0.75 0.25

round(prop.table(smoker_counts) * 100, 1)

  No  Yes
75.0 25.0
75% of participants are non-smokers, 25% are current smokers — the exact percentage your professor asked for, computed directly from the raw counts you already knew how to produce.

title: 96  Frequency Tables and Proportions
text:
96.3 Explore
Try table(participants$county) followed by round(prop.table(table(participants$county)) * 100, 1). With 5 counties, confirm the percentages sum to (approximately) 100% — small rounding differences can occur when percentages don’t divide evenly.

title: 96  Frequency Tables and Proportions
text:
96.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

county_counts <- table(participants$county)
county_percentages <- round(prop.table(county_counts) * 100, 1)

county_counts

Cedar Falls   Millbrook     Oakford   Riverdale  Springvale
         20          21          17          16          26

county_percentages

Cedar Falls   Millbrook     Oakford   Riverdale  Springvale
       20.0        21.0        17.0        16.0        26.0
Springvale is both the largest county by raw count (26 participants) and by percentage (26.0%) — with Riverdale the smallest at 16 participants (16.0%). Since these percentages happen to divide evenly (all counts are whole percentages of 100), they sum to exactly 100%.

title: 96  Frequency Tables and Proportions
text:
96.5 Interpret the Result
Reporting “26% of participants are from Springvale” is far more interpretable to most readers than “26 participants are from Springvale” without knowing the total — prop.table() is what turns Level 4’s raw table() counts into the percentages that actually appear in published health reports.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting prop.table() operates on the table, not the original vector. prop.table(participants$county) (passing the raw column) doesn’t work as intended — prop.table() expects a table() object as input, not the original data.

Not rounding before displaying percentages. prop.table(counts) * 100 alone can produce long, hard-to-read decimals (like 16.666666...) — round(..., 1) keeps a report readable without implying false precision.

Reporting percentages without also reporting the underlying counts. A percentage alone (“25% smoke”) hides the sample size behind it — 25% of 100 participants carries very different weight than 25% of just 8, so both numbers usually belong together in a report.

title: 96  Frequency Tables and Proportions
text:
96.6 Practice Problem
Compute the percentage of participants who are "Female" versus "Male" using table() and prop.table() on the sex column.

title: 96  Frequency Tables and Proportions
text:
96.7 Challenge Problem
Compute table(participants$smoker) percentages both including and excluding any missing values (check whether smoker has any NAs first with sum(is.na(participants$smoker))). Does handling missing values change the reported percentages meaningfully in this case?

title: 96  Frequency Tables and Proportions
text:
96.8 Key Takeaways


prop.table(table(x)) converts a frequency table’s raw counts into proportions (summing to 1).
Multiplying by 100 and rounding produces reader-friendly percentages.
Always report percentages alongside their underlying counts, so a reader can judge how much data the percentage is actually based on.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does prop.table() expect as its input?

A raw data frame
A table() object of counts
A single numeric value
A character vector


True or false. Proportions from prop.table() always sum to exactly 1.
Predict the output. If 40 out of 100 participants are male, what would prop.table(table(sex))["Male"] return?

Find the error.
prop.table(participants$smoker)



Show answers


b) A table() object of counts — prop.table() converts counts to proportions, so it needs a table as input.

True. By definition, every category’s count divided by the total will sum to exactly 1 (aside from potential floating-point rounding at many decimal places).

0.4.

prop.table() was given the raw smoker column directly, rather than a table() of its counts. It should be prop.table(table(participants$smoker)).

title: 97  Cross-Tabulations
text:
97.1 The R Concept
table() with two arguments builds a cross-tabulation — a grid with one variable’s categories as rows and the other’s as columns:
table(row_variable, column_variable)
table(participants$sex, participants$smoker)
To convert this into row-wise percentages (e.g., “of all women, what percent smoke?”), use prop.table() with margin = 1 (percentages within each row):
prop.table(table(participants$sex, participants$smoker), margin = 1)

title: 97  Cross-Tabulations
text:
97.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sex_smoker_table <- table(participants$sex, participants$smoker)
sex_smoker_table

        No Yes
Female  42  10
Male    33  15
Now you can see, at a glance, that among the 52 women, 42 don’t smoke and 10 do — and among the 48 men, 33 don’t smoke and 15 do.

round(prop.table(sex_smoker_table, margin = 1) * 100, 1)

          No  Yes
Female  80.8 19.2
Male    68.8 31.2
margin = 1 computes each row’s percentages within that row — 80.8% and 19.2% for women sum to 100%, and 68.8% and 31.2% for men sum to 100% separately, rather than all four percentages summing to 100% together.

title: 97  Cross-Tabulations
text:
97.3 Explore
Try prop.table(sex_smoker_table) with no margin argument at all. Notice all four percentages now sum to 100% overall, answering a different question (“what percent of the entire study is female non-smokers?”) than the row-wise version above (“what percent of women don’t smoke?”).

title: 97  Cross-Tabulations
text:
97.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sex_smoker_table <- table(participants$sex, participants$smoker)
sex_smoker_pct <- round(prop.table(sex_smoker_table, margin = 1) * 100, 1)

sex_smoker_table

        No Yes
Female  42  10
Male    33  15

sex_smoker_pct

          No  Yes
Female  80.8 19.2
Male    68.8 31.2
Yes — the smoking rate does differ meaningfully by sex in this sample: 31.2% of men smoke, compared to 19.2% of women, a 12-percentage-point difference directly answering your professor’s question.

title: 97  Cross-Tabulations
text:
97.5 Interpret the Result
Reporting the overall 25% smoking rate (previous page) alone would have hidden this sex difference entirely — the cross-tabulation reveals that men in this sample smoke at nearly double the rate of women. As always, this is a descriptive pattern in n=48 men and n=52 women, not yet a tested statistical claim (Level 11 covers how to test whether a difference like this is likely to reflect a real, generalizable pattern versus chance).

Common Mistakes


Forgetting to specify margin and misreading the resulting percentages. Without margin = 1 or margin = 2, prop.table() computes percentages relative to the entire table, not within each row or column — a very different (and often not what you intended) question.

Confusing margin = 1 (row percentages) with margin = 2 (column percentages). margin = 1 makes each row sum to 100%; margin = 2 makes each column sum to 100% instead — mixing these up flips which variable you’re computing percentages “within.”

Drawing a causal conclusion from a descriptive cross-tabulation. “Men smoke more than women in this sample” is a valid descriptive finding — it is not, by itself, evidence that being male causes higher smoking rates; cross-tabulations describe patterns, they don’t establish causation.

title: 97  Cross-Tabulations
text:
97.6 Practice Problem
Build a cross-tabulation of county and smoker, then compute column percentages (margin = 2) to answer: “of all current smokers, what percentage live in each county?”

title: 97  Cross-Tabulations
text:
97.7 Challenge Problem
Use table(participants$sex, participants$smoker) together with margin = 2 (instead of margin = 1) to compute: “of all smokers, what percentage are male versus female?” Explain in words why this answers a different question than the row-percentage version already computed above.

title: 97  Cross-Tabulations
text:
97.8 Key Takeaways


table(var1, var2) builds a two-way cross-tabulation, showing how two categorical variables relate.

prop.table(cross_tab, margin = 1) computes row-wise percentages; margin = 2 computes column-wise percentages; omitting margin computes percentages relative to the whole table.
A cross-tabulation can reveal patterns (like a sex difference in smoking rate) that an overall single-variable summary completely hides — but it remains a descriptive finding, not a causal claim.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does margin = 1 mean in prop.table(tab, margin = 1)?

Percentages computed within each column
Percentages computed within each row
Percentages computed relative to the whole table
It has no effect


True or false. A cross-tabulation can reveal patterns that an overall single-variable frequency table would hide.
Predict the output. In sex_smoker_table, do the row percentages for “Female” (80.8, 19.2) sum to 100?
Short answer. Why is “men smoke more than women in this sample” a safer statement than “being male causes higher smoking rates,” based on this cross-tabulation alone?


Show answers


b) Percentages computed within each row.

True. The overall 25% smoking rate hid a substantial sex difference (19.2% vs. 31.2%) that only the cross-tabulation revealed.
Yes — 80.8 + 19.2 = 100, exactly as expected for row percentages.
A cross-tabulation only describes an association observed in this particular sample — it doesn’t rule out other explanations (like differences in age, county, or other unmeasured factors between the male and female participants) that a purely descriptive table can’t account for.

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
98.1 The R Concept
cor() computes the Pearson correlation coefficient between two numeric variables, a single number between -1 and 1:
cor(x, y, use = "complete.obs")
Values in between describe weaker versions of these patterns — a common rough guide treats values below about 0.3 in magnitude as “weak,” 0.3-0.7 as “moderate,” and above 0.7 as “strong,” though these thresholds vary by field. The use = "complete.obs" argument tells cor() to use only rows where both variables are non-missing — cor() has no separate na.rm argument like mean() does.

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
+1 means a perfect positive relationship (as one variable increases, so does the other, exactly proportionally).

-1 means a perfect negative relationship (as one increases, the other decreases, exactly proportionally).

0 means no linear relationship at all.

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
98.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

cor(participants$bmi, participants$systolic_bp, use = "complete.obs")

[1] 0.1631
A weak positive correlation: higher BMI is very slightly associated with higher systolic blood pressure in this sample, but the relationship is far from strong.

cor(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp, use = "complete.obs")

[1] -0.2211
A weak negative correlation between age and blood pressure — the opposite direction you might have expected, and a reminder that real (and simulated) data doesn’t always match intuition.

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
98.3 Explore
Try cor(participants$systolic_bp, participants$diastolic_bp, use = "complete.obs"). Since both are blood pressure readings taken from the same participants at the same time, you’d expect a much stronger relationship than either of the two above — confirm whether that’s the case.

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
98.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

cor_bmi_bp <- cor(participants$bmi, participants$systolic_bp, use = "complete.obs")
cor_age_bp <- cor(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp, use = "complete.obs")
cor_sbp_dbp <- cor(participants$systolic_bp, participants$diastolic_bp, use = "complete.obs")

cor_bmi_bp

[1] 0.1631

cor_age_bp

[1] -0.2211

cor_sbp_dbp

[1] 0.9204
Systolic and diastolic blood pressure correlate very strongly (0.92) — participants with higher systolic readings consistently have higher diastolic readings too, exactly as clinically expected — while BMI (0.16) and age (-0.22) show only weak relationships with systolic blood pressure in this particular sample.

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
98.5 Interpret the Result
The very strong systolic-diastolic correlation (0.92) makes clinical sense: both numbers describe the same underlying cardiovascular measurement taken at the same moment, so it would be surprising if they didn’t move together closely. The weak BMI-blood pressure and age-blood pressure correlations are worth reporting honestly rather than downplaying — this dataset simply doesn’t show the strong BMI-BP or age-BP relationship sometimes assumed by default, a useful (if less dramatic) finding in its own right, and a genuine contrast against just how strong a correlation can look when two variables are this closely related.

Common Mistakes


Assuming a weak correlation means “no relationship at all.” A correlation of 0.16 is weak, but not necessarily zero — cor() measures linear relationship strength specifically; a strong non-linear relationship could still produce a low cor() value.

Interpreting correlation as causation. A positive BMI-blood pressure correlation doesn’t establish that higher BMI causes higher blood pressure — other explanations (age, genetics, unmeasured factors) could produce the same pattern.

Forgetting cor()’s missing-value argument is use, not na.rm. Unlike mean(), sd(), or quantile(), cor() uses use = "complete.obs" (or other options) rather than na.rm = TRUE — a different argument name for a related idea.

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
98.6 Practice Problem
Compute the correlation between age and bmi. Is it stronger, weaker, or similar in magnitude to the age-blood pressure correlation computed above?

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
98.7 Challenge Problem
Compute cor(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp, use = "complete.obs") separately for smokers and non-smokers (hint: use filter() from Level 5 to split the data first). Does the age-blood pressure relationship look different within each smoking group?

title: 98  Correlation with cor()
text:
98.8 Key Takeaways


cor(x, y, use = "complete.obs") computes a correlation coefficient between -1 and 1, describing the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two numeric variables.
Values near 0 indicate a weak (or no) linear relationship; values near ±1 indicate a strong one, with the sign indicating direction.
Correlation, however strong, never by itself establishes that one variable causes changes in another.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does a correlation of -0.85 indicate?

A strong positive relationship
A strong negative relationship
No relationship at all
An invalid result, since correlations can’t be negative


True or false. A high correlation between two variables proves that one causes the other.
Predict the output. If two variables have absolutely no linear relationship, what value would cor() return, approximately?

Find the error.
cor(participants$bmi, participants$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE)



Show answers


b) A strong negative relationship — the sign indicates direction (negative means as one variable rises, the other tends to fall), and 0.85 in magnitude indicates a strong relationship.

False. Correlation never establishes causation by itself — a strong relationship could arise from a third, unmeasured factor influencing both variables.
Approximately 0.

cor() doesn’t accept na.rm — its missing-value argument is called use. It should be cor(participants$bmi, participants$systolic_bp, use = "complete.obs").

title: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report
text:
99.1 The R Concept
A complete descriptive report typically combines, for each numeric variable: mean(), median(), sd(), range(), and a missing-value count; and for each categorical variable: table() and prop.table(). dplyr’s group_by() + summarise() (Level 5) extends this to grouped numeric summaries, like blood pressure by county.
numeric_summary <- function(x) {
  c(
    n_valid = sum(!is.na(x)),
    n_missing = sum(is.na(x)),
    mean = round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    sd = round(sd(x, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    median = median(x, na.rm = TRUE),
    min = min(x, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = max(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  )
}
crumbs: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report

title: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report
text:
99.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

nrow(participants)

[1] 100

round(mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE), 2)

[1] 47.43

round(sd(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE), 2)

[1] 15.03

round(prop.table(table(participants$sex)) * 100, 1)

Female   Male
  52.0   48.0
crumbs: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report

title: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report
text:
99.3 Explore
Try wrapping the numeric-summary calculations above into a small reusable function (a Level 9 preview), taking a column as input and returning a named vector of statistics — so you don’t have to retype mean(), sd(), median(), min(), and max() separately for every variable in the report.
crumbs: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report

title: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report
text:
99.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Sample size
n_total <- nrow(participants)

# Age: center, spread, missingness
age_mean <- round(mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
age_sd <- round(sd(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
age_median <- median(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
age_missing <- sum(is.na(participants$age))

# BMI: center and spread
bmi_mean <- round(mean(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
bmi_sd <- round(sd(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE), 2)

# Sex breakdown
sex_pct <- round(prop.table(table(participants$sex)) * 100, 1)

# Smoking status
smoker_pct <- round(prop.table(table(participants$smoker)) * 100, 1)

# Blood pressure by county
bp_by_county <- participants |>
  group_by(county) |>
  summarise(n = n(), mean_sbp = round(mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 2)) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_sbp))

n_total

[1] 100

age_mean

[1] 47.43

sex_pct

Female   Male
  52.0   48.0

bp_by_county

# A tibble: 5 x 3
  county          n mean_sbp
  <chr>       <int>    <dbl>
1 Millbrook      21   133.30
2 Springvale     26   131.46
3 Oakford        17   129.71
4 Cedar Falls    20   128.16
5 Riverdale      16   124.36
Every number your professor’s Table 1 paragraph needs is now computed: sample size, age center and spread, sex breakdown, smoking rate, and blood pressure ranked by county.
crumbs: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report

title: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report
text:
99.5 Interpret the Result
Putting it into a written paragraph:
“The study sample included 100 participants, with a mean age of 47.4 years (SD = 15.0, 6 missing) and mean BMI of 27.1 (SD = 4.6). The sample was 52% female and 48% male, and 25% of participants reported current smoking. Mean systolic blood pressure ranged from 124.4 mmHg in Riverdale to 133.3 mmHg in Millbrook across the study’s five counties.”
Every number in that paragraph traces directly back to a specific function call from this level — nothing was estimated or eyeballed. This is exactly the kind of opening paragraph a published health study begins with, built entirely from tools covered across eight pages.

Common Mistakes


Reporting a mean without its missing-value count. “Mean age was 47.4” is incomplete without also noting that 6 of 100 ages were missing — a reader needs to know how much of the sample that mean actually reflects.

Rounding at different points inconsistently. Rounding age_mean to 2 decimals but leaving bp_by_county’s means unrounded (or rounded differently) makes a report look inconsistent — decide on a rounding convention and apply it uniformly.

Writing the narrative paragraph before double-checking every number against its source calculation. It’s easy to transpose two percentages, or misremember which county had the highest blood pressure, when translating numbers into prose — always re-verify each stated number against the code that produced it before finalizing a report.
crumbs: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report

title: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report
text:
99.6 Practice Problem
Extend the report to include the smoking percentage by sex (from Level 7’s cross-tabulation page) as an additional sentence in the paragraph.
crumbs: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report

title: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report
text:
99.7 Challenge Problem
Write a complete “Table 1” for just the subset of participants aged 65 and older (Level 5’s filter()), reporting sample size, mean age, mean BMI, sex breakdown, and smoking rate for this older subgroup specifically. How does this subgroup’s profile compare to the full sample’s?
crumbs: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report

title: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report
text:
99.8 Key Takeaways

A complete descriptive report combines central tendency, spread, and missingness for numeric variables, and counts with percentages for categorical variables.

group_by() + summarise() (Level 5) extends single-variable descriptive statistics into grouped comparisons, like blood pressure by county.
Every number in a written report should be traceable back to a specific, reproducible calculation — never eyeballed or estimated.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Which piece of information should always accompany a reported mean for a variable with missing data?

The variable’s storage type
The count (or percentage) of missing values
The variable’s alphabetical position in the data frame
Nothing extra is needed


True or false. A complete descriptive report only needs numeric summaries; categorical variables don’t need to be described.
Predict the output. Given the reported percentages (52% female, 48% male), would you expect these two numbers to sum to 100?
Short answer. Why is it good practice to re-verify every number in a written report paragraph against its source calculation before finalizing it?


Show answers


b) The count or percentage of missing values, so a reader knows how much of the sample the reported mean actually reflects.

False. Categorical variables (like sex and smoker) need their own counts and percentages — a complete report describes every relevant variable, numeric and categorical alike.
Yes — since every participant has exactly one recorded sex in this dataset, the two percentages should sum to 100.
Numbers are easy to transpose, mis-round, or misremember when translating from code output into prose — a final verification pass catches these errors before they reach a reader who may take the report at face value.
crumbs: 99  Level 7 Capstone: A Descriptive Statistics Report

title: Level 8 — Data Visualization with ggplot2
text:
What you’ll build toward
You’ll return to participants.csv (and revisit the multi-visit data from Level 6) to build every major chart type in ggplot2: scatter plots, trend lines, bar charts, histograms and density curves, boxplots, and line plots for change over time. You’ll then learn to control how a plot looks — titles, labels, themes, colors, legends, and faceting into multiple panels — before saving a finished figure to a file and combining everything into one publication-style capstone figure.

title: Level 8 — Data Visualization with ggplot2
text:
Topics in this level

The grammar of graphics: ggplot() and aes()
geom_point() for scatter plots, and geom_smooth() for trend lines
geom_bar() for categorical counts
geom_histogram() and geom_density() for distributions
geom_boxplot() for comparing groups
geom_line() for change over time
Titles, labels, and themes
Colors, grouping, and legends
Faceting into multi-panel figures
Saving plots with ggsave()

title: Level 8 — Data Visualization with ggplot2
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: show your research team how blood pressure differs by age and smoking status — in one figure clear enough to stand on its own in a report, built entirely from the grammar of graphics this level teaches.

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
100.1 The R Concept
ggplot2 (part of the tidyverse) builds every plot from the same three ingredients, specified in layers:
ggplot(data = df, mapping = aes(x = x_column, y = y_column)) +
  geom_point()
These pieces are combined with +, not |> — ggplot2 predates the tidyverse’s pipe-based style and uses its own layering syntax, adding one visual element at a time.

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
ggplot(data = df) says which data frame to plot.

aes(x = ..., y = ...) (“aesthetic mapping”) says which columns map to which visual properties — here, position on the x- and y-axes.

geom_point() says how to represent each row — as a point, for a scatter plot.

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
100.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp)) +
  geom_point()

This produces a scatter plot: one point per participant, BMI along the x-axis and systolic blood pressure along the y-axis. Consistent with Level 7’s weak correlation (0.16), the points form a loose, scattered cloud without a strong visible upward or downward trend — a picture that confirms, rather than contradicts, what cor() already reported numerically.

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
100.3 Explore
Try swapping which variable goes on which axis: aes(x = systolic_bp, y = bmi). The overall shape of the scattered cloud looks similar either way (correlation doesn’t have a “direction” in that sense) — but by convention, the variable you consider more likely to predict or explain the other (here, BMI) usually goes on the x-axis.

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
100.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_bp_plot <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp)) +
  geom_point()

bmi_bp_plot

Saving the plot to a named object (bmi_bp_plot) — rather than only ever creating and immediately displaying it — lets you keep building on it in later pages (adding a title, a trend line, or color grouping) without retyping the base layer each time.

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
100.5 Interpret the Result
The scatter plot visually confirms Level 7’s numeric finding: BMI and systolic blood pressure show only a weak relationship in this sample — points are spread broadly across the plot rather than clustering along a clear diagonal line. A plot like this is often more convincing to a non-technical audience than a bare correlation coefficient, since the absence of a strong pattern is immediately visible rather than requiring the reader to interpret what “0.16” means.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting library(ggplot2). Like every tidyverse package, ggplot2 must be loaded before use — ggplot() alone won’t be recognized otherwise.

Using %>% or |> between ggplot2 layers instead of +. ggplot2 layers combine with +, not the pipe — a very common point of confusion for anyone who has just learned Level 5’s and 6’s |>-based chaining.

Putting aes() mappings outside aes(). Writing geom_point(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp) (without aes()) tries to treat bmi and systolic_bp as fixed values, not column mappings, and typically produces an error — column mappings always belong inside aes().

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
100.6 Practice Problem
Build a scatter plot of age (x-axis) against systolic_bp (y-axis). Based on Level 7’s correlation (-0.22), would you expect a stronger or weaker visible pattern than the BMI-blood pressure plot above?

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
100.7 Challenge Problem
Build a scatter plot of systolic_bp against diastolic_bp. Given Level 7’s much stronger correlation (0.92) between these two variables, describe in words how you’d expect this plot’s point pattern to look different from the BMI-blood pressure plot above.

title: 100  The Grammar of Graphics
text:
100.8 Key Takeaways

Every ggplot2 plot starts with ggplot(data = df, aes(x = ..., y = ...)), then adds a geom_*() layer describing how to represent the data visually.

aes() maps data columns to visual properties (position, color, size, etc.); ggplot2 layers are combined with +, not the pipe.
A plot can make a numeric relationship (like a correlation coefficient) immediately visible in a way a single number cannot.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp) specify?

That bmi and systolic_bp should be added together
Which columns map to the x- and y-axis positions
The plot’s title
The colors of each point


True or false. ggplot2 layers are combined using the pipe operator (|>).
Predict the output. What geometry (geom) would you add to represent each row as a single point on a scatter plot?

Find the error.
ggplot(data = participants) +
  geom_point(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp)



Show answers


b) Which columns map to the x- and y-axis positions.

False. ggplot2 layers are combined with +, not the pipe.

geom_point().
The column mappings are missing aes() — writing geom_point(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp) doesn’t tell ggplot2 these are column names to map. It should be geom_point(mapping = aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp)), or better, specify aes() once in the initial ggplot() call.

title: 101  Adding Trend Lines with geom_smooth()
text:
101.1 The R Concept
geom_smooth() adds a fitted trend line (with a shaded confidence band by default) on top of existing data, layered with + exactly like any other geom:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = x_col, y = y_col)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
method = "lm" fits a straight line (linear model, previewing Level 11); omitting method lets ggplot2 choose a curved, more flexible trend line instead, which can be useful for spotting non-linear patterns a straight line would miss.

title: 101  Adding Trend Lines with geom_smooth()
text:
101.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

This adds a straight trend line through the scattered points, sloping gently upward — consistent with the weak positive correlation (0.16) from Level 7 — along with a shaded gray band showing the uncertainty around that line. Because the underlying relationship is weak, the line’s upward slope is subtle, and the confidence band is fairly wide relative to the data’s spread.

title: 101  Adding Trend Lines with geom_smooth()
text:
101.3 Explore
Try the same plot with age and systolic_bp instead of bmi. Given Level 7’s correlation there was negative (-0.22, both weak but opposite in sign from the BMI relationship), predict which direction the trend line will slope before running the code, then confirm.

title: 101  Adding Trend Lines with geom_smooth()
text:
101.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

age_bp_trend <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = age, y = systolic_bp)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")

age_bp_trend

The trend line here slopes gently downward as age increases — matching the negative correlation (-0.22) found in Level 7. As with the BMI plot, the relationship is weak enough that the line’s slope is subtle and the surrounding confidence band is fairly wide, visually reinforcing that this isn’t a strong, dependable pattern.

title: 101  Adding Trend Lines with geom_smooth()
text:
101.5 Interpret the Result
Both trend lines make Level 7’s correlation coefficients visually concrete: a gentle upward line for the weak positive BMI-BP relationship, and a gentle downward line for the weak negative age-BP relationship. In both cases, the direction of the trend line matches the sign of the correlation exactly, while its subtlety (a nearly flat slope, a wide confidence band) reflects just how weak both relationships are — exactly consistent with correlation magnitudes both under 0.25 in absolute value.

Common Mistakes


Interpreting a fitted trend line as proof of a strong relationship. A geom_smooth() line can be drawn through any scatter of points, even ones with essentially no real relationship — always look at how tightly the points hug the line, and how wide the confidence band is, not just whether a line was drawn at all.

Forgetting method = "lm" fits a straight line specifically. Without it, geom_smooth() defaults to a flexible curved fit (loess), which can look more dramatic than a straight-line relationship actually warrants — be explicit about which you intend.

Adding geom_smooth() without geom_point() first and expecting to see the raw data. geom_smooth() alone only shows the fitted line and confidence band — without geom_point(), the underlying scatter of individual observations disappears entirely.

title: 101  Adding Trend Lines with geom_smooth()
text:
101.6 Practice Problem
Build a scatter plot with a geom_smooth(method = "lm") trend line for systolic_bp against diastolic_bp. Given Level 7’s much stronger correlation (0.92) here, describe how you’d expect the points to hug the trend line more tightly than in either plot above.

title: 101  Adding Trend Lines with geom_smooth()
text:
101.7 Challenge Problem
Build the same BMI-blood pressure plot twice: once with geom_smooth(method = "lm") and once with geom_smooth() (no method specified, defaulting to loess). Compare the two trend lines — do they tell a meaningfully different story for this particular relationship, or do they look similar given how weak the underlying pattern is?

title: 101  Adding Trend Lines with geom_smooth()
text:
101.8 Key Takeaways


geom_smooth() adds a fitted trend line (with a confidence band) on top of a scatter plot, layered with +.

method = "lm" fits a straight line; omitting method fits a more flexible curve, useful for spotting non-linear patterns.
A trend line’s slope direction should match the sign of the corresponding cor() value — always check the two against each other.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does method = "lm" specify inside geom_smooth()?

A flexible curved fit
A straight-line (linear) fit
The plot’s color scheme
The confidence level


True or false. A geom_smooth() trend line can be drawn even when the underlying relationship between two variables is very weak.
Predict the output. If two variables have a correlation of -0.22, which direction would you expect their geom_smooth(method = "lm") trend line to slope?

Find the error.
ggplot(data = participants, aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
# intended to show both the raw data points and the trend line



Show answers


b) A straight-line (linear) fit.

True. A trend line can be fit through any scatter of points, regardless of how weak the actual relationship is — the confidence band’s width and how loosely points scatter around the line are what reveal that weakness.
Downward (negative slope), since a negative correlation means the variables tend to move in opposite directions.

geom_point() is missing — this code only shows the trend line and confidence band, not the individual data points. It should be geom_point() + geom_smooth(method = "lm").

title: 102  Bar Charts with geom_bar()
text:
102.1 The R Concept
geom_bar() counts the number of rows for each category automatically — you only map the categorical variable to x; ggplot2 handles the counting internally, similar to how table() did in Level 4:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = category_column)) +
  geom_bar()
This is different from geom_point() and geom_smooth(), which plot values that already exist in your data — geom_bar() computes a new value (the count) as part of drawing the plot.

title: 102  Bar Charts with geom_bar()
text:
102.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = county)) +
  geom_bar()

This produces five bars, one per county, with height equal to that county’s participant count — Springvale’s bar is the tallest (26 participants), and Riverdale’s is the shortest (16 participants), matching Level 5’s count(participants, county) exactly.

title: 102  Bar Charts with geom_bar()
text:
102.3 Explore
Try adding + coord_flip() to the plot above. This rotates the chart so county names run along the y-axis and bars extend horizontally — often more readable when category names (like “Cedar Falls”) are long enough to overlap when printed underneath vertical bars.

title: 102  Bar Charts with geom_bar()
text:
102.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

county_bar_chart <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = county)) +
  geom_bar()

county_bar_chart

Five bars in the order R’s default alphabetical sorting places county names: Cedar Falls (20), Millbrook (21), Oakford (17), Riverdale (16), Springvale (26) — Springvale’s bar visibly the tallest, Riverdale’s the shortest, exactly matching the raw counts from Level 4’s table().

title: 102  Bar Charts with geom_bar()
text:
102.5 Interpret the Result
The bar chart makes the county size ranking immediately visible without requiring a reader to compare five separate numbers by eye — Springvale’s larger contribution to the study (26 of 100 participants) and Riverdale’s smaller one (16 of 100) are now a visual fact rather than a row in a table. This is exactly the kind of quick, scannable summary a report’s opening figure often needs.

Common Mistakes


Mapping y to a count that doesn’t exist yet. geom_bar() computes counts internally — writing aes(x = county, y = count) looks for a column literally named count in your data frame, which doesn’t exist, and produces an error. Just map x; let geom_bar() handle the rest.

Confusing geom_bar() with geom_col(). geom_bar() counts rows automatically; geom_col() expects you to have already computed the heights yourself (for example, with Level 5’s summarise()) and maps a column directly to bar height instead of counting.

Leaving bars in default alphabetical order when a ranked order would communicate more clearly. Sorting bars by height (largest to smallest) is often more readable than alphabetical order — a refinement covered later in this level alongside labels and themes.

title: 102  Bar Charts with geom_bar()
text:
102.6 Practice Problem
Build a bar chart of smoker status. With only two categories (“No” and “Yes”), confirm the relative bar heights visually match the 75/25 split found in Level 7.

title: 102  Bar Charts with geom_bar()
text:
102.7 Challenge Problem
Level 5’s count(participants, county, sort = TRUE) already sorts counties by size. Research (or predict from what you already know about ggplot2’s “factor” handling) how you might reorder geom_bar()’s x-axis categories by count rather than alphabetically, without changing the underlying data.

title: 102  Bar Charts with geom_bar()
text:
102.8 Key Takeaways


geom_bar() automatically counts rows per category — map only x to the categorical column, and let ggplot2 compute the bar heights.

geom_bar() (auto-counting) differs from geom_col() (plotting pre-computed values) — choosing the right one depends on whether your data already has the heights you want to plot.
A bar chart turns a table()-style count comparison into an instantly scannable visual ranking.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does geom_bar() do when you map only x to a categorical column?

It errors, requiring a y mapping too
It automatically counts rows per category and uses that as bar height
It plots every row as a separate point
It computes the mean of x per category


True or false. geom_bar() and geom_col() work identically in every situation.
Predict the output. Given county counts of 20, 21, 17, 16, and 26, which county’s bar would be tallest?

Find the error.
ggplot(data = participants, aes(x = county, y = count)) +
  geom_bar()



Show answers


b) It automatically counts rows per category and uses that count as the bar height.

False. geom_bar() counts rows automatically; geom_col() requires the bar heights to already exist as a column in the data — they serve different purposes.
Springvale, with 26 participants — the largest count among the five counties.
There is no column literally named count in participants — geom_bar() computes counts internally and doesn’t need (or expect) a y mapping at all. It should simply be aes(x = county).

title: 103  Histograms and Density Plots
text:
103.1 The R Concept
geom_histogram() divides a numeric variable into bins and counts how many observations fall into each — visually, like geom_bar() but for continuous rather than categorical data:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = numeric_column)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10)
binwidth controls how wide each bin is — too wide, and you lose detail; too narrow, and the shape becomes noisy and hard to read.
geom_density() shows the same kind of information as a smoothed curve instead of discrete bars — useful for seeing the overall shape without committing to a specific bin width:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = numeric_column)) +
  geom_density()

title: 103  Histograms and Density Plots
text:
103.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10)

With 10-year bins, this produces bars roughly following this pattern: a small bar for ages 10-19 (5 participants), rising through 20-29 (8), 30-39 (16), peaking around 40-49 (20) and 50-59 (24), then declining through 60-69 (16), 70-79 (3), and 80-89 (2) — a distribution that rises to a peak in the 40s-50s and tapers off on both sides, roughly consistent with the mean (47.43) and median (49) sitting near that peak.

title: 103  Histograms and Density Plots
text:
103.3 Explore
Try the same plot with binwidth = 5 instead of 10. Finer bins reveal more detail (and more visual noise) — compare how much the overall “rises then falls, peaking in the 40s-50s” shape still holds versus how much finer fluctuation appears.

title: 103  Histograms and Density Plots
text:
103.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)
