participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

age_histogram <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10)

age_histogram


age_density <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = age)) +
  geom_density()

age_density

The histogram shows discrete 10-year bars rising to a peak around 40-59 and tapering at both ends; the density plot shows the same overall shape as a single smooth curve, without the bin-width choice affecting how it looks.

title: 103  Histograms and Density Plots
text:
103.5 Interpret the Result
Age in this sample isn’t uniformly spread across 18-81 — it’s concentrated in the 40s and 50s, with progressively fewer participants at the youngest and oldest ends. This is exactly the kind of shape information Level 7’s five-number summary alone couldn’t show: two datasets could share an identical mean, median, and range while having completely different distribution shapes (one peaked in the middle, one flat, one with two separate peaks) — only a histogram or density plot reveals which one you actually have.

Common Mistakes


Choosing a binwidth without trying a few options first. Too wide a bin can hide real structure (like a second peak); too narrow can make the plot look noisy and uninterpretable — always try 2-3 different bin widths before settling on one for a final report.

Forgetting geom_histogram() needs only x, not y. Like geom_bar(), geom_histogram() computes bin counts (the y-axis) automatically — mapping a y variable yourself isn’t necessary and usually isn’t what you want.

Treating a density plot’s y-axis as a literal count. A density curve’s y-axis is scaled so the total area under the curve equals 1 — it should be read as showing relative likelihood across values, not as a raw count the way a histogram’s y-axis is.

title: 103  Histograms and Density Plots
text:
103.6 Practice Problem
Build a histogram of bmi with binwidth = 5. Based on Level 7’s finding that BMI’s mean (27.12) and median (27.70) sit close together, would you expect a roughly symmetric shape, or a heavily skewed one?

title: 103  Histograms and Density Plots
text:
103.7 Challenge Problem
Build a histogram of systolic_bp with three different bin widths (5, 10, and 20). Describe in words how the visual impression of the distribution’s shape changes as bin width increases — at what point does the histogram start losing meaningful detail?

title: 103  Histograms and Density Plots
text:
103.8 Key Takeaways


geom_histogram(binwidth = ...) groups a numeric variable into bins and shows counts per bin, revealing distribution shape that a five-number summary alone cannot.

geom_density() shows the same kind of shape information as a smooth curve, without needing to choose a bin width.
Two variables can share identical means, medians, and ranges while having completely different distribution shapes — always check the shape directly rather than assuming it from summary statistics alone.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does binwidth control in geom_histogram()?

The color of the bars
How wide each bin (group of values) is
The number of rows in the data
The plot’s title


True or false. A density plot’s y-axis represents a raw count, exactly like a histogram’s.
Predict the output. If age is concentrated in the 40s and 50s with fewer participants at the extremes, what general shape would you expect the histogram to show?

Find the error.
ggplot(data = participants, aes(x = age, y = count)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10)



Show answers


b) How wide each bin (group of values) is.

False. A density plot’s y-axis is scaled so the total area under the curve equals 1, representing relative likelihood rather than a raw count.
A shape that rises toward a peak in the 40s-50s and tapers off toward both younger and older ages.

geom_histogram() computes bin counts automatically — there is no count column to map, and specifying y isn’t necessary. It should simply be aes(x = age).

title: 104  Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
text:
104.1 The R Concept
geom_boxplot() draws a box spanning the 1st to 3rd quartile (the IQR, Level 7), a line at the median, and “whiskers” extending to the most extreme non-outlier values — with individual points beyond the whiskers flagged as potential outliers, echoing Level 7’s 1.5×IQR rule:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = group_column, y = numeric_column)) +
  geom_boxplot()
Mapping a categorical variable to x and a numeric variable to y produces one boxplot per category, side by side for direct comparison.

title: 104  Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
text:
104.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = smoker, y = bmi)) +
  geom_boxplot()

This produces two boxplots side by side: non-smokers’ box spans roughly 23.3 to 29.65 (median 27.8), while smokers’ box spans roughly 24.12 to 30.32 (median 26.25) — visibly similar in spread, with the non-smoker group’s median sitting slightly higher than the smoker group’s.

title: 104  Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
text:
104.3 Explore
Try ggplot(data = participants, aes(x = county, y = systolic_bp)) + geom_boxplot() — five boxplots side by side instead of two. This extends the same idea from Level 5’s group_by(county) + summarise() grouped means, but shows each county’s full blood pressure distribution rather than collapsing it to a single number.

title: 104  Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
text:
104.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_smoker_boxplot <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = smoker, y = bmi)) +
  geom_boxplot()

bmi_smoker_boxplot

Non-smokers (n=71): median BMI 27.8, middle 50% spanning roughly 23.3-29.65, full range 15.6-38.6. Smokers (n=24): median BMI 26.25, middle 50% spanning roughly 24.12-30.32, full range 18.1-35.8. The two boxes overlap substantially, visually confirming that BMI doesn’t differ dramatically by smoking status in this sample.

title: 104  Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
text:
104.5 Interpret the Result
The boxplot shows the two groups’ BMI distributions are broadly similar — overlapping boxes and comparable medians — rather than one group being dramatically higher or lower than the other. This is a more complete picture than comparing two means alone: it also shows that non-smokers have a slightly wider full range (down to 15.6) than smokers (down to 18.1), a detail a bare mean-comparison would miss entirely.

Common Mistakes


Reading the line inside the box as the mean. The line inside a geom_boxplot() box is the median, not the mean — the two can differ, especially for skewed data, and conflating them leads to a misreading of the plot.

Assuming points beyond the whiskers are always errors. Points plotted individually beyond a boxplot’s whiskers are flagged as potential outliers by the 1.5×IQR rule (Level 7) — worth investigating, not automatically wrong or removable.

Comparing boxplots without checking group sizes. A boxplot for a group of 5 participants and one for a group of 95 look visually similar in a plot, even though the smaller group’s summary is far less stable — always report each group’s n alongside a boxplot comparison.

title: 104  Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
text:
104.6 Practice Problem
Build a boxplot comparing systolic_bp between smokers and non-smokers. Does blood pressure appear to differ more between smoking groups than BMI did?

title: 104  Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
text:
104.7 Challenge Problem
Build a boxplot of bmi across all five counties (aes(x = county, y = bmi)). Combine this with Level 5’s group_by(county) + summarise() output for mean BMI per county, and check whether the boxplot’s visual median ranking matches the numeric mean ranking — they don’t always agree, especially for skewed distributions.

title: 104  Boxplots with geom_boxplot()
text:
104.8 Key Takeaways


geom_boxplot() shows the median, interquartile range, and potential outliers for a numeric variable, one box per category when a categorical variable is mapped to x.
The line inside the box is the median, not the mean — don’t conflate the two when reading a boxplot.
Comparing full distributions (via boxplots) reveals more than comparing single summary statistics like means alone.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does the line inside a geom_boxplot() box represent?

The mean
The median
The mode
The maximum value


True or false. Points plotted beyond a boxplot’s whiskers are always data entry errors.
Predict the output. If you map a categorical variable with 5 categories to x and a numeric variable to y, how many boxes would geom_boxplot() draw?

Find the error.
ggplot(data = participants, aes(x = bmi, y = smoker)) +
  geom_boxplot()
# intended: one BMI boxplot per smoking status, side by side



Show answers


b) The median.

False. Points beyond the whiskers are potential outliers worth investigating, not automatically confirmed errors.
5 boxes, one per category.
The x and y mappings are reversed — smoker (the categorical grouping variable) should be on x, and bmi (the numeric variable being compared) should be on y. It should be aes(x = smoker, y = bmi).

title: 105  Line Plots with geom_line()
text:
105.1 The R Concept
geom_line() connects data points in order along the x-axis — appropriate when there’s a natural sequence (like time or visit number) connecting the values:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = ordered_variable, y = numeric_column)) +
  geom_line()
Unlike geom_point(), geom_line() assumes consecutive x-axis values belong to the same continuous trend — so it’s the right choice for a variable like “visit number,” but not for an unordered category like county.

title: 105  Line Plots with geom_line()
text:
105.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_long <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_prefix = "sbp_",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

visit_means <- bp_long |>
  group_by(visit) |>
  summarise(mean_sbp = mean(sbp, na.rm = TRUE))

ggplot(data = visit_means, mapping = aes(x = visit, y = mean_sbp, group = 1)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point()

This draws a line connecting three points — one per visit — dropping from about 124.96 at visit 1 to 120.96 at visit 2, then leveling off only slightly further to 120.69 at visit 3: a clear visual of the pattern Level 6 first found in table form.

title: 105  Line Plots with geom_line()
text:
105.3 Explore
Try removing group = 1 from the aes() mapping and re-running the plot. Since visit is a character column (not a single continuous numeric sequence), ggplot2 doesn’t automatically know all three points belong to one connected line without group = 1 telling it so explicitly.

title: 105  Line Plots with geom_line()
text:
105.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_long <- pivot_longer(bp_wide,
  cols = starts_with("sbp_visit"),
  names_to = "visit",
  names_prefix = "sbp_",
  values_to = "sbp"
)

visit_means <- bp_long |>
  group_by(visit) |>
  summarise(mean_sbp = round(mean(sbp, na.rm = TRUE), 2))

visit_means

# A tibble: 3 x 2
  visit  mean_sbp
  <chr>     <dbl>
1 visit1   124.96
2 visit2   120.96
3 visit3   120.69

visit_trend_plot <- ggplot(data = visit_means, mapping = aes(x = visit, y = mean_sbp, group = 1)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point()

visit_trend_plot

A clearly declining line from visit 1 to visit 2, followed by a much flatter segment from visit 2 to visit 3 — the shape of the improvement isn’t linear across all three visits; most of the drop happens early, then levels off.

title: 105  Line Plots with geom_line()
text:
105.5 Interpret the Result
The line plot reveals something the three raw numbers alone made you compute mentally: blood pressure didn’t decline at a steady rate across visits — most of the improvement happened between visit 1 and visit 2, with much less additional change by visit 3. This “front-loaded improvement, then plateau” shape is a genuinely useful clinical observation that’s far easier to see in a line plot than in a table of three numbers.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting group = 1 when the x-axis variable is categorical (like “visit1”, “visit2”, “visit3”). Without it, ggplot2 may not connect the points into a single line, since it doesn’t automatically assume unrelated categorical labels form one continuous sequence.

Using geom_line() for genuinely unordered categories. A line plot implies a meaningful order and connection between consecutive x-axis values — using it for something like county (which has no natural order) would visually imply a trend that doesn’t exist.

Plotting raw individual-participant data with geom_line() instead of the summarised means. Connecting each of the 28 individual participants’ three visits with geom_line() (rather than the grouped mean) would produce 28 overlapping lines — useful for some purposes, but a very different (and much busier) plot than the clean mean trend shown here.

title: 105  Line Plots with geom_line()
text:
105.6 Practice Problem
Reshape and plot the mean weight (instead of sbp) across the three visits using geom_line(). Does weight show a similar “front-loaded change, then plateau” pattern?

title: 105  Line Plots with geom_line()
text:
105.7 Challenge Problem
Build a line plot showing each of the 28 individual participants’ blood pressure across the three visits (map id to group, and consider adding alpha = 0.3 to geom_line() to make the overlapping lines more readable). How does this “spaghetti plot” of individual trajectories compare to the single clean mean-trend line above?

title: 105  Line Plots with geom_line()
text:
105.8 Key Takeaways


geom_line() connects points in x-axis order, appropriate for a naturally sequential variable like visit number or time.

group = 1 (or another grouping variable) tells ggplot2 which points belong to the same connected line, especially important when the x-axis is categorical.
A line plot can reveal a trend’s shape (front-loaded change, a plateau, a steady decline) that a table of the same numbers requires more effort to notice.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. When is geom_line() an appropriate choice?

For any categorical variable, regardless of order
When there’s a meaningful sequence connecting the x-axis values, like time or visit number
Only for scatter plots
Only when there are more than 100 data points


True or false. geom_line() automatically knows to connect all points into a single line, even when the x-axis is a character column, without any extra arguments.
Predict the output. Given mean blood pressure of 124.96, 120.96, and 120.69 across three visits, does the largest single-visit drop happen between visit 1-2, or visit 2-3?
Short answer. Why would geom_line() be a poor choice for plotting mean BMI across the five counties?


Show answers


b) When there’s a meaningful sequence connecting the x-axis values, like time or visit number.

False. For a categorical (character) x-axis, ggplot2 typically needs an explicit group mapping (like group = 1) to know all the points belong to one connected line.
Visit 1 to visit 2 (124.96 → 120.96, a drop of about 4.0), compared to visit 2 to visit 3 (120.96 → 120.69, a drop of only about 0.27).
Counties have no natural order or sequential relationship to each other — connecting them with a line would visually imply a trend or progression from one county to the next that doesn’t actually exist; a bar chart is the more honest choice for unordered categories.

title: 106  Titles, Labels, and Themes
text:
106.1 The R Concept
labs() sets a plot’s title, subtitle, axis labels, and caption, all in one layer:
+ labs(
    title = "Plot Title",
    subtitle = "Optional subtitle",
    x = "Readable X-Axis Label",
    y = "Readable Y-Axis Label",
    caption = "Optional footnote"
  )
theme_*() functions replace ggplot2’s default gray-background theme with alternatives — theme_minimal(), theme_bw(), and theme_classic() are common, cleaner choices for reports:
+ theme_minimal()

title: 106  Titles, Labels, and Themes
text:
106.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "BMI and Systolic Blood Pressure",
    subtitle = "Weak positive relationship (r = 0.16)",
    x = "Body Mass Index (BMI)",
    y = "Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The same scatter plot from Page 1, now with a clear title stating what’s being shown, a subtitle previewing the finding, readable axis labels with units, and a cleaner white background in place of the default gray.

title: 106  Titles, Labels, and Themes
text:
106.3 Explore
Try swapping theme_minimal() for theme_bw() or theme_classic(). All three remove the default gray background, but with subtly different gridline and border styles — try each and decide which reads most clearly for your own report.

title: 106  Titles, Labels, and Themes
text:
106.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

county_bar_chart <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = county)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Participants by County",
    subtitle = "n = 100 total participants across 5 counties",
    x = "County",
    y = "Number of Participants"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

county_bar_chart

The county bar chart from Page 3, now clearly labeled: a reader immediately knows this shows participant counts by county, out of 100 total, without needing to guess what “county” and the unlabeled y-axis numbers meant.

title: 106  Titles, Labels, and Themes
text:
106.5 Interpret the Result
A plot’s raw content doesn’t change when you add labs() and a theme — the same five bars, the same heights — but its readability to someone who isn’t you changes enormously. A title and readable axis labels turn a plot that requires you to explain it out loud into one that explains itself.

Common Mistakes


Leaving default column names as axis labels in a final report figure. bmi and systolic_bp mean nothing to a general reader — always replace them with readable labels and units via labs(x = ..., y = ...) before sharing a plot outside your own analysis.

Adding a title that describes the chart type instead of the finding. “Scatter Plot of BMI vs Blood Pressure” restates what’s visually obvious; “Weak Positive Relationship Between BMI and Blood Pressure” tells the reader what to notice — titles are more useful when they communicate the finding, not just the chart type.

Forgetting theme_*() replaces the entire theme, not just one element. If you want to change just the background but keep other default styling, theme_minimal() isn’t a targeted tweak — it’s a wholesale theme swap; individual style tweaks use theme(...) (lowercase, no suffix) instead.

title: 106  Titles, Labels, and Themes
text:
106.6 Practice Problem
Add a title, readable axis labels, and theme_minimal() to the age histogram from Page 4. Include a subtitle noting the mean and median age found in Level 7.

title: 106  Titles, Labels, and Themes
text:
106.7 Challenge Problem
Take the boxplot comparing BMI by smoking status (Page 5) and add a title, axis labels, and a caption noting the sample sizes in each group (n=71 non-smokers, n=24 smokers). Consider what additional context a reader would need to correctly interpret the plot without seeing your code.

title: 106  Titles, Labels, and Themes
text:
106.8 Key Takeaways


labs(title = ..., x = ..., y = ...) replaces raw column names and adds context, making a plot interpretable without needing to see the underlying code.

theme_minimal(), theme_bw(), and theme_classic() replace ggplot2’s default gray background with cleaner alternatives suited for reports.
A good title communicates the finding, not just the chart type — “what should the reader notice,” not merely “what kind of chart this is.”


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does labs(x = "Body Mass Index (BMI)") do?

Filters the data to only BMI values
Replaces the x-axis label with readable text
Changes the color of the x-axis
Removes the x-axis entirely


True or false. theme_minimal() only changes the plot’s background color and leaves everything else untouched.
Predict the output. Would labs(title = "My Plot") + labs(subtitle = "A subtitle") (two separate labs() calls) work, or does labs() need to be called only once?
Short answer. Why is “Weak Positive Relationship Between BMI and Blood Pressure” a more useful title than “Scatter Plot of BMI and Blood Pressure”?


Show answers


b) Replaces the x-axis label with readable text.

False. theme_*() functions replace the entire visual theme (gridlines, borders, fonts, background), not just one specific element.
Both work — multiple labs() calls combine their arguments, though it’s more common (and clearer) to set all label arguments in a single labs() call.
The first title tells the reader what pattern to notice in the data; the second only restates the chart type, which is already visually obvious from looking at the plot itself.

title: 107  Colors, Grouping, and Legends
text:
107.1 The R Concept
Mapping a categorical variable to color (for points and lines) or fill (for bars and boxes) inside aes() adds a grouping dimension to a plot, with a legend generated automatically:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = x_col, y = y_col, color = grouping_column)) +
  geom_point()
No extra step is needed for the legend — ggplot2 builds it automatically from the color mapping, labeled with the grouping column’s actual category values.

title: 107  Colors, Grouping, and Legends
text:
107.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp, color = smoker)) +
  geom_point()

This produces the same scatter plot as Page 1, but now every point is colored by smoking status, with a legend on the side labeled “smoker” showing which color corresponds to “Yes” and which to “No.” Visually, the two colors appear thoroughly intermixed across the plot rather than occupying separate regions — consistent with smokers’ and non-smokers’ mean blood pressure (130.54 vs. 129.61) being nearly identical.

title: 107  Colors, Grouping, and Legends
text:
107.3 Explore
Try mapping color = county instead of color = smoker on the same plot. With 5 counties instead of 2 smoking categories, ggplot2 automatically assigns 5 distinct colors and builds a legend with 5 entries — no additional code needed beyond changing which column is mapped.

title: 107  Colors, Grouping, and Legends
text:
107.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_bp_by_smoker <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp, color = smoker)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "BMI and Blood Pressure by Smoking Status",
    x = "Body Mass Index (BMI)",
    y = "Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)",
    color = "Current Smoker"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

bmi_bp_by_smoker

Note labs(color = "Current Smoker") renames the legend title itself (from the default “smoker” to a more readable “Current Smoker”) — labs() can relabel any aesthetic, not just axes.

title: 107  Colors, Grouping, and Legends
text:
107.5 Interpret the Result
Coloring by smoking status doesn’t reveal any visually obvious separation between the two groups on this plot — smokers and non-smokers scatter across the same overall region of BMI and blood pressure values. This visual finding is consistent with Level 7’s numeric result: smoking status and blood pressure showed little relationship in this sample. A color mapping doesn’t manufacture a pattern that isn’t there — it simply makes an existing (or absent) pattern easier to check by eye.

Common Mistakes


Using color for a continuous numeric variable when a distinct palette is expected. Mapping color to a categorical variable (like smoker) gives discrete colors with a labeled legend; mapping it to a continuous variable (like age) instead produces a color gradient — a genuinely different kind of legend and visual encoding.

Confusing color and fill. color controls outlines and point colors (used for geom_point(), geom_line()); fill controls the interior color of shapes with area (used for geom_bar(), geom_boxplot()) — using the wrong one for a given geom often produces no visible change at all.

Adding a color mapping and assuming it reveals a meaningful group difference. Sometimes, as here, coloring by a group reveals no visible separation — that’s a legitimate and useful finding (echoing Level 7’s weak smoking-blood pressure relationship), not a failed plot.

title: 107  Colors, Grouping, and Legends
text:
107.6 Practice Problem
Recreate the BMI-blood pressure scatter plot with color = sex instead of color = smoker. Does mapping sex reveal any more visible separation than smoking status did?

title: 107  Colors, Grouping, and Legends
text:
107.7 Challenge Problem
Build a boxplot of bmi by county (Page 5’s technique), adding fill = county to color each box differently. Since county is already mapped to x, is a fill legend actually necessary here, or would it be redundant with the x-axis labels? Explain your reasoning.

title: 107  Colors, Grouping, and Legends
text:
107.8 Key Takeaways

Mapping a variable to color (or fill) inside aes() adds a grouping dimension to any plot, with a legend generated automatically.

color applies to outlines and points; fill applies to the interior of shapes with area, like bars and boxes.
A color mapping that reveals no visible group separation is still a legitimate and informative result — it doesn’t mean the plot failed.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does mapping color = smoker inside aes() do?

Changes the plot’s background color
Colors points differently based on smoking status, with an automatic legend
Removes non-smokers from the plot
Requires a separate legend() function call


True or false. color and fill always produce the same visual effect, regardless of which geom is used.
Predict the output. If you map color = county (5 categories) instead of color = smoker (2 categories), how many legend entries would you expect?

Find the error.
ggplot(data = participants, aes(x = smoker, y = bmi)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = county)



Show answers


b) Colors points differently based on smoking status, with an automatic legend.

False. color affects outlines/points, while fill affects the interior of shapes with area — for a geom_boxplot(), fill typically produces a more visible effect than color.
5 legend entries, one per county.

color = county is placed outside aes(), so ggplot2 tries to interpret county as a literal fixed value rather than a column mapping, which errors. It should be inside aes(): geom_boxplot(aes(fill = county)) (using fill, since boxplots have an interior area).

title: 108  Faceting into Multiple Panels
text:
108.1 The R Concept
facet_wrap() splits a plot into multiple small panels, one per category of a specified variable — each panel shares the same x- and y-axis scale by default, making the panels directly comparable to each other:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = numeric_column)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = ...) +
  facet_wrap(~ grouping_column)
The ~ (tilde) before the grouping column is required syntax, borrowed from R’s formula notation (also used in modeling, previewed in Level 11).

title: 108  Faceting into Multiple Panels
text:
108.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5) +
  facet_wrap(~ county)

This produces 5 small histogram panels, one per county, arranged in a grid — each panel showing that county’s BMI distribution specifically, all on the same shared x-axis scale so panel-to-panel comparisons remain fair.

title: 108  Faceting into Multiple Panels
text:
108.3 Explore
Try adding nrow = 1 inside facet_wrap(~ county, nrow = 1), forcing all 5 panels into a single horizontal row instead of ggplot2’s default grid arrangement. Compare readability between the default layout and the single-row version.

title: 108  Faceting into Multiple Panels
text:
108.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_by_county_facets <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5) +
  facet_wrap(~ county) +
  labs(
    title = "BMI Distribution by County",
    x = "Body Mass Index (BMI)",
    y = "Number of Participants"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

bmi_by_county_facets

Five panels, each shaped roughly like the overall BMI histogram from Page 4 — but Springvale’s panel visibly shifts further left (lower BMI values) than the other four counties, consistent with Springvale’s mean BMI (25.24) sitting noticeably below Cedar Falls (28.42), Oakford (28.02), Millbrook (27.37), and Riverdale (27.38).

title: 108  Faceting into Multiple Panels
text:
108.5 Interpret the Result
Faceting reveals a pattern the single combined histogram (Page 4) couldn’t show: Springvale’s BMI distribution is shifted toward lower values relative to the other four counties, which look broadly similar to each other. This is exactly the kind of subgroup pattern that a single aggregated plot averages away — faceting trades one plot for several smaller, directly comparable ones, at the cost of each individual panel having less data (and therefore looking noisier) than the combined version would.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting the ~ before the grouping variable. facet_wrap(county) (without the tilde) produces an error — facet_wrap() requires formula syntax, facet_wrap(~ county).

Faceting by a variable with too many categories. Faceting by id (100 distinct participants) would produce 100 tiny, nearly unreadable panels — faceting works best with a handful of meaningful categories, not a variable that’s nearly unique per row.

Forgetting that facets share the same axis scale by default, which can hide within-panel detail for smaller groups. A county with only 16 participants (Riverdale) gets the same y-axis range as one with 26 (Springvale) by default — scales = "free" inside facet_wrap() allows each panel its own scale, at the cost of making direct height comparisons across panels less straightforward.

title: 108  Faceting into Multiple Panels
text:
108.6 Practice Problem
Facet the systolic_bp histogram (instead of bmi) by smoker (2 panels instead of 5). Does blood pressure’s shape look meaningfully different between the two panels?

title: 108  Faceting into Multiple Panels
text:
108.7 Challenge Problem
Facet a scatter plot of bmi against systolic_bp by county (facet_wrap(~ county) applied to Page 1’s plot). With Level 7’s weak overall BMI-blood pressure correlation (0.16) in mind, would you expect any single county’s facet to show a visibly stronger or weaker pattern than the others, given how few points each individual county panel now contains?

title: 108  Faceting into Multiple Panels
text:
108.8 Key Takeaways


facet_wrap(~ grouping_column) splits one plot into multiple small panels, one per category, sharing the same axis scale by default for fair comparison.
Faceting can reveal subgroup patterns (like Springvale’s lower BMI) that a single combined plot averages away.
Faceting works best for a handful of meaningful categories — too many categories produce panels too small and numerous to read.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What is required syntax when specifying the faceting variable?

A pipe (|>) before the variable name
A tilde (~) before the variable name
Quotation marks around the variable name
A group = argument


True or false. Facets always use independent, separately scaled axes for each panel by default.
Predict the output. If county has 5 distinct values, how many panels would facet_wrap(~ county) produce?
Short answer. Why might faceting by id (100 distinct participants) be a poor choice, even though it’s technically valid code?


Show answers


b) A tilde (~) before the variable name — required formula syntax for facet_wrap().

False. By default, facets share the same axis scale across all panels; scales = "free" must be specified to give each panel its own independent scale.
5 panels, one per county.
With 100 distinct ids, faceting would produce 100 tiny panels, each containing just a single data point — far too many small panels to read meaningfully, and no more informative than looking at the raw data table itself.

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
109.1 The R Concept
ggsave() writes the most recently displayed (or a specifically named) plot to an image file, with the format determined automatically by the file extension:
ggsave("filename.png", plot = my_plot, width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
"filename.png" — the file to create; .png, .pdf, .jpg, and .svg are all supported, chosen by the extension.

plot = my_plot — which saved plot object to write; if omitted, ggsave() saves whatever plot was most recently displayed.

width, height — the image’s dimensions, typically in inches.

dpi — resolution (“dots per inch”); 300 is a common standard for print-quality output.

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
109.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_bp_plot <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = bmi, y = systolic_bp)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "BMI and Systolic Blood Pressure",
    x = "Body Mass Index (BMI)",
    y = "Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave("bmi_bp_scatter.png", plot = bmi_bp_plot, width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)

This creates a file called bmi_bp_scatter.png in your working directory (Level 1) — a standalone image file, ready to attach to an email, insert into a Word document, or embed in a slide deck.

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
109.3 Explore
Try saving the same plot twice: once as "bmi_bp_scatter.png" and once as "bmi_bp_scatter.pdf". Both calls use the exact same ggsave() syntax — only the file extension changes, and ggsave() picks the appropriate image format based on it.

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
109.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

county_bar_chart <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = county)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Participants by County",
    x = "County",
    y = "Number of Participants"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave("county_bar_chart.png", plot = county_bar_chart, width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)

file.exists("county_bar_chart.png")

[1] TRUE
file.exists() (Level 3) confirms the image file was actually written to disk — ready to attach to your professor’s report exactly as requested.

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
109.5 Interpret the Result
county_bar_chart.png now exists as a genuine file on disk, independent of your R session — closing RStudio, restarting your computer, or emailing the file to someone without R installed at all doesn’t affect it, unlike a plot that only exists as an object in your current session’s memory.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting to specify plot = ... when the intended plot isn’t the most recently displayed one. ggsave() defaults to saving whatever was last shown on screen — if you’ve displayed a different plot since building the one you actually want saved, omitting plot = ... silently saves the wrong image.

Choosing a dpi too low for print use. The default dpi = 300 is standard for print-quality output; much lower values (like 72, common for web images) can look blurry or pixelated when printed or zoomed into.

Not checking the file actually saved where expected. Like Level 3’s write_csv(), ggsave() writes relative to your current working directory (Level 1) unless given a full file path — always confirm with file.exists() or by checking your file browser.

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
109.6 Practice Problem
Save the BMI-by-smoking-status boxplot (Page 5) as "bmi_smoker_boxplot.png", with width = 7, height = 5, and dpi = 300. Confirm the file exists afterward.

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
109.7 Challenge Problem
Save the same plot in three different formats (.png, .pdf, .svg) using three separate ggsave() calls, and research (or predict from what you know about file formats generally) one practical difference between a .png and an .svg file that might matter for a report intended to be resized or printed at large scale.

title: 109  Saving Plots with ggsave()
text:
109.8 Key Takeaways


ggsave("filename.ext", plot = my_plot, width = ..., height = ..., dpi = ...) writes a ggplot2 plot to an image file, with format determined by the file extension.
Without an explicit plot = ... argument, ggsave() saves whichever plot was most recently displayed — not necessarily the one you intend.
A saved image file persists independently of your R session, unlike a plot object that exists only in memory until saved.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What determines the image format ggsave() writes?

The dpi argument
The file extension in the filename
The width and height arguments

ggsave() always writes .png regardless


True or false. If plot = ... is omitted, ggsave() saves whichever plot object appears first (alphabetically) in your environment.
Predict the output. After ggsave("my_plot.png", plot = my_plot), what would file.exists("my_plot.png") return, assuming the save succeeded?

Find the error.
ggplot(data = participants, aes(x = bmi)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5)

plot1 <- ggplot(data = participants, aes(x = age)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10)

ggsave("age_histogram.png", width = 8, height = 6)
# intended to save plot1 (the age histogram)



Show answers


b) The file extension in the filename (.png, .pdf, .jpg, .svg, etc.).

False. Without plot = ..., ggsave() saves whichever plot was most recently displayed, not the first one alphabetically in the environment.

TRUE.
The BMI histogram was displayed last (even though plot1, the age histogram, was assigned afterward but never explicitly shown), so ggsave() without plot = ... would save the wrong plot. It should explicitly specify ggsave("age_histogram.png", plot = plot1, width = 8, height = 6).

title: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story
text:
110.1 The R Concept
When color (or fill) is mapped inside the initial ggplot() call, every subsequent layer — including geom_smooth() — automatically applies per group, without any extra code:
ggplot(data = df, aes(x = x_col, y = y_col, color = grouping_col)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm")
This produces one trend line per category of the grouping variable, each colored to match its group’s points — because the color mapping set in ggplot() applies to every layer that follows, not just geom_point().
crumbs: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story

title: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story
text:
110.2 Demonstration

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = age, y = systolic_bp, color = smoker)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)

This produces two trend lines instead of one — a separate line fit only to smokers’ data, and another fit only to non-smokers’ data — both layered over the same color-coded scatter of points, with se = FALSE hiding the confidence bands to keep the figure less cluttered with two groups already present.
crumbs: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story

title: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story
text:
110.3 Explore
Try removing se = FALSE and comparing how much busier the plot looks with both groups’ confidence bands shown. With relatively small group sizes (22 smokers, 68 non-smokers) and weak underlying correlations, decide for yourself whether the added confidence-band detail helps or clutters this particular figure.
crumbs: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story

title: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story
text:
110.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_by_age_smoking <- ggplot(data = participants, mapping = aes(x = age, y = systolic_bp, color = smoker)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Blood Pressure by Age and Smoking Status",
    subtitle = "Weak downward trend in both groups; non-smokers show a slightly steeper decline",
    x = "Age (years)",
    y = "Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)",
    color = "Current Smoker"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

bp_by_age_smoking

Two gently downward-sloping trend lines, one per smoking group: non-smokers’ line slopes slightly more steeply downward (correlation -0.25, n=68) than smokers’ line (correlation -0.14, n=22), though both relationships remain weak. alpha = 0.7 makes overlapping points partially transparent, helping visually dense regions of the plot stay readable.
crumbs: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story

title: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story
text:
110.5 Interpret the Result
This single figure answers the level’s opening mission directly: blood pressure shows a weak negative relationship with age in both smoking groups, with non-smokers showing a marginally steeper downward trend than smokers — though given both groups’ correlations are well below 0.3 in magnitude, and the smoker group is fairly small (n=22), this difference between the two slopes shouldn’t be over-interpreted as a confirmed, meaningfully different pattern. What the figure does successfully avoid is any false impression of a strong relationship in either group — the loosely scattered points and shallow trend lines honestly represent how weak these patterns actually are.

Common Mistakes


Building an impressively multi-layered figure that overstates a weak finding. Combining many ggplot2 techniques into one figure doesn’t make an underlying weak relationship (correlations of -0.14 and -0.25) look stronger than it is — a well-built figure should still honestly represent uncertainty and weak effects, not disguise them with visual polish.

Forgetting that color mapped once in ggplot() cascades to every later layer. This is what makes geom_smooth() automatically fit separate lines per group here — a useful shortcut, but one that can surprise you if you expected a single combined trend line instead.

Overcrowding a figure with too many simultaneous elements. Adding confidence bands, multiple colors, dense point overlap, and heavy labeling all at once can make a figure harder to read, not easier — alpha (transparency) and se = FALSE (hiding confidence bands) are both tools for keeping a multi-layered figure legible.
crumbs: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story

title: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story
text:
110.6 Practice Problem
Build the same style of combined figure (geom_point() + geom_smooth() + color + labels + theme) for BMI and blood pressure by sex instead of age and smoking status. Does mapping color = sex reveal a more or less visible difference between groups than smoking status did?
crumbs: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story

title: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story
text:
110.7 Challenge Problem
Combine faceting (Page 9) with color grouping in a single figure: facet the age-blood pressure scatter plot by county (5 panels) while still coloring points by smoker within each panel. With county-level sample sizes as small as 16, discuss whether this much subdivision (5 counties × 2 smoking groups) still produces a figure with enough data per group to draw any meaningful visual conclusions.
crumbs: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story

title: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story
text:
110.8 Key Takeaways

Mapping color (or fill) once in the initial ggplot() call cascades automatically to every subsequent layer, including geom_smooth(), producing one trend line per group without extra code.
A polished, multi-layered figure should still honestly represent the strength (or weakness) of the underlying pattern — visual sophistication is not a substitute for accurately representing uncertainty.

alpha (point transparency) and se = FALSE (hiding confidence bands) are both tools for keeping a busy, multi-group figure legible rather than cluttered.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. If color = smoker is mapped in the initial ggplot() call, what happens when you add geom_smooth(method = "lm")?

One trend line is fit to the entire dataset, ignoring smoking status
One trend line is fit separately for each smoking group

geom_smooth() ignores the color mapping entirely
The plot errors, since geom_smooth() can’t be combined with color mapping


True or false. A visually polished, multi-layered figure can make a weak relationship look stronger than it actually is if built carelessly.
Predict the output. With smokers’ correlation at -0.14 and non-smokers’ at -0.25, which group’s trend line would slope more steeply downward?
Short answer. Why does se = FALSE help keep a two-group trend line figure more readable?


Show answers


b) One trend line is fit separately for each smoking group, since the color mapping set in ggplot() cascades to every subsequent layer.

True. Visual polish (multiple colors, trend lines, clean themes) doesn’t change the underlying data — a careless figure could visually overstate a weak pattern’s strength if built without care.
Non-smokers, with the stronger (more negative) correlation of -0.25.

se = FALSE hides each group’s confidence band — with two overlapping trend lines already present, adding two shaded confidence regions on top could make the figure visually cluttered and harder to read.
crumbs: 110  Level 8 Capstone: One Figure, Two Variables, One Story

title: Level 9 — Functions and Programming
text:
What you’ll build toward
You’ve called dozens of functions since Level 1 — mean(), filter(), pivot_longer(), ggplot() — without ever writing one yourself. This level flips that around: you’ll write your own functions from scratch, give them default arguments, add conditional logic and validation inside them, and then apply a single function across many columns at once with base R’s sapply()/lapply() and the tidyverse’s purrr::map() family.

title: Level 9 — Functions and Programming
text:
Topics in this level

What a function actually is, and how R’s built-in functions you already use are no different in kind from ones you’ll write yourself
Writing your first function: inputs (arguments), outputs (return values)
Default argument values
Conditional logic inside a function
Variable scope — what a function can and can’t see or change outside itself
Anonymous (“throwaway”) functions
sapply() and lapply() for applying a function across many columns
purrr::map(), map_dbl(), and map_chr()
Basic error handling with stop() and warning()

title: Level 9 — Functions and Programming
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: participants.csv has 8 columns, several with their own missing-value rate (6% for age, 4% for systolic_bp, 5% for bmi). Write a single function that computes “percent missing” for one column, then apply it across every column in the dataset at once — write it once, run it on all 8 columns automatically, rather than retyping sum(is.na(...)) / nrow(...) * 100 eight separate times.

title: 111  What Is a Function?
text:
111.1 The R Concept
A function is a named, reusable block of code that takes zero or more inputs (called arguments or parameters), does something with them, and produces an output (called the return value). Every function you’ve used since Level 1 — mean(), sum(), read.csv(), filter(), ggplot() — follows this exact same pattern:
function_name(input1, input2, ...)   # produces some output
mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE) takes a vector (participants$age) and a setting (na.rm = TRUE) as input, and returns a single number as output. There is nothing magical about built-in functions — they’re written in exactly the same style you’re about to learn to write your own functions in.

title: 111  What Is a Function?
text:
111.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Using a built-in function - input goes in, output comes out
mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 47.42553

# The pattern you'll replicate: some calculation, wrapped up and named
sum(is.na(participants$age)) / length(participants$age) * 100

[1] 6
The second line computes the percentage of missing age values by hand — exactly the calculation you’d want to name and reuse, the same way someone once wrote mean() so you’d never have to type sum(x) / length(x) yourself.

title: 111  What Is a Function?
text:
111.3 Explore
Try running sum(is.na(participants$systolic_bp)) / length(participants$systolic_bp) * 100 and sum(is.na(participants$bmi)) / length(participants$bmi) * 100. Notice you’re retyping the same pattern with only the column name changing — exactly the repetition a function is meant to eliminate.

title: 111  What Is a Function?
text:
111.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

age_pct_missing <- sum(is.na(participants$age)) / length(participants$age) * 100
bp_pct_missing <- sum(is.na(participants$systolic_bp)) / length(participants$systolic_bp) * 100
bmi_pct_missing <- sum(is.na(participants$bmi)) / length(participants$bmi) * 100

age_pct_missing

[1] 6

bp_pct_missing

[1] 4

bmi_pct_missing

[1] 5
Three correct answers (6%, 4%, 5% missing) — but three nearly identical lines of code, differing only in which column is referenced. This is precisely the kind of repetition the next page’s custom function will eliminate.

title: 111  What Is a Function?
text:
111.5 Interpret the Result
age, systolic_bp, and bmi are each missing a small percentage of their values (6%, 4%, and 5% respectively) — useful individually, but the process of computing each one was identical. Recognizing “I keep typing the same pattern with only one thing changing” is the signal that a function belongs here — the same signal that should have applied a hundred times over across every prior level, now finally something you can act on.

Common Mistakes


Assuming built-in and custom functions work fundamentally differently. They don’t — mean() and a function you write yourself both take input and produce output the exact same way; the only difference is who wrote them and when.

Not noticing repeated code as a signal to write a function. Copy-pasting the same calculation three times with one small change (Page 1’s age/systolic_bp/bmi example) is the clearest sign a function is overdue — the next page turns exactly this repetition into one reusable tool.

Confusing a function’s definition with calling it. Writing a function (next page) and calling it are two separate steps — defining pct_missing <- function(x) {...} doesn’t run anything by itself; you still have to call pct_missing(participants$age) afterward to get a result.

title: 111  What Is a Function?
text:
111.6 Practice Problem
Compute the percentage missing for diastolic_bp and smoker by hand, using the same sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100 pattern. Confirm both are 0%, since Level 7 found no missing values in either column.

title: 111  What Is a Function?
text:
111.7 Challenge Problem
Before reading the next page, try to write out in plain English (not R code) the steps a function called pct_missing would need to perform, given any column as input. What would its single input be? What single output would it produce?

title: 111  What Is a Function?
text:
111.8 Key Takeaways

A function takes input (arguments) and produces output (a return value) — every function you’ve used since Level 1 follows this same basic pattern.
Built-in functions like mean() and functions you write yourself work identically in kind — the only difference is authorship.
Retyping the same calculation with only one small change is the clearest signal that code belongs in a function instead.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What are a function’s inputs called?

Return values
Arguments (or parameters)
Comments
Vectors


True or false. Built-in functions like mean() work fundamentally differently from functions a user writes.
Predict the output. What would sum(is.na(participants$diastolic_bp)) / length(participants$diastolic_bp) * 100 return, given diastolic_bp has no missing values?
Short answer. What’s the clearest signal that a repeated piece of code should be turned into a function?


Show answers


b) Arguments (or parameters).

False. Built-in and user-written functions work identically in kind — both take input and produce output the same way.

0 — with no missing values, the calculation gives 0%.
Typing the same (or nearly the same) calculation more than once, with only a small piece — like a column name — changing between repetitions.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.2 Demonstration

pct_missing <- function(x) {
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

pct_missing(participants$age)

[1] 6

pct_missing(participants$systolic_bp)

[1] 4

pct_missing(participants$bmi)

[1] 5
The exact same three answers as the previous page (6%, 4%, 5%) — but now produced by calling one short, named function three times, instead of retyping the full calculation each time.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.3 Explore
Try pct_missing(participants$diastolic_bp). Confirm it correctly returns 0, exactly matching your hand calculation from the previous page’s practice problem — your function works on any vector passed to it, not just the ones you tested first.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.4 Solve the Problem

pct_missing <- function(x) {
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

pct_missing(participants$id)

[1] 0

pct_missing(participants$smoker)

[1] 0

pct_missing(participants$county)

[1] 0
id, smoker, and county all correctly return 0 — none of these columns have any missing values, and pct_missing() handles them exactly as correctly as it handled the columns that did have missing data, without needing any special-casing.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.5 Interpret the Result
pct_missing() now works as a genuine, general-purpose tool — callable on any column in participants, or in any other dataset entirely, without ever needing to see or modify its internal code again. This is the core payoff of writing a function: the calculation was written once, correctly, and now every future use of it is just a short function call.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting the curly braces {} for multi-line functions. A single-line function body can skip the braces (pct_missing <- function(x) sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100), but any function with more than one line needs {} to group them together.

Assuming a function needs an explicit return() to produce output. R automatically returns the last evaluated expression — return() is optional and mainly useful for exiting a function early (covered later in this level) or for clarity in longer functions.

Confusing the argument name (x) with any specific column. x inside the function body is just a placeholder name — it takes on whatever value is passed in when the function is called (participants$age, participants$bmi, or anything else), and has no meaning outside the function itself.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.6 Practice Problem
Write a function called range_width that takes a numeric vector x and returns the difference between its maximum and minimum values (using na.rm = TRUE inside), then test it on participants$bmi.

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.7 Challenge Problem
Write a function called mean_and_sd that takes a numeric vector x and returns both the mean and standard deviation as a named vector (hint: c(mean = ..., sd = ...)), handling missing values appropriately. Test it on participants$age and confirm both values match Level 7’s earlier findings (mean 47.43, SD 15.03).

title: 112  Writing Your First Function
text:
112.8 Key Takeaways

A function is created with function(arguments) { code }, and is called later by name with actual values in place of its arguments.
R automatically returns the last evaluated expression from a function — an explicit return() is optional, not required.
Once written and tested, a function becomes a reusable tool that works on any appropriate input, not just the specific examples used while building it.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does R return from a function by default, with no explicit return()?

Nothing — an error occurs
The first evaluated expression
The last evaluated expression

NULL, always


True or false. Curly braces {} are required for every function, even one-line ones.
Predict the output. Given pct_missing <- function(x) sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100, what would pct_missing(c(1, NA, 3, NA)) return?

Find the error.
pct_missing <- function(x)
  sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100
  cat("Done!")



Show answers


c) The last evaluated expression.

False. A single-line function body can omit the curly braces; they’re only required when the body spans multiple lines that need to be grouped together.

50 — 2 of the 4 values are NA, so 2 / 4 * 100 = 50.
Without curly braces, only the very next single line (sum(is.na(x)) / length(x) * 100) is treated as the function’s body — the cat("Done!") line runs separately, outside the function, every time this code is sourced, rather than being part of pct_missing()’s behavior. Wrapping both lines in {} would fix this ambiguity.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.2 Demonstration

describe_numeric <- function(x, digits = 2) {
  c(
    mean = round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits),
    sd = round(sd(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits)
  )
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

describe_numeric(participants$age)

 mean    sd
47.43 15.03

describe_numeric(participants$age, digits = 0)

mean   sd
  47   15
Calling the function with no digits argument at all uses the default (2 decimal places); explicitly passing digits = 0 overrides that default, rounding to whole numbers instead.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.3 Explore
Try describe_numeric(participants$bmi, digits = 4). Confirm the function happily accepts any value for digits, not just 0 or 2 — the default is only ever a fallback, never a restriction on what values are allowed.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.4 Solve the Problem

describe_numeric <- function(x, digits = 2) {
  c(
    mean = round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits),
    sd = round(sd(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits)
  )
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

describe_numeric(participants$systolic_bp)

  mean     sd
129.84  18.87

describe_numeric(participants$systolic_bp, digits = 1)

 mean    sd
129.8  18.9
Both calls compute the exact same underlying mean and SD — only the rounding precision changes, controlled entirely by whether digits is left at its default (2) or explicitly overridden (1).

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.5 Interpret the Result
describe_numeric() now serves both the quick-internal-check case (default 2 decimals) and the more precise or more rounded reporting case (any digits value explicitly requested), without needing two separate functions or repeating the round()-wrapped calculation twice. A well-chosen default lets the common case stay simple, while still leaving full flexibility available when needed.

Common Mistakes


Choosing a default that silently produces wrong-looking results for typical use. A poorly chosen default (like digits = 10) could produce overly precise, hard-to-read output for the common case — defaults should reflect what’s usually wanted, not just any arbitrary value.

Forgetting default arguments can reference other arguments. More advanced function definitions can have one default depend on another argument’s value — while not needed here, it’s worth knowing defaults aren’t limited to fixed constants.

Assuming a default value means an argument can never be changed. A default is only what’s used if omitted — every default-valued argument remains fully overridable at the calling site, exactly as shown with digits = 0 and digits = 1 above.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.6 Practice Problem
Modify pct_missing() from Page 2 to accept a digits argument with a default of 1, rounding its result before returning it. Test it on participants$age both with and without specifying digits.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.7 Challenge Problem
Write a function called flag_high_bp that takes a numeric vector of systolic blood pressure values and a threshold argument defaulting to 130 (Level 4’s hypertension cutoff), returning TRUE/FALSE for each value indicating whether it exceeds the threshold. Test it once with the default threshold, and once with threshold = 140 (a stricter cutoff), and compare how many values are flagged under each.

title: 113  Default Argument Values
text:
113.8 Key Takeaways

A default argument value (argument = default_value in the function definition) is used automatically whenever that argument is omitted from a call.
Defaults make the common case simple to call, while every default remains fully overridable when a different value is needed.
This is the exact same pattern behind built-in functions like mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), whose na.rm argument defaults to FALSE.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In function(x, digits = 2), when is digits equal to 2?

Always, regardless of how the function is called
Only when the caller explicitly writes digits = 2

Whenever the caller omits the digits argument entirely
Never — defaults are just documentation, not actual behavior


True or false. A default argument value can always be overridden by the caller.
Predict the output. Given describe_numeric <- function(x, digits = 2) {...}, what would digits equal inside a call to describe_numeric(participants$bmi)?

Find the error.
describe_numeric <- function(x, digits) {
  c(mean = round(mean(x, na.rm = TRUE), digits))
}
describe_numeric(participants$age)



Show answers


c) Whenever the caller omits the digits argument entirely.

True. A default is only ever a fallback for when an argument is omitted — it never prevents the caller from specifying a different value.

2 — the default value, since the call didn’t specify digits.

digits has no default value here, so calling describe_numeric(participants$age) without specifying digits produces an error (argument "digits" is missing, with no default). Adding digits = 2 (or any default) to the function definition would fix this.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.3 Explore
Try bp_category(120) and bp_category(129) — both should return "Elevated", since the boundaries use < (strictly less than), meaning the lower bound of each category belongs to that category, not the one before it. Confirm this matches Level 4’s case_when() boundary logic exactly.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.5 Interpret the Result
bp_category() reproduces Level 4’s classification logic exactly, but packaged as a reusable tool that works on one value at a time — useful in contexts where you don’t want to build (or don’t have) a full data frame, like classifying a single new reading as it comes in. The matching counts (18/32/17/29) confirm the function was written correctly, cross-checked against a completely different implementation from an earlier level.

Common Mistakes


Getting boundary conditions backwards inside a function, same as in Level 4. sbp < 120 versus sbp <= 120 produces different classifications right at the boundary value 120 — the same care needed for case_when() conditions applies identically inside a function’s if statements.

Forgetting else needs to handle every remaining case. If the final else branch were missing, a systolic BP of 150 (falling through every if/else if condition) would return NULL instead of a category — always ensure the final else genuinely catches everything not handled above it.

Passing a vector to a function written for a single value. bp_category() as written expects one number at a time — passing an entire column directly (bp_category(participants$systolic_bp)) doesn’t work as intended, because if in R expects a single logical value, not a vector; sapply() (Page 8) is what applies a single-value function across an entire column correctly.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.6 Practice Problem
Write a function called age_group that takes a single age value and returns "Senior" for ages 65 and older, "Adult" for ages 18-64, using if/else. Test it on a few individual values.

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.7 Challenge Problem
Modify bp_category() to also handle a "Low" category for sbp < 90 (add this as the very first condition checked), and re-run it across participants$systolic_bp. Given the minimum recorded systolic BP is exactly 90, would you expect this new category to ever actually appear in the results?

title: 114  Conditional Logic Inside Functions
text:
114.8 Key Takeaways

A function’s body can contain the same if/else if/else logic used outside a function (Level 4) — whichever branch runs becomes the function’s automatic return value.
Boundary conditions (< versus <=) require the same care inside a function as they do in any standalone conditional.
Cross-checking a function’s output against a previously verified result (like Level 4’s case_when() counts) is a reliable way to confirm the function was written correctly.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In bp_category(), what does the function return for sbp = 135?

"Normal"
"Elevated"
"Stage 1"
"Stage 2"


True or false. A missing final else branch could cause a function to return NULL for values not covered by any earlier condition.
Predict the output. What would bp_category(119) and bp_category(120) each return?
Short answer. Why does cross-checking bp_category()’s counts (18/32/17/29) against Level 4’s case_when() counts provide good evidence the function is correct?


Show answers


c) "Stage 1" — 135 is at least 130 but less than 140.

True. If no if/else if condition matches and there’s no final else, the function returns NULL for that input, rather than a meaningful category.

bp_category(119) returns "Normal" (since 119 < 120); bp_category(120) returns "Elevated" (since 120 is not less than 120, so it falls to the next condition).
Two independently written implementations (Level 4’s case_when() inside mutate(), and this level’s if/else if function) producing identical counts on the same data is strong evidence neither has a logic error — if either had a boundary mistake, the two counts would very likely disagree.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.2 Demonstration

is_at_risk <- function(age, sbp, age_cutoff = 65, bp_cutoff = 130) {
  age >= age_cutoff & sbp >= bp_cutoff
}

is_at_risk(70, 145)

[1] TRUE

is_at_risk(50, 145)

[1] FALSE
The first participant (age 70, BP 145) meets both thresholds and is flagged TRUE; the second (age 50, BP 145) fails the age condition alone, so the combined result is FALSE — exactly the & (“and”) logic from Level 1, now packaged inside a reusable function.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.3 Explore
Try is_at_risk(70, 145, age_cutoff = 75). Since 70 no longer meets a stricter age cutoff of 75, this should return FALSE — confirming the default-valued arguments (Page 3) remain fully overridable even in a function with multiple parameters.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.4 Solve the Problem

is_at_risk <- function(age, sbp, age_cutoff = 65, bp_cutoff = 130) {
  age >= age_cutoff & sbp >= bp_cutoff
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

at_risk_flags <- is_at_risk(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp)

sum(at_risk_flags, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 4
Because age >= age_cutoff & sbp >= bp_cutoff uses R’s vectorized & operator (Level 2), is_at_risk() works correctly whether called on two single numbers or two entire columns at once — no sapply() or loop required here, since & already compares element-by-element automatically. The result (4 at-risk participants) exactly matches Level 5’s filter(age >= 65 & systolic_bp >= 130) count.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.5 Interpret the Result
is_at_risk() reproduces Level 5’s flagship finding (4 at-risk participants) exactly, but as a named, reusable, customizable tool — the age and blood pressure cutoffs can be adjusted per call without editing the function’s internals, unlike Level 5’s filter() call, which would need its condition rewritten by hand for a different threshold.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting argument order matters when calling a function positionally. is_at_risk(145, 70) (blood pressure first, age second) silently swaps the two values’ roles — always double check argument order, or use named arguments (is_at_risk(age = 70, sbp = 145)) to avoid ambiguity entirely.

Assuming every function automatically vectorizes like is_at_risk() did here. This function happened to work correctly on whole columns because &, >= are already vectorized operators (Level 2) — not every function behaves this way automatically, especially ones containing if (Page 4), which expects a single value, not a vector.

Mixing required and default arguments in the wrong order. In R, arguments with defaults should generally come after arguments without defaults in the function definition — function(age_cutoff = 65, age, sbp) would still technically work when called with named arguments, but is much more error-prone and confusing to read.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.6 Practice Problem
Write a function called is_high_risk_smoker that takes bmi, smoker, and a bmi_cutoff defaulting to 30, returning TRUE if BMI meets or exceeds the cutoff and the participant is a current smoker. Test it on the full participants data frame and report how many are flagged.

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.7 Challenge Problem
Modify is_at_risk() to accept a third required argument, smoker, and flag a participant only if all three conditions hold: age 65+, systolic BP 130+, and current smoker status. How many participants meet this stricter combined definition, compared to the original 4?

title: 115  Functions with Multiple Arguments
text:
115.8 Key Takeaways

A function can take multiple arguments, listed in order and separated by commas, freely mixing required and default-valued arguments.
Whether a function automatically works on whole vectors depends on whether its internal operations (like &, >=) are already vectorized — this isn’t automatic for every function.
Named arguments (is_at_risk(age = 70, sbp = 145)) avoid the ambiguity of positional argument order, especially as a function’s argument count grows.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In is_at_risk(age, sbp, age_cutoff = 65, bp_cutoff = 130), which arguments are optional?


age and sbp


age_cutoff and bp_cutoff

All four are required
None — all four have defaults


True or false. Calling is_at_risk(145, 70) (with arguments in the wrong order) will produce an error rather than a silently wrong answer.
Predict the output. What would is_at_risk(80, 150, age_cutoff = 70, bp_cutoff = 140) return?

Find the error.
is_at_risk(sbp = 70, 145)
# intended: age = 70, sbp = 145



Show answers


b) age_cutoff and bp_cutoff — both have default values, making them optional.

False. R doesn’t detect that the arguments are “backwards” — it silently treats the first positional value as age and the second as sbp, producing a plausible-looking but incorrect result rather than an error.

TRUE — age 80 ≥ 70 and blood pressure 150 ≥ 140, so both conditions hold.

sbp was named explicitly as 70, so the remaining positional value 145 fills the next unfilled argument, age — meaning this call actually evaluates as age = 145, sbp = 70, the reverse of what was intended. Mixing named and positional arguments is legal in R, but it’s easy to misread; naming both arguments explicitly (is_at_risk(age = 70, sbp = 145)) avoids this kind of silent mix-up entirely.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.1 The R Concept
Every function call creates its own local scope — a private workspace for variables created or received as arguments inside that function. Variables inside a function’s local scope:
age <- 100   # global variable

check_age <- function(age) {
  age <- age + 1   # this only changes the LOCAL copy of age
  age
}

check_age(30)   # returns 31
age             # still 100 - completely unaffected

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
Are isolated from your main (“global”) workspace — a variable named age inside a function has no connection to any age object outside it, even if they share the exact same name.

Disappear once the function finishes running — they exist only for the duration of that one function call.
Can read variables from the global environment if not shadowed by a local one of the same name, but cannot modify the global version by simply assigning to a same-named local variable.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.2 Demonstration

age <- 100

check_age <- function(age) {
  age <- age + 1
  age
}

check_age(30)

[1] 31

age

[1] 100
Even though the function’s argument is also named age, and the function reassigns age internally, the global age (100) is completely untouched — the function’s age was always a separate, local copy that ceased to exist the moment the function finished.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.3 Explore
Try defining local_total <- 0 inside a function body, running the function, and then checking whether local_total exists in your main environment afterward (it won’t — exists("local_total") will return FALSE). Variables created inside a function’s body are just as local as its arguments.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.4 Solve the Problem

pct_missing <- function(x) {
  n_missing <- sum(is.na(x))       # local variable, exists only inside this call
  n_total <- length(x)             # also local
  n_missing / n_total * 100
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

pct_missing(participants$age)

[1] 6

exists("n_missing")

[1] FALSE
n_missing and n_total were created and used entirely inside pct_missing()’s local scope — after the function call finishes, exists("n_missing") confirms they never leaked into your main workspace at all.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.5 Interpret the Result
This isolation is a genuine safety feature, not a limitation: it means you can freely reuse short, convenient variable names like x, n, or age inside any function without ever worrying about accidentally overwriting an important variable of the same name in your main script. Functions act as self-contained units — what happens inside, stays inside, unless you explicitly return a value out.

Common Mistakes


Expecting a function to modify a global variable by assigning to a same-named local one. check_age()’s internal age <- age + 1 only ever changes the local copy — to actually update something in your main workspace, the function would need to return() the new value and you’d need to reassign it yourself (age <- check_age(age)).

Assuming variables created inside a function are available for debugging afterward. If a function errors partway through, its local variables (like n_missing above) are simply gone — this is why printing intermediate values during development (before wrapping code in a function) is often easier for debugging than trying to inspect a function’s internals after the fact.

Reusing a global variable’s exact name as a function argument and assuming this causes a conflict. As shown above, this is actually completely safe — R always prioritizes the local (argument) version inside the function body, with no naming conflict at all.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.6 Practice Problem
Write a small function that takes an argument called bmi, adds 1 to it internally, and returns the result. Call it with participants$bmi[1] as the argument, then confirm that participants$bmi[1] itself is completely unchanged afterward.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.7 Challenge Problem
Predict, without running any code, what this snippet prints for each line:

total <- 10
add_five <- function(total) {
  total <- total + 5
  total
}
print(add_five(total))
print(total)

Then run it to confirm your prediction.

title: 116  Variable Scope
text:
116.8 Key Takeaways

Every function call creates its own local scope — variables inside it (arguments and internally created ones alike) are isolated from your main workspace.
A function can read global variables it hasn’t shadowed, but assigning to a same-named local variable never modifies the global version.
Local variables disappear once a function finishes running, which is a safety feature protecting your main workspace from accidental modification.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. After check_age(30) runs (as defined above), what is the value of the global age?

31
30
100 (unchanged)
NULL


True or false. Variables created inside a function’s body continue to exist in your main workspace after the function finishes running.
Predict the output. If a function argument is named x, and your main workspace also has a variable called x, does the function’s internal use of x affect the global one?
Short answer. Why is it considered safe practice to reuse short variable names like x or n inside many different functions?


Show answers


c) 100 — the global age is completely unaffected by the function’s local reassignment.

False. Local variables exist only for the duration of the function call and disappear once it finishes.
No — the function’s x is entirely local and isolated from any global variable of the same name, unless the function explicitly returns a value that you then reassign to the global x yourself.
Because each function call creates its own isolated local scope, reusing a name like x in one function has no effect on any variable of the same name used elsewhere, including in your main workspace or inside other functions.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.1 The R Concept
A function doesn’t need to be assigned a name to be used immediately — this is called an anonymous function:
function(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)
R also provides a shorter syntax for anonymous functions, using a backslash \ instead of the full word function:
\(x) max(x, na.rm = TRUE) - min(x, na.rm = TRUE)
Both versions define the exact same function — the \(x) shorthand is simply less typing, and is especially common when passing a quick function directly into another function (like sapply(), previewed next page), rather than defining it separately first.

title: 117  Anonymous Functions
text:
117.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Anonymous function, called immediately - no name assigned
