You’ll move from probability foundations through confidence intervals, t-tests, chi-square, correlation testing, simple linear regression, ANOVA, and logistic regression — testing many of the exact patterns Levels 5, 7, and 8 already described numerically and visually, now with a formal answer to “is this real, or noise?” Multivariable models, confounding, and more advanced regression families are covered in Level 12, building directly on the foundation this level establishes.

title: Level 11 — Statistical Analysis in R
text:
Topics in this level

Probability and the normal distribution
The Central Limit Theorem, demonstrated by simulation
Confidence intervals
One-sample, two-sample, and paired t-tests
The chi-square test of independence
Testing a correlation for statistical significance
Simple linear regression with lm()
One-way ANOVA
Logistic regression for a binary outcome
A capstone: formally testing several of this course’s earlier descriptive findings

title: Level 11 — Statistical Analysis in R
text:
A preview mission
Your mission across this level: revisit Level 7’s finding that smoking rates differ by sex (19.2% vs. 31.2%) and Level 8’s finding that blood pressure trends gently downward with age — and formally test both. Are they statistically significant, or could patterns this size plausibly arise by chance in a sample of 100 participants?

title: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution
text:
130.1 The R Concept
The normal distribution (“bell curve”) is defined entirely by its mean and standard deviation. R’s pnorm() computes the probability of a value falling below a cutoff, under a normal distribution with a given mean and SD:
pnorm(cutoff, mean = mu, sd = sigma)              # P(X <= cutoff)
1 - pnorm(cutoff, mean = mu, sd = sigma)           # P(X > cutoff)
qnorm() works in reverse — given a probability, it returns the corresponding cutoff value (“quantile function,” extending Level 7’s quantile() to a theoretical distribution rather than actual data):
qnorm(0.95, mean = mu, sd = sigma)   # the value below which 95% of a normal distribution falls
crumbs: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution

title: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution
text:
130.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_mean <- mean(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)
bmi_sd <- sd(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)

bmi_mean

[1] 27.12316

bmi_sd

[1] 4.639157

# theoretical probability of BMI > 30, assuming a normal distribution
1 - pnorm(30, mean = bmi_mean, sd = bmi_sd)

[1] 0.2676
crumbs: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution

title: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution
text:
130.3 Explore
Try comparing this theoretical prediction against the actual data: mean(participants$bmi > 30, na.rm = TRUE). The two numbers won’t match exactly — real data never follows a theoretical distribution perfectly — but a close match suggests the normal approximation is reasonable for this variable.
crumbs: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution

title: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution
text:
130.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_mean <- mean(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)
bmi_sd <- sd(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)

theoretical_prop <- 1 - pnorm(30, mean = bmi_mean, sd = bmi_sd)
empirical_prop <- mean(participants$bmi > 30, na.rm = TRUE)

theoretical_prop

[1] 0.2676

empirical_prop

[1] 0.2632
The theoretical prediction (26.76%) and the actual empirical proportion (26.32%) are remarkably close — under a one-percentage-point difference — strong evidence that BMI in this sample is well-approximated by a normal distribution with mean 27.12 and SD 4.64.
crumbs: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution

title: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution
text:
130.5 Interpret the Result
This close agreement matters beyond curiosity: many of the statistical tests in the rest of this level (t-tests, linear regression) formally assume the data (or, more precisely, certain quantities derived from it) follows something close to a normal distribution. Confirming that assumption is reasonable here — rather than blindly trusting it — is good statistical practice, not a formality to skip.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting pnorm() gives a cumulative probability (P(X ≤ cutoff)), not P(X = cutoff) or P(X > cutoff) directly. To get “greater than,” you must subtract from 1: 1 - pnorm(cutoff, ...), exactly as done above.

Assuming every numeric variable is normally distributed without checking. Some variables (income, hospital length of stay, and many others) are heavily skewed and poorly approximated by a normal distribution — always compare the theoretical prediction against the actual data, as this page did, rather than assuming normality by default.

Mixing up pnorm() and qnorm(). pnorm() takes a value and returns a probability; qnorm() takes a probability and returns a value — they’re inverses of each other, and using the wrong one produces a nonsensical result (e.g., qnorm() given a value over 1 errors, since probabilities can’t exceed 1).
crumbs: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution

title: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution
text:
130.6 Practice Problem
Use pnorm() to compute the theoretical probability that a participant’s systolic blood pressure is below 110, using the sample mean and SD from Level 7. Compare it against the actual empirical proportion in the data.
crumbs: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution

title: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution
text:
130.7 Challenge Problem
Use qnorm(0.90, mean = bmi_mean, sd = bmi_sd) to find the BMI value below which 90% of a normal distribution with this sample’s mean and SD would fall. Then check what percentage of participants in the actual data fall below that same cutoff, using mean(participants$bmi < cutoff, na.rm = TRUE).
crumbs: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution

title: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution
text:
130.8 Key Takeaways

The normal distribution is fully described by its mean and standard deviation; pnorm() computes probabilities and qnorm() computes cutoff values, inverses of each other.
Comparing a theoretical (normal-distribution) prediction against the actual empirical proportion in real data is a simple, useful check of whether the normal approximation is reasonable.
Many statistical tests later in this level assume approximate normality — checking this assumption, rather than blindly trusting it, is good practice.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does pnorm(30, mean = 27.12, sd = 4.64) compute?

The probability that X equals exactly 30
The probability that X is less than or equal to 30
The value below which 30% of the distribution falls
The standard deviation of the distribution


True or false. A normal distribution is fully described by its mean and standard deviation alone.
Predict the output. What would 1 - pnorm(30, mean = 27.12, sd = 4.64) represent?

Find the error.
pnorm(0.95, mean = bmi_mean, sd = bmi_sd)
# intended: find the BMI value below which 95% of the distribution falls



Show answers


b) The probability that X is less than or equal to 30.

True. Unlike many other distributions, a normal distribution requires only these two parameters to be fully specified.
The probability that a value from this distribution exceeds 30 (P(X > 30)).

pnorm() takes a value and returns a probability — the intended calculation (finding a value from a probability) requires the inverse function, qnorm(). It should be qnorm(0.95, mean = bmi_mean, sd = bmi_sd).
crumbs: 130  Probability and the Normal Distribution

title: 131  The Central Limit Theorem
text:
131.1 The R Concept
The Central Limit Theorem states that the distribution of sample means (computed from repeated samples of a given size) approaches a normal distribution as sample size grows — regardless of the original variable’s own distribution shape — with a spread (“standard error”) shrinking predictably as sample size increases:
set.seed(42)
sample_means <- replicate(n_draws, mean(sample(population, size = n, replace = TRUE)))
The theoretical standard error of the mean is the population standard deviation divided by the square root of the sample size:
\[SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\]

title: 131  The Central Limit Theorem
text:
131.2 Demonstration

set.seed(42)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
ages <- participants$age[!is.na(participants$age)]

sample_means <- replicate(1000, mean(sample(ages, size = 30, replace = TRUE)))

mean(sample_means)

[1] 47.5   # your exact value will vary slightly - this is randomness-dependent

sd(sample_means)

[1] 2.75   # again, expect a value close to this, not identical to the decimal
The mean of 1,000 sample means lands almost exactly on the true population mean (47.43) — and the spread of those sample means is far narrower than the original data’s spread (SD = 15.03), exactly as the CLT predicts. Note: because this involves R’s own random number generator, your exact decimal values will differ slightly from anyone else’s (and from what’s printed here) even with the same set.seed(42) — what matters, and what will always hold, is that the mean lands close to 47.43 and the spread lands close to the theoretical prediction computed next.

title: 131  The Central Limit Theorem
text:
131.3 Explore
Try computing the theoretical standard error directly: sd(ages) / sqrt(30). Compare it to sd(sample_means) from the simulation above — the two should be close, confirming the CLT’s formula matches what actually happens when you simulate it, even though the simulated value itself will vary slightly from run to run and system to system.

title: 131  The Central Limit Theorem
text:
131.4 Solve the Problem

set.seed(42)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
ages <- participants$age[!is.na(participants$age)]

sample_means <- replicate(1000, mean(sample(ages, size = 30, replace = TRUE)))

empirical_se <- sd(sample_means)
theoretical_se <- sd(ages) / sqrt(30)

empirical_se

[1] 2.75   # a representative value - simulation-dependent, will vary slightly

theoretical_se

[1] 2.744756
theoretical_se is an exact, deterministic number — computed directly from the actual data (sd(ages), 15.033646, divided by sqrt(30)) with no randomness involved at all. empirical_se, by contrast, comes from a random simulation, so it will land close to 2.744756 but essentially never exactly equal to it — the whole point of the simulation is to confirm that closeness, not to reproduce the theoretical number exactly.

title: 131  The Central Limit Theorem
text:
131.5 Interpret the Result
This simulation demonstrates something genuinely powerful: no matter what shape age’s own distribution takes, the distribution of its sample means (across repeated samples of size 30) clusters tightly and symmetrically around the true population mean, with a spread landing close to what sd(ages) / sqrt(n) predicts. This is the theoretical foundation underneath every t-test and confidence interval in the rest of this level — they all rely on the sample mean behaving this predictably.

Common Mistakes


Confusing the standard deviation of the original data with the standard error of the mean. sd(ages) (15.03) describes how spread out individual ages are; sd(sample_means) (2.757) describes how spread out sample means are — these are fundamentally different quantities, and confusing them is one of the most common statistical mistakes.

Forgetting set.seed() for reproducible simulations. Without it, sample() and replicate() produce different random results every time the code runs — set.seed(42) (or any fixed number) ensures the exact same “random” sequence every time, essential for a reproducible demonstration.

Assuming the CLT requires the original variable to already be normal. The power of the CLT is precisely that it applies even when the original variable isn’t normal — sample means still tend toward a normal distribution as sample size grows, though how large a sample size is “enough” depends on how far from normal the original variable is.

title: 131  The Central Limit Theorem
text:
131.6 Practice Problem
Repeat the simulation with a larger sample size (size = 60 instead of 30) and compare the resulting standard error to the original. Does it shrink or grow as sample size increases, and does the direction of change match the sd(ages) / sqrt(n) formula’s prediction?

title: 131  The Central Limit Theorem
text:
131.7 Challenge Problem
Repeat the simulation using systolic_bp instead of age. Given systolic_bp’s different mean and SD (Level 7), predict the expected standard error using the formula, then confirm it against your simulation’s result.

title: 131  The Central Limit Theorem
text:
131.8 Key Takeaways

The Central Limit Theorem states that sample means, from repeated sampling, tend toward a normal distribution with a predictable standard error (population SD / sqrt(n)), regardless of the original variable’s own distribution shape.
The standard error of the mean (spread of sample means) is a fundamentally different, and much smaller, quantity than the standard deviation of the original data.
This theorem is the foundation underlying t-tests and confidence intervals, both covered in the rest of this level.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. According to the CLT, as sample size increases, what happens to the standard error of the sample mean?

It stays the same
It increases
It decreases
It becomes undefined


True or false. The Central Limit Theorem requires the original variable to already follow a normal distribution.
Predict the output. If sd(ages) = 15.03 and sample size is 100 (instead of 30), what would the theoretical standard error be (approximately)?
Short answer. Why is set.seed() important when demonstrating a concept through simulation?


Show answers


c) It decreases — larger samples produce sample means clustered more tightly around the true population mean.

False. The CLT’s key strength is that it applies even when the original variable isn’t normally distributed, as sample size grows sufficiently large.
Approximately 1.503 (15.03 / sqrt(100) = 15.03 / 10).

set.seed() makes a “random” simulation fully reproducible — anyone re-running the exact same code gets the exact same results, essential for verifying, debugging, or teaching from a simulation’s specific output.

title: 132  Confidence Intervals
text:
132.1 The R Concept
A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for a population parameter (like the true mean), built around a sample estimate using its standard error. R’s t.test() — primarily a hypothesis test (next page) — conveniently also reports a confidence interval for the mean as part of its output:
t.test(x)$conf.int
The interval is built from the sample mean, the standard error (Page 2), and a critical value from the t-distribution (a close relative of the normal distribution, adjusted for the extra uncertainty of estimating the standard deviation from a limited sample):
\[\text{CI} = \bar{x} \pm t_{crit} \times SE\]

title: 132  Confidence Intervals
text:
132.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sbp <- participants$systolic_bp[!is.na(participants$systolic_bp)]

t.test(sbp)$conf.int

[1] 126.0201 133.6674
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95
The sample mean (129.84) comes with a 95% confidence interval of roughly 126.02 to 133.67 — a range of plausible values for the true population mean, not just the single point estimate alone.

title: 132  Confidence Intervals
text:
132.3 Explore
Try t.test(sbp, conf.level = 0.99)$conf.int — a 99% confidence interval instead of the default 95%. Notice the interval widens: a higher confidence level requires a wider range to remain equally confident of capturing the true population mean.

title: 132  Confidence Intervals
text:
132.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sbp <- participants$systolic_bp[!is.na(participants$systolic_bp)]

sbp_mean <- mean(sbp)
sbp_se <- sd(sbp) / sqrt(length(sbp))
t_critical <- qt(0.975, df = length(sbp) - 1)

lower_bound <- sbp_mean - t_critical * sbp_se
upper_bound <- sbp_mean + t_critical * sbp_se

c(lower_bound, upper_bound)

[1] 126.0201 133.6674

t.test(sbp)$conf.int

[1] 126.0201 133.6674
attr(,"conf.level")
[1] 0.95
Building the interval manually (mean ± critical value × SE) produces exactly the same result as t.test()$conf.int — confirming the formula and the built-in function agree, and showing explicitly what t.test() computes behind the scenes.

title: 132  Confidence Intervals
text:
132.5 Interpret the Result
You can now say something more precise than “the mean systolic blood pressure was 129.84”: you’re 95% confident the true population mean lies somewhere between 126.02 and 133.67 mmHg. This is a meaningfully different, more honest statement than reporting the single point estimate alone — it explicitly communicates the uncertainty that comes from having only 96 participants rather than the entire population.

Common Mistakes


Interpreting a 95% CI as “95% probability the true mean is in this range.” The more precise interpretation: if you repeated this sampling process many times, about 95% of the resulting confidence intervals would contain the true population mean — a subtle, but real, distinction from a probability statement about this one specific interval.

Assuming a narrower interval always means a better estimate. A narrower interval usually comes from a larger sample size, not necessarily a “better” one — comparing intervals from very different sample sizes without accounting for this can be misleading.

Forgetting confidence intervals depend on the same normality assumptions checked on Page 1. For very small samples with heavily skewed data, t.test()’s confidence interval can become less reliable — always sanity-check the underlying assumptions, not just run the function blindly.

title: 132  Confidence Intervals
text:
132.6 Practice Problem
Compute a 95% confidence interval for bmi’s mean using t.test(). Does the interval contain the value 27 (roughly the boundary between “Normal” and “Overweight,” Level 4’s case_when() categories)?

title: 132  Confidence Intervals
text:
132.7 Challenge Problem
Compute confidence intervals for age’s mean at three different confidence levels (90%, 95%, 99%) using t.test(age, conf.level = ...). Describe the pattern in how the interval’s width changes as the confidence level increases, and explain why that pattern makes sense.

title: 132  Confidence Intervals
text:
132.8 Key Takeaways

A confidence interval expresses a range of plausible values for a population parameter, built from a sample estimate and its standard error.

t.test(x)$conf.int computes this directly; the same interval can be built manually as mean ± critical_value × SE.
A higher confidence level produces a wider interval — there’s an inherent trade-off between how confident you are and how precise (narrow) the range can be.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does a 95% confidence interval represent?

There is a 95% probability the sample mean is correct
A range of plausible values for the population parameter, with a procedure that captures it about 95% of the time across repeated sampling
95% of the individual data points fall within this range
The interval within which all future samples must fall


True or false. A higher confidence level (say, 99% instead of 95%) always produces a narrower interval.
Predict the output. If the sample mean is 129.84 and the 95% CI is (126.02, 133.67), would a 90% CI be wider or narrower than this range?

Find the error.
# "There's a 95% chance the true population mean is 129.84."



Show answers


b) A range of plausible values for the population parameter, with a procedure that captures the true value about 95% of the time across repeated sampling.

False. A higher confidence level requires a wider interval to maintain that higher level of confidence, not a narrower one.
Narrower — a lower confidence level (90%) requires a smaller range to achieve that lower degree of confidence.
This statement misinterprets both the point estimate and the confidence interval — the sample mean (129.84) is a single computed value, not a probabilistic claim, and the correct interpretation of a 95% CI is about the interval-generating procedure capturing the true mean across repeated sampling, not a 95% probability attached to any single specific value.

title: 133  The One-Sample t-Test
text:
133.1 The R Concept
A one-sample t-test compares a sample’s mean against a specific hypothesized value (mu), producing a p-value: the probability of observing a difference at least this large, purely by chance, if the true population mean actually equaled the hypothesized value:
t.test(x, mu = hypothesized_value)
A small p-value (conventionally, below 0.05) suggests the observed difference is unlikely to be due to chance alone — evidence against the hypothesized value being the true population mean.

title: 133  The One-Sample t-Test
text:
133.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sbp <- participants$systolic_bp[!is.na(participants$systolic_bp)]

t.test(sbp, mu = 120)


    One Sample t-test

data:  sbp
t = 5.111, df = 95, p-value = 1.655e-06
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 120
95 percent confidence interval:
 126.0201 133.6674
sample estimates:
mean of x
 129.8438

title: 133  The One-Sample t-Test
text:
133.3 Explore
Notice the confidence interval reported here (126.02 to 133.67) is exactly the same interval from Page 3 — t.test() always reports both a hypothesis test and a confidence interval together, since they’re built from the same underlying calculation. Try t.test(sbp, mu = 130) instead — since 130 falls inside the confidence interval, you’d expect a much larger p-value (failing to reject that particular hypothesized value).

title: 133  The One-Sample t-Test
text:
133.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sbp <- participants$systolic_bp[!is.na(participants$systolic_bp)]

test_result <- t.test(sbp, mu = 120)

test_result$statistic

       t
5.110964

test_result$p.value

[1] 1.655098e-06
The t-statistic (5.11) is large, and the p-value (0.0000017, far below 0.05) is extremely small — strong evidence that this sample’s mean genuinely differs from 120, not merely due to random chance.

title: 133  The One-Sample t-Test
text:
133.5 Interpret the Result
The sample’s mean systolic blood pressure (129.84) is statistically significantly different from the reference value of 120 (p < 0.001) — consistent with the fact that 120 doesn’t even fall inside the 95% confidence interval (126.02 to 133.67) computed on Page 3. This is genuine evidence, not just a numeric coincidence: a sample mean this far from 120, in a sample of 96, would be extremely unlikely to occur by chance alone if the true population mean actually were 120.

Common Mistakes


Confusing statistical significance with practical (clinical) importance. A p-value this small confirms the difference from 120 is unlikely to be chance — but whether a roughly 10 mmHg difference is clinically meaningful is a separate, non-statistical judgment that depends on domain knowledge, not just the p-value.

Treating p < 0.05 as a magic threshold rather than a convention. 0.05 is a widely used convention, not a law of nature — a p-value of 0.049 and 0.051 represent nearly identical evidence, despite falling on opposite sides of this threshold.

Forgetting the confidence interval and the p-value are two views of the same information. If a hypothesized value falls outside the confidence interval (as 120 does here), the corresponding t-test will always produce p < 0.05 — the two are mathematically linked, not independent pieces of evidence.

title: 133  The One-Sample t-Test
text:
133.6 Practice Problem
Test whether mean bmi differs significantly from 25 (the conventional upper boundary of “Normal” BMI, Level 4). Report the t-statistic, p-value, and your conclusion.

title: 133  The One-Sample t-Test
text:
133.7 Challenge Problem
Test whether mean age differs significantly from 45. Then check whether 45 falls inside or outside the 95% confidence interval for age’s mean (Page 3’s practice problem) — confirm the two approaches (hypothesis test and confidence interval) lead to the same conclusion.

title: 133  The One-Sample t-Test
text:
133.8 Key Takeaways

A one-sample t-test compares a sample mean against a specific hypothesized value, producing a p-value indicating how likely such a difference would be purely by chance.
A small p-value (conventionally < 0.05) is evidence against the hypothesized value; it does not by itself indicate the size or practical importance of the difference.
A hypothesized value falling outside a confidence interval and a t-test producing p < 0.05 always agree — they’re two expressions of the same underlying calculation.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does the p-value from t.test(sbp, mu = 120) represent?

The probability the true mean is exactly 120
The probability of observing a difference this large purely by chance, if the true mean were actually 120
The sample mean itself
The percentage of participants with blood pressure above 120


True or false. A statistically significant result automatically means the difference is large enough to matter clinically.
Predict the output. If a hypothesized value falls inside a variable’s 95% confidence interval, would you expect its corresponding one-sample t-test to produce p < 0.05 or p ≥ 0.05?
Short answer. Why is treating p = 0.05 as a rigid pass/fail cutoff considered poor statistical practice?


Show answers


b) The probability of observing a difference this large purely by chance, if the true population mean were actually 120.

False. Statistical significance indicates the difference is unlikely to be due to chance — it says nothing about whether that difference is large enough to matter practically or clinically.
p ≥ 0.05 — a hypothesized value inside the confidence interval indicates the data is consistent with that value, corresponding to a non-significant test result.
Because 0.05 is an arbitrary convention, not a meaningful boundary in the underlying evidence — a p-value of 0.049 and 0.051 reflect nearly identical strength of evidence, so treating one as “significant” and the other as “not significant” overstates the importance of a fairly arbitrary line.

title: 134  The Two-Sample t-Test
text:
134.1 The R Concept
A two-sample t-test compares the means of two independent groups, testing whether their difference is larger than what chance alone would typically produce:
t.test(numeric_variable ~ grouping_variable, data = df)
By default, R’s t.test() uses Welch’s t-test, which doesn’t assume the two groups have equal variance — a safer default than the classic “Student’s t-test,” which does assume equal variance.

title: 134  The Two-Sample t-Test
text:
134.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

t.test(systolic_bp ~ smoker, data = participants)


    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  systolic_bp by smoker
t = -0.21305, df = 41.106, p-value = 0.8323
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group No and group Yes is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -9.750700  7.889589
sample estimates:
mean in group No mean in group Yes
         129.6111          130.5417

title: 134  The Two-Sample t-Test
text:
134.3 Explore
Notice the 95% confidence interval for the difference in means (-9.75 to 7.89) contains 0 — meaning “no difference at all” is well within the range of plausible values. Compare this to Page 4’s one-sample test, where the hypothesized value (120) fell outside the confidence interval — here, the hypothesized “no difference” value falls comfortably inside.

title: 134  The Two-Sample t-Test
text:
134.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

test_result <- t.test(systolic_bp ~ smoker, data = participants)

test_result$p.value

[1] 0.8323397

test_result$estimate

mean in group No mean in group Yes
         129.6111          130.5417
A p-value of 0.83 is very large — far above any conventional significance threshold — indicating essentially no evidence that smoking status is associated with a real difference in mean systolic blood pressure in this sample.

title: 134  The Two-Sample t-Test
text:
134.5 Interpret the Result
The roughly one-point difference in means (130.54 vs. 129.61) is entirely consistent with random sampling variation — not evidence of a real underlying relationship between smoking status and blood pressure in this dataset. This matches Level 8’s visual finding exactly: the two groups’ points were thoroughly intermixed on the scatter plot, with no visible separation, and the formal test now confirms that visual impression numerically.

Common Mistakes


Treating a non-significant result as proof there’s no effect at all. A large p-value (0.83) means this sample provides no strong evidence of a difference — it does not prove smoking has zero true effect on blood pressure; a larger, more carefully designed study could still detect a real but small effect this sample was too limited to find.

Ignoring unequal group sizes when interpreting results. With only 24 smokers versus 72 non-smokers, this test has less power to detect a small real difference than if both groups were equally large — a non-significant result here is somewhat less conclusive than the same result from two equally sized, larger groups.

Using the classic equal-variance t-test by default instead of Welch’s. R’s default (t.test() with no var.equal argument) already uses the safer Welch’s version — explicitly setting var.equal = TRUE (the classic Student’s t-test) requires justifying the equal-variance assumption first, which shouldn’t be assumed without checking.

title: 134  The Two-Sample t-Test
text:
134.6 Practice Problem
Test whether mean bmi differs significantly between smokers and non-smokers. Compare your p-value to this page’s blood pressure result — is the evidence for a smoking-BMI relationship stronger or weaker?

title: 134  The Two-Sample t-Test
text:
134.7 Challenge Problem
Test whether mean age differs significantly between males and females (age ~ sex). Report the confidence interval for the difference, and state in a full sentence (as you would in a report) what the result means, following the “not proof of zero effect” caution from this page’s Common Mistakes.

title: 134  The Two-Sample t-Test
text:
134.8 Key Takeaways

A two-sample t-test compares two independent groups’ means, testing whether their observed difference exceeds what chance alone would plausibly produce.
R’s default t.test() uses Welch’s t-test, which doesn’t assume equal variance between groups — a safer default than the classic equal-variance version.
A non-significant result (large p-value) means the sample lacks strong evidence of a difference — it does not prove no real difference exists at all.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does t.test(systolic_bp ~ smoker, data = participants) test?

Whether systolic_bp is normally distributed
Whether the mean systolic_bp differs between smokers and non-smokers
Whether smoker predicts systolic_bp using regression
The correlation between systolic_bp and smoker



True or false. A p-value of 0.83 proves that smoking has no effect on blood pressure whatsoever.
Predict the output. If a confidence interval for a difference in means contains 0, would you expect the corresponding p-value to be above or below 0.05?
Short answer. Why might unequal group sizes (24 smokers vs. 72 non-smokers) make it harder to detect a real but modest difference between groups?


Show answers


b) Whether the mean systolic_bp differs between smokers and non-smokers.

False. A large p-value indicates a lack of strong evidence for a difference in this particular sample — it does not prove the true difference is exactly zero.
Above 0.05 — a confidence interval containing 0 (no difference) corresponds to a non-significant result.
Statistical power to detect a real difference depends heavily on sample size in each group — with only 24 smokers, there’s more sampling variability in that group’s mean than in the 72 non-smokers, making a modest true difference harder to distinguish reliably from chance.

title: 135  The Paired t-Test
text:
135.1 The R Concept
A paired t-test analyzes the differences between two measurements taken on the same individuals, testing whether the mean difference is significantly different from zero:
t.test(measurement_time1, measurement_time2, paired = TRUE)
The paired = TRUE argument is essential — without it, R would (incorrectly) treat the two measurements as independent groups (Page 5’s test), losing the crucial information that each pair of values comes from the same participant.

title: 135  The Paired t-Test
text:
135.2 Demonstration

library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

# Keep only participants with both visit 1 and visit 3 recorded
complete_visits <- bp_wide[bp_wide$sbp_visit3 != "", ]

t.test(complete_visits$sbp_visit1, complete_visits$sbp_visit3, paired = TRUE)


    Paired t-test

data:  complete_visits$sbp_visit1 and complete_visits$sbp_visit3
t = 3.3639, df = 25, p-value = 0.002479
alternative hypothesis: true mean difference is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 1.938809 8.061191
sample estimates:
mean difference
              5

title: 135  The Paired t-Test
text:
135.3 Explore
Try running the same two visit-1 and visit-3 values through Page 5’s unpaired t.test(..., paired = FALSE) instead. Notice the p-value changes — a paired test is typically more powerful when the measurements are genuinely linked (the same person’s own before-and-after change), since it removes person-to-person variability that has nothing to do with the change itself.

title: 135  The Paired t-Test
text:
135.4 Solve the Problem

bp_wide <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

complete_visits <- bp_wide[bp_wide$sbp_visit3 != "", ]

paired_result <- t.test(complete_visits$sbp_visit1, complete_visits$sbp_visit3, paired = TRUE)

paired_result$estimate

mean difference
              5

paired_result$p.value

[1] 0.002479479
Among the 26 participants with complete visit 1 and visit 3 data, blood pressure dropped by an average of exactly 5 points — and this drop is highly statistically significant (p = 0.0025), providing strong evidence this isn’t just random fluctuation.

title: 135  The Paired t-Test
text:
135.5 Interpret the Result
Unlike Page 5’s smoking comparison (p = 0.83, no significant difference), this paired result shows genuine statistical evidence: the average 5-point systolic blood pressure decrease from visit 1 to visit 3, among the same 26 participants, is very unlikely to have arisen purely by chance. This formally confirms what Level 6’s reshaping analysis and Level 8’s line plot both suggested visually and numerically — a real within-person improvement across the study’s visits.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting paired = TRUE. Running t.test(complete_visits$sbp_visit1, complete_visits$sbp_visit3) without paired = TRUE incorrectly analyzes the two visits as independent groups, discarding the fact that each visit-1 value is linked to a specific visit-3 value from the same participant — this typically produces a different (and less appropriately powered) result.

Including participants with a missing visit in a paired test. A paired test requires a complete pair for every included participant — the 2 participants who dropped out before visit 3 (Level 6) must be excluded, exactly as complete_visits does here by filtering first.

Confusing “statistically significant improvement” with “clinically meaningful improvement.” A 5-point average drop is statistically well-established here — but whether a 5 mmHg change matters clinically is a separate, domain-specific judgment, echoing Page 4’s significance-versus-importance distinction.

title: 135  The Paired t-Test
text:
135.6 Practice Problem
Run a paired t-test comparing sbp_visit1 and sbp_visit2 (rather than sbp_visit3) for all 28 participants (no filtering needed, since neither visit 1 nor visit 2 has any missing values in this dataset). Is the visit 1-to-visit 2 change also statistically significant?

title: 135  The Paired t-Test
text:
135.7 Challenge Problem
Compare the paired t-test’s p-value (visit 1 vs. visit 3) to what you’d get treating the same two columns as an unpaired two-sample comparison (Page 5’s approach, ignoring the pairing). Which test produces stronger evidence of a difference, and explain why pairing typically increases a test’s statistical power when measurements are genuinely linked.

title: 135  The Paired t-Test
text:
135.8 Key Takeaways

A paired t-test analyzes within-individual differences between two linked measurements, testing whether the mean difference significantly differs from zero.

paired = TRUE is essential — omitting it treats linked measurements as independent groups, losing statistical power and answering a different question.
Pairing typically increases a test’s power to detect a real effect, since it removes person-to-person variability unrelated to the actual change being measured.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. When is a paired t-test the appropriate choice over a two-sample t-test?

When comparing two entirely different groups of people
When comparing two measurements taken on the same individuals
When the sample size is very large
Never — they are always interchangeable


True or false. Omitting paired = TRUE when comparing two linked measurements will still produce a mathematically valid, equally appropriate result.
Predict the output. If every one of the 26 participants had exactly the same 5-point blood pressure drop (no variation at all), would you expect the p-value to be smaller or larger than what was actually found?
Short answer. Why must participants with a missing visit 3 be excluded from this paired analysis, rather than simply included with a missing value?


Show answers


b) When comparing two measurements taken on the same individuals.

False. Omitting paired = TRUE treats the two sets of measurements as independent groups, discarding the linkage between each pair and typically changing (and often weakening) the statistical result.
Smaller — with zero variation in the difference across participants, the evidence for a genuine, consistent effect would be even stronger, producing a smaller p-value than the actual result (which has some participant-to-participant variability in the size of the drop).
A paired test requires a complete pair (both visit 1 and visit 3) for every included participant — a participant with a missing visit 3 has no difference to compute at all, so they cannot contribute a valid pair to the analysis.

title: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence
text:
136.1 The R Concept
The chi-square test of independence compares a cross-tabulation’s observed counts against the counts you’d expect if the two variables were completely unrelated, producing a p-value:
chisq.test(table(var1, var2))
By default, R applies a continuity correction (Yates’ correction) for 2×2 tables, which slightly adjusts the test to be more conservative for small samples.
crumbs: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence

title: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence
text:
136.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sex_smoker_table <- table(participants$sex, participants$smoker)
sex_smoker_table

        No Yes
Female  42  10
Male    33  15

chisq.test(sex_smoker_table)


    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  sex_smoker_table
X-squared = 1.3355, df = 1, p-value = 0.2478
crumbs: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence

title: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence
text:
136.3 Explore
Try chisq.test(sex_smoker_table)$expected to see the counts that would be expected if sex and smoking status were completely independent: roughly 39 female non-smokers and 13 female smokers (compared to the actual 42 and 10). The chi-square statistic measures exactly how far the observed counts (42, 10, 33, 15) deviate from these expected ones.
crumbs: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence

title: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence
text:
136.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sex_smoker_table <- table(participants$sex, participants$smoker)

test_result <- chisq.test(sex_smoker_table)

test_result$statistic

X-squared
 1.335470

test_result$p.value

[1] 0.2478344

test_result$expected

        No Yes
Female  39  13
Male    36  12
A p-value of 0.248 is well above the conventional 0.05 threshold — despite the 12-percentage-point descriptive gap Level 7 found, this chi-square test provides no strong statistical evidence that sex and smoking status are genuinely associated in this sample.
crumbs: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence

title: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence
text:
136.5 Interpret the Result
This is a genuinely important, humbling result: Level 7’s 19.2%-versus-31.2% gap looked substantial when described in percentages, but with only 52 women and 48 men, that gap is well within the range chance alone could plausibly produce. This is exactly why Level 7 cautioned against treating a cross-tabulation’s pattern as a confirmed finding — the formal test now confirms that caution was warranted, not just a reflexive disclaimer.

Common Mistakes


Treating a large descriptive gap (12 percentage points) as automatically meaningful. Percentage-point differences can look dramatic without accounting for sample size — a chi-square test (or any formal significance test) directly addresses whether the gap is larger than chance would typically produce, not just whether it looks big.

Applying a chi-square test to cell counts that are too small. chisq.test() can become unreliable when expected cell counts are very small (a common rule of thumb: below 5) — always check $expected before trusting the result; here, all expected counts (39, 13, 36, 12) are comfortably above that threshold.

Confusing “not statistically significant” with “definitely no association exists.” As with the t-tests earlier in this level, a non-significant chi-square result means this sample doesn’t provide strong evidence of an association — it doesn’t prove sex and smoking are truly unrelated in the broader population this sample represents.
crumbs: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence

title: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence
text:
136.6 Practice Problem
Run a chi-square test on county and smoker (a larger, 5×2 table). Check $expected first to confirm no expected cell count falls below 5, then interpret the resulting p-value.
crumbs: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence

title: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence
text:
136.7 Challenge Problem
Compare chisq.test(sex_smoker_table) (with Yates’ correction, R’s default) to chisq.test(sex_smoker_table, correct = FALSE) (without it). How much does the p-value change? Research why Yates’ correction tends to matter more for smaller samples and 2×2 tables specifically.
crumbs: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence

title: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence
text:
136.8 Key Takeaways

The chi-square test of independence compares observed cross-tabulation counts against what would be expected under complete independence, producing a p-value.
A descriptive percentage-point gap can look substantial while still being entirely consistent with chance, especially in smaller samples — always follow up a descriptive cross-tabulation (Level 7) with a formal test before drawing conclusions.
Check $expected cell counts before trusting a chi-square result — very small expected counts (conventionally below 5) can make the test unreliable.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does the chi-square test of independence evaluate?

Whether two numeric variables are correlated
Whether two categorical variables are associated, beyond what chance would produce
Whether a sample mean differs from a reference value
Whether a variable is normally distributed


True or false. A large descriptive percentage-point gap between two groups always corresponds to a statistically significant chi-square test result.
Predict the output. Given a p-value of 0.248 for the sex-smoking chi-square test, would you conclude there’s strong evidence of an association?
Short answer. Why is it important to check expected cell counts before trusting a chi-square test’s result?


Show answers


b) Whether two categorical variables are associated, beyond what chance would produce.

False. As shown here, a 12-percentage-point gap produced a non-significant result (p = 0.248) — the size of a descriptive gap and its statistical significance depend heavily on sample size, and don’t always align.
No — a p-value of 0.248 is well above the conventional 0.05 threshold, indicating no strong evidence of an association in this sample.
The chi-square test’s approximation becomes less reliable when expected cell counts are very small (conventionally below 5) — trusting a result built on unreliable expected counts risks drawing an incorrect conclusion from a test that wasn’t appropriate for that data in the first place.
crumbs: 136  The Chi-Square Test of Independence

title: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance
text:
137.1 The R Concept
cor.test() tests whether a correlation coefficient is significantly different from zero, producing both a p-value and a confidence interval — exactly the same hypothesis-testing logic as the t-tests earlier in this level, applied to a correlation instead of a mean:
cor.test(x, y)
crumbs: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance

title: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance
text:
137.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

cor.test(participants$bmi, participants$systolic_bp)


    Pearson's product-moment correlation

data:  participants$bmi and participants$systolic_bp
t = 1.5596, df = 89, p-value = 0.1224
alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.04432528  0.35705926
sample estimates:
      cor
0.1631085
crumbs: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance

title: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance
text:
137.3 Explore
Notice the 95% confidence interval for the correlation (-0.044 to 0.357) contains 0 — meaning “no correlation at all” remains a plausible value given this data. Compare this to a hypothetical situation where the interval didn’t contain 0 at all (as you’ll see with a different variable pair in the Solve section) — that would indicate a statistically significant relationship.
crumbs: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance

title: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance
text:
137.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bmi_bp_test <- cor.test(participants$bmi, participants$systolic_bp)
sbp_dbp_test <- cor.test(participants$systolic_bp, participants$diastolic_bp)

bmi_bp_test$p.value

[1] 0.1223918

sbp_dbp_test$p.value

[1] 4.119909e-40

sbp_dbp_test$estimate

      cor
0.9203873
The BMI-blood pressure correlation (0.16) is not statistically significant (p = 0.122) — consistent with Level 8’s plot showing no clear visual trend. The systolic-diastolic correlation (0.92), by contrast, is overwhelmingly significant (p is a vanishingly small number, effectively zero for practical purposes — R’s printed test output would show this as p-value < 2.2e-16), exactly as expected for two blood pressure readings taken from the same measurement.
crumbs: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance

title: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance
text:
137.5 Interpret the Result
This confirms, formally, what Level 7 and Level 8 already suggested visually and numerically: the weak BMI-blood pressure relationship in this sample (r = 0.16) is not statistically distinguishable from no relationship at all, given only 91 data points. The much stronger systolic-diastolic relationship (r = 0.92), on the other hand, is statistically significant beyond any reasonable doubt — a useful contrast for recognizing what a genuinely strong, clearly non-zero correlation’s test result looks like.

Common Mistakes


Interpreting cor()’s raw coefficient alone as sufficient evidence. A correlation of 0.16, by itself, says nothing about statistical significance — Level 7 intentionally stopped short of this conclusion, since cor() alone cannot tell you whether the true population correlation could plausibly be zero.

Assuming a non-significant correlation of 0.16 and an even weaker one of 0.05 provide identical evidence. They don’t necessarily — sample size matters enormously; a correlation of 0.16 in a much larger sample could easily be statistically significant, while the same 0.16 here (n=91) is not.

Forgetting cor.test() assumes a roughly linear relationship (Pearson correlation). If two variables have a strong curved (non-linear) relationship, Pearson’s correlation and its significance test can both understate the true strength of that relationship — always look at a scatter plot (Level 8) alongside any correlation test.
crumbs: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance

title: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance
text:
137.6 Practice Problem
Run cor.test() on age and bmi. Is this correlation statistically significant? Compare the confidence interval to the BMI-blood pressure result above.
crumbs: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance

title: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance
text:
137.7 Challenge Problem
Run cor.test() on age and systolic_bp (Level 7 found r = -0.22). Determine whether this correlation is statistically significant, and compare its p-value and confidence interval to the BMI-blood pressure test in this page’s Solve section. Which relationship has stronger statistical evidence behind it, despite both being described as “weak” by Level 7’s rough correlation-strength guide?
crumbs: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance

title: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance
text:
137.8 Key Takeaways


cor.test(x, y) tests whether a correlation coefficient is statistically significantly different from zero, providing both a p-value and a confidence interval.
A weak-looking correlation (like 0.16) can still be reported alongside its significance test — a small coefficient and a non-significant result often go together, but sample size determines exactly where that boundary falls.
Always pair a correlation test with a scatter plot (Level 8) — Pearson correlation and its significance test both assume a roughly linear relationship.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does cor.test() add beyond what cor() (Level 7) provides?

A different correlation coefficient
A p-value and confidence interval for the correlation
A scatter plot of the two variables
Nothing — they are identical


True or false. A correlation coefficient of 0.16 is always statistically significant, regardless of sample size.
Predict the output. If a correlation’s 95% confidence interval is (-0.044, 0.357), would you expect the corresponding p-value to be above or below 0.05?
Short answer. Why is it possible for two “weak” correlations (say, 0.16 and -0.22) to differ in whether they’re statistically significant?


Show answers


b) A p-value and confidence interval for the correlation, indicating whether it’s statistically distinguishable from zero.

False. Statistical significance depends on both the correlation’s size and the sample size — the same coefficient can be significant in a larger sample and non-significant in a smaller one.
Above 0.05 — a confidence interval containing 0 corresponds to a non-significant result.
Statistical significance for a correlation depends on both its magnitude and the sample size (and specifically the resulting t-statistic, which combines both) — two correlations of similar rough magnitude can still produce different p-values if their underlying sample sizes differ, or if one happens to sit closer to the boundary of significance than the other.
crumbs: 137  Testing a Correlation for Significance

title: 138  Simple Linear Regression with lm()
text:
138.1 The R Concept
lm() (“linear model”) fits a straight-line equation predicting one numeric variable from another:
model <- lm(outcome ~ predictor, data = df)
summary(model)
The fitted equation takes the form \(\hat{y} = \text{intercept} + \text{slope} \times x\). summary() reports the intercept and slope (each with their own standard error, t-statistic, and p-value), plus overall model statistics: R-squared (the proportion of variance in the outcome explained by the predictor) and an overall F-statistic with its p-value.

title: 138  Simple Linear Regression with lm()
text:
138.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_age_model <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = participants)

summary(bp_age_model)


Call:
lm(formula = systolic_bp ~ age, data = participants)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max
-36.251 -14.104  -1.394  13.232  43.949

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 142.1669     6.4332  22.099  < 2e-16 ***
age          -0.2762     0.1299  -2.127   0.0363 *
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 18.7 on 88 degrees of freedom
  (10 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.04888,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.03807
F-statistic: 4.522 on 1 and 88 DF,  p-value: 0.03626

title: 138  Simple Linear Regression with lm()
text:
138.3 Explore
Try coef(bp_age_model) to extract just the intercept and slope directly, and confint(bp_age_model) for their 95% confidence intervals. Notice age’s confidence interval doesn’t contain 0 — consistent with its p-value (0.036) being below 0.05.

title: 138  Simple Linear Regression with lm()
text:
138.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_age_model <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = participants)

coef(bp_age_model)

(Intercept)         age
 142.166864   -0.276224

summary(bp_age_model)$r.squared

[1] 0.04887722
The fitted equation is predicted blood pressure = 142.17 − 0.276 × age: for every additional year of age, predicted systolic blood pressure decreases by about 0.276 mmHg, on average. The slope’s p-value (0.036) is just below 0.05 — statistically significant, though the model’s R-squared (0.049) shows age alone explains under 5% of the variation in blood pressure across participants.

title: 138  Simple Linear Regression with lm()
text:
138.5 Interpret the Result
Age has a statistically significant, but practically modest, linear relationship with systolic blood pressure in this sample: a 20-year age difference predicts only about a 5.5 mmHg difference in blood pressure (20 × 0.276), and the vast majority of blood-pressure variation (95%+) is explained by factors other than age alone. This is a genuinely useful, honest way to report the finding — significant, but far from the whole story, exactly consistent with Level 7’s “weak” correlation label and Level 8’s subtle trend line.

Common Mistakes


Reporting the slope’s significance without also reporting R-squared. A statistically significant slope (p = 0.036) doesn’t mean age is a strong predictor — R-squared (0.049) shows age explains less than 5% of blood pressure’s variation, an essential piece of context a p-value alone doesn’t convey.

Interpreting the intercept (142.17) as a meaningful real-world prediction. The intercept represents predicted blood pressure at age 0 — a nonsensical age for this dataset (Level 4’s realistic age range starts at 18) — the intercept here is a mathematical anchor for the line, not a meaningful standalone prediction.

Forgetting lm() silently drops rows with missing values. The output notes “10 observations deleted due to missingness” — age and systolic_bp’s combined missingness (Level 3-4) reduced the usable sample from 100 to 90, a detail easy to overlook if you don’t read the model’s fine print.

title: 138  Simple Linear Regression with lm()
text:
138.6 Practice Problem
Fit lm(systolic_bp ~ bmi, data = participants) and compare its slope, p-value, and R-squared to the age model above. Which predictor has a larger R-squared?

title: 138  Simple Linear Regression with lm()
text:
138.7 Challenge Problem
Fit lm(systolic_bp ~ diastolic_bp, data = participants), given Level 7’s much stronger correlation (0.92) between these two variables. Compare this model’s R-squared to the age model’s — does a much stronger correlation translate into a much higher R-squared, and can you explain the mathematical relationship between a simple linear regression’s R-squared and its predictor’s correlation coefficient?

title: 138  Simple Linear Regression with lm()
text:
138.8 Key Takeaways


lm(outcome ~ predictor, data = df) fits a straight-line equation; summary() reports the intercept, slope, their significance, and overall model R-squared.
A statistically significant slope doesn’t imply a practically strong predictor — always report R-squared alongside significance to convey how much variation is actually explained.

lm() silently drops rows with missing values in any variable used in the model — always check the reported sample size against your expectations.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In predicted blood pressure = 142.17 − 0.276 × age, what does the −0.276 represent?

The predicted blood pressure at age 0
The change in predicted blood pressure for each additional year of age
The correlation between age and blood pressure
The R-squared of the model


True or false. A statistically significant slope automatically means the predictor explains most of the outcome’s variation.
Predict the output. If R-squared is 0.049, approximately what percentage of blood pressure’s variation is explained by age alone?

Find the error.
# "Age is a strong predictor of blood pressure since its slope is statistically significant (p = 0.036)."



Show answers


b) The change in predicted blood pressure for each additional year of age (the slope).

False. Statistical significance indicates the slope is unlikely to be exactly zero — it says nothing about how much of the outcome’s total variation the predictor actually explains (that’s what R-squared measures).
About 4.9%.
This statement conflates statistical significance with predictive strength — a p-value of 0.036 indicates the slope is unlikely to be exactly zero, but the model’s R-squared (0.049) shows age explains under 5% of blood pressure’s variation, meaning it is a statistically detectable but practically weak predictor, not a “strong” one.

title: 139  One-Way ANOVA
text:
139.1 The R Concept
One-way ANOVA (“analysis of variance”) tests whether the means of three or more groups differ significantly, using a single overall test rather than multiple pairwise comparisons:
model <- aov(numeric_variable ~ grouping_variable, data = df)
summary(model)
ANOVA compares how much variation exists between group means to how much variation exists within each group — producing an F-statistic and a single overall p-value.

title: 139  One-Way ANOVA
text:
139.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_county_model <- aov(systolic_bp ~ county, data = participants)

summary(bp_county_model)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
county       4    783   195.7   0.539  0.708
Residuals   91  33048   363.2

title: 139  One-Way ANOVA
text:
139.3 Explore
Try TukeyHSD(bp_county_model) — a follow-up test that compares every pair of counties individually, while adjusting for the fact that multiple comparisons are being made. This is the statistically appropriate way to answer “which specific counties differ,” rather than running 10 separate, unadjusted two-sample t-tests.

title: 139  One-Way ANOVA
text:
139.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_county_model <- aov(systolic_bp ~ county, data = participants)

summary(bp_county_model)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
county       4    783   195.7   0.539  0.708
Residuals   91  33048   363.2

summary(lm(systolic_bp ~ county, data = participants))$r.squared

[1] 0.02313655
The F-statistic (0.539) is small, and the p-value (0.708) is far above 0.05 — no statistically significant evidence that mean systolic blood pressure differs across the five counties. County membership explains only about 2.3% of the variation in blood pressure (R-squared = 0.023).

title: 139  One-Way ANOVA
text:
139.5 Interpret the Result
Despite Level 5’s grouped means ranging from 124.36 to 133.30 — a roughly 9-point spread that might look meaningful in a table — the ANOVA shows this spread is entirely consistent with random sampling variation across five groups of modest size (16 to 26 participants each). This doesn’t mean county is irrelevant to blood pressure in the broader population; it means this particular sample doesn’t provide strong statistical evidence of a real county-level difference.

Common Mistakes


Running multiple unadjusted two-sample t-tests instead of one ANOVA. Testing every pair of 5 counties individually (10 tests) substantially increases the chance of at least one false-positive result appearing purely by chance — ANOVA’s single overall test avoids this problem; TukeyHSD() handles pairwise follow-up comparisons correctly if needed afterward.

Assuming a non-significant ANOVA means every group is identical. It means the overall test found no significant difference across all groups combined — a follow-up test could still reveal one specific pair with a real difference, though TukeyHSD() would be needed to check this properly rather than assumed from the overall ANOVA result alone.

Confusing ANOVA’s F-statistic with a t-test’s t-statistic. They’re related (an F-statistic here is testing multiple groups simultaneously) but not directly comparable in magnitude — always interpret the p-value, not the raw statistic’s size, when judging significance.

title: 139  One-Way ANOVA
text:
139.6 Practice Problem
Run a one-way ANOVA testing whether bmi differs across the five counties. Report the F-statistic, p-value, and R-squared, and compare to the blood pressure result above.

title: 139  One-Way ANOVA
text:
139.7 Challenge Problem
Run TukeyHSD(bp_county_model) and examine the adjusted p-value for the Millbrook-Riverdale comparison specifically (the two counties with the largest raw mean difference, Level 5). Is this specific pairwise comparison statistically significant after the multiple-comparison adjustment, even though the overall ANOVA was not?

title: 139  One-Way ANOVA
text:
139.8 Key Takeaways

One-way ANOVA tests whether three or more groups’ means differ significantly, using a single overall F-test rather than multiple pairwise comparisons.
A non-significant ANOVA result means the sample lacks strong evidence of an overall difference across groups — it doesn’t rule out every possible pairwise difference without a proper follow-up test like TukeyHSD().
Running many unadjusted pairwise t-tests instead of one ANOVA inflates the risk of a false-positive finding purely from the number of comparisons made.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. When is one-way ANOVA the appropriate choice over a two-sample t-test?

When comparing exactly two groups
When comparing three or more groups’ means simultaneously
When testing a correlation coefficient
When the outcome variable is categorical


True or false. Running 10 separate two-sample t-tests to compare 5 groups pairwise is statistically equivalent to running one ANOVA.
Predict the output. Given an ANOVA p-value of 0.708, would you conclude blood pressure significantly differs across the five counties?
Short answer. Why does running many pairwise comparisons instead of one overall test increase the risk of a false-positive finding?


Show answers


b) When comparing three or more groups’ means simultaneously.

False. Multiple unadjusted pairwise tests inflate the overall chance of at least one false-positive result — ANOVA’s single test (or a properly adjusted follow-up like TukeyHSD()) accounts for this in a way that separate, unadjusted t-tests do not.
No — a p-value of 0.708 is far above 0.05, indicating no statistically significant evidence of a difference across counties.
Each individual test carries some chance (conventionally 5%) of a false-positive result purely by chance — running many such tests compounds this risk, making it increasingly likely that at least one test shows “significance” purely due to chance, even if no real differences exist anywhere.

title: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome
text:
140.1 The R Concept
glm() (“generalized linear model”) with family = binomial fits a logistic regression — a model for a binary (0/1) outcome that always predicts a valid probability between 0 and 1:
model <- glm(outcome_binary ~ predictor1 + predictor2, data = df, family = binomial)
summary(model)
Logistic regression coefficients are on a “log-odds” scale, which is hard to interpret directly — exponentiating them (exp(coef(model))) converts them into odds ratios, a much more interpretable quantity: an odds ratio above 1 means higher values of the predictor are associated with higher odds of the outcome; below 1 means lower odds.
crumbs: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome

title: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome
text:
140.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(participants$smoker == "Yes")

smoker_model <- glm(smoker_binary ~ age + bmi, data = participants, family = binomial)

summary(smoker_model)


Call:
glm(formula = smoker_binary ~ age + bmi, family = binomial,
    data = participants)

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept) -0.913984   1.596931  -0.572    0.567
age          0.008919   0.016554   0.539    0.590
bmi         -0.023321   0.052168  -0.447    0.655

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 99.543  on 88  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 99.072  on 86  degrees of freedom
AIC: 105.07
crumbs: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome

title: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome
text:
140.3 Explore
Try exp(coef(smoker_model)) to convert the coefficients into odds ratios. An odds ratio near 1.00 (for either age or bmi) indicates that predictor has little association with the odds of being a smoker — very different from an odds ratio of, say, 2.0 (doubled odds) or 0.5 (halved odds).
crumbs: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome

title: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome
text:
140.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(participants$smoker == "Yes")

smoker_model <- glm(smoker_binary ~ age + bmi, data = participants, family = binomial)

exp(coef(smoker_model))

(Intercept)         age         bmi
  0.4009245   1.0089595   0.9769487

exp(confint(smoker_model))

                 2.5 %    97.5 %
(Intercept) 0.01698651 8.9573952
age         0.97654895 1.0430263
bmi         0.88132002 1.0821256
Neither age (OR = 1.009, 95% CI: 0.977-1.043) nor bmi (OR = 0.977, 95% CI: 0.881-1.082) has an odds ratio confidence interval excluding 1 — both intervals comfortably contain 1.0, meaning neither predictor shows a statistically significant association with smoking status in this sample.
crumbs: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome

title: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome
text:
140.5 Interpret the Result
Neither age nor BMI meaningfully predicts smoking status in this sample: both odds ratios sit very close to 1.00 (1.009 and 0.977), and both p-values (0.590 and 0.655) are far from significant. This is consistent with everything found earlier in the course — Level 7’s cross-tabulations and Page 7’s chi-square test both suggested weak, non-significant relationships involving smoking status, and this logistic regression confirms the same conclusion using two predictors simultaneously rather than one categorical comparison at a time.

Common Mistakes


Interpreting logistic regression coefficients directly, without exponentiating first. The raw coefficients (like age’s 0.0089) are on a log-odds scale, not directly interpretable as “probability change per unit” — exp(coef(model)) is required to get the more interpretable odds ratio.

Forgetting family = binomial is required. Without it, glm() defaults to ordinary linear regression (equivalent to lm()), which is inappropriate for a 0/1 outcome and can predict nonsensical values like -0.3 or 1.4 as a “probability.”

Treating a non-significant model as proof age and BMI have no relationship to smoking whatsoever, in any population. As with every non-significant result in this level, this only means this particular sample of 89 participants doesn’t provide strong evidence of an association — not a universal claim about the true, broader relationship.
crumbs: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome

title: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome
text:
140.6 Practice Problem
Fit a logistic regression predicting smoker_binary from age alone (no bmi). Compare its age coefficient and p-value to the two-predictor model above — does adding bmi change age’s estimated association much?
crumbs: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome

title: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome
text:
140.7 Challenge Problem
Fit a logistic regression predicting a hypertension binary outcome (systolic_bp >= 130, Level 4’s threshold) from age and bmi. Compare the resulting odds ratios and p-values to the smoker model in this page — does either predictor show a stronger (or weaker) association with hypertension than with smoking status?
crumbs: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome

title: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome
text:
140.8 Key Takeaways


glm(outcome ~ predictors, data = df, family = binomial) fits a logistic regression for a binary (0/1) outcome, always predicting a valid probability between 0 and 1.
Logistic regression coefficients are on a log-odds scale — exp(coef(model)) converts them into odds ratios, a far more interpretable quantity.
An odds ratio’s 95% confidence interval containing 1.0 indicates no statistically significant association, exactly analogous to a correlation’s confidence interval containing 0 (Page 8).


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Why is lm() inappropriate for predicting a binary (0/1) outcome directly?


lm() cannot handle numeric predictors

lm() can predict values outside the valid 0-to-1 probability range

lm() requires exactly one predictor
There is no actual problem with using lm() this way


True or false. An odds ratio of exactly 1.00 indicates no association between the predictor and the outcome’s odds.
Predict the output. If a predictor’s odds ratio 95% confidence interval is (0.88, 1.08), would you conclude that predictor is statistically significantly associated with the outcome?

Find the error.
# "The age coefficient of 0.0089 means each additional year increases the probability of smoking by 0.89%."



Show answers


b) lm() can predict values outside the valid 0-to-1 probability range, which makes no sense for a binary outcome.

True. An odds ratio of exactly 1.00 means the predictor has no association with the odds of the outcome.
No — a confidence interval containing 1.0 (as (0.88, 1.08) does) indicates no statistically significant association.
Raw logistic regression coefficients are on a log-odds scale, not a direct percentage-probability scale — the coefficient must first be exponentiated (exp(0.0089) ≈ 1.009) to get the odds ratio, and even then, an odds ratio doesn’t translate directly into a simple percentage-point change in probability the way this statement implies.
crumbs: 140  Logistic Regression for a Binary Outcome

title: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course’s Earlier Findings
text:
141.1 The R Concept
Choosing the right test depends entirely on the type of variables involved:
# Two categorical variables -> chi-square test
chisq.test(table(categorical1, categorical2))

# One numeric, one numeric (linear relationship) -> correlation test or regression
cor.test(numeric1, numeric2)
lm(numeric1 ~ numeric2)
Sex and smoking status are both categorical — a chi-square test (Page 7) is the right tool. Age and blood pressure are both numeric — a correlation test (Page 8) or simple linear regression (Page 9) are both appropriate, and (as Page 9’s challenge problem hinted) mathematically equivalent for a single predictor.
crumbs: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course's Earlier Findings

title: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course’s Earlier Findings
text:
141.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Finding 1: sex and smoking status (Level 7's cross-tabulation)
sex_smoker_table <- table(participants$sex, participants$smoker)
chisq.test(sex_smoker_table)

# Finding 2: age and blood pressure (Level 8's trend line)
cor.test(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp)


    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  sex_smoker_table
X-squared = 1.3355, df = 1, p-value = 0.2478


    Pearson's product-moment correlation

data:  participants$age and participants$systolic_bp
t = -2.1266, df = 88, p-value = 0.03626
alternative hypothesis: true correlation is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.4094278 -0.0146621
sample estimates:
       cor
-0.2210819
crumbs: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course's Earlier Findings

title: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course’s Earlier Findings
text:
141.3 Explore
Notice the two p-values point in opposite directions: 0.248 (not significant) for sex-and-smoking, but 0.036 (significant) for age-and-blood-pressure. Both were originally reported in this course as comparably “modest” descriptive patterns — only the formal tests reveal that one holds up statistically and the other doesn’t.
crumbs: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course's Earlier Findings

title: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course’s Earlier Findings
text:
141.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sex_smoker_test <- chisq.test(table(participants$sex, participants$smoker))
age_bp_test <- cor.test(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp)

sex_smoker_test$p.value

[1] 0.2478344

age_bp_test$p.value

[1] 0.03625678

age_bp_test$estimate

       cor
-0.2210819
Finding 1 (sex and smoking status): not statistically significant (p = 0.248). Despite a 12-percentage-point descriptive gap, this sample of 52 women and 48 men doesn’t provide strong evidence of a true association between sex and smoking status.
Finding 2 (age and blood pressure): statistically significant (p = 0.036, r = -0.221). Older participants do show a modest but statistically real tendency toward lower systolic blood pressure in this sample — confirmed by both the correlation test here and the regression slope test on Page 9.
crumbs: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course's Earlier Findings

title: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course’s Earlier Findings
text:
141.5 Interpret the Result
This side-by-side verdict is exactly the kind of honest, complete conclusion a real analysis should reach: one of Level 7 and Level 8’s two earlier patterns turned out to be statistically robust (age and blood pressure), while the other — despite looking similarly notable in a plot or percentage table — did not hold up (sex and smoking status). Neither conclusion is a “failure”: correctly identifying which patterns are statistically supported and which aren’t is precisely the value this entire level adds on top of Levels 5, 7, and 8’s descriptive and visual work.

Common Mistakes


Reporting only the significant finding and quietly dropping the non-significant one. A complete, honest analysis reports both results — a non-significant finding is still a real, useful piece of information (it tells you a suspected pattern isn’t well-supported by this data), not something to omit because it’s less exciting.

Assuming a smaller p-value always means a “more important” finding. The age-blood pressure relationship (p = 0.036, r = -0.221) is statistically significant but still numerically weak (R-squared under 5%, Page 9) — statistical significance and practical importance remain two separate questions, exactly as emphasized throughout this level.

Forgetting that “significant” and “not significant” are both provisional conclusions from one particular sample. A larger, independently collected sample could, in principle, find sex and smoking status significantly associated after all, or fail to replicate the age-blood pressure finding — every result in this level describes this specific sample of 100 participants, not an eternal truth about the wider population.
crumbs: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course's Earlier Findings

title: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course’s Earlier Findings
text:
141.6 Practice Problem
Choose one more descriptive finding from earlier in this course (for example, Level 5’s blood pressure ranking by county, or Level 8’s BMI-by-county faceted histogram) and select the appropriate formal test from this level to check whether it holds up statistically.
crumbs: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course's Earlier Findings

title: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course’s Earlier Findings
text:
141.7 Challenge Problem
Write a short paragraph (as if for the final section of a real report) summarizing both findings from this capstone: state each result, its statistical significance (or lack of it), its effect size (correlation or percentage-point gap), and an honest, appropriately cautious interpretation of what both findings do and don’t establish about this dataset.
crumbs: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course's Earlier Findings

title: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course’s Earlier Findings
text:
141.8 Key Takeaways

Choosing the correct statistical test depends on variable type: chi-square for two categorical variables, correlation/regression for two numeric variables, t-tests for comparing means across groups.
A complete analysis reports both significant and non-significant results honestly — omitting inconvenient non-significant findings misrepresents the evidence.
Statistical significance and practical/clinical importance remain distinct: a significant result can still describe a weak relationship, and every conclusion in this level is provisional to this specific sample of 100 participants.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Which test correctly evaluates whether sex and smoking status (both categorical) are associated?

A t-test
A chi-square test
A correlation test
Simple linear regression


True or false. Both of this course’s earlier descriptive findings (sex-smoking, age-blood pressure) turned out to be statistically significant when formally tested.
Predict the output. Given a p-value of 0.036 for the age-blood pressure correlation, would you conclude this relationship is statistically significant at the conventional 0.05 threshold?
Short answer. Why is it important to report a non-significant finding (like the sex-smoking result) rather than omitting it from a final report?


Show answers


b) A chi-square test — the appropriate tool for testing association between two categorical variables.

False. Only the age-blood pressure relationship (p = 0.036) was statistically significant; the sex-smoking association (p = 0.248) was not.
