Yes — 0.036 is below the conventional 0.05 threshold, indicating statistical significance.
Omitting non-significant findings misrepresents the true state of evidence — a reader deserves to know that a plausible-looking descriptive pattern (like the sex-smoking gap) did not hold up to formal testing, just as much as they deserve to know which patterns did.
crumbs: 141  Level 11 Capstone: Testing This Course's Earlier Findings

title: Level 12 — Advanced Data Analysis
text: What this level covers

title: Level 12 — Advanced Data Analysis
text:
Fitting multiple regression with more than one numeric predictor at once
Using a categorical variable (like county) directly as a regression predictor, and reading its dummy-coded coefficients
Interaction terms — testing whether one predictor’s effect depends on the value of another
Confounding and adjustment — checking whether a predictor’s effect changes once another variable is controlled for
Formally comparing nested models with anova() and AIC, instead of eyeballing R-squared
Basic regression diagnostics: checking residual normality and constant variance
Multicollinearity — what it is, how to detect it, and why it matters
Making predictions for new, unobserved data with predict()


A preview mission
Level 11’s simple regression found age predicts systolic blood pressure (Page 9), on its own. Your mission across this level: build up a fuller model — age, BMI, and smoking status together — and find out whether age’s effect survives once those other variables are accounted for, or whether it was confounded all along.







A note on scope



This level focuses on the multiple-regression toolkit every analyst reaches for first: multiple predictors, categorical predictors, interactions, confounding, diagnostics, and prediction — all built on the same 100-participant dataset used throughout this course. Topics like multiple imputation, propensity score matching, survey weighting, mixed-effects models, and time-to-event (survival) analysis are genuinely important in public health research, but each requires either specialized data this course’s dataset doesn’t have (repeated survey waves, censored event times) or a level of mathematical background beyond this course’s scope. Consider this level a solid on-ramp to those topics, not the final word on them.

title: 142  Multiple Regression with lm()
text:
142.1 The R Concept
Adding a second predictor to lm() is as simple as joining it with +:
model <- lm(outcome ~ predictor1 + predictor2, data = df)
summary(model)
Each coefficient in a multiple regression is now interpreted holding the other predictor(s) constant — the age coefficient represents the predicted change in the outcome per year of age, for participants with the same BMI. This is a subtly different (and usually more honest) interpretation than a simple regression’s coefficient, which does not account for anything else.

title: 142  Multiple Regression with lm()
text:
142.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_age_bmi_model <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi, data = participants)

summary(bp_age_bmi_model)


Call:
lm(formula = systolic_bp ~ age + bmi, data = participants)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max
-33.454 -12.720  -1.673  10.982  44.223

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 123.1658    13.4226   9.176  3.21e-14 ***
age          -0.3052     0.1337  -2.283    0.0250 *
bmi           0.7345     0.4400   1.669    0.0989 .
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 18.36 on 82 degrees of freedom
  (15 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.08657,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.06429
F-statistic: 3.886 on 2 and 82 DF,  p-value: 0.02439

title: 142  Multiple Regression with lm()
text:
142.3 Explore
Try comparing this model’s R-squared (0.087) to Page 9’s age-only model (0.049, on a slightly different sample of 90 due to bmi’s own missing values). Adding BMI roughly doubled the explained variation — but notice the sample size also dropped, since lm() drops any row missing either age, bmi, or systolic_bp.

title: 142  Multiple Regression with lm()
text:
142.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_age_bmi_model <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi, data = participants)

coef(bp_age_bmi_model)

(Intercept)         age         bmi
123.1658246  -0.3052224   0.7345009

summary(bp_age_bmi_model)$r.squared
summary(bp_age_bmi_model)$adj.r.squared

[1] 0.08656528
[1] 0.06428639
The fitted equation is predicted blood pressure = 123.17 − 0.305 × age + 0.735 × bmi: holding BMI constant, each additional year of age predicts about 0.305 mmHg lower systolic blood pressure; holding age constant, each additional BMI unit predicts about 0.735 mmHg higher systolic blood pressure. Age remains statistically significant (p = 0.025); BMI is not quite significant at the conventional 0.05 threshold (p = 0.099).

title: 142  Multiple Regression with lm()
text:
142.5 Interpret the Result
Age’s coefficient barely changed once BMI joined the model (−0.305 here vs. −0.276 in Page 9’s simple model, on a smaller matched sample) — a first hint that BMI isn’t strongly confounding the age-blood-pressure relationship in this data (Page 4 investigates this properly). The model overall explains more variation than age alone (R-squared 0.087 vs. 0.049), though both predictors together still leave over 90% of blood pressure’s variation unexplained — entirely consistent with blood pressure being influenced by many factors this course’s dataset doesn’t capture.

Common Mistakes


Interpreting a multiple regression coefficient the same way as a simple regression coefficient. In lm(bp ~ age + bmi), age’s coefficient means “the effect of age holding BMI constant” — not “the overall effect of age ignoring everything else,” which is what Page 9’s simple model estimated instead.

Not noticing the sample size changed. Adding a predictor with its own missing values (bmi has 5 missing entries) can shrink the usable sample further than a single-predictor model — always check “observations deleted due to missingness” in the output before comparing models.

Assuming more predictors always means a better model. R-squared can only stay the same or increase as predictors are added (Page 5 shows why adjusted R-squared, and a formal test, are the honest way to judge whether a new predictor earns its place).

title: 142  Multiple Regression with lm()
text:
142.6 Practice Problem
Fit lm(systolic_bp ~ age + diastolic_bp, data = participants). Given Level 7’s very strong correlation (0.92) between systolic and diastolic blood pressure, do you expect diastolic_bp’s coefficient to be large or small, and its p-value to be significant or not?

title: 142  Multiple Regression with lm()
text:
142.7 Challenge Problem
Fit a three-predictor model: lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + diastolic_bp, data = participants). Compare the age and bmi coefficients here to the two-predictor model above — do they change much once diastolic_bp also joins the model? This previews Page 7’s multicollinearity discussion, since systolic and diastolic blood pressure are very strongly correlated with each other.

title: 142  Multiple Regression with lm()
text:
142.8 Key Takeaways


lm(outcome ~ predictor1 + predictor2, data = df) fits a multiple regression; each coefficient represents that predictor’s effect holding the other predictor(s) constant.
Adding predictors can change both the estimated coefficients and the usable sample size (due to each predictor’s own missing values) — always check both.
A higher R-squared with more predictors doesn’t automatically mean a meaningfully better model — Page 5 covers the formal way to test this.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi), what does the coefficient on age represent?

The overall correlation between age and blood pressure
The effect of age on blood pressure, holding BMI constant
The effect of BMI on blood pressure, holding age constant
The combined effect of age and BMI together


True or false. Adding a second predictor to a regression model can only increase (or leave unchanged) the model’s R-squared, never decrease it.
Predict the output. If a model drops from n = 90 (one predictor) to n = 85 (two predictors), what is the most likely explanation?

Find the error.
# "Age's coefficient is -0.305 in the two-predictor model, meaning age alone
#  explains a 0.305 mmHg drop in blood pressure per year, regardless of BMI."



Show answers


b) The effect of age on blood pressure, holding BMI constant.

True. R-squared is mathematically guaranteed to never decrease when a predictor is added (even a useless one) — this is exactly why adjusted R-squared and formal model comparison tests (Page 5) matter.
The second predictor (bmi) likely has its own missing values in rows that were previously usable when only age was in the model — lm() drops any row missing a value for any variable used in the model.
This statement is backwards — the coefficient of −0.305 explicitly is age’s effect while accounting for (not ignoring) BMI; describing it as “regardless of BMI” would actually describe the simple regression’s coefficient (Page 9’s −0.276), not this multiple regression’s.

title: 143  Categorical Predictors in Regression
text:
143.1 The R Concept
When a character or factor column is used as an lm() predictor, R automatically converts it into dummy variables: one reference category (alphabetically first, by default) is “baked into” the intercept, and every other category gets its own coefficient representing its difference from that reference category.
model <- lm(numeric_outcome ~ categorical_variable, data = df)
summary(model)
With 5 counties, R creates 4 dummy coefficients (not 5) — the reference county’s mean is exactly the model’s intercept.

title: 143  Categorical Predictors in Regression
text:
143.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_county_lm <- lm(systolic_bp ~ county, data = participants)

summary(bp_county_lm)


Call:
lm(formula = systolic_bp ~ county, data = participants)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max
-32.158 -12.658  -1.158  11.877  46.929

Coefficients:
                 Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)      128.1579     4.3719  29.314  < 2e-16 ***
countyMillbrook    5.1421     6.1051   0.842    0.402
countyOakford      1.5480     6.3621   0.243    0.808
countyRiverdale   -3.8008     6.7122  -0.566    0.573
countySpringvale   3.3036     5.7517   0.574    0.567
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 19.06 on 91 degrees of freedom
  (4 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.02314,   Adjusted R-squared: -0.0198
F-statistic: 0.5388 on 4 and 91 DF,  p-value: 0.7076

title: 143  Categorical Predictors in Regression
text:
143.3 Explore
Notice Cedar Falls never appears as its own coefficient — it’s alphabetically first, so R used it as the reference level, and its mean (128.16) is the intercept. Try levels(factor(participants$county)) to confirm which category R would treat as the reference.

title: 143  Categorical Predictors in Regression
text:
143.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_county_lm <- lm(systolic_bp ~ county, data = participants)

coef(bp_county_lm)

     (Intercept)  countyMillbrook    countyOakford  countyRiverdale countySpringvale
      128.157895         5.142105         1.547988        -3.800752         3.303644

# Reconstruct Millbrook's predicted mean from the coefficients
coef(bp_county_lm)["(Intercept)"] + coef(bp_county_lm)["countyMillbrook"]

[1] 133.3

summary(bp_county_lm)$fstatistic

    value    numdf    dendf
0.5388230 4.0000000 91.0000000
Cedar Falls’ mean (128.16, the intercept) plus Millbrook’s coefficient (5.14) reconstructs exactly Millbrook’s raw mean from Level 5’s group_by(county) table (133.30) — the dummy coefficients are nothing more than “difference from the reference county.” The overall F-statistic (0.539, p = 0.708) exactly matches Level 11’s aov() result, because a one-way ANOVA is a regression on a categorical predictor.

title: 143  Categorical Predictors in Regression
text:
143.5 Interpret the Result
None of the four county coefficients are statistically significant (all p-values well above 0.05), and the overall F-test confirms no significant difference in blood pressure across counties overall — identical conclusions to Level 11’s ANOVA, just reached through lm()’s dummy-coding lens instead. This equivalence is not a coincidence: aov() is, mathematically, a special case of lm() for exactly this kind of categorical-predictor question.

Common Mistakes


Treating a categorical coefficient like a numeric one. countyMillbrook’s coefficient (5.14) doesn’t mean “each additional unit of Millbrook adds 5.14 mmHg” — categories aren’t numeric, so this coefficient exclusively means “Millbrook’s mean is 5.14 higher than the reference county’s mean.”

Forgetting the reference category is invisible in the coefficient table. Cedar Falls has no row of its own — its information is folded entirely into the intercept, which is easy to miss if you only skim the coefficient names.

Assuming R always picks the reference level you’d want. R defaults to alphabetical order, which may not be the most clinically meaningful baseline — relevel(factor(county), ref = "Riverdale") (Level 2’s factor concept) would let you choose a different, more meaningful reference county instead.

title: 143  Categorical Predictors in Regression
text:
143.6 Practice Problem
Fit lm(bmi ~ county, data = participants) and identify which county (if any) has a statistically significant BMI difference from the reference category.

title: 143  Categorical Predictors in Regression
text:
143.7 Challenge Problem
Use relevel(factor(participants$county), ref = "Riverdale") inside a new lm(systolic_bp ~ relevel(factor(county), ref = "Riverdale"), data = participants) call. Do the individual coefficients change? Does the overall F-statistic and p-value change? Explain why one changes and the other doesn’t.

title: 143  Categorical Predictors in Regression
text:
143.8 Key Takeaways

A categorical predictor in lm() is automatically dummy-coded: one reference level becomes the intercept, and every other level gets a coefficient representing its difference from that reference.
A one-way ANOVA (Level 11, Page 10) and a regression on a single categorical predictor are mathematically the same test — aov()’s F-statistic and lm()’s overall F-statistic match exactly.
The choice of reference level changes individual coefficients’ interpretation, but never changes the model’s overall fit or F-test.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. With 5 categories in a predictor, how many dummy coefficients does R create (beyond the intercept)?

5
4
1
0


True or false. The reference category’s mean value is not shown anywhere in the coefficient table.
Predict the output. If the intercept is 128.16 and countySpringvale’s coefficient is 3.30, what is Springvale’s predicted mean blood pressure?
Short answer. Why do a one-way ANOVA and a regression on a single categorical predictor produce the exact same F-statistic and p-value?


Show answers


b) 4 — one category is always absorbed into the intercept as the reference.

False. The reference category’s mean is shown — it’s exactly the intercept value.
128.16 + 3.30 = 131.46.
Both methods partition the same total variation into “between-group” and “within-group” components and test the same null hypothesis (all group means are equal) — ANOVA and single-categorical-predictor regression are two notations for the same underlying statistical model.

title: 144  Interaction Terms
text:
144.1 The R Concept
An interaction term, written predictor1 * predictor2 in an lm() formula, tests whether one predictor’s effect depends on another’s value:
model <- lm(outcome ~ predictor1 * predictor2, data = df)
summary(model)
x1 * x2 is shorthand for x1 + x2 + x1:x2 — it includes both main effects and their interaction. The interaction coefficient (x1:x2) is the key number: if it’s statistically significant, x1’s effect genuinely differs depending on x2’s value.

title: 144  Interaction Terms
text:
144.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_interaction_model <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age * smoker, data = participants)

summary(bp_interaction_model)


Call:
lm(formula = systolic_bp ~ age * smoker, data = participants)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max
-35.858 -13.412  -1.198  12.677  44.500

Coefficients:
                  Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept)       144.8631     7.7593  18.669  < 2e-16 ***
smokerYes          -8.6409    14.2813  -0.605    0.547
age                -0.3352     0.1586  -2.114    0.037 *
age:smokerYes       0.1859     0.2823   0.659    0.512
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 18.62 on 86 degrees of freedom
  (10 observations deleted due to missingness)
Multiple R-squared:  0.05368,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.02067
F-statistic: 1.626 on 3 and 86 DF,  p-value: 0.1892

title: 144  Interaction Terms
text:
144.3 Explore
Try computing the predicted slope for smokers directly: age’s coefficient (−0.335) plus age:smokerYes’s coefficient (0.186) gives the smokers’ age slope (−0.149), while non-smokers keep the plain age coefficient (−0.335) as their slope. Compare how different those two slopes really are.

title: 144  Interaction Terms
text:
144.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

bp_interaction_model <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age * smoker, data = participants)

coef(bp_interaction_model)["age"]                                        # non-smoker slope
coef(bp_interaction_model)["age"] + coef(bp_interaction_model)["age:smokerYes"]  # smoker slope

       age
-0.3352289
      age
-0.1493318

summary(bp_interaction_model)$coefficients["age:smokerYes", "Pr(>|t|)"]

[1] 0.5119558
The interaction term’s p-value (0.512) is far from significant — despite the two slopes looking numerically different (−0.335 for non-smokers vs. −0.149 for smokers), this sample doesn’t provide strong statistical evidence that age’s effect on blood pressure genuinely differs by smoking status.

title: 144  Interaction Terms
text:
144.5 Interpret the Result
The interaction term isn’t statistically significant, meaning this data doesn’t support the idea that aging affects blood pressure differently for smokers versus non-smokers — the apparent slope difference (−0.335 vs. −0.149) is plausibly just sampling noise around one true, shared age effect. This is a useful negative result: it justifies Page 1’s simpler model (one shared age slope for everyone) over a more complicated one that assumes smokers and non-smokers age differently.

Common Mistakes


Interpreting the main effect of age alone as “the” age effect once an interaction is in the model. Once age:smoker is included, plain age’s coefficient specifically means “the age slope for the reference group (non-smokers)” — not an overall age effect averaged across everyone.

Adding an interaction term without first checking if it’s significant. A non-significant interaction (like this one, p = 0.512) should usually be dropped in favor of the simpler main-effects-only model (Page 1) — keeping insignificant interactions needlessly complicates the model without improving it.

Confusing “the two slopes look numerically different” with “the difference is statistically significant.” −0.335 and −0.149 aren’t identical numbers, but the interaction test (p = 0.512) shows this sample can’t distinguish that difference from pure chance.

title: 144  Interaction Terms
text:
144.6 Practice Problem
Fit lm(systolic_bp ~ bmi * smoker, data = participants). Is BMI’s effect on blood pressure significantly different between smokers and non-smokers?

title: 144  Interaction Terms
text:
144.7 Challenge Problem
Fit lm(bmi ~ age * sex, data = participants), testing whether age’s relationship with BMI differs by sex. Report the interaction term’s coefficient and p-value, and explain in plain language what a significant (or non-significant) result would mean.

title: 144  Interaction Terms
text:
144.8 Key Takeaways


predictor1 * predictor2 in an lm() formula tests whether one predictor’s effect on the outcome depends on the other predictor’s value.
A significant interaction term means the two group-specific slopes are meaningfully different; a non-significant one supports using a simpler, shared-slope model instead.
Once an interaction term is in a model, a main effect’s coefficient describes that predictor’s effect only for the reference level of the other variable, not an overall effect.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does a significant age:smoker interaction coefficient indicate?

Age and smoking are correlated with each other
Age’s effect on the outcome differs depending on smoking status
Smoking has no effect on the outcome
The model has too many predictors


True or false. x1 * x2 in an lm() formula includes both main effects and their interaction.
Predict the output. If age’s coefficient is −0.335 and age:smokerYes’s coefficient is 0.186, what is the estimated age slope specifically for smokers?
Short answer. Why should a non-significant interaction term usually be dropped in favor of a simpler main-effects-only model?


Show answers


b) Age’s effect on the outcome differs depending on smoking status.

True. x1 * x2 is shorthand for x1 + x2 + x1:x2.
−0.335 + 0.186 = −0.149.
A non-significant interaction means the data doesn’t support the extra complexity of separate slopes for each group — keeping it would make the model harder to interpret without any real gain in explanatory power, and Page 5 shows how to confirm this formally with a nested model comparison.

title: 145  Confounding and Adjustment
text:
145.1 The R Concept
There’s no special R function called confounding() — checking for confounding is a comparison, done by fitting two models on the exact same rows and watching whether the predictor of interest’s coefficient shifts meaningfully:
model_unadjusted <- lm(outcome ~ exposure, data = df)
model_adjusted   <- lm(outcome ~ exposure + potential_confounder, data = df)
If the exposure’s coefficient changes substantially between these two models, the added variable was likely confounding the relationship. A common rule of thumb (not a strict statistical test) is a change of more than about 10%.

title: 145  Confounding and Adjustment
text:
145.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

# Keep only rows with no missing age, bmi, or systolic_bp, so both
# models below are fit on the identical set of participants
complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "systolic_bp")]), ]

model_unadjusted <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = complete_rows)
model_adjusted   <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi, data = complete_rows)

coef(model_unadjusted)["age"]
coef(model_adjusted)["age"]

       age
-0.2982686
       age
-0.3052224

title: 145  Confounding and Adjustment
text:
145.3 Explore
Try computing the percent change between the two age coefficients: (coef(model_adjusted)["age"] - coef(model_unadjusted)["age"]) / coef(model_unadjusted)["age"] * 100. A small percent change (under about 10%) suggests weak or no confounding by BMI.

title: 145  Confounding and Adjustment
text:
145.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "systolic_bp")]), ]

model_unadjusted <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = complete_rows)
model_adjusted   <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi, data = complete_rows)

age_unadjusted <- coef(model_unadjusted)["age"]
age_adjusted   <- coef(model_adjusted)["age"]

percent_change <- (age_adjusted - age_unadjusted) / age_unadjusted * 100
percent_change

     age
2.330989

nrow(complete_rows)

[1] 85
Age’s coefficient moved from −0.298 (unadjusted) to −0.305 (adjusted for BMI) — a change of only about 2.3%, well under the common 10% rule-of-thumb threshold for meaningful confounding. Both models here were fit on the exact same 85 complete-case participants, making this a fair, apples-to-apples comparison.

title: 145  Confounding and Adjustment
text:
145.5 Interpret the Result
BMI does not appear to meaningfully confound the age-blood-pressure relationship in this sample: age’s effect estimate is essentially unchanged whether or not BMI is in the model. This means age’s modest, statistically significant relationship with blood pressure (Level 11, Page 9) is not simply an artifact of older participants also happening to have higher or lower BMI — it holds up on its own, independent of BMI.

Common Mistakes


Comparing coefficients from models fit on different samples. If the unadjusted model uses n = 90 and the adjusted model uses n = 85 (Page 1’s naive comparison), some of the coefficient change could come from the different participants, not confounding — always restrict both models to the same complete.cases() subset first, exactly as done here.

Assuming any coefficient change at all means “confounding.” Coefficients almost always shift slightly between models due to ordinary sampling variability — the question is whether the change is large enough to matter (the ~10% rule of thumb), not whether it’s exactly zero.

Confusing confounding with interaction (Page 3). Confounding asks “does adjusting for this variable change the main effect estimate?” Interaction asks “does this variable change how strong the effect is for different subgroups?” These are different questions with different R code (+ vs. *).

title: 145  Confounding and Adjustment
text:
145.6 Practice Problem
Repeat this confounding check for smoker instead of bmi: does adjusting for smoking status change age’s coefficient by more than 10%?

title: 145  Confounding and Adjustment
text:
145.7 Challenge Problem
Level 7 found smoking rates differ meaningfully by sex (19.2% women vs. 31.2% men, though Level 11 Page 7’s chi-square test found this difference wasn’t statistically significant). Check whether sex confounds the relationship between smoker and systolic_bp: fit lm(systolic_bp ~ smoker, data) and lm(systolic_bp ~ smoker + sex, data) on a matched complete-case sample, and compare the smokerYes coefficient between the two models.

title: 145  Confounding and Adjustment
text:
145.8 Key Takeaways

Checking for confounding means comparing a predictor’s coefficient between an unadjusted and adjusted model, fit on the exact same rows.
A common rule of thumb: if adjusting for a variable changes the exposure’s coefficient by more than about 10%, that variable is likely a meaningful confounder.
Confounding (does the main effect estimate change?) and interaction (does the effect’s strength differ by subgroup?) are different questions, tested with different model formulas (+ vs. *).


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does it mean if a predictor’s coefficient barely changes after adjusting for a second variable?

The second variable is a strong confounder
The second variable is likely not a meaningful confounder of that relationship
The model is definitely wrong
The predictor is not statistically significant


True or false. Comparing coefficients between models fit on different sample sizes (due to different missing data) is a fair way to check for confounding.
Predict the output. If age’s coefficient is −0.298 unadjusted and −0.305 adjusted, is the percent change closer to 2% or 20%?
Short answer. What is the difference between checking for confounding and checking for an interaction?


Show answers


b) The second variable is likely not a meaningful confounder of that relationship.

False. Both models should be fit on the identical set of complete-case rows, or any coefficient difference could reflect a different sample rather than genuine confounding.
About 2% — (−0.305 − (−0.298)) / −0.298 × 100 ≈ 2.3%.
Confounding checks whether adjusting for a variable changes the main effect estimate (comparing + models); interaction checks whether a variable changes how strong the effect is across subgroups (using a * model, Page 3).

title: 146  Comparing Nested Models
text:
146.1 The R Concept
anova() applied to two nested fitted models (not a single categorical predictor, despite the same function name as Level 11 Page 10) performs a formal F-test of whether the larger model’s extra predictors improve the fit significantly:
model_small <- lm(outcome ~ x1, data = df)
model_big   <- lm(outcome ~ x1 + x2 + x3, data = df)
anova(model_small, model_big)
AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) offers a second, complementary way to compare models — it rewards better fit but penalizes extra predictors, so a lower AIC indicates a better-balanced model. Unlike anova(), AIC doesn’t require nested models, but here both approaches are used together on the same nested pair.

title: 146  Comparing Nested Models
text:
146.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

model_small <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = complete_rows)
model_big   <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)

anova(model_small, model_big)

Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: systolic_bp ~ age
Model 2: systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary
  Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)
1     83 29818
2     81 28808  2    1009.7 1.4195 0.2478

title: 146  Comparing Nested Models
text:
146.3 Explore
Try AIC(model_small, model_big) to see both models’ AIC values side by side. A lower AIC is generally preferred — but notice how close the two models’ AIC values are here, consistent with the non-significant anova() result above.

title: 146  Comparing Nested Models
text:
146.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

model_small <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = complete_rows)
model_big   <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)

comparison <- anova(model_small, model_big)
comparison$`Pr(>F)`[2]

[1] 0.2477964

AIC(model_small, model_big)

             df      AIC
model_small   3 743.3381
model_big     5 744.4100
The nested F-test’s p-value (0.248) is far from significant — adding BMI and smoking status does not significantly improve the model beyond age alone. AIC agrees: model_small’s AIC (743.34) is actually slightly lower (better) than model_big’s (744.41), meaning the simpler model is the better-balanced choice by this criterion too.

title: 146  Comparing Nested Models
text:
146.5 Interpret the Result
Both the formal nested F-test and AIC point to the same conclusion: BMI and smoking status don’t earn their place in this particular model once age is already included. This is an important, sometimes counter-intuitive result — Page 1 showed the bigger model’s raw R-squared was numerically higher (0.087 vs. 0.056), but that increase is entirely consistent with random noise once properly tested, not a genuine improvement. A smaller, simpler model is often the more honest, defensible choice when a larger model doesn’t earn its added complexity.

Common Mistakes


Comparing R-squared alone to decide between models. R-squared mechanically increases with every added predictor (Page 1) — anova()’s nested F-test and AIC both account for that extra complexity, while raw R-squared does not.

Using anova() on two models that aren’t actually nested. anova(model1, model2) only produces a valid test when one model’s predictors are a strict subset of the other’s — comparing two unrelated models this way produces a meaningless result.

Treating “not significant” as “these predictors are worthless in every context.” This result is specific to predicting blood pressure from age already in the model, on this sample — BMI and smoking status could still matter for other outcomes or in a differently specified model.

title: 146  Comparing Nested Models
text:
146.6 Practice Problem
Compare lm(systolic_bp ~ age) against lm(systolic_bp ~ age + county) (adding the categorical county predictor from Page 2) using anova(). Does county significantly improve the model beyond age alone?

title: 146  Comparing Nested Models
text:
146.7 Challenge Problem
Build three nested models: lm(bp ~ age), lm(bp ~ age + bmi), and lm(bp ~ age + bmi + diastolic_bp). Run anova(model1, model2, model3) to compare all three simultaneously in one table. Given Level 7’s very strong systolic-diastolic correlation (0.92), do you expect adding diastolic_bp to significantly improve the model?

title: 146  Comparing Nested Models
text:
146.8 Key Takeaways


anova(model_small, model_big) formally tests whether a larger model’s additional predictors (over a nested smaller model) improve the fit significantly, unlike comparing raw R-squared values.
AIC offers a complementary comparison that penalizes model complexity — a lower AIC indicates a better-balanced trade-off between fit and simplicity.
A model with a numerically higher R-squared is not necessarily a genuinely “better” model once formally tested — simpler models are often the more defensible choice.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does anova(model_small, model_big) test, when model_small’s predictors are a subset of model_big’s?

Whether the two models have the same R-squared
Whether the larger model’s additional predictors significantly improve the fit
Whether either model contains missing data
Whether the outcome variable is normally distributed


True or false. A lower AIC value indicates a better-balanced model (accounting for both fit and complexity).
Predict the output. If a nested F-test gives p = 0.248, should the additional predictors be kept in the model?
Short answer. Why is comparing raw R-squared values alone not a valid way to decide whether extra predictors are worth keeping?


Show answers


b) Whether the larger model’s additional predictors significantly improve the fit beyond the smaller, nested model.

True. AIC rewards better fit while penalizing extra predictors — a lower value indicates a more favorably balanced model.
No — a p-value of 0.248 is far above 0.05, indicating the additional predictors don’t significantly improve the model.
R-squared mechanically increases (or stays the same) every time a predictor is added, regardless of whether that predictor is genuinely useful — it provides no way to distinguish a meaningful improvement from one due to random noise, which is exactly what the nested F-test and AIC are designed to do instead.

title: 147  Regression Diagnostics
text:
147.1 The R Concept
residuals(model) extracts every observation’s prediction error. Two common diagnostic checks:
shapiro.test(residuals(model))       # tests residual normality
plot(model, which = 1)               # residuals vs. fitted values (constant variance check)
A shapiro.test() p-value above 0.05 suggests no strong evidence against normality (Level 11 Page 1’s same logic, applied to residuals instead of raw data). For constant variance (“homoscedasticity”), the Breusch-Pagan test (from the lmtest package) formalizes what a residuals-vs-fitted plot shows visually:
library(lmtest)
bptest(model)

title: 147  Regression Diagnostics
text:
147.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

model_big <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)

shapiro.test(residuals(model_big))


    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  residuals(model_big)
W = 0.98499, p-value = 0.4303

title: 147  Regression Diagnostics
text:
147.3 Explore
Try plot(model_big, which = 1) (a residuals-vs-fitted plot) and plot(model_big, which = 2) (a Q-Q plot) — the two most common visual diagnostics. A Q-Q plot with points falling roughly along the diagonal line supports the same normality conclusion the Shapiro-Wilk test gives numerically.

title: 147  Regression Diagnostics
text:
147.4 Solve the Problem

library(lmtest)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

model_big <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)

shapiro.test(residuals(model_big))$p.value
bptest(model_big)

[1] 0.4303420

    studentized Breusch-Pagan test

data:  model_big
BP = 1.262, df = 3, p-value = 0.7382
Both diagnostic tests come back reassuring: Shapiro-Wilk’s p-value (0.430) shows no significant evidence against residual normality, and Breusch-Pagan’s p-value (0.738) shows no significant evidence against constant variance. Neither test result proves the assumptions hold perfectly — but neither raises a red flag either.

title: 147  Regression Diagnostics
text:
147.5 Interpret the Result
This model’s residuals behave close to what lm() assumes: roughly normal and roughly evenly spread. That means the p-values reported throughout Pages 1-5 (for age, bmi, smoker_binary, and the model comparisons) can be reasonably trusted — if either diagnostic test had come back strongly significant instead, it would be a signal to interpret those p-values with real caution, or consider a different modeling approach entirely.

Common Mistakes


Treating a non-significant diagnostic test as definitive proof assumptions are perfectly met. shapiro.test() and bptest() failing to detect a violation isn’t the same as guaranteeing there isn’t one, especially in smaller samples (n = 85 here) where these tests have limited power to detect subtle problems.

Skipping diagnostics entirely because a model’s coefficients “look reasonable.” A model’s coefficients and p-values can look perfectly plausible even when its underlying assumptions are badly violated — diagnostics are a required step, not an optional one, for any model whose conclusions matter.

Running diagnostics but never acting on a bad result. If a diagnostic test flags a real problem (severe non-normality or non-constant variance), the appropriate response is usually to reconsider the model — a data transformation, a different type of model, or robust standard errors — not to report the flawed model’s p-values unchanged.

title: 147  Regression Diagnostics
text:
147.6 Practice Problem
Run shapiro.test() and bptest() on Page 1’s simpler two-predictor model (systolic_bp ~ age + bmi). Do both diagnostics still look reassuring?

title: 147  Regression Diagnostics
text:
147.7 Challenge Problem
Run plot(model_big, which = 1) and describe, in your own words, what an ideal residuals-vs-fitted plot looks like (in terms of the spread and pattern of points), versus what a problematic one (indicating non-constant variance) would look like instead.

title: 147  Regression Diagnostics
text:
147.8 Key Takeaways


shapiro.test(residuals(model)) checks whether a regression model’s residuals are approximately normally distributed.

bptest(model) (from the lmtest package) formally tests for constant variance (“homoscedasticity”) across the range of predicted values.
A non-significant diagnostic test result supports (but never definitively proves) that a model’s assumptions are reasonably met — always pair numeric diagnostic tests with a visual check like plot(model).


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does shapiro.test(residuals(model)) check?

Whether the predictors are correlated with each other
Whether the model’s residuals are approximately normally distributed
Whether the model has enough predictors
Whether the outcome variable has missing values


True or false. A non-significant Breusch-Pagan test result definitively proves a model has constant variance with no possible exceptions.
Predict the output. If bptest() returns a p-value of 0.738, is there strong evidence of non-constant variance in the residuals?
Short answer. Why is it important to pair a numeric diagnostic test (like shapiro.test()) with a visual check (like a Q-Q plot)?


Show answers


b) Whether the model’s residuals are approximately normally distributed.

False. A non-significant result supports, but never definitively proves, that the assumption holds — especially in smaller samples where the test has limited power.
No — a p-value of 0.738 is far above 0.05, showing no significant evidence of non-constant variance.
Numeric tests provide an overall summary, but a visual check can reveal patterns (like a specific range of values behaving oddly) that a single p-value might not fully capture — used together, they give a more complete diagnostic picture.

title: 148  Multicollinearity
text:
148.1 The R Concept
The variance inflation factor (VIF) measures how much a predictor’s coefficient variance is “inflated” due to its correlation with the other predictors in the model. It’s computed, for each predictor, by regressing it on all the other predictors and applying:
\[VIF = \frac{1}{1 - R^2}\]
where \(R^2\) comes from that auxiliary regression. A VIF near 1 indicates little correlation with other predictors (no problem); VIFs above about 5-10 are commonly flagged as concerning.
library(car)
vif(model)

title: 148  Multicollinearity
text:
148.2 Demonstration

library(car)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

model_big <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)

vif(model_big)

          age           bmi smoker_binary
     1.004025      1.001726      1.003712

title: 148  Multicollinearity
text:
148.3 Explore
Try manually reproducing age’s VIF: regress age on the other two predictors (lm(age ~ bmi + smoker_binary)), pull out that model’s R-squared, and compute 1 / (1 - r_squared). You should get the same value vif() reported.

title: 148  Multicollinearity
text:
148.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

age_aux_model <- lm(age ~ bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)
age_r_squared <- summary(age_aux_model)$r.squared

age_r_squared
1 / (1 - age_r_squared)

[1] 0.004008406
[1] 1.004025
This manually-computed VIF (1.004) matches vif()‘s reported value exactly, confirming the formula. All three predictors’ VIFs (1.004, 1.002, 1.004) sit right around 1 — essentially no multicollinearity at all between age, BMI, and smoking status in this sample.

title: 148  Multicollinearity
text:
148.5 Interpret the Result
Age, BMI, and smoking status are each close to independent of one another in this dataset — knowing a participant’s age tells you almost nothing about their BMI or smoking status, and vice versa. This means Page 1’s model coefficients can be trusted individually: each predictor’s estimated effect isn’t being distorted by overlap with the others. This would not have been true for a model including both systolic_bp and diastolic_bp as predictors of some other outcome, given their 0.92 correlation (Page 1’s challenge problem) — that combination would likely show high VIFs instead.

Common Mistakes


Confusing a predictor’s correlation with the outcome with its correlation with other predictors. VIF only measures the second kind — a predictor can be weakly related to the outcome (low R-squared contribution) while still being perfectly fine on the VIF front, and vice versa.

Assuming a low VIF for one predictor means the whole model is problem-free. VIF is computed separately for each predictor — always check every predictor’s VIF, not just one.

Panicking over any VIF above 1. A VIF of 1 means zero correlation with other predictors, which is rare in real data — commonly-used thresholds (5 or 10) allow for realistic, moderate correlation before flagging a genuine problem.

title: 148  Multicollinearity
text:
148.6 Practice Problem
Fit lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + diastolic_bp, data = participants) (Page 1’s challenge problem) and compute vif() for this model. Which predictor has the highest VIF, and does it exceed the common threshold of 5?

title: 148  Multicollinearity
text:
148.7 Challenge Problem
Level 7 found systolic and diastolic blood pressure are correlated at 0.92. Using the VIF formula from this page, calculate approximately what VIF you’d mathematically expect for two predictors correlated at exactly 0.92 in a two-predictor model (hint: for two predictors, \(R^2\) in the auxiliary regression equals the squared correlation between them). Compare your hand-calculated estimate to what vif() actually reports for the model in the practice problem above.

title: 148  Multicollinearity
text:
148.8 Key Takeaways

Multicollinearity occurs when predictors in the same model are highly correlated with each other, not with the outcome — it can make individual coefficients unstable and hard to interpret.

vif(model) (from the car package) computes the variance inflation factor for every predictor; values near 1 indicate little concern, while values above roughly 5-10 are commonly flagged.
VIF is calculated per-predictor by regressing each one on all the others in the model and applying \(VIF = 1 / (1 - R^2)\).


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does a high VIF for a predictor indicate?

That predictor is strongly correlated with the outcome variable
That predictor is highly correlated with other predictors in the same model
The model has too few predictors
The outcome variable has missing values


True or false. A VIF of exactly 1 means a predictor has zero correlation with the other predictors in the model.
Predict the output. If age, bmi, and smoker_binary all have VIFs close to 1.00, does this model show a multicollinearity problem?
Short answer. Why would including both systolic_bp and diastolic_bp as predictors in the same model (for some other outcome) likely produce high VIFs for both?


Show answers


b) That predictor is highly correlated with other predictors in the same model.

True. A VIF of 1 corresponds to an auxiliary \(R^2\) of 0 — no correlation with the other predictors at all.
No — VIFs close to 1.00 indicate essentially no multicollinearity among these three predictors.
Systolic and diastolic blood pressure are correlated at 0.92 (Level 7) — regressing either one on the other predictors (including the other blood pressure measure) would produce a very high auxiliary R-squared, and therefore a high VIF for both, since each is largely predictable from the other.

title: 149  Predicting New Data with predict()
text:
149.1 The R Concept
predict(model, newdata = ...) generates predictions for new rows of predictor values that don’t need to be part of the original dataset — as long as newdata is a data frame with the same column names as the model’s predictors.
new_patients <- data.frame(age = 50, bmi = 28, smoker_binary = c(0, 1))
predict(model, newdata = new_patients, interval = "confidence")
interval = "confidence" gives a range for the average outcome at those predictor values; interval = "prediction" gives a (necessarily wider) range for a single new individual, since an individual’s outcome is less certain than a group average.

title: 149  Predicting New Data with predict()
text:
149.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

model_big <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)

new_patients <- data.frame(age = c(50, 50), bmi = c(28, 28), smoker_binary = c(0, 1))

predict(model_big, newdata = new_patients, interval = "confidence")

       fit      lwr      upr
1 128.1298 123.2902 132.9694
2 129.5004 121.2342 137.7666

title: 149  Predicting New Data with predict()
text:
149.3 Explore
Try interval = "prediction" instead of "confidence" for the same new_patients. Compare the width of the prediction interval to the confidence interval above — the prediction interval should be noticeably wider, since it accounts for individual-level variability on top of uncertainty about the average.

title: 149  Predicting New Data with predict()
text:
149.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

model_big <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)

new_patients <- data.frame(age = c(50, 50), bmi = c(28, 28), smoker_binary = c(0, 1))

predict(model_big, newdata = new_patients, interval = "confidence")
predict(model_big, newdata = new_patients, interval = "prediction")

       fit      lwr      upr
1 128.1298 123.2902 132.9694
2 129.5004 121.2342 137.7666
       fit      lwr      upr
1 128.1298  90.2957 165.9640
2 129.5004  91.0773 167.9234
For a hypothetical 50-year-old, BMI-28 patient, the model predicts an average systolic blood pressure of 128.1 mmHg (non-smoker) or 129.5 mmHg (smoker) — both close together, consistent with smoker_binary’s non-significant coefficient (Page 1). The 95% confidence interval for the average such patient (123.3-133.0) is far narrower than the 95% prediction interval for one specific new patient (90.3-166.0) — individual blood pressure varies far more than the average does.

title: 149  Predicting New Data with predict()
text:
149.5 Interpret the Result
The two interval types answer genuinely different questions: the confidence interval says “the average 50-year-old, BMI-28 patient’s blood pressure is somewhere between about 123 and 133 mmHg,” while the prediction interval says “any one new patient matching that description could plausibly have a blood pressure anywhere from about 90 to 166 mmHg.” The enormous width of the prediction interval is an honest reflection of how much individual variation this model leaves unexplained (recall Page 1’s R-squared of only 0.087) — a clinician using this model for one specific patient should treat that wide range, not the narrower confidence interval, as the realistic uncertainty.

Common Mistakes


Reporting a confidence interval when a prediction interval is what’s actually needed. If the question is about one specific new individual (not a group average), interval = "prediction" is the honest choice — using the narrower confidence interval instead would understate how uncertain that individual’s prediction really is.

Passing newdata with different column names than the model’s predictors. predict() matches by column name — if the model used smoker_binary but newdata has a column called smoker, predict() will fail or silently ignore it, so column names must match exactly.

Extrapolating far outside the range of the original data. Predicting for an age or BMI value nowhere near what the original 85-100 participants had is far less trustworthy — the model has no real information about relationships in that unobserved range.

title: 149  Predicting New Data with predict()
text:
149.6 Practice Problem
Use predict() to compare predicted blood pressure for a 30-year-old versus a 70-year-old, both with BMI 25 and non-smoking status. How much does the predicted difference match what you’d expect from the age coefficient alone (age difference × age coefficient)?

title: 149  Predicting New Data with predict()
text:
149.7 Challenge Problem
Predict blood pressure for a hypothetical patient with an unrealistic age of 5 years old (well outside this dataset’s adult-only age range, Level 4). Does the model produce a plausible-looking prediction anyway? Explain why a model will always produce some number, even for inputs that don’t make clinical sense, and why that’s a risk when using predict() carelessly.

title: 149  Predicting New Data with predict()
text:
149.8 Key Takeaways


predict(model, newdata = df) generates predictions for new predictor combinations that don’t need to already exist in the original dataset, as long as column names match.

interval = "confidence" estimates uncertainty around the average outcome for given predictor values; interval = "prediction" estimates the (always wider) uncertainty for one specific new individual.
A model will produce a numeric prediction for any input, even nonsensical or far-out-of-range values — predict() doesn’t automatically warn about extrapolation.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What’s the key difference between a confidence interval and a prediction interval from predict()?

They are always identical
The confidence interval is for the average outcome; the prediction interval is for one new individual
The prediction interval is always narrower
Only the confidence interval uses the fitted model’s coefficients


True or false. predict() will automatically warn you if newdata contains values far outside the range of the original data.
Predict the output. Would you expect a 95% prediction interval to be wider or narrower than a 95% confidence interval for the same predicted value?
Short answer. Why might a clinician using this model for one individual patient be misled by looking only at the confidence interval instead of the prediction interval?


Show answers


b) The confidence interval estimates uncertainty for the average outcome; the prediction interval estimates uncertainty for one new individual.

False. predict() does not automatically flag extrapolation — it will silently produce a numeric prediction for any input, including implausible or out-of-range values.
Wider — a prediction interval accounts for individual-level variability on top of the uncertainty about the average, making it necessarily wider.
The confidence interval (123-133 mmHg here) looks reassuringly narrow, but it only describes the average patient — any one individual patient’s true blood pressure could reasonably fall anywhere in the much wider prediction interval (90-166 mmHg), and treating the narrow confidence interval as if it applied to a single patient would understate the real uncertainty.

title: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model
text:
150.1 The R Concept
Choosing a final model isn’t about maximizing R-squared — it’s about weighing every piece of evidence gathered so far:
# Evidence gathered across this level:
anova(model_age_only, model_age_bmi_smoker)  # Page 5: does the bigger model earn its complexity?
# confounding check (Page 4): does age's coefficient shift once bmi is added?
# interaction check (Page 3): does age's effect differ by smoker status?
# diagnostics (Page 6) and VIF (Page 7): is the simpler model's fit trustworthy?
A model earns extra predictors only when they measurably improve prediction (Page 5), aren’t already redundant with what’s in the model (Page 7), and aren’t hiding a confounding relationship that changes an existing predictor’s meaning (Page 4).
crumbs: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model

title: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model
text:
150.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

complete_rows <- participants[complete.cases(participants[, c("age", "bmi", "smoker", "systolic_bp")]), ]
complete_rows$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(complete_rows$smoker == "Yes")

model_age_only        <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = complete_rows)
model_age_bmi_smoker  <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, data = complete_rows)

anova(model_age_only, model_age_bmi_smoker)
AIC(model_age_only, model_age_bmi_smoker)

Analysis of Variance Table

Model 1: systolic_bp ~ age
Model 2: systolic_bp ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary
  Res.Df   RSS Df Sum of Sq      F Pr(>F)
1     83 29818
2     81 28808  2    1009.7 1.4195 0.2478
                     df      AIC
model_age_only        3 743.3381
model_age_bmi_smoker  5 744.4100
crumbs: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model

title: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model
text:
150.3 Explore
Review every piece of evidence gathered across this level, side by side:







Question
Page
Result



Does adding bmi + smoker significantly improve the model?
5
No (p = 0.248)


Is age’s coefficient confounded by bmi?
4
No (2.3% change, under 10% threshold)


Does age’s effect interact with smoker status?
3
No (interaction p = 0.512)


Are age, bmi, smoker multicollinear?
7
No (all VIFs ≈ 1.0)


Does the simpler model pass diagnostics?
6
Yes (Shapiro-Wilk p = 0.430, Breusch-Pagan p = 0.738)
crumbs: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model

title: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model
text:
150.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

model_final <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = participants)

summary(model_final)


Call:
lm(formula = systolic_bp ~ age, data = participants)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) 142.1669     6.4332  22.099  < 2e-16 ***
age          -0.2762     0.1299  -2.127   0.0363 *
---
Multiple R-squared:  0.04888,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.03807
F-statistic: 4.522 on 1 and 88 DF,  p-value: 0.03626
Every piece of evidence gathered this level points the same direction: the simple age-only model, exactly as fit back in Level 11 Page 9, remains the most defensible choice. BMI and smoking status neither significantly improve prediction (Page 5), nor confound age’s effect (Page 4), nor interact with age (Page 3) — and there’s no multicollinearity concern preventing them from being added if they were needed (Page 7).
crumbs: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model

title: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model
text:
150.5 Interpret the Result
This capstone’s real lesson isn’t a specific number — it’s a way of thinking. A more sophisticated model isn’t automatically a better one; every additional predictor or interaction term must earn its place with evidence, not just intuition or a marginally higher R-squared. Here, that evidence consistently favored simplicity: age alone predicts blood pressure about as well as age with BMI and smoking status combined, and the added complexity doesn’t buy any real predictive power. A good analyst reports this simpler, well-diagnosed model with confidence, rather than a more complicated one that only looks more sophisticated on the surface.

Common Mistakes


Choosing the model with the highest R-squared without checking whether the difference is meaningful. Page 1 showed R-squared always favors the bigger model — Page 5’s formal test is what actually determines whether that difference matters.

Treating “no confounding, no interaction, no significant improvement” as a disappointing result. These are genuinely useful findings — they justify keeping the simpler, more interpretable model with confidence, rather than being a failure to find something more complex.

Forgetting to check diagnostics on whichever model is ultimately chosen. A simpler model isn’t automatically exempt from the same normality and constant-variance checks (Page 6) that any model deserves before its p-values are trusted.
crumbs: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model

title: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model
text:
150.6 Practice Problem
Using this level’s full toolkit, evaluate whether county (Page 2) deserves a place in the final model: does adding it significantly improve the age-only model (via anova(), Page 5), and does it introduce any multicollinearity concern (Page 7)?
crumbs: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model

title: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model
text:
150.7 Challenge Problem
Write a short paragraph (as if for a methods section) justifying the final choice of lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = participants) as this level’s recommended model. Reference the specific evidence from at least three different pages in this level (confounding, interaction, model comparison, diagnostics, or multicollinearity) to support the decision.
crumbs: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model

title: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model
text:
150.8 Key Takeaways

A final model should be chosen using evidence — nested model comparison, confounding checks, interaction tests, diagnostics, and multicollinearity checks — not by maximizing R-squared alone.
Sometimes the most defensible final model is also the simplest one; that’s a legitimate, well-supported conclusion, not a failure to find something more sophisticated.
Every tool introduced in this level (Pages 1-8) exists to help make and justify exactly this kind of final modeling decision, on any dataset, in any future analysis.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. According to this capstone’s evidence, which model was ultimately the most defensible choice for predicting blood pressure?

The model with age, bmi, and smoker together
The model with age and county together
The simple age-only model
The model with an age-smoker interaction


True or false. A simpler model can be the more defensible choice even when a more complex model has a higher raw R-squared.
Predict the output. If a nested F-test, a confounding check, and an interaction test all fail to show any effect from adding new predictors, what should generally happen to those predictors?
Short answer. Explain, in your own words, why “the most sophisticated model” and “the best model” are not always the same thing.


Show answers


c) The simple age-only model — no evidence across this level supported adding bmi, smoker, county, or an interaction term.

True. R-squared mechanically favors more complex models (Page 1); a formal comparison (Page 5) is what determines whether that higher R-squared reflects a genuine, meaningful improvement.
They should generally be left out of the final model — added complexity is only worth keeping when there’s real evidence it improves prediction, resolves confounding, or captures a genuine interaction.
A more complex model can look more impressive (more predictors, a marginally higher R-squared) while adding no genuine predictive value and making the results harder to interpret — the “best” model is the one best supported by formal evidence and diagnostics, which is very often the simplest model that adequately explains the data.
crumbs: 150  Level 12 Capstone: Choosing a Final Model

title: Level 13 — Advanced R Programming
text: What this level covers

title: Level 13 — Advanced R Programming
text:
Environments — how R actually stores and looks up variables, and why a function’s variables normally disappear once it finishes
Closures — functions that “remember” values from where they were created, enabling reusable function factories
A first look at R’s S3 object system, for building your own structured result types
Robust error handling with tryCatch(), beyond Level 9’s stop() and warning()
Debugging tools for finding why code fails, not just that it failed
Functional programming patterns like Reduce() for combining values one at a time
Regular expressions for detecting and extracting text patterns
General string manipulation beyond what Levels 1-12 have needed
Working with dates — parsing, formatting, and calculating differences
The basic anatomy of an R package, for turning reusable functions into shareable tools


A preview mission
Your mission across this level: build a small, reusable toolkit — a function factory for flexible threshold checks, a custom result type, and an error-safe file reader — and bring it all together in a capstone that automates reading and validating a folder of clinic files, extending Level 10’s automation with real error handling.







A note on scope



This level focuses on the R-programming skills that generalize far beyond this course’s dataset: environments, closures, error handling, debugging, functional patterns, regex, strings, dates, and package structure. Deeper object-oriented systems (S4, R6) and full package publishing workflows (roxygen2 documentation generation, CRAN submission, Git/GitHub collaboration) are mentioned where relevant but are beyond this course’s scope — Level 10’s browser()-free debugging and this level’s S3 introduction give you a solid, practical foundation to build on later.

title: 151  Environments
text:
151.1 The R Concept
An environment is R’s container for variable names and their values — every time you assign x <- 5, that binding is stored in some environment. environment() reveals which one a function belongs to, and new.env() creates a brand-new, empty one:
new_environment <- new.env()
assign("count", 0, envir = new_environment)
get("count", envir = new_environment)
Your R session’s main workspace is called the global environment (globalenv()). Every time a function runs, R creates a new, temporary environment just for that call — which is exactly why a function’s local variables vanish once it returns: that temporary environment (and everything in it) is discarded.

title: 151  Environments
text:
151.2 Demonstration

counter_env <- new.env()
assign("count", 0, envir = counter_env)

get("count", envir = counter_env)

[1] 0

assign("count", get("count", envir = counter_env) + 1, envir = counter_env)
get("count", envir = counter_env)

[1] 1

exists("count", envir = globalenv())

[1] FALSE

title: 151  Environments
text:
151.3 Explore
Try ls(envir = counter_env) to list every variable name stored in counter_env — just like ls() (Level 1) lists everything in your global environment. Notice count exists inside counter_env, but exists("count") (checking the global environment by default) still returns FALSE.

title: 151  Environments
text:
151.4 Solve the Problem

threshold_env <- new.env()
assign("threshold", 140, envir = threshold_env)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

count_above_threshold <- function(values, env) {
  cutoff <- get("threshold", envir = env)
  sum(values >= cutoff, na.rm = TRUE)
}

count_above_threshold(participants$systolic_bp, threshold_env)

[1] 29

assign("threshold", 130, envir = threshold_env)
count_above_threshold(participants$systolic_bp, threshold_env)

[1] 46
Storing threshold inside threshold_env (rather than as a plain global variable) means count_above_threshold() always reads whatever value is currently stored there — changing the environment’s value from 140 to 130 changes the function’s behavior without touching the function itself, reproducing Level 4’s Stage 2 (140+) count of 29 and Stage 1-or-above (130+) count of 46.

title: 151  Environments
text:
151.5 Interpret the Result
Every function call in R creates its own temporary environment for local variables, which is discarded when the function returns — this is precisely why Level 9’s scope rules work the way they do. Explicitly creating and passing around an environment (like threshold_env here) is a more advanced technique for when you deliberately want state to persist between function calls, rather than disappearing — exactly the idea Page 2’s closures build on next.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting get() and assign() need an explicit envir argument to target a non-default environment. Without envir = threshold_env, both functions default to the current function’s local environment, not the one you intended.

Assuming a variable inside a custom environment is visible everywhere. exists("count") without specifying envir only checks the calling environment (usually global) by default — a variable stored inside counter_env is invisible to a plain exists("count") call.

Creating a new environment when a simple return value would do. Most of the time, a function should simply return() a value (Level 9) rather than reach for an environment — environments are for the specific cases (like closures, Page 2) where genuinely persistent, mutable state is needed.

title: 151  Environments
text:
151.6 Practice Problem
Create a new environment called bmi_env, store a cutoff value of 30 inside it, and write a function that counts how many participants’ bmi exceeds that cutoff using get(..., envir = bmi_env).

title: 151  Environments
text:
151.7 Challenge Problem
Modify the count_above_threshold() function so that, in addition to returning the count, it also updates a last_count variable inside the same environment using assign(). Call the function twice with different threshold values and show that get("last_count", envir = threshold_env) reflects the most recent call each time.

title: 151  Environments
text:
151.8 Key Takeaways

An environment is R’s container for variable name-to-value bindings; new.env() creates a new, empty one, and assign()/get() read and write values inside a specific environment.
Every function call creates its own temporary environment for local variables, which is discarded when the function returns — the mechanism behind Level 9’s scoping rules.
Explicitly created environments let state persist and be shared across multiple function calls, unlike a function’s normal, temporary local variables.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Why does a variable created inside a function disappear once the function returns?

R deletes all variables starting with lowercase letters
The function’s temporary environment is discarded when the function finishes
Local variables are never actually created
R only allows one variable per script


True or false. get("x", envir = some_env) will find x even if it’s only defined in the global environment, not in some_env.
Predict the output. If assign("threshold", 130, envir = threshold_env) is run after threshold was previously 140, what would get("threshold", envir = threshold_env) return next?
Short answer. Why might a function that reads its cutoff value from an environment (rather than a hardcoded number) be more flexible than a function with the cutoff written directly into its code?


Show answers


b) The function’s temporary environment is discarded when the function finishes, taking its local variables with it.

False. get(..., envir = some_env) looks specifically inside some_env (and, by default, its parent environments) — it will not find a variable that exists only in a completely unrelated environment outside that chain.
130 — assign() overwrites the previous value stored in that environment.
Reading the cutoff from an environment lets the same function’s behavior be changed later (by updating the environment) without editing or re-defining the function itself — exactly the flexibility closures (Page 2) build on directly.

title: 152  Closures and Function Factories
text:
152.1 The R Concept
A closure is a function that captures (“closes over”) variables from the environment where it was created, not where it’s later called. A function factory is a function whose entire job is to build and return such closures:
make_threshold_checker <- function(cutoff) {
  function(x) x >= cutoff
}

is_high <- make_threshold_checker(100)
is_high(120)   # TRUE — remembers cutoff = 100, even called later, elsewhere
Each closure produced this way keeps its own private copy of cutoff — calling make_threshold_checker() again with a different value creates a completely independent function.

title: 152  Closures and Function Factories
text:
152.2 Demonstration

make_threshold_checker <- function(cutoff) {
  function(x) x >= cutoff
}

is_stage2 <- make_threshold_checker(140)
is_stage1_or_above <- make_threshold_checker(130)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

sum(is_stage2(participants$systolic_bp), na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 29

sum(is_stage1_or_above(participants$systolic_bp), na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 46

title: 152  Closures and Function Factories
text:
152.3 Explore
Try environment(is_stage2) — you’ll see it has its own environment containing cutoff = 140, completely separate from is_stage1_or_above’s environment containing cutoff = 130. This is what “each closure remembers its own value” looks like under the hood, directly connecting back to Page 1’s environments.

title: 152  Closures and Function Factories
text:
152.4 Solve the Problem

make_threshold_checker <- function(cutoff) {
  function(x) x >= cutoff
}

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

is_stage2 <- make_threshold_checker(140)
is_crisis <- make_threshold_checker(180)

count_stage2 <- sum(is_stage2(participants$systolic_bp), na.rm = TRUE)
count_crisis <- sum(is_crisis(participants$systolic_bp), na.rm = TRUE)

count_stage2
count_crisis

[1] 29
[1] 0
is_stage2 and is_crisis were both built from the exact same make_threshold_checker() factory, yet each behaves completely differently — 29 participants meet the Stage 2 cutoff (matching Level 4’s case_when() count exactly), while zero reach the Hypertensive Crisis cutoff of 180. Neither closure needed its cutoff passed in again at call time; each one already “remembers” it.

title: 152  Closures and Function Factories
text:
152.5 Interpret the Result
Closures let you build an entire family of specialized, ready-to-use functions from one general-purpose factory, instead of repeating a slightly different hardcoded expression for every cutoff (as Level 4’s case_when() did in one big block). This is a genuinely different way of organizing reusable logic than Level 9’s plain functions with default arguments — here, the “configuration” (the cutoff) is baked permanently into each generated function, rather than needing to be supplied every time it’s called.

Common Mistakes


Confusing a closure with a plain function that has a default argument. function(x, cutoff = 140) x >= cutoff still requires (or defaults) cutoff on every call — a closure instead permanently bakes the value in during creation, and the resulting function only ever needs x.

Expecting closures created from the same factory to share state. is_stage2 and is_crisis each have their own independent copy of cutoff — changing one has zero effect on the other, unlike Page 1’s shared environment example.

Overusing closures where a simple function argument would be clearer. If a value genuinely changes on every call, an ordinary function parameter (Level 9) is simpler and more readable — closures earn their complexity specifically when you want a whole family of pre-configured, reusable functions.

title: 152  Closures and Function Factories
text:
152.6 Practice Problem
Use make_threshold_checker() to build is_underweight <- make_threshold_checker(18.5) for BMI (note this checks x < cutoff conceptually — you’ll need a second factory function, make_below_checker, that returns function(x) x < cutoff instead). How many participants have bmi < 18.5?

title: 152  Closures and Function Factories
text:
152.7 Challenge Problem
Write a function factory make_range_checker(lower, upper) that returns a closure checking whether a value falls between lower and upper (inclusive). Use it to build a function checking Level 4’s “Hypertension Stage 1” range (130-139 inclusive) and confirm it reproduces that category’s original count from Level 4’s case_when() classification.

title: 152  Closures and Function Factories
text:
152.8 Key Takeaways

A closure is a function that remembers variables from the environment where it was created, not where it’s later called — this is what lets is_stage2 keep working correctly no matter where it’s used afterward.
A function factory is a function whose job is to build and return closures, letting you generate an entire family of pre-configured functions from one general-purpose template.
Each closure produced by a factory keeps its own independent copy of the captured variable(s) — changing one closure’s captured value never affects another.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does a function factory do?

Runs a function many times in a loop
Returns a new function that remembers a value from when it was created
Converts a function into a data frame
Deletes unused functions from memory


True or false. Two closures created from the same factory function, with different arguments, share the exact same captured value.
Predict the output. Given make_threshold_checker(180) applied to participants$systolic_bp, and knowing this course’s data has no values at or above 180, how many TRUE results would sum(..., na.rm = TRUE) return?
Short answer. How is a closure different from an ordinary function with a default argument value?


Show answers


b) Returns a new function that remembers a value from when it was created.

False. Each closure keeps its own independent copy of the captured value — changing one has no effect on another closure built from the same factory.
0 — no participant’s systolic blood pressure reaches the 180 “Hypertensive Crisis” threshold in this dataset.
A default argument still requires (or silently reuses a default for) a value passed at call time and can be overridden on any call; a closure permanently bakes its captured value in at creation time, and the resulting function doesn’t accept or need that value as an argument at all.

title: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects
crumbs: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects

title: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects
crumbs: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects

title: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects
text:
153.3 Explore
Try class(result) — it returns "risk_summary", confirming the tag structure() attached. Try unclass(result) to see the object’s true underlying structure: an ordinary list, with the class tag stripped off, printed the default (untidy) way.
crumbs: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects

title: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects
crumbs: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects

title: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects
text:
153.5 Interpret the Result
S3 lets you build result objects that behave like R’s own built-in objects: informative, readable, and tailored to what they represent, rather than generic list dumps. This is precisely the mechanism underneath everything from lm() and aov() (Level 11 and 12) to ggplot() objects (Level 8) — each has its own class and its own print (and often summary, plot) methods written specifically for it.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting the method name must exactly match print.<classname>. structure(list(...), class = "risk_summary") will only dispatch correctly to a function named exactly print.risk_summary — a typo or mismatched name means R silently falls back to the default list-printing behavior instead.

Manually calling print.risk_summary(result) instead of relying on dispatch. While this technically works, typing result alone (or calling generic print(result)) is how dispatch is meant to be used — S3’s whole benefit is that you never need to remember which specific method to call.

Assuming every object needs its own S3 class. Most of Levels 1-12’s work used plain vectors, data frames, and lists just fine — S3 classes earn their complexity specifically when you want custom printing or behavior for a genuinely reusable, structured result type.
crumbs: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects

title: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects
text:
153.6 Practice Problem
Write a summary.risk_summary() method (a second S3 method for the same class) that prints a one-line summary in a different format from print.risk_summary(), such as "[LOW]" or "[HIGH RISK]" bracketed tags.
crumbs: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects

title: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects
