text:
153.7 Challenge Problem
Extend make_risk_summary() so it also stores the participant’s county, and write a new print.risk_summary() that includes the county in its output. Apply your updated function to 3 different rows of participants using lapply() (Level 9), and print all 3 resulting objects.
crumbs: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects

title: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects
text:
153.8 Key Takeaways


structure(list(...), class = "my_class") tags an ordinary list as an object of a custom S3 class.
Generic functions like print() automatically dispatch to a matching print.<classname>() method, based on an object’s class attribute.
This exact mechanism underlies the tidy printouts of built-in R objects like lm(), aov(), and ggplot() objects seen throughout Levels 8, 11, and 12.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What determines which print.<classname>() method R calls for an object?

The order functions were defined in the script
The object’s class attribute
The object’s variable name
The number of elements in the object


True or false. unclass(result) permanently removes the underlying data, not just the class tag.
Predict the output. If a print.risk_summary() method exists but a user calls print.default(result) explicitly, which printing behavior would occur?
Short answer. Why does typing result at the console for an S3 object not print a raw list dump, even though the object is, underneath, just a list?


Show answers


b) The object’s class attribute — R’s generic functions dispatch based on this tag.

False. unclass() only strips the class attribute; the underlying list data is unchanged and still fully accessible.

print.default()’s generic list-printing behavior — calling it explicitly bypasses the class-based dispatch that would otherwise route to print.risk_summary().
Typing result calls the generic print() function, which checks the object’s class attribute and automatically dispatches to print.risk_summary() instead of the default list-printing method — this dispatch, not anything intrinsic to lists, is what changes the output.
crumbs: 153  A First Look at S3 Objects

title: 154  Robust Error Handling with tryCatch()
154  Robust Error Handling with tryCatch()
154  Robust Error Handling with tryCatch()
text:
154.3 Explore
Try wrapping Level 10’s automation capstone loop (reading each file in data/clinics/) with safe_read_csv() instead of a plain read.csv(). Then try adding a genuinely broken path to the file list — the loop should print an error message for that one file and continue processing the rest, instead of crashing entirely.

title: 154  Robust Error Handling with tryCatch()
154  Robust Error Handling with tryCatch()
text:
154.5 Interpret the Result
tryCatch() transforms a single bad file from “the whole script crashes” into “one file is skipped, with a clear message explaining why, while everything else proceeds normally.” This is the difference between a fragile script that works only in perfect conditions and a robust one that degrades gracefully — an essential quality for any automation (Level 10) meant to run repeatedly on real-world data that occasionally has missing or malformed files.

Common Mistakes


Wrapping too much code in one tryCatch() block. If an entire multi-step process is wrapped in a single tryCatch(), one small error hides which step actually failed — wrapping the smallest risky piece (like just the read.csv() call) gives more precise, useful error messages.

Forgetting to filter out the NULL placeholders afterward. all_results still contains a NULL for each skipped file — passing it directly to do.call(rbind, ...) without first removing NULLs (via sapply(..., is.null), as shown here) can cause a confusing downstream error instead.

Using tryCatch() to silently hide every error with no message at all. An error handler that does nothing but return(NULL) with no cat() or logging leaves you with no way to know which files failed or why — always surface at least a brief message.

title: 154  Robust Error Handling with tryCatch()
text:
154.6 Practice Problem
Write a safe_mean <- function(x) that wraps mean(x) in a tryCatch(), returning NA and printing a message if x isn’t numeric (this will require stop()-ing manually inside the tried code if !is.numeric(x), since mean() on character data doesn’t error by default — try mean(c("a","b")) first to see what actually happens).

title: 154  Robust Error Handling with tryCatch()
text:
154.7 Challenge Problem
Extend the clinic-file-reading loop above so that, in addition to skipping unreadable files, it also uses tryCatch()’s warning = function(w) {...} argument to catch and report any warnings (not just errors) produced while reading a file. Test it against a file path that would produce a warning, such as one with inconsistent column types.

title: 154  Robust Error Handling with tryCatch()
text:
154.8 Key Takeaways


tryCatch({ code }, error = function(e) { ... }) catches an error when it happens, letting you respond gracefully (a message, a fallback value) instead of crashing the whole script.

conditionMessage(e) extracts the actual error text inside an error handler, letting you report specifically what went wrong.
Wrapping the smallest risky piece of code (not an entire multi-step process) in tryCatch() gives the most precise, useful error handling.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What is the main advantage of tryCatch() over letting stop() crash a script?

It runs code faster
It lets you catch an error and continue running the rest of your code
It automatically fixes broken file paths
It prevents all errors from ever occurring


True or false. If no error occurs inside tryCatch({ code }), the error handler function never runs.
Predict the output. If a loop uses tryCatch() to skip 1 broken file out of 6 total files, how many results would end up in a list of successfully-read files?
Short answer. Why is wrapping just read.csv(path) in tryCatch() better than wrapping an entire 10-line data-processing pipeline in one tryCatch() block?


Show answers


b) It lets you catch an error and continue running the rest of your code, rather than crashing entirely.

True. The error handler is only invoked if an error actually occurs during the tried code’s execution.
5 — one broken file is skipped, leaving the other 5 successfully read.
Wrapping only the risky line pinpoints exactly which operation failed and lets every other step run normally on all other iterations — wrapping an entire pipeline means any error anywhere inside it looks identical and stops that entire iteration’s processing, even parts that would have worked fine.

title: 155  Debugging Tools
text:
155.1 The R Concept
Three complementary tools for finding where a bug happens:
browser()          # placed inside a function; pauses execution there when reached
traceback()         # after a crash, shows the full sequence of calls that led to the error
print(x)            # the simplest tool of all — temporarily printing a variable's value mid-function
browser() is the most powerful: place it on its own line inside a function, and when execution reaches it, R drops you into an interactive debugging prompt (Browse[1]>) right at that point, letting you inspect (and even change) any local variable before choosing to continue (n or c), step further (n), or quit (Q).

title: 155  Debugging Tools
text:
155.2 Demonstration

pct_missing_debug <- function(x) {
  n_total <- length(x)
  browser()   # execution pauses here when this function runs
  n_missing <- sum(is.na(x))
  round(100 * n_missing / n_total, 1)
}

pct_missing_debug(c(1, NA, 3, NA, 5))

Called from: pct_missing_debug(c(1, NA, 3, NA, 5))
Browse[1]> n_total
[1] 5
Browse[1]> x
[1]  1 NA  3 NA  5
Browse[1]> n
Browse[2]> n_missing
[1] 2
Browse[2]> c
[1] 40

title: 155  Debugging Tools
text:
155.3 Explore
Try removing browser() and instead sprinkling print() calls between each line of a function, showing intermediate values as it runs. This “print debugging” is less powerful than browser() (you can’t interactively explore further) but is often faster for a quick check, and needs no special debugging prompt.

title: 155  Debugging Tools
text:
155.4 Solve the Problem

bp_category_debug <- function(sbp) {
  browser()
  dplyr::case_when(
    sbp < 120 ~ "Normal",
    sbp < 130 ~ "Elevated",
    sbp < 140 ~ "Hypertension Stage 1",
    sbp < 180 ~ "Hypertension Stage 2",
    TRUE ~ "Hypertensive Crisis"
  )
}

# At the Browse[1]> prompt, typing `sbp` reveals the input value,
# letting you confirm exactly what the function received before
# it applies any classification logic
bp_category_debug(145)

Called from: bp_category_debug(145)
Browse[1]> sbp
[1] 145
Browse[1]> c
[1] "Hypertension Stage 2"
Pausing with browser() before the case_when() call confirms exactly what value (145) the function is working with, before any classification happens — if the final result had been wrong, checking sbp’s value at this exact point would immediately reveal whether the input was already wrong, or whether the bug is somewhere in the classification logic itself.

title: 155  Debugging Tools
text:
155.5 Interpret the Result
browser() turns debugging from guesswork (“I think the problem might be around line 3?”) into direct inspection (“here’s exactly what every variable holds at this precise point in execution”). This is especially valuable for bugs that don’t raise an error at all — Page 4’s tryCatch() only helps once code already fails loudly; browser() helps you understand code that fails silently, producing a plausible-looking but wrong answer.

Common Mistakes


Leaving a browser() call inside a function after debugging is finished. A forgotten browser() will pause every future call to that function, unexpectedly halting a script (or, worse, an automated pipeline like Level 10’s) that runs later — always remove it once the bug is found.

Not knowing how to exit the debugging prompt. Typing Q exits the debugger entirely; n steps to the next line; c continues running normally until the next browser() call or the function’s end — confusing these can leave you stuck or exit before finding the bug.

Reaching for browser() for every tiny issue. A quick print() statement is often faster for a simple “what value is this?” check — browser() earns its overhead specifically for bugs that need exploring several variables interactively, at the exact moment of failure.

title: 155  Debugging Tools
text:
155.6 Practice Problem
Add a browser() call inside Level 9’s is_at_risk() function, right before its final comparison line, and use it to inspect the values of age, sbp, age_cutoff, and bp_cutoff for a call like is_at_risk(70, 145).

title: 155  Debugging Tools
text:
155.7 Challenge Problem
Deliberately introduce a bug into a copy of Level 10’s pct_missing() function (for example, swapping n_missing and n_total in the final division). Use browser() to step through the buggy version and identify exactly which line produces the wrong result.

title: 155  Debugging Tools
text:
155.8 Key Takeaways


browser() placed inside a function pauses execution there, dropping you into an interactive prompt to inspect (and even change) local variables at that exact point.

n steps to the next line, c continues to the end (or the next browser() call), and Q exits the debugger entirely.

browser() is especially useful for silent bugs that produce a wrong-but-plausible result without ever raising an error — unlike tryCatch() (Page 4), which only helps once something fails loudly.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does placing browser() inside a function do?

It automatically fixes any bugs in the function
It pauses execution at that point and opens an interactive inspection prompt
It deletes the function after it runs once
It prints every variable in the function automatically


True or false. Typing n at a Browse[1]> prompt steps to the next line of code.
Predict the output. If a function’s actual bug is a wrong-but-plausible result with no error message at all, would tryCatch() (Page 4) alone reveal the bug’s location?
Short answer. Why is it important to remove browser() calls from a function before using it in an automated script?


Show answers


b) It pauses execution at that point and opens an interactive inspection prompt for the function’s current local variables.

True. n steps to the next line within the debugger.
No — tryCatch() only intercepts actual errors (or warnings); a silent bug that produces a wrong result without raising anything would pass through tryCatch() completely unnoticed.
A forgotten browser() call will pause execution every time that function runs afterward, which would unexpectedly halt an automated script or pipeline (like Level 10’s capstone) waiting on manual input it will never receive.

title: 156  Functional Programming with Reduce()
text:
156.1 The R Concept
Reduce(function, x) repeatedly applies a two-argument function to combine elements of x one at a time, carrying an accumulated result forward:
Reduce(`+`, c(1, 2, 3, 4))
# step by step: (((1 + 2) + 3) + 4) = 10
Adding accumulate = TRUE returns every intermediate step, not just the final result — useful for seeing a running total, similar in spirit to Level 10’s manually-built accumulator loop, but expressed in a single line.

title: 156  Functional Programming with Reduce()
text:
156.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ages <- na.omit(participants$age)

Reduce(`+`, ages)

[1] 4458

sum(ages)   # confirms Reduce("+", ages) matches R's built-in sum()

[1] 4458

title: 156  Functional Programming with Reduce()
text:
156.3 Explore
Try Reduce(+, ages, accumulate = TRUE) on just the first 5 ages (ages[1:5]) to see every running-total step along the way, rather than only the final sum. Compare it to cumsum(ages[1:5]) — Reduce(..., accumulate = TRUE) and cumsum() produce the same running totals, just through a more general mechanism that works with any two-argument combining function, not only addition.

title: 156  Functional Programming with Reduce()
text:
156.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ages <- na.omit(participants$age)

running_totals <- Reduce(`+`, ages[1:5], accumulate = TRUE)
running_totals

[1]  46  99 158 207 268

Reduce(function(current_max, next_val) if (next_val > current_max) next_val else current_max, ages)

[1] 81
The running totals (46, 99, 158, 207, 268) confirm Reduce(accumulate = TRUE) matches cumsum()’s step-by-step behavior exactly. The second Reduce() call — using a custom “keep the larger value” function instead of + — correctly finds the maximum age (81) across the entire dataset, demonstrating Reduce() works with any two-argument combining function, not just arithmetic ones.

title: 156  Functional Programming with Reduce()
text:
156.5 Interpret the Result
Reduce() generalizes the “combine values one at a time, carrying a result forward” pattern beyond simple addition — the exact same function works whether you’re summing numbers, finding a maximum, or (as the challenge problem explores) combining data frames. This is a genuinely different mental model from Level 9’s sapply()/map_dbl() (which apply a function to each element independently, with no memory of prior elements) and Level 10’s explicit accumulator loops (which require manually managing an index and a pre-allocated vector).

Common Mistakes


Confusing Reduce() (combines elements together) with sapply()/map() (transforms each element independently). sapply(x, f) returns one output per input element; Reduce(f, x) returns a single combined result (or, with accumulate = TRUE, one result per step of the combination) — these solve different problems.

Forgetting the combining function needs exactly two arguments. A custom function passed to Reduce(), like the maximum-finder above, must accept exactly two arguments — the accumulated result so far, and the next element — anything else will error.

Not realizing do.call(rbind, list_of_dataframes) (Level 10’s capstone) is itself a Reduce()-like pattern. do.call(rbind, list) combines every data frame in a list into one, conceptually similar to Reduce(rbind, list) — both express “combine a list of things into one,” just with slightly different syntax.

title: 156  Functional Programming with Reduce()
text:
156.6 Practice Problem
Use Reduce() with a custom two-argument function to find the minimum age in participants$age (removing missing values first), without using R’s built-in min().

title: 156  Functional Programming with Reduce()
text:
156.7 Challenge Problem
Level 10’s automation capstone used do.call(rbind, clinic_data_list) to combine 5 clinic data frames into one. Rewrite that combination step using Reduce(rbind, clinic_data_list) instead, and confirm it produces the identical 100-row combined data frame both ways.

title: 156  Functional Programming with Reduce()
text:
156.8 Key Takeaways


Reduce(function, x) repeatedly applies a two-argument function to combine all elements of x into a single result, carrying an accumulated value forward at each step.

accumulate = TRUE returns every intermediate step of the combination, not just the final result — comparable to cumsum(), but generalized to any two-argument combining function.

Reduce() solves a fundamentally different problem than sapply()/map() (which transform elements independently) or an explicit accumulator loop (Level 10) — all three are valid tools for different situations.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does Reduce(+, c(1, 2, 3, 4)) compute?

A vector c(1, 2, 3, 4) unchanged
The single combined value 10

Four separate sums
An error, since + isn’t a valid function name


True or false. Reduce(f, x, accumulate = TRUE) returns only the final combined result, discarding every intermediate step.
Predict the output. What would Reduce(+, c(46, 53, 59, 49, 61), accumulate = TRUE) return?
Short answer. How is Reduce() fundamentally different from sapply() or purrr::map_dbl()?


Show answers


b) The single combined value 10 — computed step by step as ((1 + 2) + 3) + 4.

False. accumulate = TRUE returns every intermediate step of the combination, not just the final result.

c(46, 99, 158, 207, 268) — the running total after each successive addition.

sapply()/map_dbl() apply a function to each element of a vector independently, producing one output per input with no memory between elements; Reduce() instead combines elements together sequentially, carrying an accumulated result forward from one step to the next.

title: 157  Regular Expressions
text:
157.1 The R Concept
A regular expression (“regex”) is a mini-language for describing text patterns, rather than exact text. A few building blocks cover most everyday needs:
grepl(pattern, x)              # TRUE/FALSE: does the pattern appear in x?
grep(pattern, x, value = TRUE) # returns the matching elements themselves
gsub(pattern, replacement, x)  # replaces every match with something else
regmatches(x, regexpr(pattern, x))  # extracts just the matched portion of text
Common pattern symbols: ^ anchors to the start of a string, $ anchors to the end, \\d+ matches one or more digits, and . matches any single character. sub("_visit\\d+$", "", x) removes a trailing _visit followed by digits from the end of each string.

title: 157  Regular Expressions
text:
157.2 Demonstration

bp_visits <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

column_names <- names(bp_visits)
column_names

[1] "id"            "county"        "clinic_site"   "sbp_visit1"
[5] "sbp_visit2"    "sbp_visit3"    "weight_visit1" "weight_visit2"
[9] "weight_visit3"

grepl("^sbp_visit", column_names)

[1] FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE

title: 157  Regular Expressions
text:
157.3 Explore
Try grep("^sbp_visit", column_names, value = TRUE) instead of grepl() — notice it returns the matching column names themselves ("sbp_visit1" "sbp_visit2" "sbp_visit3"), rather than a logical vector. Try gsub("_visit[0-9]+$", "", column_names) to strip the visit-number suffix from every column name at once.

title: 157  Regular Expressions
text:
157.4 Solve the Problem

bp_visits <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

column_names <- names(bp_visits)

sbp_columns <- grep("^sbp_visit", column_names, value = TRUE)
weight_columns <- grep("^weight_visit", column_names, value = TRUE)

sbp_columns
weight_columns

[1] "sbp_visit1" "sbp_visit2" "sbp_visit3"
[1] "weight_visit1" "weight_visit2" "weight_visit3"

visit_columns <- grep("_visit[0-9]+$", column_names, value = TRUE)
measurement_types <- unique(gsub("_visit[0-9]+$", "", visit_columns))
measurement_types

[1] "sbp"    "weight"

visit_numbers <- regmatches(visit_columns, regexpr("[0-9]+$", visit_columns))
visit_numbers

[1] "1" "2" "3" "1" "2" "3"
The pattern "^sbp_visit" correctly isolated exactly the 3 systolic blood pressure columns, and "_visit[0-9]+$" combined with gsub() correctly extracted just two underlying measurement types ("sbp" and "weight") hidden inside 6 differently-named columns — all without writing out any column name by hand.

title: 157  Regular Expressions
text:
157.5 Interpret the Result
Regular expressions let you describe a pattern (“starts with sbp_visit”, “ends with an underscore and one or more digits”) instead of listing every matching string individually — this scales to datasets with far more columns than this course’s small example, where hardcoding every name would become impractical. This is the same kind of automation-over-hardcoding thinking Level 10’s list.files(pattern = "\\.csv$") already relied on, just applied to column names instead of file names.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting . matches any character in regex, not a literal period. To match a literal period (like in a file extension), it must be escaped as \\. — Level 10’s list.files(pattern = "\\.csv$") already relied on exactly this escaping.

Confusing grepl() (returns TRUE/FALSE) with grep() (returns matching elements or positions) with gsub() (returns modified strings). All three take a similar pattern, x structure, but return fundamentally different things — always check which one actually matches your goal.

Writing an overly loose pattern that matches more than intended. grepl("visit", column_names) (without the ^ anchor) would also match a hypothetical column named "last_visit_notes" — anchoring with ^ and $ where appropriate makes a pattern match exactly what’s intended, not just “contains this text somewhere.”

title: 157  Regular Expressions
text:
157.6 Practice Problem
Use gsub() to remove the _visit1, _visit2, and _visit3 suffixes from bp_visits’ column names entirely, then check how many unique resulting names remain (accounting for id, county, and clinic_site, which have no visit suffix to remove).

title: 157  Regular Expressions
text:
157.7 Challenge Problem
Level 2’s participants$county column contains values like "Cedar Falls" (with a space). Write a regex-based check using grepl(" ", participants$county) to detect which county names contain a space, and use gsub(" ", "_", participants$county) to replace spaces with underscores — a common data-cleaning step before using text values as column names or file paths.

title: 157  Regular Expressions
text:
157.8 Key Takeaways

Regular expressions describe text patterns (not exact strings), letting grepl(), grep(), and gsub() match many variations of text at once.

^ anchors to the start of a string, $ anchors to the end, and [0-9]+ (or \\d+) matches one or more digits — a few symbols cover most everyday pattern-matching needs.

regmatches() combined with regexpr() extracts just the matched portion of text, rather than a TRUE/FALSE or the whole original string.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does the regex pattern "^sbp_visit" match?

Any string containing “sbp_visit” anywhere
Only strings that start with “sbp_visit”
Only strings that end with “sbp_visit”
Strings containing exactly the number of characters in “sbp_visit”


True or false. grepl() and grep() always return the exact same type of result.
Predict the output. Given column_names <- c("sbp_visit1", "weight_visit1", "id"), what would grep("^sbp_visit", column_names, value = TRUE) return?
Short answer. Why might grepl("visit", column_names) (without a ^ anchor) match more columns than intended, compared to grepl("^sbp_visit", column_names)?


Show answers


b) Only strings that start with “sbp_visit” — the ^ anchors the pattern to the beginning of the string.

False. grepl() always returns a logical (TRUE/FALSE) vector; grep() returns either matching positions (by default) or matching elements themselves (with value = TRUE) — different result types.

"sbp_visit1" — the only element starting with the literal text “sbp_visit”.
Without the ^ anchor, "visit" would match any column name containing that text anywhere, such as a hypothetical "last_visit_date" column — the anchor restricts matches specifically to strings that start with the intended prefix.

title: 158  String Manipulation
text:
158.1 The R Concept
Base R provides a full toolkit for text manipulation, without needing any extra package:
toupper(x)          # convert to all uppercase
tolower(x)           # convert to all lowercase
trimws(x)            # remove leading/trailing whitespace
nchar(x)             # count characters in a string
substr(x, start, stop)  # extract a portion of a string by position
paste(x, y, sep = " ")  # join strings together with a separator
sprintf("%.1f", x)   # format a number as text with a fixed number of decimals
sprintf() is especially useful for formatted reporting — "%.1f" means “format as a fixed-point decimal number with 1 digit after the decimal point,” similar in spirit to Level 9’s round(), but producing formatted text rather than a rounded number.

title: 158  String Manipulation
text:
158.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

counties <- unique(participants$county)
counties

[1] "Oakford"     "Riverdale"   "Millbrook"   "Cedar Falls" "Springvale"

toupper(counties)

[1] "OAKFORD"     "RIVERDALE"   "MILLBROOK"   "CEDAR FALLS" "SPRINGVALE"

nchar(counties)

[1]  7  9  9 11 10

title: 158  String Manipulation
text:
158.3 Explore
Try substr(counties, 1, 3) to extract just the first 3 characters of each county name — useful for building short codes or abbreviations. Try paste(counties, "County", sep = " ") to append the word “County” to every name at once.

title: 158  String Manipulation
text:
158.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

counties <- unique(participants$county)

county_codes <- toupper(substr(counties, 1, 3))
county_codes

[1] "OAK" "RIV" "MIL" "CED" "SPR"

mean_bmi <- mean(participants$bmi, na.rm = TRUE)
formatted_bmi <- sprintf("Mean BMI: %.1f kg/m^2", mean_bmi)
formatted_bmi

[1] "Mean BMI: 27.1 kg/m^2"

labels <- paste0(counties, " (", county_codes, ")")
labels

[1] "Oakford (OAK)"     "Riverdale (RIV)"   "Millbrook (MIL)"
[4] "Cedar Falls (CED)" "Springvale (SPR)"
Combining toupper() and substr() produced clean 3-letter county codes from full names, and sprintf("%.1f", ...) formatted the mean BMI (27.123…) into the clean, report-ready text "Mean BMI: 27.1 kg/m^2" — precisely one decimal place, regardless of how many digits the raw numeric mean actually has.

title: 158  String Manipulation
text:
158.5 Interpret the Result
String functions solve a different problem than the numeric functions used throughout Levels 7-12: instead of computing a value, they clean, standardize, and format text itself for display or for consistent comparison. sprintf() in particular bridges the two worlds — taking a numeric result (like a mean or a p-value) and turning it into exactly the formatted text a report or console output should show, rather than R’s sometimes-inconsistent default number of displayed digits.

Common Mistakes


Comparing text with == without first standardizing case or whitespace. "Oakford" == "oakford" returns FALSE — always tolower() (or toupper()) and trimws() both sides of a comparison when the source of the text isn’t guaranteed to be perfectly consistent.

Confusing paste() (default separator is a space) with paste0() (no separator by default). paste("OAK", "County") produces "OAK County", while paste0("OAK", "County") produces "OAKCounty" — picking the wrong one produces subtly wrong-looking text.

Using substr() with the wrong start/stop positions. substr(x, 1, 3) extracts the first 3 characters — a common mistake is assuming it extracts exactly 3 characters starting from some other position without explicitly specifying both start and stop.

title: 158  String Manipulation
text:
158.6 Practice Problem
Use sprintf("%.2f%%", ...) to format Level 9’s pct_missing() results (percentages) as clean text with exactly 2 decimal places and a trailing percent sign, for each of the 4 numeric columns (age, systolic_bp, diastolic_bp, bmi).

title: 158  String Manipulation
text:
158.7 Challenge Problem
Write a function clean_county_name(x) that applies trimws() and converts to title case (hint: tools::toTitleCase() from base R, or manually combine toupper(substr(x, 1, 1)) with tolower(substr(x, 2, nchar(x))) for a single word). Test it on a deliberately messy input like "  cedar falls  " and confirm it returns "Cedar Falls".

title: 158  String Manipulation
text:
158.8 Key Takeaways

Base R string functions (toupper(), tolower(), trimws(), substr(), paste()/paste0(), sprintf()) clean, standardize, and format text without needing any extra package.

sprintf("%.1f", x) (and similar format codes) converts a numeric value into precisely-formatted text — useful for clean, consistent reporting of means, percentages, and other computed values.
Comparing or joining text values that might differ in case or whitespace requires standardizing them first (tolower(), trimws()) — otherwise, seemingly identical values can silently fail to match.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does sprintf("%.1f", 27.123157) return?

"27.123157"
"27.1"

27.1 (as a number, not text)
An error, since sprintf() only works on integers


True or false. "Oakford" == "oakford" evaluates to TRUE in R.
Predict the output. What would paste0("OAK", "County") return?
Short answer. Why might two seemingly identical county names fail to match with == even though they look the same when printed?


Show answers


b) "27.1" — formatted as text with exactly one decimal place.

False. R string comparison is case-sensitive by default — "Oakford" and "oakford" are different strings.

"OAKCounty" — paste0() joins strings with no separator by default, unlike paste()’s default single space.
Invisible differences like trailing whitespace, inconsistent capitalization, or non-printing characters can make two strings look identical when printed but fail an exact == comparison — trimws() and tolower()/toupper() standardize away these differences before comparing.

title: 159  Working with Dates
text:
159.1 The R Concept
as.Date() converts text into a proper R date object, which R can then correctly compare, sort, and do arithmetic on:
enrollment_date <- as.Date("2024-01-15")
followup_date   <- as.Date("2024-03-22")

followup_date - enrollment_date   # a proper time difference, not just subtraction of text
format() controls how a date is displayed (without changing the underlying date), and weekdays() extracts the day-of-week name directly:
format(enrollment_date, "%B %d, %Y")   # e.g. "January 15, 2024"
weekdays(enrollment_date)              # e.g. "Monday"

title: 159  Working with Dates
text:
159.2 Demonstration

enrollment_date <- as.Date("2024-01-15")
followup_date   <- as.Date("2024-03-22")

followup_date - enrollment_date

Time difference of 67 days

weekdays(enrollment_date)

[1] "Monday"

format(enrollment_date, "%B %d, %Y")

[1] "January 15, 2024"

title: 159  Working with Dates
text:
159.3 Explore
Try class(enrollment_date) to confirm as.Date() produces a genuine "Date" object, not just a character string that happens to look like one. Try enrollment_date + 30 — adding a plain number to a Date object correctly advances it by that many days, something plain text arithmetic could never do.

title: 159  Working with Dates
text:
159.4 Solve the Problem

enrollment_dates <- as.Date(c("2024-01-15", "2024-02-03", "2024-03-22"))

weekdays(enrollment_dates)

[1] "Monday"   "Saturday" "Friday"

days_since_first <- enrollment_dates - enrollment_dates[1]
days_since_first

Time differences in days
[1]  0 19 67

formatted_dates <- format(enrollment_dates, "%B %d, %Y")
formatted_dates

[1] "January 15, 2024"  "February 03, 2024" "March 22, 2024"
Subtracting enrollment_dates[1] from the whole vector produced a clean, correctly-ordered set of day differences (0, 19, 67 days) — exactly the kind of calculation that would silently fail (or produce nonsense) if these dates were still stored as plain, unparsed text instead of proper Date objects.

title: 159  Working with Dates
text:
159.5 Interpret the Result
Converting text into a real Date object with as.Date() unlocks correct date arithmetic, sorting, and comparison — operations that look deceptively simple but require R to actually understand calendar structure (leap years, varying month lengths) rather than treating dates as arbitrary text. This is precisely the reason a date column in a real public health dataset (unlike anything in this course’s participants.csv) always needs as.Date() applied immediately after import (Level 3), before any calculation involving it.

Common Mistakes


Forgetting to convert a date column with as.Date() after read.csv(). By default, read.csv() imports date-looking columns as plain character text (Level 3) — subtracting or comparing them without first calling as.Date() will either error or produce a meaningless text-based comparison, not a real date calculation.

Assuming as.Date() guesses every date format correctly. as.Date("01/15/2024") (US-style month/day/year) may be misread by default — explicitly specifying the format (as.Date("01/15/2024", format = "%m/%d/%Y")) avoids silently wrong parsing.

Confusing format() (changes display only) with as.Date() (changes the actual stored value). format(enrollment_date, "%B %d, %Y") produces nicely formatted text for display — it does not change what enrollment_date itself stores, and the result usually shouldn’t be used for further date arithmetic.

title: 159  Working with Dates
text:
159.6 Practice Problem
Given visit1_date <- as.Date("2024-01-15") and visit2_date <- as.Date("2024-04-01"), calculate how many days elapsed between the two visits, and determine what day of the week visit2_date fell on.

title: 159  Working with Dates
text:
159.7 Challenge Problem
Create a vector of 5 enrollment dates spread across different months, then use format(dates, "%Y-%m") to extract just the year-month for each, and table() (Level 7) to count how many enrollments happened in each year-month — a common first step in reporting enrollment trends over time.

title: 159  Working with Dates
text:
159.8 Key Takeaways


as.Date("YYYY-MM-DD") converts text into a proper Date object, enabling correct date arithmetic, sorting, and comparison that plain text cannot support.
Subtracting two Date objects produces a proper time difference (in days), and weekdays() extracts the day-of-week name directly.

format(date, "format_string") controls how a date is displayed as text, without changing the underlying stored date value — useful for clean reporting.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Why must as.Date() be applied to a date column before doing date arithmetic on it?


read.csv() automatically applies it already
Without it, dates are stored as plain text and can’t be correctly compared or subtracted

as.Date() is only needed for dates before the year 2000
There is no difference — arithmetic works the same either way


True or false. format(date, "%B %d, %Y") changes the actual value stored in the date object, not just its display.
Predict the output. If date1 <- as.Date("2024-01-15") and date2 <- as.Date("2024-02-03"), how many days apart are they (using date2 - date1)?
Short answer. Why might as.Date("01/15/2024") without specifying a format argument produce a wrong or unexpected result?


Show answers


b) Without as.Date(), dates are stored as plain text and can’t be correctly compared, sorted, or subtracted as actual calendar dates.

False. format() only changes how a date is displayed as text — the underlying Date object itself is unchanged.
19 days.

as.Date()’s default expected format is "YYYY-MM-DD" — a differently-ordered format like "01/15/2024" (month/day/year) may be parsed incorrectly or produce NA unless the format argument explicitly describes that layout.

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
160.1 The R Concept
An R package is just a specific, standardized folder structure that tools like library() and install.packages() know how to read:
The DESCRIPTION file is the package’s “identity card”:
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
mypackage/
├── DESCRIPTION      # package name, version, author, and dependencies
├── NAMESPACE        # which functions the package exposes to users
├── R/               # folder containing your actual .R function definition files
│   └── risk_functions.R
├── man/             # documentation files (usually auto-generated from comments)
└── tests/
    └── testthat/    # automated tests confirming your functions work correctly

Package: mypackage
Title: Public Health Risk Screening Helpers
Version: 0.1.0
Depends: R (>= 4.0)
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
160.2 Demonstration
A minimal DESCRIPTION file for a package built from this level’s functions might look like:
Package: pblhelpers
Title: Helper Functions from the R for Public Health Course
Version: 0.1.0
Author: Your Name
Description: Reusable risk-screening and data-cleaning helpers developed
    throughout the R for Public Health PBL course.
Depends: R (>= 4.0)
Imports: dplyr
Each function (like is_at_risk() from Level 9, or make_threshold_checker() from Page 2) would live in its own .R file inside the package’s R/ folder — exactly the same function definitions already written throughout this course, just relocated into this standard structure.
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
160.3 Explore
A documentation comment (using roxygen2 syntax) placed directly above a function automatically generates that function’s help page:

#' Check whether a participant is at elevated risk
#'
#' @param age Participant's age in years
#' @param sbp Participant's systolic blood pressure
#' @param age_cutoff Age threshold for risk (default 65)
#' @param bp_cutoff Blood pressure threshold for risk (default 130)
#' @return A logical vector indicating risk status
is_at_risk <- function(age, sbp, age_cutoff = 65, bp_cutoff = 130) {
  age >= age_cutoff & sbp >= bp_cutoff
}
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
160.4 Solve the Problem
A basic automated test for is_at_risk(), using the testthat package’s conventions, would look like this inside tests/testthat/test-is_at_risk.R:

library(testthat)

test_that("is_at_risk correctly flags high-risk participants", {
  expect_true(is_at_risk(age = 70, sbp = 145))
  expect_false(is_at_risk(age = 50, sbp = 145))
  expect_equal(sum(is_at_risk(age = c(70, 50), sbp = c(145, 145))), 1)
})

Test passed
This test encodes exactly the same logic Level 9 verified by hand: a 70-year-old with SBP 145 should be flagged at-risk (age_cutoff = 65, bp_cutoff = 130 are both exceeded), while a 50-year-old with the same SBP should not (the age cutoff isn’t met). Running testthat::test_dir("tests/testthat") would automatically re-run every such test and report which ones pass or fail.
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
160.5 Interpret the Result
A package isn’t fundamentally different code from what you’ve already written throughout this course — it’s the same functions, wrapped in a standardized structure (DESCRIPTION, R/, documentation, tests) that lets tools like library() find, load, and verify them reliably. This structure is exactly what makes it possible for dplyr, ggplot2, and every other package used throughout this course to be installed with one line and trusted to work as documented — the same discipline (clear documentation, automated tests) that makes their code trustworthy is available to your own functions too.

Common Mistakes


Believing a package must be published to CRAN to be useful. A package only needs the standard folder structure to be installed locally (via devtools::install() or R CMD INSTALL) and shared with a small team — CRAN submission is an entirely separate, optional step for public distribution.

Skipping automated tests because functions “already work.” A function working correctly today, tested manually once, is different from having a test that automatically re-confirms it still works after every future change — exactly the safety net testthat provides.

Writing documentation as an afterthought, disconnected from the code. roxygen2-style comments (placed directly above each function) keep documentation and code physically together, making it far more likely documentation stays accurate as a function changes over time.
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
160.6 Practice Problem
Write a roxygen2-style documentation comment (like the is_at_risk() example above) for Page 2’s make_threshold_checker() function, describing its cutoff parameter and what it returns.
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
160.7 Challenge Problem
Sketch out (in comments or a short written plan, not necessarily runnable code) what a tests/testthat/test-pct_missing.R file would need to contain to properly test Level 9’s pct_missing() function — what specific input/output pairs would make good test cases, including at least one edge case (like a vector with no missing values at all, or all missing values)?
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package
text:
160.8 Key Takeaways

An R package is a standardized folder structure (DESCRIPTION, NAMESPACE, R/, man/, tests/) that library() and install.packages() know how to read and load.

roxygen2-style comments placed directly above a function generate its documentation automatically, keeping code and documentation together.

testthat provides a standard way to write automated tests that re-confirm a function still works correctly, every time it (or its dependencies) change.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What is the purpose of a package’s DESCRIPTION file?

It contains the actual function code
It stores the package’s name, version, author, and dependencies
It automatically tests every function
It is only needed for packages published on CRAN


True or false. A package must be submitted to CRAN before it can be installed and used locally.
Predict the output. If a testthat test calls expect_true(is_at_risk(age = 70, sbp = 145)) and is_at_risk() correctly returns TRUE for those inputs, would the test pass or fail?
Short answer. Why is keeping documentation (via roxygen2 comments) directly above a function’s code better than writing documentation in a completely separate file?


Show answers


b) It stores the package’s name, version, author, and dependencies — its “identity card.”

False. A package only needs the standard folder structure to be installed locally and shared with a team — CRAN submission is a separate, optional step.
Pass — the test’s expectation (TRUE) matches the function’s actual output for those inputs.
Keeping documentation directly above the code it describes makes it far more likely to be updated whenever the function itself changes, reducing the risk of documentation silently becoming outdated or inaccurate over time.
crumbs: 160  The Anatomy of an R Package

title: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit
crumbs: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit

title: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit
text:
161.2 Demonstration

batch <- read_clinic_folder("data/clinics")

batch

Clinic batch: 5/5 files read successfully (100 rows total)

class(batch)

[1] "clinic_batch"
crumbs: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit

title: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit
text:
161.3 Explore
Try running read_clinic_folder() against a folder containing a deliberately broken or missing file — copy data/clinics/ to a new folder, add a non-CSV file, or remove read permissions from one file, and observe how batch$failed_files reports exactly which file(s) failed, without crashing the whole function.
crumbs: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit

title: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit
crumbs: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit

title: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit
text:
161.5 Interpret the Result
This capstone function is a genuine step up from Level 10’s automation: it doesn’t just automate a repeated task, it does so resiliently, reporting exactly what succeeded and what didn’t through a clean, custom-printed S3 object rather than a raw data frame or a script that halts on the first problem. This combination — regex-based file discovery, graceful error handling, and an informative custom result type — is close to what a genuinely reusable, shareable R function (Page 10’s package structure) should look like.

Common Mistakes


Using <- instead of <<- inside the tryCatch() error handler. failed <<- c(failed, csv_files[i]) uses the super-assignment operator (Page 1’s environments) specifically because the error handler runs in its own function environment — a plain <- would create a new local failed variable instead of updating the one the loop can see afterward.

Forgetting do.call(rbind, successful) fails if successful is empty. If every single file fails to read, successful is an empty list, and do.call(rbind, list()) produces NULL rather than an error — code using batch$data afterward should check for this case before assuming it’s a proper data frame.

Not testing the failure path at all. It’s tempting to only test a function like this against files that work — deliberately testing against a missing or malformed file (as the Explore section suggests) is the only way to confirm the error-handling logic actually behaves as intended.
crumbs: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit

title: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit
text:
161.6 Practice Problem
Modify print.clinic_batch() to also report the mean systolic_bp across all successfully-combined rows, alongside the existing file-count summary.
crumbs: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit

title: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit
text:
161.7 Challenge Problem
Add a summary.clinic_batch() S3 method (Page 3’s pattern) that returns a data frame with one row per county, showing each county’s row count and mean BMI — effectively reproducing Level 5’s group_by(county) %>% summarise() output, but generated from this capstone’s resilient file-reading function instead of a single pre-combined CSV.
crumbs: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit

title: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit
text:
161.8 Key Takeaways

A genuinely resilient automation function combines several ideas at once: pattern-based file discovery (regex), graceful error handling (tryCatch()), and an informative, custom-printed result object (S3) — rather than any single technique alone.

<<- (super-assignment) is often necessary inside a tryCatch() error handler or other nested function, specifically to update a variable in an enclosing environment rather than creating a new local one.
Testing a function’s failure path deliberately (not just its success path) is the only reliable way to confirm error-handling logic actually works as intended.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Why does the error handler inside read_clinic_folder() use failed <<- c(failed, csv_files[i]) instead of failed <- c(failed, csv_files[i])?


<<- is required inside every tryCatch() call

<<- updates the failed variable in the enclosing function’s environment, rather than creating a new local one

<- doesn’t work with character vectors
There is no actual difference between the two


True or false. This capstone’s function will crash entirely if even one file in the folder is unreadable.
Predict the output. If read_clinic_folder() is run on a folder with 5 valid CSV files, what would batch$n_files_read equal?
Short answer. Why is combining regex, error handling, and an S3 result object a meaningfully more resilient design than Level 10’s original automation capstone?


Show answers


b) <<- updates the failed variable in the enclosing function’s environment, rather than creating a new local variable that would disappear once the error handler finishes.

False. The whole point of wrapping each file’s read.csv() call in tryCatch() is that one failed file is skipped and recorded, while the rest of the loop continues normally.
5 — all 5 valid files would be successfully read.
Level 10’s original version assumed every file would always be present and well-formed, and would crash entirely on the first bad file; this version discovers files by pattern, survives individual file failures gracefully, and reports exactly what happened through a clean, custom-printed summary object — a combination that degrades gracefully instead of failing all-or-nothing.
crumbs: 161  Level 13 Capstone: A Resilient File-Reading Toolkit

title: Level 14 — Reproducible Research
text: What this level covers

title: Level 14 — Reproducible Research
text:
R Markdown vs. Quarto, and why this course is written in the latter
YAML front matter — the metadata block controlling how a document renders, examined using this course’s own _quarto.yml
Code chunks, chunk options, and inline code that weaves computed values directly into prose
Tables and figures with proper captions
Cross-references — the same {#sec-...} labels every page in this course already uses
Citations and bibliographies with a .bib file
Parameterized reports — one template, rendered differently for each county’s clinic data
Choosing HTML, PDF, or Word as an output format
The difference between a single document, a website, and a full book project — like this one


A preview mission
Your mission across this level: build a parameterized clinic report template that can regenerate a polished, county-specific summary for any of Level 10’s five clinic files, just by changing one parameter — no copy-pasting a script five times.







A note on scope



This level teaches Quarto’s document-authoring features using this course’s own source files as real, working examples — you’ll recognize the YAML, section labels, and code-chunk patterns you’ve been reading in every single lesson. Full package publishing, Git-based collaboration, and continuous-integration pipelines are beyond this course’s scope, but everything here transfers directly to a real RStudio + Quarto workflow.

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
162.1 The R Concept
Both R Markdown (.Rmd) and Quarto (.qmd) combine plain-text Markdown formatting with executable code chunks, then render the whole thing into a finished document:
Quarto is R Markdown’s successor: built by the same company (Posit, formerly RStudio), but language-agnostic from the ground up — the exact same .qmd file format and rendering engine works identically for R, Python, Julia, or Observable JavaScript code chunks, not just R. This course happens to use only R, but every .qmd file you’ve read could just as easily mix in a Python code chunk without switching tools.

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
# R Markdown chunk syntax
ˋˋˋ{r, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="A caption"}
plot(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp)
ˋˋˋ

# Quarto chunk syntax
ˋˋˋ{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "A caption"
plot(participants$age, participants$systolic_bp)
ˋˋˋ

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
162.2 Demonstration

# This course's own file 09-simple-linear-regression.qmd contains chunks like:

ˋˋˋ{r}
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
bp_age_model <- lm(systolic_bp ~ age, data = participants)
summary(bp_age_model)
ˋˋˋ
Rendering this .qmd file (via "Render" in RStudio, or `quarto render`) executes
every code chunk in order, captures its printed output, and weaves the results
back into the surrounding prose — producing the finished HTML page you're
reading right now.

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
162.3 Explore
Open any earlier page in this course’s source files (for example, 11-statistics/09-simple-linear-regression.qmd) and notice its structure directly: plain Markdown headings (##), fenced ::: {.pbl-box} callout divs, and R code chunks — all in one plain-text file, with no special editor required to write it.

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
162.4 Solve the Problem

# Quarto's chunk-option syntax (the "#|" lines, called "YAML chunk options")
# is the main visible difference from R Markdown's older
# curly-brace syntax:

{r, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.width=6}   # R Markdown style (older)

#| echo: false                                 # Quarto style (this course)
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 6
Both achieve the same result - hiding the code, suppressing package-load
messages, and setting a figure width - but Quarto's line-by-line "#|" syntax
is easier to read, edit, and extend with new options over time, and works
identically no matter which programming language the chunk contains.

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
162.5 Interpret the Result
Quarto isn’t a completely different tool from R Markdown — it’s a more general, better-organized evolution of the same core idea: plain text plus executable code, woven into one reproducible document. This course uses Quarto specifically because its unified #| chunk-option syntax and multi-language support make it the more future-proof choice, even though every concept in this level (chunks, YAML, citations, cross-references) has a direct R Markdown equivalent too.

Common Mistakes


Assuming .Rmd and .qmd files can’t be mixed in the same project. They can coexist, and Quarto can render .Rmd files too — teams migrating from R Markdown don’t need to rewrite every existing file overnight.

Thinking Quarto only works with R. Quarto’s multi-language support (R, Python, Julia, Observable) is one of its core design goals, not an afterthought — a single .qmd book could mix R and Python chapters.

Believing the choice of .Rmd vs. .qmd changes the underlying statistical or data-cleaning skills. Every technique from Levels 1-13 (functions, tidyverse verbs, regression, loops) works identically inside either format — this level is about the surrounding document, not the analysis itself.

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
162.6 Practice Problem
Find any two other .qmd files from earlier in this course and compare their chunk syntax — do they all consistently use Quarto’s #| option style, or do you spot any R Markdown-style curly-brace options?

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
162.7 Challenge Problem
Write out, in R Markdown’s older curly-brace syntax, the chunk options you’d need to reproduce Level 11’s 09-simple-linear-regression.qmd demonstration chunk (which currently just uses default Quarto options) if you wanted to additionally hide the code and set a figure width of 7 inches.

title: 162  R Markdown vs. Quarto
text:
162.8 Key Takeaways

Both .Rmd and .qmd files weave plain-text Markdown together with executable code chunks, then render the mixture into a finished document.
Quarto is R Markdown’s more general successor: the same rendering engine and #| chunk-option syntax works identically across R, Python, Julia, and Observable JavaScript.
This entire course — every page you’ve read — is itself written as a collection of .qmd files, rendered by the exact tool this level teaches.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What is the main difference between Quarto and R Markdown?

Quarto cannot execute any code
Quarto is a more general, multi-language successor to R Markdown, using a unified #| chunk-option syntax
R Markdown can only produce PDF output
There is no meaningful difference between them


True or false. A single Quarto document can only ever contain R code, never Python or Julia code.
Predict the output. If a .qmd file’s code chunk includes #| echo: false, would the underlying R code be shown in the rendered document?
Short answer. Why might a team just starting a new reproducible-reporting project today choose Quarto over R Markdown?


Show answers


b) Quarto is a more general, multi-language successor to R Markdown, using a unified #| chunk-option syntax.

False. Quarto is explicitly designed to support R, Python, Julia, and Observable JavaScript code chunks within the same document or project.
No — #| echo: false hides the code itself while still showing its output, exactly like R Markdown’s echo=FALSE option.
Quarto’s multi-language support future-proofs a project against needing to switch languages later, and its unified, more readable chunk-option syntax and built-in support for books, websites, and multiple output formats make it a more general foundation for reproducible reporting than R Markdown’s R-specific design.

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
163.1 The R Concept
YAML (“YAML Ain’t Markup Language”) is a plain-text format for structured configuration, using indentation instead of curly braces. A single .qmd file’s YAML front matter sits between --- markers at the very top:
A whole Quarto book or website project has its own project-wide _quarto.yml file (not inside any individual page) controlling settings shared across every chapter — exactly the file that has been configuring this entire course since Level 1.

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
---
title: "My Report"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
format: html
---

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
163.2 Demonstration
# An excerpt from this course's own R-PBL/_quarto.yml:
book:
  title: "R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course"
  subtitle: "From Zero to Advanced R for Epidemiology, Biostatistics, and Health Data Science"
  author: "Public Health Data Science Program"
  date: today
  search: true
  sidebar:
    style: floating
    collapse-level: 2
    pinned: true

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
163.3 Explore
Open R-PBL/_quarto.yml directly and find the format: section — notice it defines settings for html: (theme, table of contents depth, code-folding) and pdf: (document class) separately, letting the exact same book content render into two very different-looking output formats from one shared source.

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
163.4 Solve the Problem
# This course's actual format: section, controlling every page's HTML rendering:
format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: flatly
      dark: darkly
    css: styles.css
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: false
    highlight-style: github
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrbook
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
Every single page in this course - every lesson, every callout box, every
knowledge check - inherits these exact settings automatically: a
table of contents 3 levels deep, numbered sections, the "flatly"/"darkly"
Bootswatch themes, and GitHub-style code highlighting - all from this one
shared configuration block, rather than being repeated 100+ times.
A single page’s own YAML (if it needed a setting different from the book’s shared default) could override just that one setting — for example, a specific page could add its own ---\nfig-width: 8\n----style front matter to widen its figures only, without affecting any other page.

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
163.5 Interpret the Result
YAML front matter is what separates a document’s content from its presentation and configuration — this course’s 100+ lesson pages contain zero repeated title/theme/format setup, because all of that lives once, in _quarto.yml, and applies automatically everywhere. This is the same “don’t repeat yourself” principle behind Level 9’s functions and Level 13’s packages, applied to document configuration instead of code.

Common Mistakes

Getting YAML indentation wrong. Unlike R code, YAML’s structure is defined entirely by consistent indentation (usually 2 spaces per level) — misaligning even one line can silently break the whole configuration or attach a setting to the wrong parent.
Confusing a single document’s front matter with a project-wide _quarto.yml. A lone .qmd file’s own ----delimited YAML controls just that file; a book or website project’s shared settings belong in _quarto.yml at the project’s root, not repeated in every chapter.
Forgetting quotes around titles containing special characters. A title containing a colon (like this course’s "R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course") needs to be wrapped in quotes — without them, YAML may misinterpret the colon as introducing a new key.

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
163.6 Practice Problem
Write the YAML front matter for a single, standalone .qmd report titled "Monthly Blood Pressure Summary", authored by you, rendering to HTML with a table of contents.

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
163.7 Challenge Problem
Look at this course’s _quarto.yml execute: section (near the bottom of the file). Explain, in your own words, what each of its four settings (echo, warning, message, freeze) controls for every single code chunk across the entire book, and why setting them once here is more efficient than repeating them in every chunk.

title: 163  YAML Front Matter
text:
163.8 Key Takeaways

YAML front matter (between --- markers) is a document’s metadata block, controlling title, author, and output format using indentation-based structure.
A Quarto book or website project’s shared, project-wide settings live in a single _quarto.yml file, not repeated inside every individual chapter.
Centralizing configuration in YAML (rather than repeating it in every document) mirrors the same “don’t repeat yourself” principle behind functions (Level 9) and packages (Level 13).


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. Where does a Quarto book’s shared, project-wide configuration live?

Repeated at the top of every chapter file
In a single _quarto.yml file at the project’s root
Inside the first code chunk of the book
It cannot be configured at all

True or false. YAML uses curly braces {} to define nested structure, the same way R code does.
Predict the output. If this course’s _quarto.yml sets toc-depth: 3 under format: html:, would that setting apply to every chapter, or only to chapters that repeat it themselves?
Short answer. Why does a title containing a colon, like "R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course", need to be wrapped in quotes in YAML?



Show answers


b) In a single _quarto.yml file at the project’s root, shared across every chapter.
False. YAML uses consistent indentation (not curly braces) to define nested structure.
Every chapter — settings in the project-wide _quarto.yml apply automatically across the whole book unless an individual chapter’s own front matter explicitly overrides them.
Without quotes, YAML could misinterpret the colon inside the title as introducing a new key-value pair, breaking the intended single string into invalid or incorrect structure.

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
164.1 The R Concept
A code chunk (the fenced ```{r} blocks you’ve seen throughout this course) executes R code and can display both the code and its output. Inline code, written as `r expression` directly inside a sentence, evaluates a short R expression and inserts just its result into the surrounding prose, with no separate output block at all.
renders as: “The mean participant age was 47.4 years.” If participants.csv changes, re-rendering the document updates that number automatically, with zero manual editing.
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
ˋˋˋ{r}
mean_age <- mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
ˋˋˋ

The mean participant age was ˋr round(mean_age, 1)ˋ years.
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
164.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
mean_age <- mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
n_participants <- nrow(participants)


# Written in prose as:
# "Across `r n_participants` enrolled participants, the mean age was
#  `r round(mean_age, 1)` years."

Across 100 enrolled participants, the mean age was 47.4 years.
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
164.3 Explore
Try changing a chunk option to #| echo: false on the setup chunk above. The code itself disappears from the rendered output, while the inline-code results computed from it (n_participants, mean_age) still render correctly in the surrounding prose. This is exactly how this course’s own prose sections (like this one) can quote precise figures without necessarily showing every calculation chunk.
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
164.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

n_participants <- nrow(participants)
mean_age <- mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)
pct_smokers <- round(100 * mean(participants$smoker == "Yes"), 1)


# Prose using three separate inline-code values from the same chunk:
# "This report summarizes `r n_participants` participants, with a mean
#  age of `r round(mean_age, 1)` years; `r pct_smokers`% identified as
#  current smokers."

This report summarizes 100 participants, with a mean age of 47.4 years;
25% identified as current smokers.
Every one of these three numbers (100, 47.4, 25) came from `r`-prefixed inline code reading directly from the same participants data frame already loaded in a prior chunk. None of them is typed as a hardcoded number anywhere in the source file.
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
164.5 Interpret the Result
Inline code turns a report’s numbers from static, easily-outdated text into live values tied directly to the underlying data, exactly the property that makes a document genuinely reproducible, rather than merely readable. If participants.csv gained 20 new enrollees tomorrow, re-rendering this exact same source file would automatically update every one of these three figures, with no prose editing required at all.

Common Mistakes


Hardcoding a number in prose instead of using inline code. Typing “47.4 years” directly, rather than `r round(mean_age, 1)`, looks identical in the rendered output today, but silently becomes wrong the moment the underlying data changes, with no warning.

Forgetting inline code needs the variable already computed in an earlier chunk. `r mean_age` will error if mean_age was never actually calculated and assigned in a code chunk that ran before that point in the document.

Not rounding inline numeric results. `r mean(participants$age, na.rm = TRUE)` without round() could display an ugly, overly precise value like 47.42553191489362 directly in a sentence. Always round inline values to a sensible number of digits for prose.
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
164.6 Practice Problem
Write a code chunk computing the number of unique counties in participants$county, then write one sentence of prose using inline code to report that count directly (for example: “Participants were enrolled from `r …` counties.”).
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
164.7 Challenge Problem
Level 7’s cross-tabulation found 42 non-smoking women and 10 smoking women. Write a code chunk computing both counts directly from participants.csv, then write a sentence using two separate inline-code expressions reporting both figures, formatted so the sentence would remain fully correct even if the underlying data changed.
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code
text:
164.8 Key Takeaways

A code chunk executes R code and can display both the code and its output; inline code (`r expression`) evaluates a short expression and inserts just its result directly into surrounding prose.
Numbers embedded via inline code stay automatically correct when underlying data changes; numbers typed directly as text do not.
Always round inline numeric results to a sensible number of digits before they appear in prose, exactly as this course’s own pages consistently round every reported statistic.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What is the main advantage of inline code (`r expression`) over typing a computed value directly as text?

Inline code renders faster
Inline code automatically updates if the underlying data changes; typed text does not
Inline code requires no prior chunk to have run
There is no difference between the two


True or false. Inline code can only be used inside a code chunk, never inside regular prose text.
Predict the output. If n_participants <- nrow(participants) evaluates to 100 in an earlier chunk, what would `r n_participants` display in the rendered prose?
Short answer. Why is hardcoding a statistic like “47.4 years” as plain text a reproducibility risk, even if it’s correct at the moment it’s written?


Show answers


b) Inline code automatically updates if the underlying data changes, while typed text stays frozen at whatever value was originally written.

False. Inline code (`r expression`) is specifically designed to be used directly inside prose text, not inside a fenced code chunk.

100. The inline code evaluates the already-computed variable and inserts its value directly into the sentence.
A hardcoded number is only correct until the underlying data changes. Nothing automatically flags it as stale, so a report can silently continue displaying an outdated figure long after it stops being true, unless every hardcoded value is manually re-checked and updated by hand.
crumbs: 164  Code Chunks and Inline Code

title: 165  Tables and Figures
text:
165.1 The R Concept
knitr::kable() turns a data frame into a properly formatted table in the rendered output, and chunk options add a caption:
knitr::kable(county_summary)
For figures, the #| fig-cap chunk option attaches a caption directly to a plot’s output, and #| fig-width / #| fig-height control its size:
ggplot(participants, aes(x = age, y = systolic_bp, color = smoker)) +
  geom_point()

title: 165  Tables and Figures
text:
165.2 Demonstration

library(dplyr)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

county_summary <- participants |>
  group_by(county) |>
  summarise(n = n(), mean_sbp = round(mean(systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 1))

knitr::kable(county_summary)

|county      |  n| mean_sbp|
|:-----------|--:|--------:|
|Cedar Falls | 20|    128.2|
|Millbrook   | 21|    133.3|
|Oakford     | 17|    129.7|
|Riverdale   | 16|    124.4|
|Springvale  | 26|    131.5|

title: 165  Tables and Figures
text:
165.3 Explore
Try adding #| tbl-cap: "Mean systolic blood pressure by county" directly above this chunk (Quarto’s convention: chunk options go on lines starting with #|, immediately inside the code chunk). Rendered, the table gains a numbered caption like “Table 1: Mean systolic blood pressure by county” — and Page 5 shows how to cross-reference that exact table by number elsewhere in the same document.

title: 165  Tables and Figures
text:
165.4 Solve the Problem

library(ggplot2)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

ˋˋˋ{r}
#| fig-cap: "Systolic blood pressure vs. age, colored by smoking status"
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 5
ggplot(participants, aes(x = age, y = systolic_bp, color = smoker)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(x = "Age (years)", y = "Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)",
       title = "Blood Pressure vs. Age by Smoking Status")
ˋˋˋ
[Renders a captioned scatter plot, sized 7 by 5 inches, with the caption
 "Figure 1: Systolic blood pressure vs. age, colored by smoking status"
 automatically numbered and placed according to this document's
 fig-cap-location setting (bottom, per this course's own _quarto.yml).]
The #| fig-cap option attached a caption directly to the chunk producing the plot — no separate prose paragraph describing “the figure above” was needed, and the caption’s wording lives right next to the code that generates the figure it describes, keeping the two in sync automatically.

title: 165  Tables and Figures
text:
165.5 Interpret the Result
knitr::kable() and #| fig-cap/#| tbl-cap chunk options turn raw R output into report-ready tables and figures without leaving the reproducible-document workflow — no manual copy-pasting a plot into a separate image-editing tool, and no retyping a table’s numbers into a word processor. Both stay directly tied to the underlying code, so re-running the same document with updated data automatically regenerates correctly-captioned tables and figures, with zero manual re-formatting.

Common Mistakes


Printing a raw data frame instead of using knitr::kable(). Simply typing county_summary in a chunk shows R’s plain console-style output, not a clean, bordered table — knitr::kable() is what actually produces report-quality formatting.

Forgetting #| fig-cap and #| tbl-cap are chunk options, not something you type into prose. These captions must be placed as #|-prefixed lines directly inside the code chunk that produces the figure or table — writing “Figure 1: …” manually as prose bypasses Quarto’s automatic numbering and cross-referencing (Page 5) entirely.

Setting figure dimensions too large or small for the intended output format. A fig-width sensible for a wide HTML page might look cramped or oversized in a narrower PDF page layout — Level 12/13’s diagnostic plots and this level’s figures should be sized with the final rendered format in mind.

title: 165  Tables and Figures
text:
165.6 Practice Problem
Use knitr::kable() to render Level 7’s cross-tabulation of sex and smoker as a formatted table, with a caption reading “Smoking status by sex.”

title: 165  Tables and Figures
text:
165.7 Challenge Problem
Recreate Level 8’s faceted BMI-by-county histogram (from 09-facets.qmd) as a properly captioned figure with #| fig-cap, #| fig-width: 9, and #| fig-height: 6, and explain in one sentence why a faceted plot with 5 panels might need a larger fig-width than the single-panel scatter plot demonstrated on this page.

title: 165  Tables and Figures
text:
165.8 Key Takeaways


knitr::kable(data_frame) renders a data frame as a clean, properly formatted table in a rendered Quarto document, rather than plain console-style text.

#| fig-cap and #| tbl-cap chunk options attach automatically-numbered captions directly to the code chunk producing a figure or table.

#| fig-width and #| fig-height control a figure’s rendered size — worth adjusting based on the final output format (HTML vs. PDF) and the plot’s complexity (a single panel vs. several facets).


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does knitr::kable() do?

Creates a new data frame from a CSV file
Renders a data frame as a clean, formatted table in the output document
Automatically fits a regression model
Converts a table into a ggplot object


True or false. #| fig-cap must be written as prose text below the figure, not as a chunk option.
Predict the output. If a chunk includes #| tbl-cap: "Mean systolic blood pressure by county" above a knitr::kable() call, would the rendered table include an automatically-numbered caption?
Short answer. Why does keeping a figure’s caption inside the same code chunk that produces the figure (rather than as separate prose) help keep a document accurate over time?


Show answers


b) Renders a data frame as a clean, formatted table in the output document.

False. #| fig-cap is a chunk option, written as a #|-prefixed line directly inside the code chunk, not as separate prose text.
Yes — the rendered table would display with an automatically-numbered caption, “Table 1: Mean systolic blood pressure by county” (or similar, depending on numbering elsewhere in the document).
Keeping the caption directly with the code that generates the figure means the two can never drift out of sync — if the figure’s content changes, the caption sits right there as an obvious reminder to check whether its wording still accurately describes what’s shown.

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
166.1 The R Concept
Quarto automatically numbers headings, figures, and tables that carry a label — an anchor written directly in the heading text, or as a chunk option for figures/tables:
Referencing that label elsewhere, with an @ prefix, produces an automatically-numbered, always-correct link:
Figures and tables work the same way, using #fig- and #tbl- label prefixes instead of #sec-:
ggplot(participants, aes(age, systolic_bp)) + geom_point()

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
# Simple Linear Regression with `lm()` {#sec-simple-linear-regression}

As shown in @sec-simple-linear-regression, age has a statistically
significant relationship with blood pressure.


See @fig-bp-age-scatter for the full scatter plot.

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
166.2 Demonstration
# This page's own heading:
# Cross-References {#sec-cross-references}

# Referenced elsewhere in this same book, this would render as:
# "See Section 14.5 for details on cross-referencing." — with "14.5"
# generated and kept correct automatically, no matter how many
# pages are added or reordered before it.

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
166.3 Explore
Search this course’s own source files for {#sec-simple-linear-regression} (Level 11, Page 9’s heading label) — then search for every place @sec-simple-linear-regression appears elsewhere in the book (if any prose used this syntax instead of the plain-text “Page 9” phrasing this course actually uses). This distinction — labels that could be referenced automatically, versus plain-text mentions that happen to look similar — is exactly what this page teaches you to use going forward.

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
166.4 Solve the Problem
# In a new report, you might write:

## Blood Pressure Model {#sec-bp-model}

... model-fitting code and results ...

## Discussion

As shown in @sec-bp-model, age has a statistically significant, though
modest, relationship with blood pressure (R-squared = 0.049).
Rendered, "@sec-bp-model" becomes an automatically-generated, clickable
link reading something like "Section 3" (whatever number that section
actually ends up being) - and if a new section were inserted before it
later, the link's number would automatically update on the next render,
with zero manual editing required anywhere.

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
166.5 Interpret the Result
Cross-references replace fragile, manually-typed phrases like “see Page 9” (which silently becomes wrong the moment content is reordered) with labels that are checked and renumbered automatically every time the document renders. This is precisely why this course’s own prose can safely say “Level 11, Page 9” today — but a more robust version of this same course, written with @sec-... references throughout instead of hardcoded page descriptions, would never risk a broken reference no matter how many future levels get inserted or reordered.

Common Mistakes
