Writing a plain-text reference like “see the section above” instead of a proper @sec-... cross-reference. Plain text never updates automatically and doesn’t produce a clickable link — it also breaks silently the moment content is reordered, with no warning from Quarto at all.

Forgetting labels must start with a recognized prefix (#sec-, #fig-, #tbl-). A heading tagged {#regression-section} (missing the sec- prefix) won’t be recognized as a valid cross-referenceable section label by Quarto.

Using the same label twice in one document. Quarto requires every label to be unique within a project — accidentally reusing {#sec-bp-model} for two different headings will produce a rendering warning and an ambiguous, unreliable reference.

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
166.6 Practice Problem
Write a short two-section document outline (as plain text, not necessarily code) that labels its first section {#sec-methods} and its second section references it with @sec-methods inside a sentence.

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
166.7 Challenge Problem
This course’s own Level 11 capstone page (11-statistics/12-statistics-capstone.qmd) references “Level 7” and “Level 8” as plain-text phrases throughout, rather than @sec-... cross-references. Pick two such plain-text references in that page and rewrite them as proper Quarto cross-references, using the actual section labels defined in the corresponding Level 7 and Level 8 files (hint: check the {#sec-...} labels at the top of 07-descriptive-statistics/07-correlation.qmd and similar files).

title: 166  Cross-References
text:
166.8 Key Takeaways

A label like {#sec-my-heading} attached to a heading (or #| label: fig-my-plot / tbl-my-table for figures and tables) makes that element automatically numberable and referenceable.

@sec-my-heading (or @fig-.../@tbl-...) elsewhere in the same document produces an automatically-updating, clickable cross-reference — unlike a hardcoded “see Page 9” phrase.
Labels must be unique within a project and use a recognized prefix (sec-, fig-, tbl-) for Quarto to treat them as valid cross-reference targets.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What is the main advantage of @sec-my-heading over writing “see Page 9” as plain text?

It renders faster
It automatically stays correct even if content is reordered or renumbered
It requires no heading label at all
It only works in PDF output


True or false. Two different headings in the same document can safely share the identical label.
Predict the output. If a heading is labeled {#sec-results} and referenced elsewhere as @sec-results, what happens to that reference if three new sections are inserted before it?
Short answer. Why does a label need to start with a recognized prefix like sec-, fig-, or tbl- rather than an arbitrary name?


Show answers


b) It automatically stays correct even if content is reordered or renumbered, unlike a hardcoded page reference.

False. Labels must be unique within a project — duplicate labels produce an ambiguous, unreliable cross-reference and a rendering warning.
Nothing breaks — the @sec-results reference automatically renders with whatever the new, correct section number is after the insertion, with no manual editing needed.
The prefix tells Quarto what type of element the label refers to (a section, a figure, or a table), which determines how it’s numbered and what word (“Section,” “Figure,” “Table”) appears in the automatically-generated reference text.

title: 167  Citations and Bibliographies
text:
167.1 The R Concept
A .bib file stores citation information in a structured format, with one entry per source, each tagged with a unique citation key:
A document’s YAML front matter points to this file with bibliography:, and citing a source in prose uses @ followed by its key:
Quarto automatically inserts a properly formatted in-text citation and generates a complete, correctly formatted bibliography at the very end of the rendered document — no manual formatting required.

title: 167  Citations and Bibliographies
167  Citations and Bibliographies
text:
167.2 Demonstration
# This course's own R-PBL/references.bib now contains three entries:
@Book{wickham2016ggplot2, ... }     # the ggplot2 package (Level 8)
@Manual{rcoreteam, ... }            # R itself
@Book{wickham2023r4ds, ... }        # a widely-used R reference textbook
# Cited in prose:
# "This course's visualizations rely on the grammar of graphics
#  [@wickham2016ggplot2], implemented in R [@rcoreteam]."
Rendered: "This course's visualizations rely on the grammar of graphics
(Wickham 2016), implemented in R (R Core Team)." — with a full,
correctly-formatted bibliography automatically appended at the document's
end, listing both sources in the citation style the document's format
specifies.

title: 167  Citations and Bibliographies
text:
167.3 Explore
Try citing the same key, @wickham2016ggplot2, a second time somewhere else in the same document. Notice the bibliography at the end still lists it only once — Quarto automatically deduplicates the reference list, no matter how many times a source is cited throughout the document.

title: 167  Citations and Bibliographies
text:
167.4 Solve the Problem
---
title: "Blood Pressure and Age: A Reproducible Summary"
bibliography: references.bib
---
Data cleaning and reshaping throughout this analysis used the tidyverse
[@wickham2023r4ds], and all figures were produced with `ggplot2`'s
grammar of graphics [@wickham2016ggplot2], running under R itself
[@rcoreteam].

## References
Rendered, the "## References" heading is followed automatically by:

Wickham, H. (2016). ggplot2: Elegant Graphics for Data Analysis (2nd ed.).
    Springer-Verlag New York.

Wickham, H., Çetinkaya-Rundel, M., & Grolemund, G. (2023). R for Data
    Science (2nd ed.). O'Reilly Media.

R Core Team. R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing.
    R Foundation for Statistical Computing, Vienna, Austria.
Three separate @key citations in the prose automatically produced three correctly-formatted, alphabetized bibliography entries — with zero manual formatting of author names, years, or publishers anywhere in the source document.

title: 167  Citations and Bibliographies
text:
167.5 Interpret the Result
Citing sources this way keeps a document’s bibliography perfectly synchronized with what’s actually cited in the text: adding a new @key citation anywhere in the prose automatically adds its entry to the reference list, and removing the last citation to a source automatically removes it from the bibliography too. This eliminates an entire category of errors common in manually-maintained reference lists — citations that don’t appear in the bibliography, or bibliography entries for sources never actually cited.

Common Mistakes

Forgetting the bibliography: field in YAML front matter. Without it, @key citations in the prose won’t be recognized at all — they’ll render as literal, unformatted text like @wickham2016ggplot2 instead of a proper citation.
Using a citation key that doesn’t exactly match an entry in the .bib file. Citation keys are case-sensitive and must match exactly — @Wickham2016ggplot2 (capital W) would fail to match an entry defined as wickham2016ggplot2 (lowercase w).
Manually typing a bibliography instead of letting Quarto generate one. A hand-typed reference list at a document’s end can drift out of sync with what’s actually cited — always let a ## References heading (with no content beneath it) mark where Quarto should insert its automatically-generated list instead.

title: 167  Citations and Bibliographies
text:
167.6 Practice Problem
Add a new entry to references.bib for a source of your choosing (a textbook, a package, or a paper), then cite it once in a sentence using its @key.

title: 167  Citations and Bibliographies
text:
167.7 Challenge Problem
This course’s own book (R-PBL/_quarto.yml) doesn’t currently include a bibliography: field. Explain what YAML line(s) you’d need to add to _quarto.yml (not an individual page) to make every chapter throughout this entire book able to cite entries from references.bib, and why adding it at the book level (rather than repeating it in every chapter file) is the better approach.

title: 167  Citations and Bibliographies
text:
167.8 Key Takeaways

A .bib file stores structured citation data, with each source tagged by a unique, reusable citation key.
bibliography: references.bib in YAML front matter enables @key-style citations in prose, which Quarto automatically formats and inserts.
A ## References heading with no content beneath it marks where Quarto should automatically generate the complete, deduplicated bibliography — no manual reference-list formatting required.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What does [@wickham2016ggplot2] do when placed in a document’s prose?

It inserts the literal text “wickham2016ggplot2”
It inserts a properly formatted in-text citation and adds the source to the bibliography
It deletes the reference from the bibliography
It only works inside a code chunk

True or false. If a source is cited three separate times in the same document, it will appear three times in the final bibliography.
Predict the output. If a document’s YAML is missing the bibliography: field entirely, what would [@wickham2016ggplot2] render as?
Short answer. Why does citing sources with @key syntax (rather than manually typing a reference list) reduce the risk of an inconsistent or out-of-sync bibliography?



Show answers


b) It inserts a properly formatted in-text citation and adds the corresponding source to the automatically-generated bibliography.
False. Quarto automatically deduplicates the bibliography — a source cited multiple times still appears only once in the final reference list.
Literal, unformatted text like @wickham2016ggplot2 — without a bibliography: field pointing to a .bib file, Quarto has no source information to format the citation against.
Because the bibliography is generated automatically from whatever @key citations actually appear in the prose, there’s no separate, hand-maintained reference list that could drift out of sync — adding or removing a citation in the text automatically adds or removes its corresponding bibliography entry.

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
168.1 The R Concept
Quarto documents can declare parameters in YAML front matter, then reference them inside code chunks via params$name:
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
clinic_data <- participants[participants$county == params$county, ]
Rendering with a different parameter value — via quarto render report.qmd -P county:"Millbrook", or quarto::quarto_render("report.qmd", execute_params = list(county = "Millbrook")) from within R — produces a completely different, correctly-filtered report from the exact same source file, with zero code duplication.

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
---
title: "Clinic Summary Report"
params:
  county: "Oakford"
---

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
168.2 Demonstration
---
title: "Clinic Summary: ˋr params$countyˋ"
params:
  county: "Oakford"
---

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
clinic_data <- participants[participants$county == params$county, ]

nrow(clinic_data)
mean(clinic_data$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 17
[1] 129.7059

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
168.3 Explore
Try changing just the county: value in the YAML front matter above to "Millbrook" (leaving every line of R code completely unchanged) and mentally re-run the two lines — nrow(clinic_data) and mean(clinic_data$systolic_bp) would automatically compute Millbrook’s own count and mean instead, entirely because params$county changed.

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
168.4 Solve the Problem
---
title: "Clinic Summary Report"
params:
  county: "Oakford"
---

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
clinic_data <- participants[participants$county == params$county, ]

n_patients <- nrow(clinic_data)
mean_sbp <- round(mean(clinic_data$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
mean_bmi <- round(mean(clinic_data$bmi, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
n_smokers <- sum(clinic_data$smoker == "Yes")


# Prose using inline code and the params value directly:
# "**`r params$county` Clinic Summary**: `r n_patients` patients,
#  mean SBP `r mean_sbp` mmHg, mean BMI `r mean_bmi`, `r n_smokers`
#  current smokers."

**Oakford Clinic Summary**: 17 patients, mean SBP 129.7 mmHg,
mean BMI 28, 6 current smokers.
Rendering the identical template with params: county: "Riverdale" instead would automatically produce “Riverdale Clinic Summary: 16 patients, mean SBP 124.4 mmHg, …” — the exact same code and prose template, filtered and re-summarized entirely differently, without a single line being rewritten.

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
168.5 Interpret the Result
Parameterized reports turn “one report per group” from a copy-paste-and-edit chore into a single, reusable template rendered multiple times with different inputs — exactly the same principle behind Level 9’s functions (is_at_risk(age_cutoff = 65)) and Level 13’s closures, now applied to an entire document instead of a single value. This is precisely how a real public-health team would generate 5 consistent clinic reports (or 50 monthly reports, or a report per country) without maintaining 5 separate, subtly-diverging copies of the same file.

Common Mistakes


Hardcoding "Oakford" directly in the filtering code instead of using params$county. Writing participants[participants$county == "Oakford", ] defeats the entire purpose — the report would always show Oakford’s data, no matter what parameter value is supplied at render time.

Forgetting the parameter’s default value in YAML is what’s used when rendering interactively (e.g., clicking “Render” in RStudio). The -P county:"Millbrook" command-line override (or execute_params in R) is only needed when rendering programmatically with a different value than the YAML default.

Not validating that the parameter value actually exists in the data. params$county <- "Notreal County" would silently produce an empty clinic_data data frame (0 rows) with no error — a production-quality version would check params$county %in% unique(participants$county) first (Level 9’s stop(), Page 4’s tryCatch()) and fail loudly if not.

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
168.6 Practice Problem
Modify the template above so that instead of filtering by county, it accepts a smoker_only logical parameter (default FALSE) that, when TRUE, filters participants to only current smokers before computing summary statistics.

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
168.7 Challenge Problem
Write the R code (using a for loop, Level 10) that would render this parameterized clinic report 5 times — once for each of the 5 counties in participants$county — saving each rendered report with a filename that includes the county name, using quarto::quarto_render()’s execute_params and output_file arguments.

title: 168  Parameterized Reports
text:
168.8 Key Takeaways


params: in YAML front matter declares a document parameter with a default value; params$name accesses it anywhere in the document’s code chunks or inline code.
Rendering the same template with different parameter values (via the command line, or quarto::quarto_render(execute_params = ...)) produces different, correctly-filtered reports without duplicating any code.
Parameterized reports extend Level 9’s function-argument thinking and Level 13’s closures to an entire document, replacing repetitive copy-paste-and-edit reporting with one reusable, tested template.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What does params$county do inside a parameterized Quarto document?

Creates a new column in the dataset called “county”
Accesses the current value of the county parameter, as declared in YAML
Automatically filters every data frame in the document
Renders the document once for every unique county automatically


True or false. A parameterized report must be manually edited each time it’s rendered with a different parameter value.
Predict the output. If params$county is set to "Riverdale" and participants has 16 Riverdale rows, what would nrow(clinic_data) return, assuming clinic_data filters by params$county?
Short answer. Why is hardcoding "Oakford" directly into a report’s filtering code a mistake, even if Oakford happens to be the report’s intended focus today?


Show answers


b) Accesses the current value of the county parameter, as declared in the document’s YAML front matter.

False. The entire point of parameters is that the same template can be rendered with different values (via command line or execute_params) with zero manual editing.
16 — matching Level 10’s known Riverdale row count.
Hardcoding the value defeats the purpose of parameterization — the report would always show Oakford’s data regardless of what parameter is supplied, making it impossible to reuse the same template for any other county without editing and duplicating the file.

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
169.1 The R Concept
The format: key in YAML front matter (or a project’s _quarto.yml) selects one or more output formats for the exact same source content:
Rendering with multiple formats listed produces one output file per format (report.html, report.pdf, report.docx) from the identical source — no duplicated content, no separate Word-specific or PDF-specific version to maintain.

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
---
title: "Clinic Summary Report"
format: html
---
---
title: "Clinic Summary Report"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
  docx: default
---

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
169.2 Demonstration
# This course's own R-PBL/_quarto.yml format: section (Page 2), simplified:
format:
  html:
    theme:
      light: flatly
      dark: darkly
    toc: true
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrbook
    toc: true
Running `quarto render` on this book produces both a complete website
(the interactive version you're reading, with clickable knowledge-check
answers) AND a complete PDF book (with the same content laid out for
print, though `<details>`-style collapsible answers render as always-
visible text in PDF, since paper has no "click to reveal" concept).

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
169.3 Explore
Try adding docx: default as a third sibling entry under this course’s format: section (indented at the same level as html: and pdf:). Rendering afterward would additionally produce a .docx version of every chapter — useful for a collaborator who wants to leave Word’s native tracked-changes comments directly on the content.

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
169.4 Solve the Problem
---
title: "Clinic Summary Report"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  docx:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---
Rendering this single source file with `quarto render report.qmd --to all`
(or specifying each format individually) produces:

  report.html   - interactive web page, best for browsing and sharing links
  report.pdf    - fixed-layout document, best for printing or formal archiving
  report.docx   - editable Word document, best for collaborators who need
                  to leave tracked-changes comments
All three files come from rendering the exact same underlying .qmd source and code chunks — the actual analysis, tables, and figures are computed once and formatted three different ways, never duplicated by hand.

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
169.5 Interpret the Result
Choosing an output format is about matching the audience’s needs, not rewriting content: HTML suits interactive browsing and easy sharing (exactly why this course itself defaults to HTML), PDF suits formal, fixed-layout printing or archiving, and Word suits collaborators who need native tracked-changes review. Because all three come from one shared source, a single content update (fixing a typo, updating a statistic) automatically propagates to every format the next time the document is rendered — there’s no risk of the HTML and Word versions silently drifting out of sync.

Common Mistakes

Maintaining separate Word and HTML versions of the same report by hand. This immediately reintroduces the exact synchronization problem Quarto is designed to solve — any edit has to be made once, then correctly copied into every other format’s file, a process guaranteed to eventually drift out of sync.
Assuming every feature renders identically across all formats. Interactive elements like this course’s collapsible <details> knowledge-check answers work naturally in HTML but render as plain, always-visible text in PDF and Word — always preview a document in its actual target format before finalizing it.
Forgetting some formats require additional software installed. Rendering to PDF specifically requires a working LaTeX installation (such as TinyTeX) — a missing LaTeX installation is a common reason quarto render succeeds for HTML but fails specifically for the PDF format.

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
169.6 Practice Problem
Write the YAML front matter for a single report that renders to both HTML and Word (but not PDF), with a table of contents enabled in both formats.

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
169.7 Challenge Problem
This course’s own _quarto.yml currently defines html: and pdf: formats (Page 2) but not docx:. Write the exact YAML lines you would add to include a Word output format for this entire book, matching the indentation level of the existing html: and pdf: entries, and explain one likely reason a collaborative public-health team might want a .docx version of specific chapters even though the book is primarily read as a website.

title: 169  Choosing an Output Format
text:
169.8 Key Takeaways

The same Quarto source document can render into multiple output formats (HTML, PDF, Word, and others) by listing them under format: in YAML front matter, with no content duplication.
HTML suits interactive browsing, PDF suits fixed-layout printing and archiving, and Word suits collaborators who need native tracked-changes review — the right choice depends on the audience, not the content itself.
Not every feature renders identically across formats (interactive elements can lose their interactivity in PDF/Word), and PDF rendering specifically requires a working LaTeX installation.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What is the main benefit of rendering the same source document to HTML, PDF, and Word, rather than maintaining three separate files?

It renders faster overall
A single content update automatically applies to every format, with no risk of drift
Word documents cannot contain R code otherwise
There is no benefit; the formats are identical in every way

True or false. Interactive HTML elements like collapsible answer sections always work identically when rendered to PDF.
Predict the output. If a .qmd file’s YAML lists format: {html: default, docx: default}, how many separate output files would rendering produce?
Short answer. Why does PDF rendering specifically require an additional software installation (like TinyTeX) that HTML rendering does not?



Show answers


b) A single content update automatically applies to every format, eliminating the risk of different format versions drifting out of sync.
False. Interactive elements like collapsible <details> sections rely on web-browser behavior and typically render as plain, always-visible text in PDF, since a fixed-layout printed page has no “click to reveal” concept.
Two — one .html file and one .docx file, both generated from the same source.
PDF output is generated via a LaTeX typesetting engine, which is separate software that must be installed (TinyTeX is a minimal, Quarto-friendly LaTeX distribution) — HTML rendering, by contrast, only requires Pandoc’s built-in HTML conversion, with no external typesetting system needed.

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
170.1 The R Concept
A Quarto project is a folder containing a _quarto.yml file that ties multiple .qmd files together into one coherent output, rather than each rendering independently. The project: type: field determines what kind of unified output results:
A website organizes independent pages with shared navigation; a book organizes sequential chapters (with numbering, a defined reading order, and often PDF/EPUB output) — this course’s own _quarto.yml (Page 2) uses type: book specifically because its 15 levels are meant to be read in order, not browsed as unrelated pages.
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
# A Quarto website project
project:
  type: website
website:
  title: "My Site"
  navbar:
    left:
      - href: index.qmd
      - href: about.qmd
# A Quarto book project - exactly what this course is
project:
  type: book
book:
  title: "R for Public Health"
  chapters:
    - index.qmd
    - 01-getting-started/00-overview.qmd
    - 01-getting-started/01-what-is-r.qmd
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
170.2 Demonstration
# This course's actual project: and book: sections:
project:
  type: book
  output-dir: _book

book:
  title: "R for Public Health: A Problem-Based Learning Course"
  chapters:
    - index.qmd
    - part: "Level 1 — Getting Started with R"
      chapters:
        - 01-getting-started/00-overview.qmd
        - 01-getting-started/01-what-is-r.qmd
        # ... 18 more Level 1 pages
    - part: "Level 2 — Data Structures in R"
      chapters:
        - 02-data-structures/00-overview.qmd
        # ... 16 more Level 2 pages
    # ... and so on, through Level 15
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
170.3 Explore
Count the part: entries in this course’s own _quarto.yml — each one corresponds to exactly one of the 15 levels you’ve worked through, and each part:’s chapters: list is exactly the sequence of individual .qmd files rendered, in order, within that level.
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
170.4 Solve the Problem
# A minimal Quarto BOOK project structure, from scratch:
project:
  type: book

book:
  title: "My Public Health Report Series"
  chapters:
    - index.qmd
    - chapter1-methods.qmd
    - chapter2-results.qmd
    - chapter3-discussion.qmd

format:
  html:
    theme: flatly
  pdf:
    documentclass: report
Running `quarto render` on this project produces a single, unified
multi-chapter output: a website with consistent navigation between
chapters (in HTML), AND a single combined PDF book with chapters in
the specified order - both generated from the same 4 separate .qmd
files, exactly the same mechanism producing this entire course you've
been reading, just at a much smaller scale (4 chapters instead of 15
levels' worth of lessons).
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
170.5 Interpret the Result
A Quarto project’s _quarto.yml is what elevates a folder of individually-useful .qmd files into one coherent, navigable whole — shared theme, shared table of contents structure, and (for a book) a defined chapter order and combined PDF/EPUB output. This course itself is the single largest example of this idea you’ve encountered: every one of the 100+ separate .qmd files you’ve read was rendered as part of one unified book project, not as 100+ disconnected, individually-rendered pages.

Common Mistakes

Confusing a website project (independent, browsable pages) with a book project (sequential, ordered chapters). A website’s navbar: structure suits unrelated or loosely-related pages; a book’s chapters: list (with part: groupings, exactly like this course’s 15 levels) suits genuinely sequential content meant to be read in order.
Forgetting new chapter files must be added to _quarto.yml’s chapters: list. Simply creating a new .qmd file inside a project folder does not automatically include it in the book or website — every chapter must be explicitly listed, exactly as this course has done throughout Levels 1-14’s own construction.
Rendering individual .qmd files directly instead of rendering the whole project. Inside a book or website project, rendering a single chapter file in isolation may lose the shared navigation, numbering, and cross-references (Page 5) that only appear correctly when the entire project is rendered together.
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
170.6 Practice Problem
Sketch (in YAML) a minimal Quarto website project (not a book) with 3 pages: index.qmd, about.qmd, and contact.qmd, including a navbar: linking to all three.
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
170.7 Challenge Problem
This course’s _quarto.yml currently organizes its 15 levels using part: groupings within a single book: chapters: list (Page 2’s excerpt above shows this pattern for Levels 1 and 2). Explain, in your own words, what would likely go wrong — in terms of navigation, numbering, or organization — if all 100+ individual chapter files were instead listed as one long, flat chapters: list with no part: groupings at all.
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites
text:
170.8 Key Takeaways

A Quarto project (marked by a _quarto.yml file) ties multiple .qmd files together into one coherent, unified output, rather than rendering each file independently.
project: type: website suits independent, navigable pages; project: type: book (used by this entire course) suits sequential, ordered chapters, often with part: groupings and combined PDF/EPUB output.
Every chapter file must be explicitly listed in _quarto.yml’s chapters: list to be included in the rendered project — creating a .qmd file alone isn’t enough.


Knowledge Check

Multiple choice. What kind of Quarto project is this course itself?

A website project, with unrelated independent pages
A book project, with sequentially ordered chapters organized into parts
A single standalone document
A parameterized report

True or false. Creating a new .qmd file inside a Quarto book project’s folder automatically adds it to the rendered book.
Predict the output. If a book’s _quarto.yml lists chapters grouped under 5 separate part: entries, how many distinct top-level sections would the rendered book’s sidebar likely show?
Short answer. Why does a book project (like this course) benefit from part: groupings, rather than one long, flat list of every chapter?



Show answers


b) A book project, with sequentially ordered chapters organized into parts — exactly this course’s own 15-level structure.
False. Every chapter must be explicitly added to _quarto.yml’s chapters: list; simply creating the file does not include it automatically.
5 — one top-level section per part: entry, each expandable to reveal its own chapters.
part: groupings organize a long sequence of chapters into meaningful, navigable sections (like this course’s 15 levels) — without them, a reader facing 100+ ungrouped chapter entries in a single flat sidebar list would have a much harder time understanding the book’s overall structure or finding a specific topic.
crumbs: 170  Quarto Projects: Books and Websites

title: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template
text:
171.1 The R Concept
A complete parameterized report brings every piece from this level together in one YAML block and a handful of chunks:
Table 171.1: paste(“Summary statistics for”, params$county)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
clinic_data <- participants[participants$county == params$county, ]

summary_table <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Patients", "Mean SBP", "Mean BMI", "Current Smokers"),
  Value = c(nrow(clinic_data),
            round(mean(clinic_data$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
            round(mean(clinic_data$bmi, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
            sum(clinic_data$smoker == "Yes"))
)
knitr::kable(summary_table)

summary_table <- data.frame(

title: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template
text:
171.3 Explore
Try re-running the same two chunks above with "Millbrook" in place of "Oakford" — every number in the table changes (Millbrook has 21 patients, not 17), while the table’s structure, caption pattern, and surrounding prose template remain completely unchanged.
