title: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template
text:
171.4 Solve the Problem
---
title: "Clinic Summary: ˋr params$countyˋ"
params:
  county: "Oakford"
bibliography: references.bib
---

## Overview {#sec-clinic-overview}

This report summarizes patients enrolled at the ˋr params$countyˋ clinic
site, as part of the broader study described in @sec-clinic-overview.
Data cleaning followed tidyverse conventions [@wickham2023r4ds].

## Summary Statistics


Table 171.2: paste(“Summary statistics for”, params$county)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
clinic_data <- participants[participants$county == params$county, ]

summary_table <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Patients", "Mean SBP", "Mean BMI", "Current Smokers"),
  Value = c(nrow(clinic_data),
            round(mean(clinic_data$systolic_bp, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
            round(mean(clinic_data$bmi, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
            sum(clinic_data$smoker == "Yes"))
)
knitr::kable(summary_table)




library(ggplot2)
ggplot(clinic_data, aes(x = systolic_bp)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 10, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(x = "Systolic Blood Pressure (mmHg)", y = "Count")

## References
Rendered with params: county: "Oakford" -> "Clinic Summary: Oakford" with
Table 1 showing 17 patients, mean SBP 129.7; Figure 1 showing Oakford's
blood pressure histogram; and a References section citing R for Data
Science.

Rendered again with params: county: "Riverdale" -> the identical template
instead produces "Clinic Summary: Riverdale" with Table 1 showing 16
patients, mean SBP 124.4; a completely different Figure 1 for Riverdale's
data; and the exact same References section, since the cited sources
don't depend on which county was selected.
crumbs: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template

title: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template
text:
171.5 Interpret the Result
This capstone demonstrates the complete reproducible-research workflow: one parameterized template (Page 7) produces a correctly-labeled, cross-referenced (Page 5) table and figure (Page 4) with inline-code-driven prose (Page 3) and a properly cited bibliography (Page 6) — and every single piece of this automatically regenerates correctly for any of the 5 counties, just by changing one params$county value. Nothing about the report’s content needed to be rewritten by hand between counties; only the parameter changed, and every downstream number, table, figure, and caption followed automatically.

Common Mistakes


Hardcoding a county-specific number somewhere in the prose “just this once.” Even a single hardcoded value (like manually typing “17 patients” instead of using inline code) breaks the whole point of parameterization — that same sentence would be silently wrong the moment the template renders for a different county.

Forgetting the bibliography and cross-references don’t need to change between parameter values. Only the county-dependent calculations (the table, the figure, county-specific inline numbers) should reference params$county — citations and section cross-references stay identical across every rendered version of the report.

Not testing the template with more than one parameter value before considering it finished. A template that only renders correctly for its YAML default ("Oakford") but silently breaks for "Millbrook" or "Springvale" isn’t actually a working parameterized report — always test at least 2-3 different values before trusting it.
crumbs: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template

title: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template
text:
171.6 Practice Problem
Extend the summary table to include a fifth row, "Mean Age", computed from clinic_data$age, and re-render the template (mentally, or by tracing through the code) for params$county = "Springvale".
crumbs: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template

title: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template
text:
171.7 Challenge Problem
Write the R code (using quarto::quarto_render() inside a for loop, Level 10) that would render this template once for each of the 5 counties in participants$county, saving each output as clinic_report_<county>.html, and briefly explain how this single capstone script replaces what would otherwise require 5 separately maintained report files.
crumbs: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template

title: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template
text:
171.8 Key Takeaways

A complete reproducible report combines this level’s tools together: parameters drive what data is shown, inline code keeps prose numbers live, tables and figures carry proper captions, cross-references link content reliably, and citations credit sources automatically.
Testing a parameterized template against multiple different parameter values (not just its YAML default) is the only way to confirm it’s genuinely reusable, not accidentally hardcoded somewhere.
This entire course — every level, every page, every verified statistic — was itself built following exactly this same reproducible-research discipline, from Level 1’s first read.csv() call through this very capstone.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In this capstone’s template, what changes between rendering for "Oakford" versus "Riverdale"?

The bibliography’s cited sources
The table’s and figure’s underlying data and captions, driven by params$county

The cross-reference labels used in the document
Nothing changes between renders


True or false. A parameterized report template should be tested with only its YAML default value before being considered complete.
Predict the output. If params$county is set to "Millbrook" (21 patients, per Level 10), what would the tbl-clinic-summary table’s “Patients” row show?
Short answer. Why does hardcoding even one county-specific number in this template’s prose undermine the entire point of building it as a parameterized report?


Show answers


b) The table’s and figure’s underlying data and captions — both driven by params$county — change between renders; the bibliography and cross-reference structure stay the same.

False. A template should be tested against multiple different parameter values to confirm it’s genuinely reusable and not accidentally hardcoded somewhere.
21 — matching Level 10’s known Millbrook row count.
A single hardcoded number defeats the purpose of parameterization for that specific value — the report would continue displaying that one incorrect, frozen number no matter which county is actually selected via params$county, silently undermining the reliability of the entire template.
crumbs: 171  Level 14 Capstone: A Complete Clinic Report Template

title: Level 15 — Real-World Capstone Projects
text: What this level covers

title: Level 15 — Real-World Capstone Projects
text:
A hypertension risk-factor investigation, using logistic regression to weigh age, BMI, and smoking status together
A smoking and cardiovascular risk comparison, combining chi-square tests, t-tests, and effect sizes into one coherent verdict
A geographic patterns investigation across all five counties, combining ANOVA, visualization, and Level 10’s file-automation together
A longitudinal blood pressure project using the multi-visit and baseline/follow-up datasets, including a real look at participant attrition
A reproducible, parameterized “dashboard” report bringing together risk modeling, geography, and longitudinal trends for any single county
Guidance for designing and executing your own independent capstone project, from a completely new dataset, using this same disciplined workflow


A preview mission
Your mission across this level: work through five complete, real capstone projects built on this course’s own data — then use everything you’ve learned to plan and execute an independent project of your own, from raw data to a report you’d be comfortable putting your name on.







A note on scope



This level’s five worked capstones (Pages 1-5) use only the datasets already built throughout this course — participants.csv, the multi-visit blood pressure files (Level 6, reused in Level 8 and 11), and the baseline/follow-up enrollment files (Level 6’s join lessons). Physical activity, cognitive decline, and neighborhood-deprivation questions mentioned in earlier drafts of this overview would require data this course’s dataset doesn’t contain — Page 6 instead gives you a general-purpose template for tackling exactly those kinds of questions with a real dataset of your own choosing.

title: 172  Capstone 1: Hypertension Risk Factors
text:
172.1 The R Concept
This capstone doesn’t introduce a new function — it asks you to sequence tools you already have:
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")                     # Level 3

participants$hypertension <- as.numeric(participants$systolic_bp >= 140)  # Level 4
participants$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(participants$smoker == "Yes")   # Level 11

model <- glm(hypertension ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary,               # Level 12
             data = participants, family = binomial)
summary(model)
exp(coef(model))    # odds ratios, Level 11

title: 172  Capstone 1: Hypertension Risk Factors
text:
172.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$hypertension <- as.numeric(participants$systolic_bp >= 140)

table(participants$hypertension, useNA = "ifany")


   0    1 <NA>
  67   29    4

title: 172  Capstone 1: Hypertension Risk Factors
text:
172.3 Explore
Try mean(participants$hypertension, na.rm = TRUE) to confirm the overall hypertension rate (29 out of 96 non-missing participants, about 30.2%) before modeling anything — always understand your outcome’s basic distribution first, exactly as Level 7’s descriptive statistics taught.

title: 172  Capstone 1: Hypertension Risk Factors
text:
172.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$hypertension <- as.numeric(participants$systolic_bp >= 140)
participants$smoker_binary <- as.numeric(participants$smoker == "Yes")

risk_model <- glm(hypertension ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary,
                   data = participants, family = binomial)

summary(risk_model)


Call:
glm(formula = hypertension ~ age + bmi + smoker_binary, family = binomial,
    data = participants)

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept)   -1.28975    1.71090  -0.754   0.4510
age           -0.03980    0.01753  -2.270   0.0232 *
bmi            0.06920    0.05724   1.209   0.2267
smoker_binary  0.81265    0.57384   1.416   0.1568
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

    Null deviance: 99.253  on 84  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 90.702  on 81  degrees of freedom
AIC: 98.702

exp(coef(risk_model))

  (Intercept)           age           bmi smoker_binary
    0.2753423     0.9610013     1.0716032     2.2536559
Only age reaches statistical significance (p = 0.023) — and its odds ratio (0.961) is below 1, meaning older participants in this sample have somewhat lower odds of hypertension, not higher. bmi (OR = 1.07, p = 0.227) and smoker_binary (OR = 2.25, p = 0.157) both trend in the clinically expected direction — higher BMI and smoking associated with higher hypertension odds — but neither reaches significance in this sample of 85 complete-case participants.

title: 172  Capstone 1: Hypertension Risk Factors
text:
172.5 Interpret the Result
The age finding is not a fluke specific to this model — it’s the exact same counter-intuitive pattern Level 8’s trend line and Level 11’s correlation test (r = −0.221, p = 0.036) already surfaced: in this particular dataset, older participants tend to have modestly lower blood pressure, and that same pattern carries through directly into hypertension risk. Meanwhile, smoking’s odds ratio of 2.25 (roughly double the odds) is clinically substantial and consistent with real-world expectations, but this sample’s size (only 25 smokers total, Level 7) isn’t large enough to rule out chance as an explanation. A responsible report states all three of these findings honestly — one statistically confirmed but clinically counter-intuitive, one clinically plausible but not statistically confirmed, and one essentially inconclusive — rather than overstating any of them.

Common Mistakes


Reporting only the significant finding (age) and implying the others don’t matter. A non-significant odds ratio of 2.25 for smoking is still worth reporting as a suggestive, clinically important trend worth following up with a larger sample — “not significant” is not the same as “no effect.”

Interpreting age’s negative coefficient as if it contradicts clinical knowledge about hypertension in general. This result describes this specific sample of 85 participants — it doesn’t overturn well-established population-level hypertension research, and the model should be reported with that caveat (exactly as Level 12’s capstone emphasized).

Skipping the missing-data check. hypertension had 4 NA values (from missing systolic_bp) before modeling — always confirm, as this page’s Explore step did, how many usable rows actually remain before trusting a model’s conclusions.

title: 172  Capstone 1: Hypertension Risk Factors
text:
172.6 Practice Problem
Add county as a fourth predictor to risk_model (as a categorical predictor, Level 12 Page 2). Does any individual county show a statistically significant association with hypertension risk, after adjusting for age, BMI, and smoking status?

title: 172  Capstone 1: Hypertension Risk Factors
text:
172.7 Challenge Problem
Fit a second model using only age and smoker_binary (dropping bmi), and compare it to the three-predictor model above using anova() (Level 12 Page 5). Does removing bmi significantly change the model’s fit? Use this result to justify whether bmi earns its place in a final, simplified model.

title: 172  Capstone 1: Hypertension Risk Factors
text:
172.8 Key Takeaways

A real risk-factor analysis requires deciding, yourself, which tools apply and in what order — this capstone combined Level 3’s import, Level 4’s threshold-based outcome definition, Level 11’s binary recoding, and Level 12’s multiple logistic regression.
Statistical significance and clinical plausibility don’t always align in the same sample — a responsible analysis reports both a significant-but-counterintuitive finding (age) and a plausible-but-inconclusive one (smoking) with equal honesty.
Every finding in this capstone describes this specific 100-participant sample — a foundational caveat that applies to every modeling result in this entire course, from Level 11 onward.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. In this capstone’s model, which predictor reached statistical significance?

BMI
Smoking status
Age
None of the predictors


True or false. Age’s odds ratio below 1.0 in this model means older participants have higher odds of hypertension.
Predict the output. Given smoking’s odds ratio of 2.25 and a p-value of 0.157, should this finding be reported as statistically confirmed?
Short answer. Why is it important to report smoking’s non-significant but clinically substantial odds ratio, rather than omitting it because it “didn’t reach significance”?


Show answers


c) Age — the only predictor with p < 0.05 in this model.

False. An odds ratio below 1.0 (0.961 here) means each additional year of age is associated with lower, not higher, odds of hypertension in this sample.
No — a p-value of 0.157 is above the conventional 0.05 threshold, meaning this sample doesn’t provide strong statistical evidence for the association, even though the estimated effect size (OR = 2.25) is clinically substantial.
Omitting a non-significant-but-substantial finding risks understating a real, clinically important pattern simply because this particular sample was too small to confirm it statistically — an honest report distinguishes “not proven” from “disproven,” exactly as Level 11’s capstone emphasized.

title: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk
text:
173.1 The R Concept
This capstone runs the same two-sample comparison logic (Level 11, Pages 5 and 7) across four different outcomes, methodically:
t.test(systolic_bp ~ smoker, data = participants)     # numeric vs. categorical
t.test(diastolic_bp ~ smoker, data = participants)    # numeric vs. categorical
t.test(bmi ~ smoker, data = participants)             # numeric vs. categorical
chisq.test(table(participants$smoker, participants$hypertension))  # categorical vs. categorical
crumbs: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk

title: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk
text:
173.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

t.test(systolic_bp ~ smoker, data = participants)


    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  systolic_bp by smoker
t = -0.21305, df = 41.106, p-value = 0.8323
95 percent confidence interval:
 -9.750749  7.889630
sample estimates:
 mean in group No mean in group Yes
         129.5000          130.4306
crumbs: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk

title: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk
text:
173.3 Explore
Try running the same t.test() pattern for bmi and diastolic_bp before looking at the Solve section below — form your own expectation first (Level 11’s “Think Before Coding” habit) about whether either will show a significant difference, given that systolic blood pressure alone did not.
crumbs: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk

title: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk
text:
173.4 Solve the Problem

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")

participants$hypertension <- as.numeric(participants$systolic_bp >= 140)

bmi_test  <- t.test(bmi ~ smoker, data = participants)
dbp_test  <- t.test(diastolic_bp ~ smoker, data = participants)
hyper_tab <- table(participants$smoker, participants$hypertension)
hyper_test <- chisq.test(hyper_tab)

bmi_test$p.value
dbp_test$p.value
hyper_test$p.value

[1] 0.5458577
[1] 0.5266651
[1] 0.2480914

hyper_tab
prop.table(hyper_tab, margin = 1)


       0  1
  No  53 19
  Yes 14 10
           0         1
  No  0.7361111 0.2638889
  Yes 0.5833333 0.4166667
None of the four comparisons reaches statistical significance (all p-values well above 0.05): BMI (p = 0.546), diastolic BP (p = 0.527), systolic BP (p = 0.832, from the Demonstration), and hypertension status (p = 0.248). Yet the directions are consistent with clinical expectation throughout: smokers show a numerically higher hypertension rate (41.7% vs. 26.4%), higher mean diastolic BP (81.96 vs. 79.93), and higher mean systolic BP — none reaching significance, but none pointing the “wrong” way either (only BMI trends slightly lower among smokers, 26.6 vs. 27.3, a small and non-significant difference).
crumbs: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk

title: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk
text:
173.5 Interpret the Result
This capstone’s honest verdict is genuinely more nuanced than “smoking is/isn’t related to cardiovascular risk”: across four separate comparisons, not one reaches statistical significance in this 100-participant sample, but three of four point in the clinically expected direction (higher hypertension rate, higher diastolic and systolic BP among smokers). This is exactly the pattern expected from a real but modest effect studied in too small a sample — Levels 7, 11, and 12 all made the same point repeatedly: “not significant” describes this sample’s evidence, not a settled claim that no true relationship exists. A well-designed follow-up study with more smokers (only 25 in this entire dataset) would be a reasonable next recommendation, rather than concluding smoking has no cardiovascular relevance here.

Common Mistakes


Concluding “smoking has no effect on cardiovascular risk” from four non-significant tests. A consistent directional pattern across multiple related outcomes, even without any single test reaching significance, is meaningfully different evidence than results that scatter randomly in both directions — the first is suggestive; pure noise typically wouldn’t line up this consistently.

Running four separate tests without acknowledging the multiple-comparisons issue. Level 12 Page 5 explained why running many separate tests inflates the chance of a false positive — the same caution applies here in reverse: running four tests and finding none significant is a stronger form of evidence for “no detected effect” than if only one or two had been run.

Cherry-picking the smallest p-value (hypertension, p = 0.248) as “the closest to significant” and overselling it. All four p-values are well above 0.05; none should be described as “trending toward significance” in a way that overstates the actual evidence.
crumbs: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk

title: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk
text:
173.6 Practice Problem
Run t.test(age ~ smoker, data = participants). Does age differ significantly between smokers and non-smokers in this sample? How might an age difference between the two groups (if one exists) complicate interpreting the blood-pressure comparisons above?
crumbs: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk

title: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk
text:
173.7 Challenge Problem
Fit glm(hypertension ~ smoker, data = participants, family = binomial) (a simple, one-predictor version of Capstone 1’s larger model) and compare its smoker coefficient and p-value to the three-predictor model from Capstone 1. Does adjusting for age and BMI change smoking’s estimated association with hypertension much?
crumbs: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk

title: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk
text:
173.8 Key Takeaways

Comparing groups across multiple related outcomes requires choosing the correct test for each variable-type pairing (t-tests for numeric outcomes, chi-square for two categorical variables) — the same decision framework from Level 11, applied systematically rather than to a single outcome at a time.
A consistent directional pattern across several non-significant comparisons is more informative than a single isolated non-significant result — it suggests a real, if modest and statistically unconfirmed, underlying relationship rather than pure noise.
Reporting “not significant” honestly (rather than as “no effect” or as “nearly significant”) is a recurring discipline throughout this entire course, from Level 7’s descriptive claims through this final capstone.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Across the four comparisons in this capstone, how many reached statistical significance at p < 0.05?

All four
Three of four
One of four
None of the four


True or false. A consistent directional pattern across several non-significant tests provides no more information than results that point in random, inconsistent directions.
Predict the output. Given a hypertension rate of 41.7% among smokers and 26.4% among non-smokers, but a chi-square p-value of 0.248, is this difference statistically significant at the conventional threshold?
Short answer. Why might a well-designed follow-up study with a larger number of smokers be a more appropriate conclusion than declaring smoking unrelated to cardiovascular risk in this dataset?


Show answers


d) None of the four comparisons reached statistical significance in this sample.

False. A consistent direction across multiple related, non-significant comparisons is meaningfully more suggestive of a real underlying effect than results scattering randomly in both directions, even though neither pattern alone confirms significance.
No — a p-value of 0.248 is well above 0.05, so this difference is not statistically significant despite the descriptive gap.
This dataset contains only 25 smokers total, which limits statistical power to detect even a real, modest effect — recommending a larger sample is a scientifically appropriate response to “not enough evidence yet,” rather than incorrectly treating a small sample’s non-significant result as proof of no true relationship.
crumbs: 173  Capstone 2: Smoking and Cardiovascular Risk

title: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties
text:
174.1 The R Concept
This capstone chains three separate tools together: automated file reading (Level 10), a statistical test (Level 12), and a visualization (Level 8):
clinic_files <- list.files("data/clinics", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
clinic_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(clinic_files, read.csv))

bmi_county_model <- aov(bmi ~ county, data = clinic_data)
summary(bmi_county_model)

ggplot(clinic_data, aes(x = county, y = bmi)) + geom_boxplot()
crumbs: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties

title: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties
text:
174.2 Demonstration

clinic_files <- list.files("data/clinics", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
clinic_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(clinic_files, read.csv))

nrow(clinic_data)
identical(sort(clinic_data$id), sort(read.csv("data/participants.csv")$id))

[1] 100
[1] TRUE
crumbs: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties

title: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties
text:
174.3 Explore
Before trusting any geographic comparison, always confirm the automated reconstruction matches the original data exactly — as just demonstrated. Try table(clinic_data$county) to reproduce Level 10’s known county counts (20/21/17/16/26) directly from the automated file-reading pipeline, independent of participants.csv.
crumbs: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties

title: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties
text:
174.4 Solve the Problem

clinic_files <- list.files("data/clinics", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
clinic_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(clinic_files, read.csv))

bmi_county_model <- aov(bmi ~ county, data = clinic_data)
summary(bmi_county_model)

            Df Sum Sq Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
county       4    135    33.8   1.614  0.178
Residuals   90   1888    21.0

summary(bmi_county_model)[[1]][["Pr(>F)"]][1]

[1] 0.1776636

library(ggplot2)
ggplot(clinic_data, aes(x = county, y = bmi)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(x = "County", y = "BMI", title = "BMI Distribution by County")

[Renders a 5-box boxplot, one per county, with Springvale's box
 visibly positioned lowest (mean 25.24) and Cedar Falls' highest
 (mean 28.42), though the ANOVA shows this spread isn't statistically
 significant.]
Like blood pressure (Level 11, F = 0.539, p = 0.708), BMI shows no statistically significant county-level difference either (F = 1.614, p = 0.178) — despite a visually noticeable spread in the boxplot (Springvale’s mean of 25.24 versus Cedar Falls’ 28.42, roughly a 3-point difference). The automated reconstruction from data/clinics/ exactly matches participants.csv (confirmed via identical() above), so this conclusion doesn’t depend on trusting a single pre-combined file.
crumbs: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties

title: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties
text:
174.5 Interpret the Result
Two separate health measurements (blood pressure and BMI), tested the same way, reach the same conclusion: no statistically significant geographic pattern across these five counties in this sample. This is a meaningfully different, more complete answer than checking blood pressure alone — a geographic health disparity claim resting on just one measurement would be considerably weaker evidence than one checked (and not found) across multiple relevant outcomes. The independent cross-validation against Level 10’s automated file-reading pipeline also confirms this conclusion isn’t an artifact of a single, possibly-corrupted source file.

Common Mistakes


Trusting a visually striking boxplot spread without checking the ANOVA’s p-value. Springvale’s visibly lower BMI box might look like a real geographic pattern, but the F-test (p = 0.178) shows this spread is consistent with random sampling variation across 5 groups of modest size (14-26 participants each).

Skipping the cross-validation step against Level 10’s clinic files. Recomputing a result from an independently-reconstructed dataset (rather than only ever reading participants.csv) is exactly the kind of due diligence that catches a corrupted or outdated file before it silently produces a wrong conclusion.

Testing only one measurement and generalizing to “geography doesn’t matter for health outcomes here.” This capstone’s stronger conclusion — that two separate measurements both show no significant county effect — required deliberately checking more than one outcome, not stopping at the first test.
crumbs: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties

title: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties
text:
174.6 Practice Problem
Run aov(age ~ county, data = clinic_data) and aov(diastolic_bp ~ county, data = clinic_data). Do either of these show a significant county-level difference, completing a four-measurement geographic comparison alongside systolic BP and BMI?
crumbs: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties

title: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties
text:
174.7 Challenge Problem
Level 10’s automation capstone reconstructed participants.csv from 5 separate clinic files. Modify the file-reading loop in this page’s Demonstration to also record, for each file, its county name and row count into a small summary data frame — reproducing Level 10’s known county counts (20, 21, 17, 16, 26) without ever reading participants.csv directly, only the 5 clinic files.
crumbs: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties

title: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties
text:
174.8 Key Takeaways

Testing a geographic hypothesis across multiple relevant measurements (not just one) produces meaningfully stronger evidence than checking a single outcome alone.
Cross-validating a result against an independently-reconstructed version of the data (Level 10’s automation) is a valuable due-diligence step before trusting a conclusion drawn from a single source file.
A visually noticeable spread in a boxplot is not the same as statistical significance — always check the underlying test’s p-value before interpreting a visualization as evidence of a real difference.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What was the purpose of reconstructing clinic_data from data/clinics/*.csv instead of just reading participants.csv directly?

The clinic files contain different participants
It cross-validates the analysis against an independently-assembled version of the same data

participants.csv no longer exists
It is required for aov() to work at all


True or false. BMI’s ANOVA (F = 1.614, p = 0.178) shows a statistically significant difference across counties.
Predict the output. If identical(sort(clinic_data$id), sort(participants$id)) returns TRUE, what does that confirm about the two datasets?
Short answer. Why is testing both blood pressure and BMI for a county effect stronger evidence than testing blood pressure alone?


Show answers


b) It cross-validates the analysis against an independently-assembled version of the same data, rather than trusting a single source file alone.

False. A p-value of 0.178 is well above 0.05, indicating no statistically significant county-level difference in BMI.
It confirms the two datasets contain the exact same set of participant IDs — strong evidence the automated reconstruction from the clinic files matches the original data with no participants lost, duplicated, or altered.
A single non-significant test could plausibly reflect that specific measurement’s own quirks or insufficient power; finding the same non-significant conclusion across two independently-tested, clinically distinct measurements makes it considerably less likely that a real geographic effect is being missed simply due to chance.
crumbs: 174  Capstone 3: Geographic Patterns Across Counties

title: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition
text:
175.1 The R Concept
Individual-level change, categorized:
bp_visits <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")
bp_visits$change <- bp_visits$sbp_visit3 - bp_visits$sbp_visit1
bp_visits$trend <- case_when(
  bp_visits$change <= -5 ~ "Improved",
  bp_visits$change >=  5 ~ "Worsened",
  TRUE                   ~ "Stable"
)
Attrition, using Level 6’s join family:
baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

matched     <- inner_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")   # completed follow-up
dropped_out <- anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")    # in baseline, missing at follow-up
new_arrivals <- anti_join(followup, baseline, by = "id")   # at follow-up, not in baseline
crumbs: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition

title: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition
text:
175.2 Demonstration

bp_visits <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_visits$change <- bp_visits$sbp_visit3 - bp_visits$sbp_visit1

summary(bp_visits$change)

   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.    NA's
 -23.00   -9.00   -6.00   -5.00   -0.25   11.00       2
crumbs: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition

title: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition
text:
175.3 Explore
Try sum(!is.na(bp_visits$change)) to confirm 26 participants have complete visit 1 and visit 3 measurements (2 are missing, matching Level 11’s paired t-test sample size exactly). Before categorizing, always confirm you understand exactly how many rows will actually be classified versus excluded.
crumbs: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition

title: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition
text:
175.4 Solve the Problem

library(dplyr)

bp_visits <- read.csv("data/bp_visits_wide.csv")

bp_visits <- bp_visits |>
  mutate(
    change = sbp_visit3 - sbp_visit1,
    trend = case_when(
      change <= -5 ~ "Improved",
      change >=  5 ~ "Worsened",
      TRUE         ~ "Stable"
    )
  )

table(bp_visits$trend, useNA = "ifany")

Improved   Stable Worsened     <NA>
      15        8        3        2

baseline <- read.csv("data/baseline_enrollment.csv")
followup <- read.csv("data/followup_visit.csv")

matched      <- inner_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")
dropped_out  <- anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id")
new_arrivals <- anti_join(followup, baseline, by = "id")

nrow(matched)
nrow(dropped_out)
nrow(new_arrivals)

[1] 15
[1] 5
[1] 5

# Did anyone's smoking status change between baseline and follow-up?
# (baseline_enrollment.csv has no smoker column of its own, so compare
#  against participants.csv's original smoker status for the same ids)
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
baseline_smoker <- participants[participants$id %in% matched$id, c("id", "smoker")]
comparison <- merge(baseline_smoker, matched[, c("id", "followup_smoker")], by = "id")
sum(comparison$smoker != comparison$followup_smoker)

[1] 0
Of 28 participants with multi-visit blood pressure data, 15 improved (dropped 5+ mmHg) and only 3 worsened (rose 5+ mmHg) between visit 1 and visit 3 — consistent with, and adding real texture to, Level 11’s significant average decrease. Separately, of the 20 baseline enrollees, 5 (25%) did not return for follow-up, while 5 entirely new participants appear at follow-up who were never in the baseline data. Among the 15 who did complete both visits, zero had a different smoking status recorded at follow-up than at baseline.
crumbs: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition

title: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition
text:
175.5 Interpret the Result
The individual-level breakdown (15 improved, 8 stable, 3 worsened) confirms Level 11’s average blood pressure decrease wasn’t driven by a handful of extreme outliers — a clear majority of individuals genuinely improved. The attrition analysis, though, adds an important caveat any real longitudinal study must confront: 25% of the baseline cohort was lost to follow-up, and the follow-up dataset gained an equal number of new participants — meaning any “before vs. after” comparison using only matched individuals describes a specific 15-person subgroup, not the original 20-person baseline cohort, and definitely not a stable, unchanging population. Finding zero smoking-status changes among the matched cohort is a genuine (if modest) finding — it means none of this particular capstone’s tracked participants quit or started smoking during the study window, not that smoking status never changes over time in general.

Common Mistakes


Reporting the average change (Level 11’s paired t-test) without also checking the individual-level breakdown. An average decrease could theoretically come from a few participants improving dramatically while most stayed flat or worsened slightly — the case_when() breakdown here confirms that’s not what happened, but that confirmation required checking, not assuming.

Ignoring attrition and treating the baseline and follow-up datasets as if they described the same 20 people. anti_join() revealed a 25% dropout rate — any longitudinal conclusion using only the matched 15 people should explicitly note this, since dropout is rarely random (health status often influences whether someone returns for follow-up).

Concluding “smoking status never changes” from zero changes in this 15-person matched subgroup. This is a specific, small-sample finding about this particular cohort and time window, not a general claim about smoking behavior — exactly the same caution Level 11 and 12 repeatedly emphasized about generalizing beyond a given sample.
crumbs: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition

title: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition
text:
175.6 Practice Problem
Using dropped_out (the 5 baseline participants missing from follow-up), check whether their baseline BMI or age differs noticeably from the 15 who did return, using mean() comparisons (Level 7). Would a systematic difference between those who dropped out and those who stayed change how you’d interpret the follow-up results?
crumbs: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition

title: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition
text:
175.7 Challenge Problem
Compute each of the 26 complete participants’ percent change in blood pressure (rather than raw mmHg change): (sbp_visit3 - sbp_visit1) / sbp_visit1 * 100. Does categorizing “Improved”/“Stable”/“Worsened” using a percent-change threshold (for example, ±4%) produce a meaningfully different breakdown than the raw 5 mmHg threshold used in this capstone?
crumbs: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition

title: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition
text:
175.8 Key Takeaways

Individual-level categorization (via case_when()) reveals whether an average trend reflects a broad pattern or a few extreme cases — an essential complement to Level 11’s aggregate paired t-test.

anti_join() (Level 6) is the right tool for identifying exactly which participants were lost between two time points — a 25% dropout rate in this capstone is too large to ignore when interpreting follow-up results.
Attrition is rarely random in real longitudinal studies — any conclusion drawn only from participants who completed follow-up should explicitly note who was excluded and why that might matter.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. What did the individual-level breakdown of blood pressure change reveal?

Most participants worsened, contradicting Level 11’s average finding
A clear majority (15 of 26) improved, consistent with Level 11’s average decrease
Exactly half improved and half worsened
No participants had complete data for both visits


True or false. All 20 baseline-enrolled participants also appear in the follow-up dataset.
Predict the output. If anti_join(baseline, followup, by = "id") returns 5 rows, what does that represent?
Short answer. Why does a 25% dropout rate matter when interpreting a longitudinal comparison that only uses the matched participants?


Show answers


b) A clear majority (15 of 26) improved, consistent with and adding detail to Level 11’s significant average decrease.

False. 5 of the 20 baseline participants (25%) do not appear in the follow-up dataset — they were lost to follow-up.
The 5 baseline participants who did not appear in the follow-up dataset — those lost to follow-up.
If participants who dropped out differ systematically from those who stayed (for example, if people with worsening health were less likely to return), any conclusion drawn only from the matched participants could be biased and not representative of the full original baseline cohort.
crumbs: 175  Capstone 4: Longitudinal Blood Pressure and Attrition

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
176.1 The R Concept
This capstone’s template combines Level 15’s own capstones 1 and 3 into one parameterized document, following Level 14’s pattern:
participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
participants$hypertension <- as.numeric(participants$systolic_bp >= 140)

clinic_data   <- participants[participants$county == params$county, ]
overall_rate  <- mean(participants$hypertension, na.rm = TRUE)
county_rate   <- mean(clinic_data$hypertension, na.rm = TRUE)
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
---
title: "County Health Dashboard: ˋr params$countyˋ"
params:
  county: "Oakford"
---
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
176.2 Demonstration

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
participants$hypertension <- as.numeric(participants$systolic_bp >= 140)

clinic_data <- participants[participants$county == "Oakford", ]

nrow(clinic_data)
mean(clinic_data$hypertension, na.rm = TRUE)

[1] 17
[1] 0.2941176
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
176.3 Explore
Try recomputing the same two lines with "Riverdale" in place of "Oakford" — Riverdale’s hypertension rate (21.4%, the lowest of all five counties) is noticeably below Oakford’s (29.4%), even though Level 15’s Capstone 3 already confirmed neither systolic BP nor BMI differs significantly by county overall. A dashboard can honestly present this county-specific number while still contextualizing it against that larger, non-significant finding.
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
176.4 Solve the Problem
---
title: "County Health Dashboard: ˋr params$countyˋ"
params:
  county: "Oakford"
---

## Risk Snapshot


Table 176.1: paste(“Health snapshot for”, params$county)

participants <- read.csv("data/participants.csv")
participants$hypertension <- as.numeric(participants$systolic_bp >= 140)

clinic_data  <- participants[participants$county == params$county, ]
overall_rate <- round(100 * mean(participants$hypertension, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
county_rate  <- round(100 * mean(clinic_data$hypertension, na.rm = TRUE), 1)

snapshot <- data.frame(
  Metric = c("Patients", "Hypertension Rate (%)", "Study-wide Rate (%)", "Mean BMI"),
  Value  = c(nrow(clinic_data), county_rate, overall_rate,
             round(mean(clinic_data$bmi, na.rm = TRUE), 1))
)
knitr::kable(snapshot)



|Metric                | Value|
|:----------------------|-----:|
|Patients               |  17.0|
|Hypertension Rate (%)  |  29.4|
|Study-wide Rate (%)    |  30.2|
|Mean BMI               |  28.0|
## Geographic Context

As shown in @sec-capstone-geographic-patterns, neither blood pressure
(F = 0.539, p = 0.708) nor BMI (F = 1.614, p = 0.178) differs
significantly across all five counties overall — so ˋr params$countyˋ's
individual numbers above should be read as a local snapshot, not as
evidence of a distinct geographic health pattern.

## References

Report generated using R [@rcoreteam] and the tidyverse [@wickham2023r4ds].
Rendering this template with params: county: "Oakford" produces a snapshot table showing 17 patients, a 29.4% hypertension rate (just under the study-wide 30.2%), and a mean BMI of 28.0 — immediately followed by the honest geographic context from Capstone 3, correctly citing its cross-reference and this level’s software citations.
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
176.5 Interpret the Result
This dashboard demonstrates the full reproducible-research payoff: a single template, parameterized by county, that automatically produces a locally-relevant snapshot while never losing the broader statistical context that makes that snapshot meaningful. Rendering the same template for all five counties (Capstone 3’s for loop pattern, Level 10) would produce five internally consistent reports, each correctly noting that no significant geographic pattern was found overall — exactly the kind of report a clinic director could trust, because it’s generated the same verified way every time, for every county, rather than assembled by hand once and potentially never updated again.

Common Mistakes


Presenting a county’s individual rate (like Oakford’s 29.4%) without the study-wide context (30.2%) or the geographic ANOVA’s non-significant result. A reader seeing only “29.4%” with no comparison point might wrongly assume it represents a meaningfully distinct local pattern.

Hardcoding the overall study-wide rate instead of computing it from the full participants data frame inside the same template. If participants.csv is later updated, a hardcoded “30.2%” would silently become wrong, while mean(participants$hypertension, na.rm = TRUE) recalculates correctly every render.

Forgetting a dashboard for one county still depends on data and conclusions computed from the entire dataset (like the ANOVA’s p-value). Parameterization filters some things (the county-specific snapshot) but not everything — the overall geographic conclusion is deliberately the same across every county’s rendered report.
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
176.6 Practice Problem
Add a fourth section to this dashboard template, “Longitudinal Note,” reporting how many of Capstone 4’s bp_visits participants belong to params$county specifically, and what fraction of those participants were categorized as “Improved.”
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
176.7 Challenge Problem
Write the R code (using quarto::quarto_render() inside a for loop, Level 10 and Level 14’s challenge problems) that would render this complete dashboard once for each of the five counties, producing five files named dashboard_<county>.html. Explain briefly why this single script is more maintainable than five hand-written, county-specific report files.
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard
text:
176.8 Key Takeaways

A reproducible dashboard combines multiple capstone analyses (risk snapshot, geographic context) into one parameterized template, rather than four disconnected scripts.
Local, county-specific numbers should always be presented alongside the relevant study-wide context and statistical conclusions — parameterization changes what’s filtered, not the underlying, honestly-reported statistical evidence.
The exact same discipline from Level 14 (inline code, cross-references, citations, parameters) scales up naturally from a single table to a genuinely complete, multi-section report.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. Why does this dashboard template compute overall_rate from the full participants data frame rather than hardcoding it?

Hardcoding is not technically possible in Quarto
It ensures the study-wide context number stays correct automatically if the underlying data changes

params$county requires it
It has no effect on the report’s correctness


True or false. A county-specific hypertension rate should be reported without any broader statistical context.
Predict the output. If this template is rendered with params: county: "Riverdale", and Riverdale’s hypertension rate is 21.4%, would the “Study-wide Rate (%)” row also change to a different value?
Short answer. Why does rendering one parameterized template five times (once per county) produce a more trustworthy set of reports than five separately hand-written report files?


Show answers


b) It ensures the study-wide context number stays correct automatically if the underlying data changes, rather than risking a stale, hardcoded figure.

False. A county-specific rate should always be presented alongside relevant broader context (like the study-wide rate and the geographic ANOVA’s result), so a reader can correctly judge whether it represents a meaningful local pattern.
No — the “Study-wide Rate (%)” is computed from the full participants data frame regardless of params$county, so it stays at 30.2% across every county’s rendered report.
A single template guarantees every county’s report applies identical logic, computations, and statistical framing — five separately hand-written files risk subtle inconsistencies (different rounding, forgotten context, copy-paste errors) that a single, tested, parameterized source eliminates entirely.
crumbs: 176  Capstone 5: A Reproducible County Dashboard

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
177.1 The R Concept
There’s no single new function here — this page is a process template, assembled from tools already covered throughout this course:
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
# 1. IMPORT (Level 3)
data <- read.csv("your_data.csv")

# 2. UNDERSTAND STRUCTURE (Level 2, Level 7)
str(data)
summary(data)
colSums(is.na(data))

# 3. CLEAN (Level 4)
# handle missing values, fix types, recode variables, remove duplicates

# 4. EXPLORE (Level 5, Level 7)
# group_by/summarise, cross-tabulations, correlations

# 5. VISUALIZE (Level 8)
# distributions, relationships, group comparisons

# 6. MODEL (Level 11, Level 12)
# choose a test/model matching your variable types and question

# 7. AUTOMATE IF REPEATED (Level 9, Level 10)
# wrap repeated steps in functions; loop over files/groups

# 8. REPORT REPRODUCIBLY (Level 14)
# Quarto document with inline code, captioned tables/figures, citations
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
177.2 Demonstration

# Applying the template to a brand-new, unfamiliar dataset:
new_data <- read.csv("clinic_survey_2025.csv")

str(new_data)          # What columns exist? What types? (Level 2)
summary(new_data)       # Ranges, missingness, obvious outliers (Level 7)
colSums(is.na(new_data))  # Exactly how much missing data, per column (Level 4)

'data.frame':   340 obs. of  8 variables:
 $ id                 : int  1 2 3 4 5 ...
 $ activity_minutes   : int  45 20 NA 60 30 ...
 $ cognitive_score    : num  87.2 79.5 91.0 NA 85.3 ...
 ...
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
177.3 Explore
Before writing a single line of cleaning or modeling code on a new dataset, spend deliberate time just looking: how many rows and columns, which columns have missing data and how much, what data types R actually assigned each column (Level 2’s str()), and whether any values look like impossible outliers (Level 4’s data-cleaning mindset). Skipping this step is the single most common cause of a modeling result that looks plausible but is quietly built on a data problem nobody caught.
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
177.4 Solve the Problem
A complete checklist for designing your own capstone, from question to finished report:


State the research question precisely. Not “does activity relate to health,” but “is average daily activity (minutes) associated with a specific, named outcome (cognitive test score), adjusting for age and sex?” — precise enough that you could say, in advance, what result would confirm or refute it.

Import and inspect structure (read.csv(), str(), summary(), Levels 2-3) before assuming anything about the data’s shape or quality.

Clean deliberately (Level 4): identify and handle missing values, fix mismatched types, remove duplicates, recode categorical variables — and document every cleaning decision, since a reader needs to know what was changed and why.

Explore descriptively first (Levels 5, 7): group-wise means, cross-tabulations, correlations — form a data-grounded expectation before fitting any model.

Visualize the key relationship (Level 8) before modeling it — a scatter plot or boxplot often reveals a nonlinearity, outlier, or grouping effect a model’s summary output alone would hide.

Choose the model matching your variable types (Levels 11-12): a t-test or ANOVA for group comparisons, correlation/regression for two numeric variables, logistic regression for a binary outcome, always checking diagnostics afterward.

Interpret honestly (every level from 7 onward): report both significant and non-significant findings, distinguish statistical significance from practical/clinical importance, and explicitly note the sample this conclusion applies to.

Write it up reproducibly (Level 14): a Quarto document with inline code (not hardcoded numbers), captioned tables and figures, cross-references, and proper citations — so the exact same source file regenerates correctly if the data is later updated.
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
177.5 Interpret the Result
This checklist isn’t a rigid recipe to follow mechanically — it’s the same underlying discipline every capstone in this level actually used, made explicit and portable to any dataset. The specific R functions you’ll reach for at each step will vary depending on your data (a survival-analysis question needs different Level-12-style modeling tools than a simple group comparison), but the order of operations — understand before cleaning, explore before modeling, interpret honestly, report reproducibly — transfers to any project, in any domain, with any dataset.

Common Mistakes


Jumping straight to modeling a new dataset without exploring it first. Skipping Level 2’s str() and Level 7’s summary() risks fitting a model on data with an unnoticed type mismatch, an implausible outlier, or missingness patterns that silently bias the result.

Choosing a familiar test or model reflexively, rather than matching it to the actual variable types and question. Level 11’s entire premise was that variable types (numeric vs. categorical, one group vs. several) determine the correct test — a mismatched choice (like a t-test on a 5-category variable) produces a meaningless result even if R runs it without error.

Skipping the reproducible write-up step because “the analysis is already done.” An analysis that only exists as scattered console output and half-remembered steps isn’t finished — Level 14’s discipline (inline code, no hardcoded numbers, proper citations) is what makes a result something someone else (including future-you) can actually trust and rebuild on.
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
177.6 Practice Problem
Using this checklist, sketch out (in writing, not necessarily runnable code) the first three steps you would take with a completely new dataset of 500 hospital patients, including columns for length_of_stay, readmitted (Yes/No), age, and department.
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
177.7 Challenge Problem
Pick one topic mentioned in this course’s original Level 15 preview but not covered by Capstones 1-5 (physical activity and health outcomes, cognitive decline, or neighborhood deprivation). Describe, using this page’s 8-step checklist, exactly what a complete capstone investigating that question would involve — what columns a suitable dataset would need, what cleaning steps might be required, what visualization would best reveal the key relationship, and which specific test or model from Levels 11-12 would fit the question.
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone

title: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
text:
177.8 Key Takeaways

Every capstone in this level followed the same underlying process — state the question precisely, understand the data before touching it, clean deliberately, explore before modeling, choose the test matching your variable types, interpret honestly, and report reproducibly.
This 8-step checklist is portable to any dataset or domain — the specific R functions change, but the order of operations and the underlying discipline do not.
Finishing an analysis means producing a reproducible report (Level 14), not just a script’s worth of console output — a result nobody else (including future-you) can rebuild isn’t actually finished.


Knowledge Check


Multiple choice. According to this page’s checklist, what should happen immediately after importing a completely new dataset?

Fit a regression model right away
Inspect its structure and summary statistics before cleaning or modeling anything
Write the final report
Delete all rows with any missing data immediately


True or false. The specific R functions used at each checklist step should be identical for every dataset and research question.
Predict the output. If a new dataset’s research question involves comparing a numeric outcome across 4 distinct groups, which test from Levels 11-12 would be the appropriate match?
Short answer. Why is “the analysis is done” not the same as “the project is finished,” according to this page’s final checklist step?


Show answers


b) Inspect its structure and summary statistics (str(), summary(), missing-data checks) before cleaning or modeling anything.

False. The specific functions depend on the dataset’s variable types and the research question — the underlying process (understand, clean, explore, model, interpret, report) stays consistent, but the tools applied at each step vary.
One-way ANOVA (Level 11, Page 10) — the appropriate test for comparing a numeric outcome’s mean across three or more groups.
A finished project must be reproducible and reportable by someone else (or future-you) — an analysis that exists only as scattered console output, without a properly written-up, reproducible document (Level 14), can’t be verified, rebuilt, or trusted the way a finished report can.
crumbs: 177  Designing Your Own Independent Capstone
